Justification: The study states it uses the exposure sensitivity resilience sliding ecological vulnerability evaluation framework to quantify the Ecological Vulnerability Index. Resilience is not quantified as recovery after a defined disturbance but as a Resilience Index defined as RI = 1 - γ where γ is the autoregressive coefficient in NPPt = α×Tempt + β×Pret + γ×NPPt-1 + εt. The target variable used in the core model is net primary productivity described as NPP anomalies and used to represent ecosystem status in the ecological vulnerability index. The approach is a developed multivariate linear autoregressive model using temperature precipitation and NPP time series with a 10 year sliding window. The first author affiliation is Beijing Normal University Beijing China. The study area is the Yellow River Basin China. The paper provides no explicit coordinates so the location is approximated to the basin scale and the habitat includes croplands grasslands forests and bare lands so the dominant habitat type cannot be reduced to a single IUCN class.

The study evaluates coral reef resilience using two reef metrics including State as observed coral cover and Performance as deviation from expected change in cover provided no acute disturbances occurred. Performance is based on model predicted rates of coral cover increase and is interpreted as an index of reef functioning that evaluates observed State relative to expected State. The Performance score system is derived from observed cover change between surveys relative to the distribution of expected changes in cover. This makes resilience quantification focused on the rate of coral cover increase and its reduction which the paper describes as reef recovery and recovery rates. The target variable used in the resilience metric is coral cover including total coral cover and cover of four coral groups. Because cover is measured across multiple coral taxa and groups the level is community. Data come from AIMS monitoring on 122 reefs in the Central and Southern Great Barrier Reef between 1992 and 2017 which is field observation. The first author affiliation is in Australia at the Australian Institute of Marine Science and the University of Queensland. The field setting is the Great Barrier Reef in Australia with regions given as Central 16 to 21 S and South 21 to 25 S so the average coordinate is approximately S20.5 E149.0 and the range is within two degrees. The habitat is shallow coral reefs on the continental shelf which matches Marine Neritic. The focal organisms are corals described as scleractinian coral and coral groups which are animals. Acute disturbances considered include heat stress and bleaching risk events and cyclones which are climatic and crown of thorns seastar outbreaks which are biotic and river runoff and low salinity exposure which are hydrological. Disturbance metrics are treated with cumulative lagged effects over 1 3 or 5 year windows and Performance is modelled using years with no acute disturbance which reflects a press style framing of ongoing stressors over the observation window. The observation duration spans from 1992 to 2017 which is 25 years or 9131 days.

The study states it tested resistance and resilience by exposing freshwater mesocosms to pulse applications of glyphosate and imidacloprid and press nutrient enrichment and it tracked bacterioplankton density and carbon substrate use and taxonomic diversity and composition over a 43 day experiment. Resilience is evaluated from observed temporal community trajectories after pesticide pulses including the statement that 24 days after glyphosate application the precise amplicon sequence variants do not return and tend to be replaced by phylogenetically close taxa. The discussion explicitly frames stability as including resistance and resilience by stating two properties of ecological stability resistance and resilience are at play. The resilience quantification is based on whether community composition returns toward control after disturbance and the text describes recovery being apparent with weighted UniFrac on day 30 when control and high glyphosate communities approach each other. The target variable used for this resilience claim is bacterioplankton community composition measured by weighted UniFrac and PRCs at phylum to genus levels. The work is a mesocosm experiment conducted at LEAP at McGill University Gault Nature Reserve in Quebec Canada with coordinates 45 degrees 32 minutes N and 73 degrees 08 minutes W. The first author affiliation is Université de Montréal in Canada. The disturbances are manipulated chemical pesticide pulses and manipulated resource nutrient enrichment. The experiment ran from day 1 to day 43 and the first and last bacterioplankton sampling days shown are day 1 and day 43 giving 42 days between first and last measurement.

The study states that it experimentally compared the effects of heatwaves and mowing and that ecosystem CO2 fluxes were recorded. Resistance indices focused on changes in ecosystem carbon fluxes during the heatwave stage and recovery indices focused on recovery processes after heatwaves. Resistance was calculated from the heatwave simulation period and recovery was calculated for each day following the simulated heatwaves with recovery equal to 1 indicating no effect anymore. The heatwave treatment was a simulated heatwave of five consecutive days using open top chambers and heaters. After the heatwaves the OTCs were removed and recovery was calculated nearly 21 days after the heatwaves with an average value for the 21 day recovery. The target variables used to quantify resistance and recovery were NEP RE and GEP which are ecosystem carbon fluxes. The framework is presented as resistance and recovery as the two stability metrics that were quantified. The first author affiliation is Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University in China. The study sites are in Inner Mongolia China with coordinates 42 02 N 116 17 E and 49 20 23 N 119 59 37 E. The dominant species are Stipa krylovii and Leymus chinensis which are plants. The disturbance pattern is pulse because the heatwave is imposed for five consecutive days and then recovery is observed after it ends. Observation duration for resilience is 5 days of heatwave plus 21 days after heatwave.

The introduction defines resilience as the capacity to return to a reference state over time following a perturbation and discusses resistance in parallel as the degree to which they remain unchanged by disturbance. The methods imposed three disturbance treatments once in 1998 light trimming heavy trimming and burning with an undisturbed control. Recovery was measured using annual vascular plant measurements from 1999 to 2012 and additional moss and belowground measurements in 2012. Resilience was quantified using the post disturbance resilience index V where a value of 0 indicates the disturbed plot has fully recovered relative to the control plot measured at the same time. This index is based on the difference between disturbed and control values rather than a rate or time to recovery. Target variables used in the resilience index include total cover species richness Shannon diversity community composition via PCA scores and cover of dominant species plus moss biomass and multiple soil abiotic and microbial variables including PLFA based microbial community measures. The study system is 30 forested lake islands in lakes Hornavan and Uddjaure in northern Sweden with coordinates given as 65 8550N to 668090N and 178430E to 17 8550E. The first author affiliation is Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences in Umea Sweden. The disturbance pattern is a single event because disturbances were applied once in August 1998 and no other disturbances were implemented. The observation window for resilience spans from pre disturbance measurement in August 1998 to final measurements in August 2012 which is 14 years or 5110 days.

The study quantified tree growth response to the extreme 1985–1986 drought using dendroecological reconstructions of radial growth transformed to basal area increment. It calculated resistance recovery and resilience growth indices following Lloret et al. 2011 with formulas Rt = Gd/Gprev Rc = Gpost/Gd and Rs = Gpost/Gprev. Resilience Rs was defined as the difference in growth between pre and post drought periods using mean BAI in 1983–1985 and 1987–1989 with 1986 as the drought year. This is an observed measurement because it uses the system state variable BAI derived from tree rings before during and after the drought event. The framework is presented as resistance recovery and resilience indices together. The resilience quantification is recovery degree because Rs compares post drought growth to pre drought growth as a degree of return. The target variable used to quantify resilience is basal area increment which is a growth metric. Analyses were performed at the tree level for 231 silver fir individuals. The approach is field observation because trees were sampled in 20 stands and increment cores were taken in the Central Spanish Pyrenees. The first author affiliation is Instituto Pirenaico de Ecologia IPE CSIC Zaragoza Spain. The study area coordinates are given as 42.220–42.500 N and 0.480 W–0.390 E and the average is reported. The disturbance is an unmanipulated climatic extreme drought event. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because the drought event is bounded to 1985–1986 and recovery is assessed in the subsequent years. The observation window for the drought response metrics spans from 1983 to 1989 which is 7 years.

The study used long term rocky reef monitoring data from 1999 to 2019 at four reserve sites and four fished sites and measured exposed sea urchin density turf complex percent cover and adult fucoid and Ecklonia radiata density in repeated surveys. It quantified temporal stability as the inverse of the coefficient of variation 1/CV for exposed sea urchin density turf complex cover adult fucoid density and E radiata density. It also assessed ecosystem stability using the number of shifts between ecosystem states and multivariate distance in state space but the explicit stability metric 1/CV is an inverse variance measure. The framework links ecosystem health and stability by stating stability is a key attribute of ecosystem health and the study focuses on ecosystem stability and resilience in that context. The approach was field observation because sampling was non destructive and done on SCUBA with repeated quadrat surveys. The first author affiliation is Institute of Marine Science The University of Auckland in New Zealand. The field sites are in the Cape Rodney to Okakari Point Leigh Marine Reserve area in northeastern New Zealand with coordinates provided for all eight sites and the average coordinate is reported. The habitat is shallow temperate rocky reef in the subtidal nearshore environment which fits Marine Neritic. The resilience related stability variables involve kelp and fucoid macroalgae and sea urchins so the taxa include Plantae and Animalia. The disturbance contrast is no take protection versus fished reefs which reflects ongoing fishing pressure so it is unmanipulated biological resource use treated as a press driver across the monitoring period.

The study states that it quantified ecosystem functional resilience and resistance in response to experimentally simulated pulses of precipitation and it used a 39 mm irrigation pulse. It measured state variables before and 1 3 7 and 15 days following irrigation including net ecosystem exchange gross ecosystem exchange evapotranspiration ecosystem respiration soil respiration net photosynthesis and leaf stomatal conductance. It quantified resistance as Euclidean distances moved immediately following the pulse and it quantified return toward the pre pulse reference state by examining Euclidean distance from each treatment initial position to later positions and noting that by day 15 treatments had nearly returned to their initial positions. It predicted that proximity to the pre pulse reference state on day 15 would reveal treatment mediated differences in ecosystem functional resilience. The framework is presented as resilience and resistance as allied concepts with resistance defined as tendency to remain in a reference state and resilience described as characteristics that return a system to a reference state. The work was conducted at the Santa Rita Experimental Range rainfall manipulation experiment at 31.78 N 110.88 W in southern Arizona. The first author affiliation is University of Arizona in the United States. The plots were monotypic stands of native and non native warm season bunchgrasses so the taxon is plants. The habitat is described as a semi arid grassland.

The study states it quantified the annual growth of Hylocomium splendens from 2016 to 2022 and compared growth during and after the 2018 extreme drought across clearcuts retention patches and mature forests. Annual maximum width of segments was used as the sole growth measure and analysed by year and habitat type which is direct observational state variable data. The paper explicitly examines growth response and recovery following the extreme drought of 2018 and reports that the growth reduction persisted in 2019 and 2020. This supports an observed resilience measurement based on post disturbance dynamics. Resilience is not defined as part of an explicit equation framework in the introduction methods or discussion. The quantification aligns with recovery rate because resilience is assessed via changes in annual growth in the years following the drought and via relationships using delta growth differences versus delta microclimate differences rather than time to return. The target variable used to quantify resilience is annual maximum width as annual growth of the moss. The organisation level is population because growth is measured across multiple shoots per plot and summarised as mean annual maximum widths per plot. The approach is field observation because moss samples and microclimate were collected in plots across forest sites in Sweden without experimental manipulation of drought. The first author affiliation is Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences in Uppsala Sweden. The field data were collected in Ånge Municipality central Sweden with coordinates spanning 62 degrees 24 minutes 23.9 seconds N to 62 degrees 27 minutes 54.7 seconds N and 15 degrees 28 minutes 55.7 seconds E to 15 degrees 39 minutes 36.3 seconds E. The habitat is boreal forest including mature forests clearcuts and retention patches. The disturbance is the 2018 summer drought described as an extreme drought event in Sweden and treated as a discrete event year. The observation window for growth spans 2016 to 2022 which is seven years corresponding to 2555 days.

The study used an individual based grassland community model and ran a full factorial simulation experiment crossing herbivore exclusion with nutrient enrichment. Nutrient enrichment was simulated as an increase in belowground resources for 100 simulation years and then resources returned to pre enrichment levels for 100 simulation years to analyze recovery. The paper states it investigated resistance and recovery sensu Hodgson et al. 2015 and defines resistance as the magnitude of change directly after a disturbance and recovery as the amount of time needed for a system to reach its original state measured as time to recovery TTR. Recovery was quantified as the number of years required for Shannon diversity to return to within two standard deviations of control diversity and stay there for 10 simulation years. Because resilience was operationalized via resistance and recovery and recovery was measured as time to return the quantification is recovery time. The target variable used for resilience metrics was Shannon diversity which is a community level diversity measure. The disturbance of interest is nutrient enrichment implemented in the model as increased belowground resources and herbivore exclusion implemented as complete elimination of aboveground and belowground herbivory submodels. The disturbance is treated as time bounded with a defined experimental window followed by a recovery window so the pattern is pulse. The first author affiliation is University of Potsdam and Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research in Germany. The model was parameterized with data from German grasslands collected through the Biodiversity Exploratories so the field country is Germany. The observation duration for disturbance and resilience spans 100 years treatment plus 100 years recovery which equals 200 years or 73000 days.

The methods state that sampling was conducted as pre-and-post hydrologic disturbance events and that a storm event caused a flash flood or spate on 17 September 2016 with post-spate sampling after one month after four months and after eight months. The methods also state that the dry period was extended from October to mid-June and stream flows were substantially reduced and that May served as a post-disturbance to reduced flow data with January taken as the pre-disturbance data. The paper states that resistance and resilience scores were computed based on the ratios of post-disturbance to pre-disturbance data following Melo et al. 2003. The introduction defines resilience as the potential of a system to recover from and maintain its basic structures and functions following disturbances and discusses resistance and resilience together as how stream communities respond. The dependent variables used throughout the analyses are taxa abundance species density and diversity and these are the variables used in the resistance and resilience score calculations. The resilience score is described as a recovery score and is calculated from post versus pre disturbance values which operationalizes recovery as a rate like ratio rather than a time to recovery. The disturbance is hydrological because the study focuses on spates and reduced flow and describes flash flood or spate and drying or low-flow. The spate is treated as a discrete event with sampling on specific days after the spates and specific months after the September 2016 event which matches a pulse. The reduced flow is treated as an extended dry period with substantially reduced flows and persistent reduced flow conditions which matches a press. The study area is Dinki watershed in the central highlands of Ethiopia with coordinates given between 9 23 19.99 to 9 31 3.90 N and 039 44 43.47 to 039 44 46.47 E and the average coordinate is reported. The first author affiliation includes Jimma University Ethiopia and Injibara University Ethiopia. The observation window spans from the September 2016 spate through the July 2017 sampling effort which is about 640 days.

The introduction states that ecosystem stability is multidimensional and is “comprising resistance, resilience and recovery” which supports the framework stability = resistance + resilience + recovery. The study defines ecological resilience as “capacity to regain structure, function and processes despite disturbance” and tests whether communities “return to predisturbance configuration” in sequential interseismic phases. Resilience is evaluated using sediment core time series data spanning “the past 1100 years” with samples grouped into postseismic and interseismic phases after four earthquakes which is direct observation of community change through time. The main response variables used in the core analyses are “microbial community composition” from 16S rRNA metabarcoding and “predicted enzymes” and “enzyme composition” from paprica inferred functions which are used in multivariate comparisons between phases. The disturbance is described as “four large earthquake induced sediment pulses” and earthquakes are a geophysical natural event treated as pulse disturbances. The methods give the study site coordinates as “43°43′10″S, 169°24′8″E” for Lake Paringa in New Zealand and the catchment is “dominated by undisturbed temperate rainforest”. The first author affiliation is the Cawthron Institute in Nelson New Zealand. The observation window is the sediment record spanning “a period of 1100 years” which is approximately 1100 times 365 days.

The study states that it analysed long-term vegetation records from two sites spanning nine decades and that it quantified the historical response of resident species to cheatgrass invasion. Vegetation data at both sites are described as density of plants in permanently marked 1 m2 quadrats sampled repeatedly between 1923 and 1973 at USSES and between 1950 and 2006 at INEEL. The analysis quantified change in vegetation composition as species dissimilarity relative to initial conditions and interpreted a decline in dissimilarity at USSES as recovery following the decline in cheatgrass. The discussion explicitly says community composition at USSES appeared to recover towards the initial conditions after peak cheatgrass density but there was no evidence for similar recovery at INEEL. Resilience is not defined with an explicit equation and is discussed in relation to thresholds and state and transition models without a stated component framework. Resilience is quantified through the degree of return of community composition toward initial conditions using species dissimilarity trajectories. The target variable used for this quantification is vegetation composition expressed as species dissimilarity and community transitions among statistically distinguishable plant communities. The focal biological level is community because the quantified variable is species composition across multiple plant species within communities. The approach is field observation because the data are long-term field records from permanently marked plots rather than an imposed experiment or simulation. The first author affiliation is Texas A and M University in the United States and the study sites are in Idaho USA with Table 1 giving locations near Dubois Idaho and Idaho Falls Idaho around 44 N 112 W and 43 N 112 W. The habitat is sagebrush steppe dominated by Artemisia shrubs and perennial grasses which fits shrubland. The disturbance involved in the resilience assessment is cheatgrass invasion described as a biotic invasion and it is treated as a long term driver across decades rather than a discrete event. The observation duration for the longest record is from 1923 to 2006 which is 83 years or 30295 days.

The study states it uses a fully parameterized simulation model and a simple analytical model and it defines critical thresholds of grazing and coral cover beyond which resilience is lost. It further states that the hysteresis plot defines thresholds and bifurcation points and that for a given level of grazing a reef will be resilient if disturbance and recovery do not force its state below the unstable equilibrium. This treats resilience as the ability to avoid a shift to an alternative stable macroalgal dominated state which matches a threshold based latitude concept. The resilience plot is generated by recording the probability that a reef drops below the mean unstable equilibrium during 25 year stochastic simulations with hurricanes. The target variables used in the resilience analysis are coral cover and grazing and the resilience plot uses initial coral cover and the unstable equilibrium boundary. Coral cover is a cover variable and it is an ecosystem state variable describing reef state. The approach is modeling and simulation because resilience is quantified from imposed stochastic hurricane disturbance in simulations. The first author affiliation is University of Exeter UK. The model is compared to field data from Jamaica and the figure caption specifies forereefs in Jamaica at 10 m depth so the field country is Jamaica. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to Jamaica. Hurricanes are treated as acute events occurring stochastically with a 20 year periodicity which is a pulse disturbance. The simulation uses a 25 year run with discrete six month steps so the observation duration is 25 years which is 9125 days.

The study states that genetic diversity can increase resistance defined as decreased biomass loss or resilience defined as recovery to pre-disturbance conditions which places resilience as a component alongside resistance under stability. Resilience was directly measured using plot time series of Zostera shoot density after disturbance with repeated measurements every 2 to 4 weeks from June 2005 to June 2007. The methods describe an experimental disturbance of above-ground biomass removal with 0 percent 33 percent or 66 percent of shoots clipped in January 2006 and the results quantify resilience as time to recovery to pre-disturbance shoot density. The results explicitly report that recovery time to pre-disturbance densities varied by diversity and disturbance and that polycultures recovered faster than monocultures at high disturbance. Because resilience quantification is based on recovery time the target variable used is shoot density. The work is a field experiment conducted in an existing seagrass bed in Bodega Harbor located about 100 km north of San Francisco Bay California USA. The experimental clipping was chosen to simulate grazing by geese which is a biotic disturbance and the natural Ulva bloom is also a biotic disturbance treated as a natural disturbance event. Both disturbances are treated as discrete events with subsequent recovery or post event response. The observation window spans from June 2005 to June 2007 which is two years. The first author affiliation is Florida State University in the United States. The habitat is a marine embayment seagrass bed in shallow coastal waters consistent with the neritic zone.

The introduction states that freeze thaw cycles are frequent and that xylem embolism induced by freeze thaw cycles threatens hydraulic integrity. The methods quantify winter embolism resilience using percentage loss of hydraulic conductivity in the dormant cold season PLCd which is attributed to the degree of embolism in dormant cold season and is calculated as ((Khmax − Kh)/Khmax) × 100%. Lower PLCd indicates less deviation from maximum conductivity after winter conditions and the results describe Acer and Betula showing a significantly lower PLCd than Populus. The discussion interprets Acer and Betula as having greater resistance to freeze thaw cycle induced xylem embolism and or greater ability in winter embolism refilling and links this to high soluble sugar concentrations measured in April. Resilience is not defined as part of an explicit equation framework in the introduction methods or discussion. The resilience quantification is therefore resistance because it is based on the degree of hydraulic conductivity loss after winter rather than a recovery rate time or degree over a post disturbance trajectory. The target variable used to quantify resilience is PLCd which is a functional performance parameter of stems rather than cover or diversity. The study uses field collected branches and cores at the Changbai Mountain Forest Ecosystems station with coordinates 128 degrees 28 minutes E and 42 degrees 24 minutes N in Jilin province northeast China. The first author affiliation includes the Institute of Applied Ecology Chinese Academy of Sciences in Shenyang China. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic because freeze thaw cycles and winter temperature fluctuations around 0C are described as the cause of embolism and no experimental manipulation is imposed. The disturbance is treated as press because the site experiences approximately 100 days per year with daily fluctuations below and above 0C and PLCd integrates the winter season effect without a defined recovery interval. PLCd sampling occurred between 17 and 19 April 2018 giving a two day measurement window for the resilience observation.

The study experimentally manipulated disturbance using a temperature gradient applied for 48 h and then measured post disturbance dynamics. Resistance was measured as compositional dissimilarity immediately post disturbance at time 0. Recovery was measured by modeling compositional dissimilarity through time at 0 3 6 and 9 months and estimating recovery rate from the slope at the last time point. Resilience was defined as the disturbance intensity at which community collapse occurs and was tested as a threshold along the disturbance gradient using segmented linear models at 9 months. The response variable used for these metrics was Bray Curtis compositional dissimilarity of disturbed communities to their relative control communities. The focal system was multi trophic microarthropod communities in moss microcosms. The first author affiliation is University of Victoria in Canada. Moss carpets were collected within a 10 km radius of Victoria British Columbia Canada. The moss mats were placed under a forest canopy of Garry Oak and Douglas fir. The disturbance was a discrete heating event so it is treated as a pulse. Sampling started 2 weeks after disturbance and continued to 9 months after disturbance which is about 319 days.

The study defines ecological resilience as capacity to maintain structure functions and processes in the face of perturbations and it operationalizes loss of resilience using early warning signals based on critical slowing down described as reduction in the recovery rate after a small disturbance. It quantifies early warning signals using AR1 and CV and composite EWSs and it states that increases in autocorrelation and variance depict the dynamics of resilience. The data are direct long term monitoring time series spanning 1956 to 2019 of water clarity nutrients temperature fish and plankton and paleo diatom records and thus resilience is measured from observed system trajectories. The main state variables used for regime shift and EWS analyses include water clarity Secchi depth total phytoplankton abundance cladoceran abundance phytoplankton community composition PC1 and body size metrics. Water clarity is treated as an ecosystem level state variable and is measured repeatedly and used in EWS analysis. The work is based on monthly surveys at two sampling stations and annual fish yield records and sediment core diatom records and thus the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation is in Wuhan China and the study site is Lake Donghu in Wuhan Hubei Province China. The coordinates are given as 30 degrees 33 minutes 02 seconds north and 114 degrees 21 minutes 40 seconds east. The habitat is a shallow lake and fits Wetland. The diatom assemblages and phytoplankton are included and diatoms are identified as taxa such as Aulacoseira granulata and Stephanodicus minutulus which are Chromista. The drivers considered are climate warming eutrophication TN and TP and fish stocking and these are treated as ongoing pressures over decades rather than discrete events so the disturbance pattern is press. The observation duration for the monitoring time series is 64 years from 1956 to 2019 which is 23360 days using 365 days per year.

The study sampled insect communities in 16 Louisiana coastal salt marshes before and after Hurricane Isaac and analysed changes over time. Resistance and resilience were studied using the Shannon index and PERMANOVA and the results state that diversity and composition decreased immediately after the cyclone and then returned to pre-hurricane states within one year. This uses observed time series community data rather than proxies. Resilience is presented alongside resistance as resistance and resilience. Resilience is quantified by the time to return to pre-hurricane levels or states as stated for the Shannon index returning in just over a month and composition returning from 22 d to one year after the hurricane and approaching pre-hurricane states within one year. The target variable used for resilience quantification is community diversity measured by the Shannon index. The focal subjects are insect communities which are Animalia and the level is community. The approach is field observation with repeated sweep net collections. The first author affiliation is Salisbury University in the USA. The field data are from coastal Louisiana USA with site coordinates given as 29.25 to 29.32 N and 89.36 to 89.52 W and the average is reported. The disturbance is an unmanipulated hurricane which is a climatic event treated as a discrete event with post-event recovery sampling. Sampling occurred from five days before landfall to one year after landfall which spans 370 days.

The study states it aims to bolster resilience by assessing risks faced by urban trees due to climatic shifts and it calculates three indicators climate exposure safety margin and climate risk. Safety margin is defined as the difference between baseline city climate and species tolerance limits estimated from MaxEnt model outputs and climate risk is defined as the disparity between exposure and safety margin. The analysis uses climate variables AMT MTWM MTCM AMP and PDQ and WorldClim baseline 1970 to 2000 and future 2041 to 2060 climate projections plus MaxEnt species distribution modeling based on occurrence records. There is no direct measurement of recovery dynamics of trees such as time series of growth mortality cover or biomass after disturbance. Therefore resilience is inferred from proxy indicators based on abiotic climate variables and modeled tolerance limits rather than observed system recovery. Resilience is discussed as a general goal and not defined in an explicit equation framework that includes resilience. The approach is modeling and simulation because it constructs and evaluates MaxEnt species distribution models and projects future risk under SSP245 and SSP585. The first author affiliation is Tianjin University in China. Species occurrence records are global from CVH and GBIF and thus the data source for quantifying tolerance is global. The focal application is urban trees in Tianjin and the habitat context is urban.

The study proposes resilience as mean exit time which it defines as the expected time required to cross the border of the basin of attraction and it estimates this from time series by fitting a Langevin equation and then solving for mean exit time. It applies the method to model generated data and to two real time series namely Cyanobacteria biomass in Lake Mendota and a GRIP ice core calcium record as a climate proxy. The Lake Mendota series is described as estimations of the biomass of Cyanobacteria measured as concentrations of phycocyanin recorded every minute throughout the year 2011 while ice free and the analysis computes mean exit time in minutes. The climate example uses logarithm of calcium concentrations from the GRIP ice core record spanning the last glaciation and computes mean exit time in years. Because resilience is quantified as an exit time it matches recovery time. The approach includes modeling and simulation because the model is fit and used to compute exit times and it includes field observation because it uses observed time series from Lake Mendota and the GRIP ice core record. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University Netherlands. The Lake Mendota data are from Wisconsin USA and the GRIP ice core record is from Greenland. The text provides no coordinates so the location and range are inferred from the named sites Lake Mendota and Central Greenland. The disturbance is treated as a never ending natural regime of shocks and fluctuations and the climate example explicitly mentions stochastic forcing from variations in wind stress heating and freshwater transport so the disturbance pattern is press and the disturbance type is unmanipulated climatic. The observation duration is based on the climate time series shown from 90 to 20 kyr before present which is 70 kyr.

The study imposed extreme drought using passive rainout shelters with a 66% reduction in growing season precipitation and also included a two year drought followed by two years of recovery which is a field experiment. Resilience was examined as recovery after drought and was quantified from aboveground net primary productivity measured by harvesting aboveground live plant material and drying and weighing it. The methods state that resilience was calculated as the percentage of recovery to control and the abstract reports ANPP of drought plots was 83% of ambient plots one year post drought and fully recovered by the second year which is recovery degree. The introduction discusses resistance and resilience together and defines resistance as maintaining function during drought and resilience as recovery after drought which supports resistance + resilience as the framework. The target variable used for resilience quantification is ANPP which is measured as harvested aboveground plant material mass so the target variable type is biomass and the level is ecosystem because it is total ANPP as an ecosystem function measure. The study site is at the Erguna Forest Steppe Ecotone Research Station with coordinates 50•10∕46∕∕N and 119•22∕56∕∕E in Inner Mongolia China. The first author affiliation includes Lanzhou University in China. The disturbance is climatic because it is extreme drought and it is manipulated because precipitation was reduced by shelters. The drought treatment had a defined start and stop with subsequent recovery years so the disturbance pattern is pulse. Measurements were taken during peak growing season from 2015 to 2018 which spans four years or 1460 days.

The introduction defines resilience as a theoretical property in a bifurcation diagram as the distance between the stable line and the unstable line. The study objective is to classify states and quantify state transition risk using hidden Markov models rather than to measure recovery after a disturbance. In the methods the models estimate transition probabilities and relate them to resilience predictors and perturbation predictors using annual survey data. Resilience predictors are defined as variables measured at time t minus 1 including current nutrient levels and SAV occurrence and fish variables. The discussion states resilience of a lake is measured by current nutrient levels and SAV cover and perturbations are measured by increases in fish biomass. Because resilience is operationalized through these proxy predictors and transition risk rather than direct recovery dynamics the measurement type is inferred. The paper does not present an explicit equation framework that defines resilience as a sum of components. The target variables used to represent resilience in the text are current nutrient levels and SAV cover or occurrence. The data come from 123 shallow lakes sampled in Minnesota USA. The first author affiliation is the University of Minnesota in the United States. The habitat is shallow lakes which fit wetland. No coordinates are provided in the text. Disturbance is not directly quantified as an observed imposed event for resilience measurement because the analysis focuses on inferred resilience predictors and transition risk. Observation duration is not applicable under inferred measurement.

The study quantified resilience using observed aboveground net primary productivity measured by harvesting biomass in quadrats and oven drying and weighing. Resilience was defined as ANPP 2019 divided by ANPP 2017 and resistance and recovery were also defined which matches the statement that resilience generally consists of two key processes resistance and recovery. Because resilience is calculated as a ratio comparing postflooding to preflooding ANPP it represents recovery degree rather than a rate or time. The target variable used to quantify resilience is ANPP which is an ecosystem productivity measure and therefore an ecosystem function at the ecosystem level. The approach is a field experiment because precipitation was manipulated with rain interception shelters and clipping was applied in plots in an alpine meadow. The first author affiliation is in Beijing China so the institution country is CN. The field site is in Hongyuan County on the Qinghai Tibetan Plateau China with coordinates given as 32.241333N and 102.241667E. The habitat is described as an alpine meadow and grassland ecosystems so the habitat type is Grassland. The subjects are plant species in the meadow so the taxon is Plantae. Disturbances include manipulated drought via precipitation reduction which is climatic and manipulated clipping which is a structural change and the focal extreme event is a 10 day flooding exposure which is unmanipulated hydrological. The flooding is treated as a 10 day event so the disturbance pattern is pulse. ANPP used for resistance recovery and resilience spans preflooding 2017 flooding 2018 and postflooding 2019 so the duration between first and last measurement is two years or 730 days.

The study states that it used a manipulative field experiment and quantified eelgrass shoot density at biweekly then monthly intervals. It reports a discrete grazing event by brant geese that caused up to 76 percent shoot loss and then tracks postherbivory recovery. It explicitly defines resilience as the rate of shoot recovery after the disturbance and reports no effect of diversity on resilience measured as the rate of shoot recovery. Therefore resilience measurement is observed and quantified as recovery rate using shoot density. The paper discusses resistance and resilience in parallel and states that diversity affected resistance but not resilience. The focal organism is the seagrass Zostera marina which is a marine angiosperm. The field site is an eelgrass bed in Bodega Bay California and the first author affiliation is University of California Davis. The disturbance includes transplant shock described as stress associated with transplantation and grazing by geese. The grazing and transplant shock are treated as discrete events with recovery observed afterward. The experiment ran from June 2002 to May 2003 which is about one year.

The study quantifies resilience using a model because it states We implemented a spatially explicit neutral metacommunity model in the MAP region. It defines the spatial resilience index as the ratio of metacommunity risks in the disturbed and undisturbed scenarios and defines metacommunity risk as the inverse of the product of average species persistence time its probability and average geographic range. Species persistence times are calculated by tracking agents in every community at each time step and tau is measured until the end of the observational window which is 1 × 10^6 generations. The disturbance scenarios are explicitly imposed in the model as drought modeled by varying habitat capacity with a 65 percent variation in average annual precipitation and as a road scenario with road modified dispersal and an instantaneous road placed in the middle of plots. Because resilience is quantified from the modeled response under disturbed versus undisturbed scenarios using SRI it is an observed type under the provided definition that includes imposed disturbances in simulations. The quantification is not recovery rate time degree resistance latitude or invariability because SRI is a ratio of risk metrics rather than a rebound speed or time to baseline. The target variables directly used in SRI are species persistence time which is a time to local extinction and geographic range defined as clusters of adjacent communities where the same species occurs. Species persistence time is a demographic parameter and geographic range based on spatial adjacency of occurrences is an interaction network like spatial connectivity measure. The level is community because SPT and geographic range are calculated for species within communities and then averaged for all species in each community. The approach is modeling and simulation because scenarios are simulated with the neutral metacommunity model. The first author affiliation is Tsinghua University Shenzhen China. The field region is the MAP trinational region where Bolivia Brazil and Peru meet and plots are in Peru Brazil and Bolivia. The MAP region extent is given as longitudinal extension 866 km and latitudinal extension 650 km and the location is therefore approximated by the MAP region center near S11 W69. The habitat is humid tropical rainforest and forest plots so the habitat type is Forest. The focal organisms are trees with DBH at least 10 cm so the taxon is Plantae. Disturbances are modeled drought and road construction corresponding to climatic and landuse and infrastructure development. The disturbance is treated as press because drought is a scenario of altered precipitation and the road scenario is a persistent infrastructure condition and the analysis compares stationary state patterns under each scenario. Observation duration is 1 × 10^6 generations and one generation is 100 years so duration is 100000000 years which is 36500000000 days.

The study uses a simple Lotka–Volterra model and states it will examine trade offs between resource storage and disturbance resilience and disturbance resistance. Disturbance is implemented in the model as an added density independent module mortality term d with disturbances occurring at a constant rate and d described as an instantaneous rate and a measure of disturbance severity. Resilience is treated alongside resistance as shown by the synthesis statement that both resistance and resilience to disturbance potentially increase competitive ability. Resilience is quantified via recovery from disturbance because inequality 13 is described as the rate of recovery from disturbance of the resilient species divided by the rate of recovery from disturbance of the persistent species and this recovery is expressed through growth terms involving d and r. The target variable used in the core analysis is predicted plant size at competitive equilibrium measured as number of modules and equilibrium module densities N which are abundances of modules within an individual genet. The biological level is individual because the model contrasts growth of individual genets and treats an individual as a population of modules. The disturbance types include fire because Figure 4 explicitly models fire regimes and fire tolerance and also include structural damage because disturbances are described as killing portions of genets and modeled as module mortality. The disturbance pattern is press because disturbances occur at a constant rate with no discrete event end. No field data location is provided for the model quantification so field country and coordinates are not available. The first author affiliation is University of Mississippi in the United States.

The study imposed a disturbance by fertilizing Peter Lake with daily additions of H3PO4 and NH4NO3 and compared it to an unenriched reference lake and a continuously enriched lake. Resilience indicators were calculated from observed time series using 28-d rolling windows of SD and lag-1 autocorrelation for chlorophyll a phycocyanin and dissolved oxygen saturation. The intervention was to halt nutrient additions when indicator alarms exceeded preset boundaries and the paper reports that chlorophyll a phycocyanin pH and DO%sat declined rapidly after cessation. The paper states the manipulated lake recovered to baseline with all four variables at or near reference lake values by approximately DOY 210 which provides a recovery time. The target variables used to quantify the recovery are pigments and oxygen saturation which the paper links to phytoplankton and cyanobacterial biomass and primary production and these are ecosystem level functional indicators. The work is a whole lake manipulation with field sensors and sampling described under Study Site and Design and High-Frequency Sensor Measurements. The first author affiliation is the University of Virginia in the United States. The field site is Peter and Paul Lakes at 46°25′N 89°50′W in Michigan United States. Nutrients were added from DOY 151 to 180 which is a discrete event window and the study then observed recovery after the halt. The study period is from May 9 to September 4 2015 which is 118 days.

The study uses monthly time series from February 2000 to December 2013 of MODIS enhanced vegetation index and MODIS derived climate variables to assess ecosystem sensitivity to climate variability. The vegetation sensitivity index is calculated from observed EVI variability relative to observed variability in air temperature water availability and cloudiness and is therefore based on direct observational trajectories rather than static proxies. The introduction links resilience to response to external forcing and states that slower responses identified through increased autocorrelation may be evidence of reduced recovery rates. The methods implement an AR1 regression including one month lagged EVI anomalies and the paper describes identifying regions with memory effects and reduced response rates to external forcing. Because the resilience related property emphasized is reduced response rates and memory effects via AR1 this corresponds to recovery rate. The target variable used in the sensitivity and AR1 analyses is vegetation productivity defined as EVI and EVI is described as correlating with chlorophyll content and photosynthetic activity so it is an ecosystem function proxy. The analysis is global at 5 km pixels across multiple biomes including tundra boreal forest tropical rainforest steppe prairie Caatinga and deserts so the habitat type is multi. The first author affiliation is University of Bergen Norway. The disturbance is climate variability in temperature water availability and cloud cover treated as environmental variability over the past 14 years without experimental manipulation so it is unmanipulated climatic. The disturbance is analysed as ongoing variability across the full record rather than a discrete event with a recovery interval so the pattern is press. The observation window from February 2000 to December 2013 is 14 years which is 5070 days using 365 days per year.

The paper quantifies resilience using a simulation model that applies environmental change as an imposed driver because it states that they used a simulation model and that environmental change was represented by an applied press disturbance that alters the mean environmental condition in the time series. The model output used as community level function is soil resource use where 100% resource use represented full acclimatisation and the recorded output was averaged over a final 10 year post disturbance period. This makes the measurement observed under the rule that a model with an explicitly imposed disturbance and resilience quantified from the system response is observed. Resilience is discussed alongside resistance and recovery in Box 1 which defines resilience resistance and recovery without defining a higher level variable that includes them. Resilience is quantified as the degree of post disturbance resource utilisation relative to full acclimatisation because they treat 100% resource use as full acclimatisation and analyse whether resource utilisation is above 50%. The target variable is therefore soil resource use or resource utilisation which is an ecosystem function. The organisation level is ecosystem because the target variable is an overall functional property of the system rather than a single species metric. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study constructed a simplified model and ran simulations for 1000 years pre change and 1000 years post change. The first author affiliation is University of Texas at Austin in the United States. No field data collection country or coordinates are provided because parameters were not based on measured data and the work is a theoretical exercise. The disturbance is model climatic because the imposed change is a shift in mean environmental condition intended to represent climate change. The disturbance pattern is press because the environmental change is applied as a press disturbance. The observation duration is 1020 years from the start of the 10 year pre disturbance sampling through the 1000 year post change simulation and the final 10 year post disturbance averaging which equals 372300 days.

The abstract states that functional alpha redundancy accumulated in the dry season enhanced community resilience and provided insurance to maintain the stability of community functional diversity during the flooding period which indicates resilience is not directly measured but inferred from functional redundancy. The methods define functional alpha redundancy and functional beta redundancy as fractions of taxonomic diversity or dissimilarity not expressed by functional diversity or dissimilarity following Ricotta and co-workers and the analyses quantify FRα and FRβ rather than any recovery rate time degree or invariability of a state variable. The study uses seasonal field surveys of macroinvertebrates at 13 wadeable sites in the Liuxi River with sampling in July and December from 2015 to 2018 which is field observation. The first author affiliation is Jinan University Guangzhou China. The study area is the Liuxi River in the Pearl River Delta China with coordinates given as 23.12 to 23.57 N and 113.10 to 114.02 E and the average coordinate is reported. The focal organisms are aquatic insects which are macroinvertebrates and thus Animalia and the target variables used to infer resilience are functional redundancy metrics derived from trait based dissimilarities and diversity relationships which fit Interaction. The habitat is a river system and is coded as Wetland under the provided habitat options. Disturbance is discussed as hydrological disturbance and flooding but because measurement is inferred the disturbance fields are NA and observation duration is NA by instruction.

The study states that thermal safety margins have been used to estimate vulnerability and that these margins overestimate resilience to warming. Resilience is not quantified from observed recovery dynamics but is evaluated using physiological heat tolerance endpoints and derived warming safety margins defined as the difference between 30 days exposure Tko and Tmax. The Methods describe compiling upper critical or lethal temperatures and exposure times from the literature and then using a two step correction procedure to predict heat tolerance at a 30 days window and calculate warming safety margin. This is a proxy based approach because it uses thermotolerance measures to quantify vulnerability rather than measuring population recovery after warming. No explicit resilience framework equation is defined and resilience is discussed in relation to thermal safety margins and vulnerability. The target variable used in the resilience related metric is heat tolerance Tko which is a physiological tolerance limit. The analysis is conducted at the level of populations and species because it uses populations collected at geographic locations and compares their heat tolerance to environmental temperatures. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study predicts 30 days Tko from TDT curves and environmental temperature and then computes margins rather than collecting new field time series. The first author affiliation is Universidad Católica de Chile in Santiago Chile. The dataset is global as shown by collection localities across the globe and the use of global sea surface temperature grids. The dominant habitat is marine neritic because Figure 1 notes a greater presence of coastal values compared to oceanic ones. The taxon is Animalia because the subjects are marine fish species. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because the measurement is inferred and no direct disturbance response time series is analysed.

Framework: stability = resistance + resilience + recovery + temporal stability
Justification: The study used two freshwater mesocosm experiments and quantified stability aspects after single pulse perturbations. It explicitly decomposed ecological stability into resistance resilience recovery and temporal stability and defined resilience as the speed of recovery from change. Resilience was quantified as the slope of regression of the log response ratio of function over time and as the slope of similarity over time for composition. This uses observed time series from repeated sampling days including days 0 1 2 4 8 16 and 32 in Sweden and every 3 days for 20 days in Austria. The disturbance was manipulated by adding dissolved organic matter to reduce light in Swedish lakes and by shading or sediment addition to reduce light in Austrian mesocosms and both are described as pulse treatments. Functional stability was assessed using bacterial abundance and phytoplankton and zooplankton biomass and compositional stability used Bray Curtis similarity. The first author affiliation is Carl von Ossietzky University Oldenburg in Germany. Field sites were three Swedish lakes and three Austrian freshwater systems with incubation in Lake Lunz and the Swedish lake coordinates are around 59.8 N and 17.4 E. The habitat is freshwater lakes which fits Wetland. Disturbance types include resource change through altered light availability and structural manipulation through sediment addition and shading.

The paper states that it uses a simple model and that the simulated lake can switch between alternate eutrophic and oligotrophic states. Resilience is explicitly defined as the amount of change required to shift the lake from one state to another and the distance between Xt and Xcrit is used as a measure of resilience. Xt is the concentration of phosphorus in the lake so the target variable used to quantify resilience is an abiotic parameter. The resilience measure is a threshold distance to a state shift which matches latitude because it is the magnitude of change required to shift to an alternative stable state. The approach is modeling and simulation because lake dynamics learning and management are implemented as equations and the model was implemented in Microsoft Excel. The first author affiliation is University of Wisconsin Madison Wisconsin USA so the institution country is US. The disturbance is modelled as random variation in P loading St drawn from a normal distribution and this corresponds to a modelled climatic type because it is environmental variation treated as shocks. The disturbance is pulse because shocks are discrete yearly draws rather than a continuous maintained driver. The time step is yearly and the model updates each simulated year so the observation duration is one year which is 365 days. No field data collection country or coordinates are provided because the study is a simulation model without a specific empirical site.

Framework: resilience = resistance + length of response time + length of recovery time
Justification: The study states that it uses tree-ring data to quantify resilience components and it defines extreme drought years using detrended tree-ring cellulose δ18O values and then examines tree-ring indices before during and after each drought. In Methods it defines resistance Rt as reduction in tree growth during disturbance relative to pre-disturbance using ring indices V1 and V2. It defines length of response time T1 as the number of years until tree-ring width reaches the minimum since the extreme drought. It defines length of recovery time T2 as the number of years for trees to return from the lowest growth to the pre-disturbance state using V3 equal or greater than V1. The Introduction explicitly lists the quantified components as resistance length of response time and length of recovery time. Because resilience is quantified from observed trajectories of tree-ring indices around identified drought events the measurement is observed. The disturbance is unmanipulated drought which is a climatic extreme and the drought years are treated as discrete events 1813 1833 1869 1941 1972 so the pattern is pulse. The target variable used in the resilience calculations is tree growth represented by tree-ring width indices at the individual tree level. The approach is field observation because increment cores were collected from natural forests and measured. The first author affiliation includes Institute of Botany Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing China so the institution country is CN. The study sites are in the central Himalayas near Nielamu and Ganesh and the coordinates given are 85°12′57″E 28°31′59″N and 85°13′42″E 28°26′20″N which are in Nepal so the field country is NP. Averaging the two site coordinates gives approximately N28.486 E85.223 and the sites are within less than two degrees so the location range is 0. The habitat is described as natural high-mountain forests dominated by juniper and fir so the habitat type is forest. The taxa whose resilience is quantified are trees Juniperus tibetica and Abies spectabilis which are plants. The observation window spans the past two centuries and the longest series are 1781 to 2005 so the duration is 224 years which is 81760 days and the reported value is based on that span.

The study frames resilience as demographic resilience by comparing demographic responses of clownfish populations to Pleistocene sea level fluctuations and associated habitat fragmentation. Resilience is evaluated using reconstructed trajectories of effective population size Ne with msmc2 and the text states that generalists maintained stable Ne during habitat fragmentation reflecting resilience while specialists had severe Ne declines without signs of population recovery following habitat reconnection. This uses system state variable data Ne over time and includes recovery dynamics so the measurement is observed. No explicit equation framework defining resilience as a sum of components is provided in the introduction or methods so the framework is none. Quantification aligns with recovery degree because the key resilience claim is whether Ne shows recovery after sea level rise and the discussion states specialists showed no signs of recovery with posterior sea level rise and struggled to recover Ne even after sea level rise and habitat reconnection. The target variable used for resilience is effective population size Ne which is a demographic parameter and it is analysed at the population level with populations defined by geographic regions. The approach combines field observation because fin clips were collected during SCUBA diving expeditions and modeling and simulation because demographic histories and Ne trajectories were reconstructed with coalescent models and analysed with GLMM and FDA. The first author affiliation is University of Lausanne in Switzerland. Field countries are taken from the listed sampling permit countries and include Mayotte New Caledonia Kenya Indonesia Thailand Madagascar Australia Maldives Solomon Islands Papua New Guinea Taiwan and Philippines. The habitat is shallow coral reef associated marine environment described as coral reef habitats and reef associated species so Marine.Neritic is the best match. The taxon is Animalia because the focal organisms are clownfishes. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic because the focal driver is Pleistocene sea level fluctuations and glacial cycles treated as environmental fluctuations rather than an experimental manipulation. The disturbance is treated as press because sea level dynamics are analysed as continuous fluctuations over the last million years with no discrete recovery experiment window. Observation duration is 11 years from 2012 sampling start to 2023 sequencing years mentioned which is 4015 days.

The study used annual field photoquadrat surveys and modelled coral cover proportions from 2002 to 2018 and then predicted coral cover at unobserved reef locations. Recovery was explicitly defined as capability to recover from disturbance(s) to within 80% of pre-disturbance cover values and probability of recovery was calculated from differences between predicted coral cover during 2008 to 2018 and 80% of baseline cover. Baseline cover was defined as the maximum coral cover predicted during the pre-disturbance period 2002 to 2007. The response variable in the spatio-temporal model was coral cover as proportions for branching plate and massive coral types aggregated from photoquadrats. The paper discusses recovery and resilience but does not define an explicit equation framework that includes resilience. The disturbance context was the white syndrome coral disease outbreak between 2004 and 2008 and storm damage in 2008 with recovery years set 2008 to 2018. Disease is a biotic disturbance and storm damage is a climatic disturbance and both were unmanipulated. The disturbance was treated as discrete events followed by a recovery period which matches a pulse pattern. The first author affiliation is Queensland University of Technology in Australia and the case study and data are from Heron Reef Queensland Australia. The habitat is a coral reef in shallow nearshore waters which fits Marine.Neritic. The target organisms are hard corals which are animals. The observation window for disturbance and recovery analysis spans 2002 to 2018 which is 16 years or 5840 days.

The study states that it implemented four disturbance recovery field experiments and that it measured recovery rates over a four week period. The disturbance was created by clipping the leaves at the start of each resilience experiment creating circular gaps. Resilience is defined as the ability to return to its initial form or state after a disturbance and the study uses recovery as a measure for resilience and as a proxy for critical slowing down. Relative recovery was calculated as LAI regrowth divided by LAI standing and was interpreted as a slower return of the system to its initial state. LAI was chosen as a proxy for seagrass cover representing seagrass presence or visible status and LAI regrowth in clipped gaps was used as a proxy for absolute recovery. The focal organism is Zostera marina described as a marine angiosperm. The sites are in Shandong Province China and the first author affiliation is in the Netherlands. The habitat is described as coastal environments and the experiments were in dense Zostera marina meadows at two sites including an open sea system and a shallow lagoon which fits shallow nearshore marine habitat. Each resilience experiment lasted 4 weeks in total which is 28 days.

The study used observational biomass data collected annually at peak standing crop and calculated yield stability as temporal mean divided by temporal SD. It also quantified resistance and resilience to a major regional drought in 2012 using ratios of biomass across years. Resilience was calculated as biomass 2013 divided by biomass 2011 and values at or above 1 indicated biomass rebounded or exceeded predrought production. The introduction presents ecological stability as multifaceted and explicitly includes constancy resistance and resilience and the rate of recovery from disruptions. The target variable used for resilience calculations was aboveground biomass production. The work was based on field plots in randomized complete block experiments at Arlington Agricultural Research Station Wisconsin and Kellogg Biological Station Michigan. The first author affiliation includes University of Wisconsin Green Bay and the W K Kellogg Biological Station Michigan State University both in the United States. The field locations are given as Arlington at 43.234556 N 89.374667 W and Kellogg at 42.713056 N 85.345167 W and the reported location is their average. The systems are bioenergy cropping systems at agricultural research stations and include planted monocultures mixtures prairie restorations and unplanted old field plots. The disturbance was an unmanipulated climatic drought in 2012. The drought is treated as a discrete event with predrought and postdrought years used for ratios. The resilience observation window spans from 2011 to 2013 which is 1095 days.

Target variable type: Socio-eco parameter; Abiotic parameter; Diversity; Cover; Regional network parameter; Land use
Justification: The study states that it established a comprehensive indicator system and developed an urban rivers resilience index URRI to diagnose health status from 2011 to 2020. Resilience is defined as including three dimensions resistance recovery and adaptability and the paper says urban rivers resilience includes three dimensions resistance recovery and adaptability. URRI is calculated using an improved FCM EWM MABAC multi attribute decision method with indicator weights and a final index value rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a defined disturbance. The data sources are monitoring data and statistical data including water quality biological composition riparian zone connectivity water resources volume urbanization rate water consumption sewage treatment rate and investment rates which are used as indicators in Table 1. Because resilience is quantified as an index constructed from these indicators rather than direct system recovery after disturbance the measurement is inferred and quantification is inferred. The target variable types follow the indicators used in Table 1 including socio economic parameters such as urbanization rate and investment rates abiotic parameters such as water resources amounts and water quality compliance rate diversity metrics such as fish diversity index and phytoplankton Shannon index cover as riparian vegetation coverage rate and regional network parameter as longitudinal continuity. The region is Jialu River in Zhengzhou China and the coordinates are given as 113 34 E to 114 06 E and 34.40 N to 34.41 N so the average is N34.675 E113.833 and the range is under two degrees. The first author affiliation is North China University of Water Conservancy and Electric Power in Zhengzhou China. The study uses on site water ecological monitoring and investigation and statistical bulletins so the approach is field observation. The paper discusses human interventions and governance measures but does not quantify resilience as response to a specific disturbance so disturbance type pattern and observation duration for disturbance response are not provided.

The study states that it measured mangrove post-freeze damage and recovery in January and November 2023 and it built on a pre-freeze baseline assessment in July 2022 across 12 sites. It defines resistance as the capacity to withstand disturbance and resilience as the capacity to recover from perturbation and it says it quantified mangrove resistance and resilience against this freeze event. It reports that freeze resilience was measured by reproductivity resprouts and biomass recovered and Table 2 lists above-ground biomass recovery as a tagged tree vegetation recovery metric. Biomass recovery is quantified as estimated biomass post-recovery versus the pre-freeze biomass using allometric equations. This is a direct measurement of recovery relative to baseline so the measurement type is observed and the quantification corresponds to recovery degree. The disturbance is the December 2022 winter storm freeze event with extremely low temperatures from December 24th to 26th 2022 so it is an unmanipulated climatic pulse. The first author affiliation is University of Florida Gainesville Florida USA so the institution country is US. The field data were collected along northern Florida's Gulf of Mexico coast USA so the field country is US and the habitat is coastal wetlands. The study region is given as 28 34 32 N to 29 54 22 N and Figure 2 shows Florida Gulf coast sites so the average coordinate is approximated from the stated latitudinal range with longitude not explicitly provided. The observation window for baseline to recovery spans July 2022 to November 2023 which is 487 days using the instruction of 30 days per month.

The paper defines stability as when a system returns to a given structure or equilibrium state after being perturbed and states this has also been called resiliency. It also states that persistence stability is the long term consequence of resilience of a given community pattern which yields the framework stability = resilience + persistence. Resilience is reserved for the seascape pattern and the study evaluates recovery or change following a disturbance relative to the ambient condition which is the pre disturbance cover of adult barnacles. The methods describe experimentally created disturbances by manually clearing rock to generate replicated experimental disturbances across four patch sizes and following trajectories of cover through time. The results state that large scale disturbances consistently return to ambient or pre disturbance conditions within roughly 1 year and the discussion repeats that large disturbances quickly revert to the pre disturbance condition. This directly quantifies resilience as recovery time to pre disturbance conditions within roughly 1 year. The target variable used for this recovery is barnacle cover and bare rock cover with cover of sessile species estimated from photos and quadrats. The work is a field experiment with predator exclusions and cleared patches and also includes field surveys in 1991 1993 and 2004. The experimental system is the mid rocky intertidal seascape of the west shore of San Juan Island Washington USA at 48 27 N 123 2 W. The first author affiliation is Pontificia Universidad Catolica de Chile in Santiago Chile. Disturbance includes manipulated structural disturbance via manual clearing to bare rock and unmanipulated geophysical disturbance via battering by drift logs causing extensive barnacle mortality. The disturbance is treated as pulse events with patches created and then observed for recovery. The experiment follows cover after roughly 1 year between exclusions and controls and states after c 1 year and within roughly 1 year which supports 365 days.

The paper develops indicators to evaluate resistance potential resilience and capacity for transformation of ecosystem service provision using plant trait composition and abiotic parameters rather than measuring recovery dynamics through time. It states that resilience is assessed by a potential operating range under hypotheses of reassembly from the community’s species pool and that the approach does not consider transient dynamics and time lag of returning to the pre-disturbed state. Resilience is quantified using operating ranges where the observed range indicates resistance and the potential range within the same ecosystem state indicates potential resilience and a new potential range after transformation indicates transformation. Ecosystem services quantified are annual biomass production forage quality carbon storage and soil fertility estimated using quantitative models based on field data and simulations of community assembly. Field data come from subalpine grasslands at the Lautaret site in the central French Alps located above Villar d’Arène at N45.03 E6.24. The first author affiliation is the University of Innsbruck in Austria. The study subjects are plant communities because indicators are based on plant functional traits and community weighted means. No specific disturbance event is imposed or tracked for recovery and disturbances are discussed generically as management or environmental change so disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery are not specified.

The paper states that it investigated the question using models for three managed systems and that computations were performed in R with simulation programs provided which indicates a modeling and simulation approach. Resilience is defined as a system tending to remain in the safe operating space despite disturbance and the analyses focus on boundaries of the safe operating space and critical thresholds. The results repeatedly quantify outcomes as earlier collapse or transitions at lower phosphorus load or lower harvest coefficient or lower stocking density which is a threshold based quantification consistent with latitude. The lake example explicitly identifies Lake Mendota in Wisconsin and gives the safe operating space for clean water as phosphorus load below 1.47 g·m−2·y−1 which grounds a US field location. The other two models are presented generically without a stated field country or coordinates so the only explicit field country is the United States. The lake system is a lake and is therefore coded as wetland habitat. The resilience related state variable in the lake case is water phosphorus concentration and time in the oligotrophic clear water state and these are ecosystem level functional state variables rather than organism level measures. Disturbances are imposed in models as phosphorus pollution loading chemical harvest of a wild population biological resource use and overgrazing by cattle biotic. Variance management is described as applied continuously to modify inputs according to a weighted average of past years which corresponds to a press type disturbance treatment. The paper reports simulation time steps such as 5000 100000 and 1000 but does not provide a mapping from time steps to days so observation duration in days cannot be determined.

The study uses Landsat MSS derived vegetation indices as surrogate measures of total plant cover and analyses patterns of change in cover over time and space. It states that indicators include the magnitude of observed vegetation response at individual points compared with that which is expected when vegetation recovers fully from grazing and that the difference between observed and expected response provides a measure of the resilience of the system. This resilience measure is therefore based on observed cover change after rainfall events and comparison to an expected fully recovered response. The paper presents time series of cover derived from Landsat for periods including October 1982 to March 1989 and model runs using rainfalls for 1970 to 1989. The resilience model approach is described as comparing observed response to expected response after large rainfalls that replenish seed banks and facilitate recruitment. Grazing is treated as a disturbance through grazing gradients from artificial waterpoints and rainfall events are treated as pulses that generate growth periods. The approach combines remote sensing field observation with simple models calibrated using sequences of cover data derived from Landsat MSS. The first author affiliation is CSIRO in Alice Springs Australia. The field examples are repeatedly described as central Australia near Alice Springs including Hamilton Downs Station Kunoth Paddock and Owen Springs Station. No explicit coordinates are provided in the text. The habitat is described as arid and semi arid rangelands including shrubby grassland and open woodland and the resilience quantification uses total plant cover so the biological level is ecosystem and the taxon is vegetation.

The study tests whether seagrass maximum vertical distribution can be predicted by rhizome diameter and growth or reproductive strategy and acclimatization capacity. Resilience is only mentioned as functional resilience defined as growth or reproductive strategy and as acclimatization potential of traits rather than as recovery after disturbance. The methods quantify acclimatization potential using a systematic review and meta analytical effect sizes from field shading experiments and depth based studies comparing extreme low light versus control and shallow versus depth limit.
