The main models relate maximum vertical distribution to weighted effect sizes for traits including saturation irradiance leaves per shoot and above ground biomass. No time series recovery dynamics are quantified in this paper and no resilience response variable is directly measured. Therefore resilience is inferred from proxy trait based indicators. The location is stated as global and the time period is publications between 1982 and 2020. The first author affiliation is in Blanes Spain. The habitat is seagrass in nearshore waters which corresponds to shallow marine environments over continental shelves.

The study quantified resilience using tree ring based annual basal area increment and basal area derived from observed ring widths and described as Pinus sylvestris tree ring chronologies. It identified an extreme drought year in 1984 using SPEI and climatic water deficit and selected 1984 for further analysis. It forecasted annual growth in a no drought scenario by replacing 1984 climate values with average 1961 to 1983 values and then forecasting 1984 to 1993. It calculated annual growth resilience as Gr equals observed BAI divided by forecasted BAI and also calculated annual and cumulative deficits by subtracting forecasted from observed values. It defined complete recovery as the year observed growth rate or size achieved the forecasted value and reported recovery occurring 1 to 6 years post drought and stand level growth recovery by 1989. Because resilience was quantified from observed growth trajectories relative to a drought event and recovery dynamics it is observed. No explicit equation framework combining resilience with other components was defined beyond presenting resilience indices so the framework is none. Quantification aligns with recovery degree because it used the ratio of observed to forecasted growth and the difference between observed and forecasted growth and size to represent degree of recovery relative to a reference scenario. The target variable is radial growth and size from tree rings specifically basal area increment and basal area. The focal measurements are at individual tree level with individual trajectories and recovery times reported. The work used field collected tree discs from a site in north east Scotland and also used modelling via dynamic regression forecasting. The first author affiliation is University of Stirling in the UK. The field site coordinates are given as 57 degrees 36 minutes 23 seconds north and 4 degrees 16 minutes 50 seconds west. The habitat is a monospecific spacing experiment of Scots pine forest. The disturbance is an unmanipulated climatic extreme drought event treated as a discrete year 1984 with post drought recovery tracked. The observation window for resilience calculations spans 1984 through 1993 which is 10 years or 3650 days.

Framework: resilience = capacity to absorb disturbances while maintaining structure and function
Justification: The study analysed long term monitoring data from 21 reef sites surveyed in 1994 2005 and 2011 spanning the 1998 bleaching event. Resilience was evaluated using observed post disturbance benthic trajectories because change in benthic condition post disturbance 2005 to 2011 was modelled and reefs were described as more likely to recover to coral dominated states after the disturbance. The disturbance was climate induced elevated water temperatures associated with El Nino and the Indian Ocean dipole that led to coral bleaching and widespread coral mortality and it was not experimentally imposed. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event in 1998 with recovery assessed afterwards so the pattern is pulse. The framework definition is explicitly given as resilience being the capacity to absorb disturbances while maintaining structure and function. Quantification used the degree of recovery because the response variable was change in benthic condition between 2005 and 2011 and recovery was described as returning close to 1994 positions on PCA1. The target variable used to quantify resilience was benthic condition derived from PCA of benthic composition separating coral cover and complexity from macroalgal cover. The level is community because the benthic condition index represents community composition of benthic components. The approach is field observation because fish and benthic communities were surveyed using underwater visual census and percent cover estimates. The first author affiliation is James Cook University Townsville Australia. The field sites were in the inner Seychelles. No coordinates were provided in the text so location and range are NA. The habitat is coral reef in shallow nearshore marine environment so Marine.Neritic. The taxon is Animalia because the resilience indicator metrics were based on herbivorous fish assemblages and fish biomass distribution across functional groups and size classes.

The study states it used a 35-year time series of carbon fixation and that it used multitemporal NPP trajectories to develop resilience and resistance metrics. It defines resilience as the rate at which process variables recover from disturbance and treats resilience as the slope of dNPP from the maximum reduction to the final year in the time series. It defines resistance as the maximum displacement from equilibrium and calculates dNPPmax as the maximum decrease in dNPP in a single year after the initial outbreak. The framework is presented as resistance and resilience concepts applied together to carbon trajectories rather than as parts of a higher level variable. The target variable used to quantify resilience is NPP anomaly slope which is an ecosystem productivity carbon fixation measure. The disturbance is a spruce beetle outbreak described as a large regional outbreak with mapped canopy tree mortality beginning in 2009 to 2010 through 2015 to 2016 and it is treated as a discrete outbreak period in the figures. The approach uses remotely sensed Landsat NPP data and field plots and is described as monitoring and modeling trajectories rather than an experiment. The study area is on the Grand Mesa Uncompahgre Gunnison National Forest in the La Garita Mountains of southwestern Colorado USA and the map shows bounds from about N37.83 to N38.92 and W107.42 to W106.33. The first author affiliation is Colorado State University in the United States. The observation window for resilience quantification uses annual NPP from 1986 to 2018 which is 33 years or 12045 days.

The study states it applies soundscape recording and analysis to quantify resilience of Puerto Rican coral reef and dry forest animal communities in relation to Hurricane Maria. It uses recordings collected between March 2017 and January 2018 at three terrestrial and three marine sites and measures daily acoustic activity for eight sound types. It explicitly defines three dimensions of resilience as resistance heterogeneity and recovery. Recovery is quantified as recovery time by stating it considered recovery time to be when the sound type reached pre-storm levels and it reports that mid-frequency insect sound type returned to pre-storm levels after 56 days and bird vocalizations returned after 67 days. The recovery modeling uses a logistic curve on time series of acoustic indices at the coastal site. The target variables used for resilience quantification are soundscape based acoustic activity metrics such as SPL and spectral saturation which are used as indices of animal community activity and diversity. The work is based on deployed automated recorders at field sites in southwest Puerto Rico including La Parguera Natural Reserve and Guanica State Forest with coordinates provided in Table 1. The disturbance is Hurricane Maria which struck in September 2017 and is treated as a discrete event with pre-storm and post-storm periods. The first author affiliation is Purdue University in the United States.

Resilience was quantified from the system response after an imposed disturbance because the study used a field experiment that manipulated disturbance intensity by partial and complete canopy removal to mimic canopy loss from storms. The paper states that resilience was assessed as canopy regeneration because it measured recovery of adult canopy as the change in stage 3 adult density within plots after 20 months and it concludes that warm climate kelp beds have reduced capacity for recovery following intense physical disturbance. This is an observed measurement because adult density was measured before and after disturbance and used to quantify recovery. The framework is none because resilience is not defined as an equation or as a component of a higher level variable in the introduction or methods. Quantification is recovery degree because recovery is measured as change in adult density after 20 months rather than a rate or time to return. The target variable type is density because canopy recovery is measured as adult density change. The level is population because the density is for a single species Ecklonia radiata adults. The approach includes field experiment for the canopy removal manipulations and field observation for the regional surveys of canopy cover biomass and recruit density. The first author affiliation is University of Western Australia in Australia. The field data were collected on subtidal rocky reefs within four regions of Western Australia including Hamelin Bay Marmion Jurien Bay and Kalbarri. The location is approximated from the stated regional latitudes 34 S to 28 S along the southwest coast of Australia with an average near 31 S and 115.5 E. The location range reflects the latitudinal span from 28 S to 34 S. The habitat is shallow subtidal rocky reef kelp beds which fall under Marine Neritic. The taxon is Chromista because the focal organism is the kelp Ecklonia radiata a macroalga. The disturbance type is manipulated structural because the disturbance was experimental canopy removal altering adult canopy structure. The disturbance pattern is pulse because canopy removal was a discrete event followed by recovery monitoring. Observation duration is 600 days because canopy recovery was measured after 20 months.

The study states it uses age structured population models and that it simulated dynamics of an age structured fish population along a generic coastline with a proportion protected in reserves and the remainder fished. Environmental variability is imposed by making survival of the first age class stochastic using a time series with the same frequency spectrum as the Multivariate ENSO Index and the harvest rate is set across a range from zero to twice FMSY. Buffering is measured as the proportion of time a simulated population spent below a target reference point and in results variability in total population biomass is measured as the proportion of simulated years spent below BMSY and variability in fishery yields is measured as the proportion of simulated years when yield was less than MSY. This is a direct quantification from model response trajectories so the measurement is observed under the provided definition. The introduction explicitly avoids using resilience as the term and says it will refer to the process as buffering rather than resilience so no explicit resilience framework is defined. The variability metric is not recovery rate time degree resistance latitude or invariability but a threshold exceedance proportion so quantification is other. The target variables used in the buffering metric are total population biomass and fishery yield which is calculated from numbers of fish harvested so the target variable types are biomass and abundance. The focal entities are fished populations of Blue Rockfish China Rockfish and Cabezon so the level is population and the taxon is Animalia. Disturbances are modelled fishing mortality and modelled climate linked recruitment variability described as ENSO like variability affecting juvenile survival so disturbance types are model biological resource use and model climatic. The harvest rate and environmental variability are maintained throughout the simulated period with no defined end so the disturbance pattern is press. Each simulation is 635 years with the first 500 years discarded and the final 135 years analyzed so the duration between first and last measurement in the simulation is 635 years which is 49275 days. The first author affiliation is Oregon State University in the United States. The coastline is generic and no field data location is used for quantifying resilience so field country and coordinates are not available and the habitat context is marine nearshore rocky reef fish so Marine.Neritic is the best match.

The paper states that it uses modelling and literature assessment to quantify safe and just Earth system boundaries and it does not quantify resilience as recovery dynamics of a focal state variable after a defined disturbance. Safe boundaries are determined using assessments of tipping point risks analyses of historical variability and expert judgement which are proxy and threshold based rather than observed recovery trajectories. Resilience is discussed together with stability in the statement the stability and resilience of the Earth system and human well-being are inseparably linked and the paper proposes boundaries for maintaining the resilience and stability of the Earth system. The quantification is therefore inferred from boundary indicators such as global mean surface temperature change surface water flow alteration groundwater drawdown nitrogen and phosphorus surplus and aerosol optical depth and PM2.5. These target variables are primarily abiotic state variables and pressures used to set boundaries. The methods describe modelling including WBM runs IMAGE model and GRACE satellite data and aggregation to global budgets indicating a modeling and simulation approach. The first author affiliation is Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research Potsdam Germany. The assessment is at global and subglobal scales and uses global datasets so the field country and location are global. Multiple domains and both land and ocean are included so the habitat type is multi. Because the resilience quantified is Earth system level and not a focal organism group the taxon is not applicable. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not defined for resilience measurement in this boundary assessment.

The study states it uses annual abundance indices from the UK Butterfly Monitoring Scheme for 1990 to 1999 and it analyses an extreme drought event in 1995 with population crash in 1996. It proposes to measure population resilience as the ability to withstand and recover from environmental perturbation and it specifies sensitivity to drought as the magnitude of population decline and recovery from drought as the rate of population increase following the crash. Recovery is quantified by fitting a linear model to the population count in 1996 and the subsequent three years and using the rate of change as the measure of population recovery. The target variable used for resilience quantification is the annual index of abundance for Aphantopus hyperantus which is a population level measure. The disturbance is the 1995 drought event which is a climatic extreme and it is not experimentally manipulated. The drought is treated as a discrete event with subsequent recovery assessed in the following years which matches a pulse disturbance. The first author affiliation is NERC Centre for Ecology and Hydrology in the UK and the data are from 79 British sites. The habitat metrics focus on broadleaved woodland around monitoring sites and the species is commonly found close to woodland edges so the dominant habitat type is forest. The paper does not provide coordinates for the sites so location and location range cannot be determined from the text. The observation window spans 1990 to 1999 which is ten years or 3650 days.

The study is an assessment of basin ecological health using a multi dimensional indicator system from natural environmental factors and anthropogenic disturbance activities and it uses a spatial similarity cloud model and random forest partial dependence plots rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a defined disturbance. Resilience is mentioned as being weakened after crossing a critical point but it is not quantified as recovery time recovery rate resistance latitude or invariability from an observed system trajectory. The methods state that assessment indicator values are inputs with health scores as outputs and the work focuses on health level identification and driver thresholds. The framework is described as basin ecological health derived from ecological integrity and the paper treats resilience as a component within a broader health concept rather than defining resilience alone. The target variables used to construct the health score include anthropogenic disturbance activities indicators such as water resource development utilization rate riparian zone disturbance discharge outlet index and flood control compliance rate which are socio ecological management indicators. Data were collected in the Beiluo River Basin in Shaanxi Province China from 2022 to 2024 with monitoring zones and biological monitoring zones indicating field observation. The first author affiliation is Xi an University of Technology in PR China. The study area is the Beiluo River Basin in the Loess Plateau and no explicit coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to the basin region. The habitat is a river basin with mixed land uses and riverine habitat which does not match the provided single dominant terrestrial categories so it is coded as Other. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not applicable.

The introduction defines resilience as the ability of the community to revert to the original state after a disturbance passes. The experiments quantify persistence and clearance of commensals during immune activation by measuring bacterial loads using CFU counts and qPCR which are direct observations of system state. AMP sensitive mutants were cleared from the gut after infection and aging induced gut inflammation in wild type but not in AMP deficient flies which links resilience to the observed response under disturbance. The study does not provide an explicit equation framework that defines resilience as a component of a larger construct or as a sum of components. Resilience is operationalized as the ability to persist with less decline under immune effectors so the quantification aligns with resistance rather than recovery time or recovery rate. The target variable used for this quantification is bacterial load or abundance reported as CFUs and qPCR loads. This target variable is at the population level for a bacterial strain within hosts. The approach includes field experiment because flies are orally infected and colonized in vivo with controlled treatments and indoor experiment because in vitro MIC and growth assays with polymyxin B and cecropin B are performed. The first author affiliation is at the Max Planck Institute for Infection Biology in Berlin Germany. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the data come from laboratory fly experiments and in vitro assays. The disturbance is manipulated biotic because oral infection with Ecc15 is imposed to induce intestinal immune responses and inflammation. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because flies are infected and then measured at defined postinfection times such as 6 h and 24 h. The longest stated postdisturbance observation window for infection related persistence is up to 72 h after colonization with immune activation and infection measurements spanning from 6 h to 72 h which is 66 h or 2.75 d and the priming protocol spans 8 h to 24 h but the clearest multi timepoint window is 6 h to 72 h.

The methods state that measures of resistance were based on loss in state or flux variables and that resilience was measured as the fractional rate of return to prehurricane structure and function. The study repeatedly measured posthurricane trajectories of LAI fine live root mass stem diameter increment and ANPP beginning immediately after Hurricane Iniki and continuing to 24 months. The disturbance was Hurricane Iniki which is described with wind speeds and passage over the site and was not experimentally imposed. The introduction defines resistance as smaller change after disturbance and resilience as the rate of return to the reference state following disturbance and discusses them together. The results report that the rate of recovery to prehurricane diameter growth increment was greatest in 1P treatments and that ANPP in 1P treatments recovered to 105 percent of prehurricane levels by the end of 2 years. ANPP is used as the functional response variable for recovery and is an ecosystem function. The site is a montane native rain forest in Kokee State Park on Kauai Hawaii with coordinates 22 8089N and 159 8389W. The first author affiliation is University of Hawaii Honolulu and the field site is on Kauai in the Hawaiian Islands. The observation window for recovery dynamics includes measurements through 24 months posthurricane which is 730 days.

The study quantified resilience using individual tree basal area increment time series and modelled growth response to drought using SPEI which is direct observational data. The Introduction states that resilience can be divided into resistance and recovery and the Methods specify that resistance is the immediate growth reduction and recovery is the linear recovery over time. The Methods model annual log basal area increment as a function of drought stress with lags and interpret the lag intercept as resistance and the lag slope as recovery rate. The target variable used for resilience quantification is individual tree radial growth expressed as basal area increment. The data come from French mountain forest plots in the French Alps and the sites listed are all in France. The disturbance is drought defined by SPEI below a threshold and treated as discrete drought years with recovery assessed over four subsequent years which matches a pulse framing. The growth series used for stability and drought analyses spans 1995 to 2012 which is 18 years or 6570 days.

The study presents a dynamic Bayesian network and states that it estimates system response in terms of resistance recovery and persistence which are described as components used to characterise resilience. The Methods define recovery as the time in months when the system first returns to and stays within 20% of baseline population levels during the consequence period and the time tR is called time to recovery. The Results and Figure 2 report expected recovery time in months under dredging hazard scenarios. The target variable used for the resilience analysis is shoot density because the Methods state that they focus on shoot density for their analysis of resilience and shoot density is defined as number of shoots per m2. The disturbance is maintenance dredging affecting light because the hazard is described as light reduction due to dredging and scenarios vary dredging duration frequency start month and light stress during dredging. The dredging scenarios are imposed in the model as hazard scenarios so the disturbance manipulation level is model. The disturbance is treated as discrete dredging campaigns lasting 1 to 6 months with a defined end followed by a consequence period so the pattern is pulse. The modelling window is stated as a 12 year simulation window used to accommodate model initialisation and up to 5 yearly maintenance dredging which gives 4380 days. The first author affiliation is Queensland University of Technology Brisbane Australia. The field locations for the resilience analysis are Jurien Bay Hay Point and Pelican Banks Gladstone all in Australia and the average coordinate is approximated from these named sites.

The study used 26 years of bird community data and quantified boundary strength using wombling R2 values derived from ordination of bird community composition and then predicted these values across the landscape with a GAM. The response variable in the hypothesis tests was GAM-predicted wombling R2 values which the paper describes as bird community boundary strength where higher R2 indicates boundaries and low R2 indicates core areas. Resilience was not defined as an equation but was discussed in relation to resilience-based management and early warnings of eroding resilience. The discussion states that tracking animal community boundaries adds to the ability to quantify ecological resilience by detecting early warnings of eroding resilience. The quantification is therefore based on spatial boundary strength rather than recovery rate time degree resistance latitude or invariability. The target variable used to quantify resilience-relevant dynamics was bird community composition summarized by PCA axis 1 and translated into boundary strength. The work was based on field surveys at Fort Riley Army Base Kansas USA with annual breeding season surveys from 1991 to 2017 and remote sensing vegetation cover and fire history layers. Fire was treated as cumulative number of fires per pixel from 1984 to 1991 1984 to 2005 and 1984 to 2016 and analyzed as a continuous driver with no recovery interval. Woody plant encroachment was described as woody plant regimes displacing grassland regimes and was analyzed through grass woody vegetation boundaries derived from tree and grass cover covariance. The first author affiliation is University of Arkansas USA and the field site is in the United States at Fort Riley in the Flint Hills ecoregion.

The introduction defines resistance as the ability of a species biomass to resist displacement from control levels and resilience as the speed of recovery back to control levels. The methods describe a large scale field experiment at Harpur Hill Derbyshire UK where extreme event treatments frost drought and fire were applied to turves and then reinserted. Monitoring occurred immediately after treatments in mid June 1991 then again 8 weeks later in August 1991 and 1 year later in June 1992 showing recovery dynamics through time. Resistance and resilience were calculated with reference only to the above ground biomass with biomass estimated from point quadrat contacts. Resilience was calculated after 8 weeks and 1 year of recovery as the ratio of mean point quadrat contacts in treated turves to that in control turves which operationalizes speed of recovery. The first author affiliation is NERC Unit of Comparative Plant Ecology University of Sheffield Sheffield UK. The field site is given as Harpur Hill Derbyshire UK with National Grid Reference SK 705053 which corresponds approximately to N53.116 W1.733. The communities are herbaceous rank grassland and related herbaceous vegetation so the dominant habitat type is grassland. The study subjects are plant species listed in figures and tables such as Festuca rubra Urtica dioica and Carex flacca. Disturbances were experimentally imposed as frost drought and fire treatments so they are manipulated climatic and manipulated fire and each treatment had a defined duration followed by recovery so the pattern is pulse. The observation window for resilience spans from the first assessment in June 1991 to the last assessment in June 1992 which is 365 days.

The introduction states resilience includes resistance and recovery and defines them as the two components of resilience. The study reports that removing Coralliophila abbreviata increased coral resilience by reducing bleaching severity described as resistance and post bleaching tissue mortality described as recovery. The methods describe a field experiment at Pickles Reef Florida manipulating snail densities on brain coral colonies and then assessing bleaching severity in October 2014 and tissue mortality in November 2014 after temperatures declined and bleaching ended. Bleaching severity is measured as percentage bleached tissue per colony and tissue mortality is measured as percentage dead tissue per colony which are direct observations of coral condition. The warming event is described using sea surface temperature above the bleaching threshold for about 7.5 weeks and is not experimentally imposed. The first author affiliation is Duke University in the USA. The field location is given as Pickles Reef in the Florida Keys USA with coordinates N 24 59.170 W 80 24.940 which converts to decimal degrees. The habitat is shallow coral reef environment within 15 to 25 m depths which fits Marine Neritic. The disturbance includes manipulated predator density and an unmanipulated climate related warming event. The disturbance is treated as a discrete 2014 warming and bleaching event with recovery assessed after it ended. The observation window spans from mid June 2014 to November 2014.

The study quantifies resilience using seedling responses to simulated herbivory by defoliation in greenhouse Experiment 4 and field Experiment 5 and it models how increasing CO2 decreases the time taken to reach critical size thresholds associated with survival through herbivory. The Results state that CO2 induced growth enhancement will reduce the time taken for individuals to reach this survival threshold and Figure 4 is described as increasing CO2 concentration decreases the time taken for seedlings to reach critical size thresholds associated with survival through herbivory. The target variable used for these thresholds is above ground and below ground biomass with thresholds reported as 0.51 g and 0.76 g and a second threshold of 17.09 g and 9.13 g. The disturbance is herbivory simulated by defoliation with treatments of 50% defoliation and 100% defoliation and single or duplicate defoliations. The disturbance is treated as discrete defoliation events with recovery measured after 3 months in the field and after recovery intervals in the greenhouse so the pattern is pulse. The field regrowth measurement is after 3 months so the observation duration for disturbance and resilience in the field experiment is 90 days. The first author affiliation is Rhodes University Grahamstown South Africa. Field data include seedlings grown from seed from Hluhluwe iMfolozi Game Reserve Kwa Zulu Natal South Africa with coordinates −28.22 31.95 and the field experiment is in the Eastern Cape South Africa.

The paper defines resilience as return time by stating that near bifurcation points the return time to equilibrium upon a small perturbation increases and that ecosystems close to a critical threshold lose resilience. The Methods derive resilience related dynamics from the linearized return rate and return time to equilibrium upon perturbation. The study quantifies the approach to critical transitions using autocorrelation and variance in the fluctuations of the ecosystem state and analytically links these to the dominant eigenvalue that determines return time. The simulation model explicitly imposes a gradual increase in grazing rate c until a fold bifurcation and collapse which is a modelled disturbance. The target state variable in the simulation is resource biomass x and Fig. 3a is mean biomass at increasing grazing rate. The approach is modeling and simulation because the core analysis uses analytical derivations and numerical simulations in MATLAB R and Mathematica. The disturbance is treated as a continuous driver because grazing rate is slowly increased and the system is under a permanent stochastic regime of perturbations rather than a discrete event. Observation duration for each grazing level is 500 time steps because after each stepwise change of grazing rate they ran the model for 500 time steps and used these 500 time steps to estimate indicators. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University in the Netherlands. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work is analytical and simulation based and the climate transition examples are only referenced as appendices without locations in the provided text.

The study used a year-long monthly time series of nine full-scale bioreactor facilities and analyzed bacterial 16S rRNA gene sequences and UniFrac distances through time which is direct observational data. The introduction explicitly defines population dynamics mechanisms as resistance resilience and redundancy and states resilience is when a population rebounds following a disturbance. The results describe two community disturbances in facility U1 followed by returns to equilibrium and state that following each disturbance the community returned to the normal facility cluster demonstrating resilience. This rebound is evaluated using community composition and phylogenetic structure including UniFrac distance from the location average and OTU relative abundance profiles that returned to original composition. The disturbance events were associated with reductions in sludge loading rate and addition of dormant granules which are treated as discrete events with recovery afterward. Sampling was monthly for 1 y from October 2007 to September 2008 so the observation duration is 365 days. The first author affiliation is Cornell University in the United States. The paper does not name the country of the brewery wastewater treatment facilities or provide coordinates so field country and location are not available. The system is an engineered anaerobic digestion bioreactor so the habitat type best fits Other. The focal organisms quantified for resilience are bacterial communities.

The study uses a dynamic population model with Bayesian parameter estimation to simulate severe 50% and 99% mortality events during early life stages. It quantifies impact by direct comparison of simulated and historical time series of population biomass and catch. Resilience is quantified as impact duration defined as the number of years during which population biomass was reduced to less than 90% of the historical biomass and this is described as a proxy for population resilience and the ability to recover after a mortality event. The target variable used for this resilience quantification is population biomass accounting for age classes 3 plus. The work is a modeling and simulation hindcasting approach using survey indices and landings data covering 1959 to 2012 which is 54 years or 19710 days. The focal organism is Northeast Arctic cod Gadus morhua which is an animal. The disturbance is a model imposed additional mortality on eggs or larvae in a single year which is a pulse event and is best classified as a model structural disturbance. The first author affiliation is University of Oslo in Norway. The stock is distributed along the coast of Norway and spawning occurs along the Norwegian coast particularly around the Lofoten islands and the study uses Norwegian fishery value and Norwegian CPI data indicating field data from Norway. The system is marine with spawning along the coast and Barents Sea context so the dominant habitat is shallow nearshore marine neritic.

The abstract states that the study evaluated resistance as grazing impact and resilience as recovery after livestock exclusion. The methods describe field sampling of biological soil crust community attributes using permanent quadrats and digital photographs and image processing to quantify species percent cover and taxonomical group cover and to evaluate species richness and species composition. The resilience models used data from a recovery gradient of differently aged livestock exclosures with 6 year 11 year and 27 year cattle exclusion which is direct observation of system state under recovery. The quantification of resilience is based on differences in cover and composition among exclusion ages and the discussion states that 10 years of disturbance removal did not lead to the ecosystem structure found after 27 years of recovery indicating recovery degree rather than recovery rate or recovery time. The target variables directly used in the resilience analysis are species richness taxonomical group cover and species composition evaluated with GLMMs NMDS and PERMANOVA. The study area is a semiarid grassland in Vaquerias Jalisco Mexico and Table 1 provides coordinates for all sites which average to N21.7673 W101.6742 with all sites within less than two degrees so the range is 0. The first author affiliation listed first is Universidad Autonoma de San Luis Potosi Mexico. The disturbance is grazing by livestock described as heavy continuous or seasonal grazing and exclusion and it is treated as a continuous regime so the pattern is press. The observation window for recovery spans the chronosequence up to 27 years so 27 years equals 9855 days. The focal organisms are biological soil crusts including cyanobacteria lichens and bryophytes and cyanobacteria are treated as cyanobacteria dominated crusts which are classified here as Chromista.

The study analyzed postspate recovery sequences in Sycamore Creek and states that it compared benthic aquatic macroinvertebrate succession following eight spates over 3 yr. It quantified resilience from observed recovery trajectories because it states that resilience was generally high and it evaluated whether trajectories converged with the centroid of preflood samples. It also quantified change using succession rate defined as the degree of change in assemblage composition and measured as distances between temporally consecutive centroids on ordination plots standardized to daily rates. This is a recovery rate type quantification because it is a rate of return in community composition after spates. The target variable used for this quantification is assemblage composition because ordinations and centroid distances were computed from taxon abundances and presence absence patterns. The level is community because the response variable is macroinvertebrate assemblage structure across multiple taxa. The approach is field observation because samples were collected in the stream reach after each spate and approximately weekly thereafter. The first author affiliation is Arizona State University in Tempe Arizona USA. The field site is Sycamore Creek in central Arizona USA and the institution address provides Tempe Arizona which supports approximate coordinates near Tempe. Disturbances include unmanipulated hydrological spates and the discussion also treats drought and drying as an important disturbance. Spates are treated as discrete events with postflood recovery sequences so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The observation window spans over 3 yr from 1984 to 1987 so the duration is 3 yr expressed as 1095 days.

The study imposed experimental removals of plant functional types and a disturbed control described as mechanical disturbance and it maintained plots free of target species throughout the whole experiment. Resilience was evaluated using repeated measurements of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi variables including total root colonization and vesicle and arbuscule colonization assessed after 5 17 and 29 months and spore density richness evenness and diversity assessed after 5 17 29 36 and 39 months. The discussion states that effects on total mycorrhizal colonization were significant at 5 months but disappeared by 17 and 29 months and it concludes that AMF communities are highly resilient. This corresponds to recovery degree because the disturbed treatments returned to no longer being detectably different from controls rather than estimating a recovery rate or a specific recovery time. The target variables used to quantify resilience are AMF colonization percentages and spore community metrics which are community level fungal properties. The site is a mountain shrubland in Sierras Grandes de Cordoba central Argentina with coordinates 31 30 N 64 35 W. The first author affiliation is Universidad Nacional de Cordoba in Argentina. The disturbance is manipulated structural because it is a removal experiment altering vegetation composition and soil disturbance rather than an unmanipulated event. The disturbance pattern is press because removals were continued throughout the whole experiment to maintain plots free of target species. The observation duration spans from initial removals in November December 1998 to the last spore sampling in February 2002 which is 39 months or 1185 days.

Framework: resilience = capacity to return to a pre-disturbance state
Justification: The introduction defines forest resilience as the capacity of a forest to return to a pre-disturbance state and states it is strongly dependent on sufficient tree regeneration. The study uses field measurements from 1485 sites across 52 wildfires and combines tree seedling presence and density data with climate data. Resilience is operationalised using post-fire tree regeneration with a recruitment threshold where a site is scored 1 if it had an equal or greater number of seedlings than the number of pre-fire trees and 0 otherwise. This compares post-fire seedling density to pre-fire tree density which is a recovery degree concept rather than a recovery rate or recovery time. The target variable used for the core resilience quantification is tree seedling density relative to pre-fire tree density. The approach is field observation because seedling data were collected in the field between 2010 and 2014 and the fires were unmanipulated wildfires. The disturbance is wildfire and it is treated as a discrete event with post-fire years 1 to 3 and time since fire analyses which matches a pulse disturbance. The study domain is the US Rocky Mountains with sites ranging from Colorado to northern Idaho and Montana. Latitude and longitude are recorded for sites and the region spans 13 degrees of latitude but specific coordinates are not provided in the text so an exact coordinate cannot be derived beyond a representative regional average. The observation window relevant to disturbance and response spans fires from 1988 to 2011 and measurements through 2014 which is 26 years or 9490 days.

The introduction defines metapopulation resilience as the ability of metapopulations to compensate and to recover from disturbances. The methods state that they developed a numerical model and explored 9504 ecological scenarios with each scenario replicated 100 times. The disturbance was imposed in the model because they reduced the metapopulation by 90% in a single year and then allowed recovery dynamics to arise from the disturbed state. The recovery metric used for resilience quantification is recovery rate defined as the time for the metapopulation to reach at least pre-disturbance abundance for five consecutive years which is a recovery time measure. The target variable used in the recovery metric is metapopulation adult abundances summed across patches. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study used model simulations and a numerical model rather than collecting new field data. The disturbance type is model structural because the disturbance is implemented as proportional loss or extirpation of local patches within the model. The disturbance pattern is pulse because the disturbance is described as a single pulsed event curtailed after a single year. Observation duration is 50 years after disturbance for the nonrecovery metric and the model figures show disturbance in year 50 with trajectories to year 100 so the modeled window spans 100 years which is 36500 days but the recovery evaluation window is within 50 years after disturbance which is 18250 days. The first author affiliation is Simon Fraser University in Canada.

The study quantifies resilience using co-extinction simulations described as estimating parrot community robustness to plant species loss and it reports the proportion of parrot species that remain as plant species are removed. The Methods state that fieldwork was observational and conducted during eight surveys in 2011–2013 and that quantitative networks used the number of individual parrots observed interacting with each plant species as a proxy for interaction frequency. Plant removal is an explicitly imposed disturbance in simulations where they removed one plant species at a time and compared scenarios that consider or ignore the mutualism–antagonism continuum. The disturbance is biotic because it is plant species loss and co-extinction cascades in a plant–parrot network. The disturbance is treated as pulse because removals are discrete sequential events with a clear stepwise progression. The quantification corresponds to resistance because robustness is operationalized as how much plant loss is required before parrot species are lost such as the loss of 96% of plant species required to lose 50% of parrot species. The target variable is abundance because extinction is determined from dependences based on the proportion of individuals feeding on remaining plants and the outcome is the proportion of parrot species remaining. The level is community because the response variable is parrot community robustness and the proportion of parrot species remaining. The approach includes field observation for the interaction data and modeling and simulation for the co-extinction simulations. The study system is in Bolivia in the inter-Andean valleys and the habitat is described as a tropical dry forest biome. The first author affiliation is in Spain at Centro de Investigaciones sobre Desertificación in Valencia. No coordinates are provided in the text for the study area. Observation duration spans 2011–2013 which is three years converted to 1095 days.

The introduction defines resilience as the ability to recover to the pre-disturbed state and resistance as the ability to withstand disturbance. The conclusions state that resistance and resilience are the two components of ecosystem stability. The methods describe a pan-European multi-site field experiment where experimental disturbance was systematic human trampling at five intensities including an undisturbed control. Vegetation surveys occurred immediately prior to disturbance then 2 to 4 weeks post-disturbance and again 1 year post-disturbance. The response variables were resistance and resilience indices calculated from total vegetation cover using disturbance intensity values. Resilience was calculated using Dint values 1 year post-disturbance and expressed as a proportional change relative to the potential maximal change which is a degree of recovery in cover. The target variable used for the main resilience index was total vegetation cover excluding litter and dead plant material. The study focuses on herb layer vascular plant species so the taxon is Plantae. The sites were in 10 European countries listed in Table 1 and the table provides site latitudes and longitudes. The first author affiliation is Goethe-Universitaet Frankfurt am Main in Germany. The disturbance is manipulated trampling by people which is a biotic disturbance and it is applied as discrete passes with post-disturbance recovery measurements so it is treated as a pulse. The observation window spans from pre-disturbance to 1 year post-disturbance which is approximately 395 days because the post-disturbance survey was 2 to 4 weeks after trampling.

The study states it was a manipulative field experiment that coincided with an unprecedented 2003 European heat wave and it assessed the short term ecological response to rapid global warming. The Methods specify that the rate of recovery from climate perturbation was estimated by calculating a linear regression of shoot density ln transformed against time for each experimental unit and using the sampling date with the lowest abundance as a starting point. The Results report that slopes were significantly higher with increasing genotypic diversity suggesting faster recovery and possibly higher ecological resilience. Therefore resilience is directly quantified from observed time series of shoot density in the field and the quantification corresponds to recovery rate. The Introduction explicitly mentions understanding factors that enhance resistance and resilience in the face of climatic extremes indicating resilience is discussed in parallel with resistance without an explicit equation. The field site is given as Maasholm Germany with coordinates 54°41 N 10°12 E. The experimental period is from May 16 through Sep. 26 2003 which is 133 days. The focal organism is the seagrass Zostera marina a marine flowering plant and the disturbance is the unmanipulated climatic heat wave.

The study states that spatial resilience is assessed with an indicator-based system and a multi-criteria evaluation method with GIS zoning. The indicator system consists of ecological sensitivity water quality and vegetation cover and ecological sensitivity is derived from distance from important ecological functional areas population density and economic density. Water quality is evaluated using NH3-N TP and COD and vegetation cover is derived from remote sensing data and field investigations. Resilience is therefore constructed from proxy indicators rather than measured as recovery dynamics of a state variable after a disturbance so the measurement is inferred and quantification is inferred. The paper discusses resilience alongside vulnerability robustness and sustainability but does not define an explicit equation framework that includes resilience. The resilience assessment is applied to the Taihu Lake watershed in the south of Jiangsu Province China and includes Taihu Lake and surrounding cities and counties. The first author affiliation lists Xiamen University in China so the institution country is China. The methods describe spatial data and field investigations in summer 2008 and water quality monitoring sections so the approach is field observation. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to Taihu Lake watershed and the range is set to 0 because only one averaged coordinate is reported. Because the target variables are spatial indicators across a watershed and zoning maps the level is landscape. Disturbance is discussed as pressures such as urbanization and pollution but resilience is not quantified as response to a defined disturbance event so disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not available for observed resilience quantification.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + maintenance
Justification: The paper proposes an ecosystem health assessment framework incorporating 4 components namely vigor organization resilience and maintenance and quantifies health as H = (V × O × R × M)1/4. Resilience is not measured from recovery dynamics but is defined as potential resilience inferred from human pressure because the component of resilience was measured by the intensity of dominating pressures induced by human beings and further corrected by the current speed and the more intensive the pressure suffered by the ecosystem is the weaker the potential resilience is. The resilience indicator is computed as R = [r × (1 − P)]1/2 where P is a pressure indicator derived from stressors of inland socioeconomic activities coastline exploitation and marine traffic and r is current speed as a correction factor. These inputs are proxy indicators rather than observed post disturbance recovery trajectories so the measurement type is inferred. The target variables used to quantify resilience are the pressure stressors including resident population and economic income and the correction factor current speed which correspond to socio eco parameter and abiotic parameter. The study uses field sampling for other components and uses simulated current speed and scenario calculations so the approach includes field observation and modeling and simulation. The first author affiliations are in Qingdao Shandong Province China so the institution country is CN. The study area is the southern waters of Miaodao Archipelago in the Bohai Strait China and stations are within 120.7667–120.5889 E and 38.0–37.8889 N so the field country is CN and the average coordinate is N37.9445 E120.6778 with a range under two degrees so location range is 0. The system is nearshore marine waters so the habitat type is Marine.Neritic. Because resilience is inferred from pressures rather than observed disturbance response the disturbance fields are NA and observation duration is NA.

The study uses tree ring width chronologies as annual growth observations and states that Gd represents mean growth levels during drought year(s) and Gpre and Gpost are mean growth levels 3 years before and after drought year(s). It defines resilience as Rs which compares post drought and pre drought growth and evaluates the capability of recovering from the disturbance and gives Rs = Gpost/Gpre. It also states that drought responses can be evaluated by drought sensitivity resistance Rt and the post drought recovery rate resilience Rs. The disturbance is extreme drought years identified by the lowest 10th SPEI percentile for 1950 to 2020 and droughts are classified into single year and multi year types. Drought events are treated as discrete years or multi year periods with a defined post drought interval which matches a pulse disturbance pattern. The target variable used in the resilience indices is ring width indices which are annual forest growth. The chronologies represent site level means quantifying annual forest growth which supports an ecosystem level response variable. The approach is field observation because the analysis uses observational tree ring databases ITRDB GenTree and tropical data rather than an experiment. The location and field country are global because the paper states Location Global and uses global databases. The first author affiliation is Peking University Beijing China which corresponds to CN. The observation window is 1950 to 2020 which is 70 years or 25550 days using 365 days per year. The habitat is forest because the study assesses drought impacts on the growth of global forests and refers to forested biomes.

Framework: resilience = recovery quickly after disturbance + return to its original state
Justification: The paper states that it analyses aboveground biomass recovery during secondary succession in 45 forest sites and about 1500 forest plots. It defines biomass resilience as the ability to recover quickly after disturbance which is the absolute recovery and return to its original pre deforestation state which is the relative recovery. In Methods biomass resilience was quantified in two ways with absolute recovery rate using site specific models to predict AGB at 20 years and relative recovery as AGB expressed as a percentage of the median AGB of old growth forest plots in the area. Because resilience is quantified from AGB trajectories over stand age since abandonment using chronosequence plot data it is based on direct observations of the system state variable AGB. The quantification selected is recovery rate because the Methods explicitly call the absolute metric the absolute recovery rate and operationalize it as predicted AGB at 20 years. The target variable used for resilience quantification is aboveground biomass AGB. AGB is summed across all trees to obtain live plot AGB and used to quantify recovery which is an ecosystem level stock. The approach is field observation because the study compiled chronosequence studies with plots where all stems were measured and identified and biomass estimated. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University in The Netherlands. The disturbance is forest cover removal for agricultural use because the study focuses on secondary forests that regrow after nearly complete removal of forest cover for agricultural use and models time since abandonment when crop production and cattle ranching ceased. The disturbance pattern is pulse because the disturbance is treated as a past event with a time since abandonment and recovery is evaluated over subsequent years. Observation duration is 36500 days because the chronosequence includes secondary forest plots up to 100 years old and the text states plots of 100 years old are also second growth.

The study monitored macroinvertebrates and periphyton biomass before and sequentially after each of 22 experimental floods over 10 years which is direct observation of system response. The Introduction defines resistance as the relative lack of loss in a specified parameter to floods and resilience as the positive slope in a specified parameter vs time following a flood. The Methods state resilience was assessed by calculating the recovery slope in periphyton biomass and macroinvertebrate density following each flood and that the steeper the slope the faster the recovery and the higher the resilience. Recovery slope is a rate based measure so quantification is recovery rate. The target variables used for resilience are macroinvertebrate density and periphyton biomass as AFDM. Periphyton biomass is measured as ash free dry mass and is treated as periphyton biomass in the resilience calculation. The work is a field based experimental flood program with 22 separate floods released between 2000 and the end of 2010 and long term monitoring at a 200 m reach. The site is the Spol River downstream of Livigno reservoir on the Swiss Italian border with coordinates given as 10 81 10 22 E and 46 83 60 38 N. The first author affiliation is Eawag in Duebendorf Switzerland. The upland vegetation is dominated by coniferous forest and the study reach is within the Swiss National Park. The disturbance is the experimental floods released from the dam and these are discrete events lasting between 6 and 8 hours except the June 2009 flood so the pattern is pulse. The Methods state recovery slopes used data covering a maximum of 70 post flood days so observation duration is 70 days.

The introduction defines an adaptation goal as approaches that promote forest resistance and resilience with resistance described as minimizing negative impacts during the drought and resilience described as maximizing recovery rates following drought. The methods quantify outcomes as stand level basal area increment and proportional growth and as tree drought stress represented by the proportion of years with extremely low growth rates. The analysis uses a predictive relationship for growth and uses SOILWAT2 and climate model scenarios and thinning scenarios to estimate future growth and low growth years rather than measuring post drought recovery dynamics from an observed time series of the focal system. Because resilience is not directly measured as recovery after a defined drought event but is evaluated through modeled vulnerability indicators such as proportional growth and percent of years with extremely low growth it is inferred. The approach is modeling and simulation because growth is estimated from Eq 1 and soil moisture is simulated with SOILWAT2 under GCM RCP scenarios. The first author affiliation is U.S. Geological Survey in Flagstaff Arizona indicating institution country US. The study area is the 4FRI landscape on the Coconino and Kaibab National Forests in Northern Arizona USA indicating field country US. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated from Northern Arizona near Flagstaff. The habitat is ponderosa pine forest types so Forest. The focal organism is ponderosa pine Pinus ponderosa so Plantae. Disturbance fields are NA because measurement is inferred.

Framework: resilience = capacity to absorb perturbation while retaining function + structure + identity
Justification: The study defines resilience as the capacity of a system to absorb perturbation while retaining function structure and thus identity. The study quantifies resilience by simulating stands and reporting the percentage of stands that remained deciduous forest or transitioned to mixed forest black spruce forest or nonforest 50 years after simulated stand replacing fire. Vegetation type outcomes are classified using importance values based on relative proportions of species density and basal area and a nonforest threshold of fewer than 50 stems per ha. The disturbance is a prescribed stand replacing fire implemented by prescribing 100% mortality to prefire trees saplings and seedlings. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event after which recovery and state are evaluated which matches a pulse disturbance. The approach is a simulation experiment using the individual based forest process model iLand. The target variable used to quantify resilience is stand dominance and vegetation type which is community composition. The study area and field data used to initialize and benchmark the model are from interior Alaska USA including stands that burned in 2004 and were sampled in 2017. The first author affiliation is Columbia University in the United States. The simulations run for tens to hundreds of years and stands are evaluated 50 years after the second fire with fire return intervals up to 250 years after the initial 2004 fire implying a maximum span of about 100 years from 2015 initialization to 50 years post second fire.

The study quantified canopy gap formation and closure using three repeat airborne lidar acquisitions in 2009 2017 and 2021 and analysed canopy height changes at 1 m resolution which is direct observation of system dynamics. Resilience was defined as having higher rates of gap closure than gap formation at the landscape scale. The core resilience comparison contrasted annual closure rates versus annual formation rates and reported closure rate 0.74 ha 100 ha−1 year−1 and formation rate 0.58 ha 100 ha−1 year−1. This operationalization treats resilience as recovery dynamics in canopy structure and thus matches recovery rate. The target variable used to quantify closure and formation was canopy height model derived gap area defined by vegetation less than 5 m in height and its change through time which is an ecosystem level structural and functional canopy property. The approach was field observation via remote sensing because the lidar acquisitions were collected over Berchtesgaden National Park. The first author affiliation is Technische Universität München in Germany. The study area is Berchtesgaden National Park Germany at the border to Austria and the data were collected there. The paper provides an elevation range and a mapped study area but no explicit coordinates so the location is approximated to the park area. Major natural disturbance agents stated for the area were bark beetles and wind throw besides smaller areas affected by avalanches and the gap dynamics were analysed as ongoing natural disturbance and recovery over the full 12 year window which corresponds to a press framing. The observation duration spans from 2009 to 2021 which the paper states as 12 years.

The study compiles field based pre and post drought community data from 131 forest and woodland sites and quantifies replacement patterns after tree mortality concomitant with drought. Replacement patterns are quantified using a community resemblance index described as a joint compositional and structural community resemblance index based on relative cover and identity of trees shrubs and saplings. The index is defined so that CRI equals 0 reflects no change and CRI equals 1 corresponds to maximum possible change and it is used to quantify forest and woodland resistance or potential vegetation type conversion. The text states drought represents a likely driving factor of species turnover and structural change with strong influences on ecosystem resistance and thus short term resilience. Because resilience is discussed alongside resistance without an explicit equation the framework is coded as resistance + resilience. Quantification is resistance because the main metric CRI is explicitly used to quantify resistance. Target variables used in CRI are relative cover and species identity which correspond to cover and composition. The level is community because the index is based on community composition and structure across multiple woody species. The approach is field observation because contributing authors performed field based studies and postmortality surveys. The first author affiliation is CREAF in Spain. The field data are across the world and the figure caption states multiple biomes across the world and the map shows global sites so field country and location are global. The disturbance is drought linked mortality which is a climatic disturbance and it is not experimentally manipulated. The disturbance is treated as an event with postmortality surveys conducted 1 to 23 years after mortality so it is a pulse. Observation duration is taken from the stated range 1 to 37 years from the initial year of tree mortality to surveys which equals 13505 days.

The introduction defines spatial stability as requiring a disturbance and the subsequent restoration of the original spatial configuration and states that the degree of similarity between pre and post disturbance configurations indicates spatial resilience. The methods describe two applied disturbances consisting of burning the substratum with a propane butane torch and scraping the substratum using a steel brush. The experiment was repeated in two consecutive years 2004 and 2005 with each trial lasting 1 year and the disturbance was applied at the beginning and after 1 year to re standardize plots. Resilience was quantified using the intensity criterion described as the return to pre disturbance intensity of grazing effects and assessed by checking consistency of grazing effects between years and by log response ratios based on algal cover or chlorophyll a in grazed versus grazer exclusion plots. The target variables used in the resilience criteria are algal cover from photographs and chlorophyll a concentration from rock chips. These variables represent community level algal assemblage responses because analyses were done at species level and functional group level and trophic level for the algal assemblage. The approach is a field experiment because the study used fenced grazer exclusion plots and open controls on a rocky shore platform. The field site is Old Woman’s River on the south coast of South Africa with coordinates given as 33 28 57 S and 27 9 05 E. The first author affiliation lists Abo Akademi University and Novia University of Applied Sciences in Finland. The discussion states stability is defined by the combination of resilience tolerance and resistance of species in a community.

The study imposed experimental chronic defoliation regimes and then monitored recovery after cessation because it states palms were subjected to five defoliation levels every 6 months over 3 years and surviving palms were monitored for 3 years after the cessation of the defoliation treatment. Resilience is treated as recovery because the introduction defines resilience as extent and speed of recovery and the methods state the speed of recovery was quantified by determining the number of years it took for full recovery with outcomes 1 2 and 3 years after defoliation stopped. Full recovery is defined as when the mean value for the defoliated treatment was not significantly different from that of the controls. The target variables used for this recovery quantification are the functional and demographic traits listed in the methods including leaf persistence leaf production rate leaf length total leaf area mortality growth and reproduction. The work is a field experiment at the Chajul Biological Field Station within the Montes Azules Biosphere Reserve in Chiapas Mexico with coordinates given as 16 17 06 N and 90 56 W. The habitat is lowland evergreen tropical forest. The first author affiliation includes Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico in Morelia Mexico. A severe drought in 2000 is described as occurring in the first year after defoliation ceased and it decreased survival growth and reproduction and the ability of plants to recover from defoliation.

The introduction defines resilience as the ability to return to initial ecosystem functioning after a perturbation and states that resilience can be measured only after the stress is released either as time till fully recovered or at a given time point quantifying the remaining stress response. The methods describe an experimental summer drought with rain out shelters and manual watering of controls and then rewetting with 50 mm of water after 5.5 weeks. The study explicitly labels peak drought as resistance labelling and the recovery phase shortly after rewetting as resilience labelling. Resilience is quantified from observed recovery after rewetting because the results state that in both grassland types the C allocation largely recovered after rewetting and that at the resilience labelling the majority of parameters completely recovered and total 13C uptake was already exceeding the control values. This corresponds to recovery degree because recovery is assessed by comparison to control values at a given recovery time point rather than estimating a recovery rate or time to recovery. The core resilience variable used repeatedly is total 13C uptake and related carbon allocation and transfer variables derived from 13C pulse labelling which are ecosystem carbon dynamics. The work is a field experiment because intact vegetation soil monoliths were taken from two sites embedded at the meadow site and subjected to drought manipulation and rewetting in a common garden experiment. The first author affiliation is Max Planck Institute for Biogeochemistry Jena Germany. The field site is near Neustift in the Stubai valley in the Austrian Central Alps with coordinates given for abandoned grassland and hay meadow and the average of these coordinates is reported. The habitat is mountain grassland and the quantified resilience concerns plant carbon allocation and uptake. The disturbance is manipulated drought and rewetting which is a climatic disturbance treated as a discrete event with a defined end when drought simulation was stopped and rewetting began. The observation window for drought and recovery spans from experiment start on 21 May 2014 to recovery labelling start on 16 July 2014.

The study defines system stability as a synthesis of resistant and resilient properties with the explicit statement that system stability is a synthesis of resistant and resilient properties. The study tests resilience by monitoring postflood trajectories and states that postflood trajectories of chlorophyll a indicated that algae at upwelling zones recovered from disturbance significantly faster than those at downwelling zones. The Methods section states that resilience of upwelling and downwelling subreaches was investigated by monitoring postflood recovery of periphyton and benthic organic matter following a flood on 15 September 1990. Recovery rates were analyzed by statistically comparing slopes of chlorophyll vs days postflood and regression analysis was used to calculate accumulation rates. Chlorophyll a is measured as periphyton standing crop and is used as the core variable for recovery and resilience. The disturbance is a flash flood with magnitude given and it is treated as a discrete event followed by an 89 d recovery sequence with sampling on days 4 13 34 59 and 89 postflood. The study site is Sycamore Creek Arizona USA located 32 km northeast of Phoenix and the field work is in sand bottomed reaches of a desert stream. The first author affiliation is Arizona State University Tempe Arizona USA. No taxonomic kingdom is specified for the primary producers beyond benthic algae and periphyton so taxon cannot be assigned from the provided options.

The introduction defines resilience as the rate of recovery to the predisturbance state and the abstract states they tested indicators against postperturbation measurements of recovery rate. The methods state they used a simulation model and then measured recovery rates from simulated pulses of available phosphorus. The perturbation is described as a single pulse of nutrients into the epilimnetic available phosphorus compartment on day 50. Recovery was quantified using displacement between reference and perturbed simulations summed over trophic levels and integrated to calculate return times which estimate the mean time for displacement to decay. Because resilience is quantified from the system response after an imposed disturbance in the model this is observed measurement under the provided definition. The target variable for resilience quantification is the whole system displacement based on nutrient stored in trophic levels and phosphorus concentration which is a system level functional state variable. The model is calibrated using data from Peter and Paul lakes near Land O'Lakes Wisconsin and the first author affiliation is University of Wisconsin Madison. The habitat is a north temperate lake which fits the wetland category. The disturbance is a modeled nutrient pulse which is a chemical change in phosphorus availability and it is treated as a pulse. Simulations were run for 16 weeks and the perturbation occurs on day 50 with measurements through the end of the simulation so the observation window spans 112 days.

Framework: population performance = population growth rate + demographic resilience
Justification: The study states that it uses Dynamic Energy Budget Integral Projection Models and that it models survival growth and reproduction under different feeding levels. It defines objective 4 as testing whether life history axes predict population growth rate and speed of recovery from perturbations described as demographic resilience. It quantifies demographic resilience as the damping ratio xi with xi = lambda divided by the absolute value of lambda2 and it calls this resilience to perturbations. Damping ratio is not a recovery degree recovery rate recovery time resistance latitude or invariability metric in the provided options so quantification is Other. The target variable used to quantify resilience is the damping ratio derived from the population projection matrix A and its eigenvalues which is a demographic parameter at the population level. The disturbance is implemented in the model as different feeding levels E(Y) representing low and high food availability and feeding level is treated as an environmental condition maintained in the model rather than a discrete event so the pattern is Press. The first author affiliation is Newcastle University in the UK so the institution country is GB. Habitat and temperature at depth are derived from global ocean temperature layers and IUCN polygons and the study does not restrict the resilience quantification to a single country so field country and location are Global. The subjects are elasmobranchs sharks skates and rays which are Animalia. The DEB IPM is annual with survival from year t to year t plus 1 and the life history traits include generation time in years so the modeled observation step is one year which is 365 days.

The introduction states that they use resilience to refer to the time period required for a vegetation to return to its pre-disturbance state and resistance to describe the ability of a system to resist change when subjected to disturbance or stress which presents resilience and resistance in parallel. The methods use historical aerial photographs to identify old tracked vehicle disturbance sites and compare time since disturbance across a chronosequence which uses observed system states rather than proxies. Resilience is quantified as time since disturbance to approach the undisturbed reference condition with results stating mesotrophic grasslands typically took between 30 and 40 years to re-establish whereas calcareous grasslands took at least 50 years and that resilience periods incorporating both the sward and seed bank exceeded 50 years. The core quantification uses vegetation species composition similarity between disturbed and adjacent undisturbed reference sites based on Sørensen’s similarity index and regeneration index combining surface vegetation and seed bank composition. These are community composition measures so the target variable type is composition and the level is community. The approach is field observation because vegetation soils and seed bank data were collected from each old disturbance site and the disturbance history was reconstructed from aerial photographs rather than imposed experimentally. The first author affiliation is Centre for Ecology and Hydrology Monks Wood UK so the institution country is GB. The field site is Salisbury Plain Training Area in southern England so the field country is GB. The disturbance is tracked vehicle disturbance identified from photographs and treated as discrete past events so it is unmanipulated structural disturbance with a pulse pattern. The chronosequence spans disturbance dates within a 50-year time period and figures and tables refer to recovery over up to 50 years so the observation duration is 50 years which is 18250 days. No coordinates are provided in the text so location and location range are NA.

The introduction states that resilience can be measured as the inverse of the recovery time following a defined impact. The methods estimate the date of the last fishing event using VMS data and link time since the last fishing event to the abundance of epifaunal benthic invertebrates to infer recovery trajectories. Recovery is modelled with a logistic equation fitted to abundance and time since last fishing event and recovery time tR is defined as the time when 90% of carrying capacity K is reached. The biological data are seabed photographs from August 2008 used to quantify abundance of epifaunal communities and functional groups. The disturbance is bottom fishing by scallop dredgers and otter trawlers and the discussion describes disturbance as applied in acute pulses where a ground is intensively fished for a few days before the fleet moves on. The study area is the territorial waters of the Isle of Man United Kingdom within the 12 nautical miles limit. The first author affiliation is Bangor University in the UK. The observation window for disturbance history is July 2007 to August 2008 and sampling occurred in August 2008 so the duration is 397 days. The habitat is seabed mixed coarse and hard substrata in territorial sea which is a shallow nearshore marine environment. The quantified organisms are benthic epifaunal invertebrates which are animals.

The study analysed Australian Institute of Marine Science Long-term Monitoring Programme estimates of Acropora coral cover as a percentage of transect area sampled between 1995 and 2011. Disturbances were identified between consecutive surveys by matching decreases in coral cover with signatures of bleaching CoTS outbreaks disease and storms and a disturbance was recorded when coral cover change between surveys exceeded 5%. The paper states it ranks disturbances in reducing the ability of coral populations to recover following disturbance and discusses resilience alongside recovery without defining a higher level variable. Resilience is therefore quantified from observed coral cover trajectories and their recovery following disturbance which aligns with recovery rate rather than a static proxy. The modelling uses a semi parametric Bayesian hierarchical model to estimate coral cover trajectories and cumulative disturbance effects and uses machine learning to rank disturbance importance which is modeling and simulation applied to field observation data. The location is the Great Barrier Reef Australia and the first author affiliation is Queensland University of Technology in Australia.
