The habitat is shallow nearshore coral reef environment consistent with Marine Neritic. The major taxa studied are Acropora species which are animals. Disturbances are unmanipulated and include bleaching as climatic CoTS predation as biotic disease as biotic and storms as fire as written. Disturbances are treated as occurring between surveys as discrete events so the pattern is pulse. The time period is 1995 to 2011 which is 16 years giving 5840 days using 365 days per year.

The study used ground based field data comparing oiled and reference sites and explicitly tracked effects over time to estimate recovery time frames using annual mean ln response ratios plotted by year over 2010 to 2017. The Methods state they examined impacts and recovery with effect size response ratios and defined recovery when terminal years of ln response ratios were zero approaching zero or oscillating around zero. The Introduction states they tracked the degree of effects temporally to estimate recovery time frames. Resilience was not defined as part of an explicit equation framework and is discussed in relation to recovery and stability without a formal component definition. Quantification aligns with recovery time because results report recovery time frames such as three years or longer and five to six years post spill and incomplete recovery through at least seven years. Target variables used in the resilience quantification were plant percent cover stem density vegetation height and aboveground and belowground biomass. These variables were measured for the total plant community and for Spartina alterniflora and Juncus roemerianus indicating a community level focus. The Methods specify only ground based field studies and excluded laboratory mesocosm and remotely sensed data indicating field observation. The first author affiliation is Research Planning Inc in Tallahassee Florida USA. Study sites were distributed across Louisiana Mississippi and Alabama in the northern Gulf of Mexico which are in the USA. The disturbance is the Deepwater Horizon oil spill and oiling of marshes which is a chemical disturbance and it was not experimentally imposed in this synthesis. The spill is treated as a discrete event beginning April 2010 with post spill monitoring which matches a pulse disturbance. The observation window spans 2010 to 2017 which is seven years corresponding to 2555 days.

The paper states that stability metrics describe three distinct properties resistance recovery and resilience and it explicitly frames them as stability properties. It defines resilience as the ability of the system to persist in its initial state after a perturbation has ceased and says it can be obtained by comparing biomass in control and drought plots during the post drought period. In Methods it quantifies resilience using Hedges g as the difference of above ground biomass between drought and control plots during the post drought period. The target variable used for resilience quantification is above ground biomass. The drought is imposed by rainfall manipulation experiments using rain out shelters which are field experiments and the study excludes artificial conditions such as greenhouses. The abstract states Location Global and the map shows global site locations. The first author affiliation is Universidade do Estado do Rio de Janeiro Brazil. Because the study is a global meta analysis and does not provide a single coordinate for the resilience data the location is Global. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not coded because the instructions require NA when field country is Global for location range and the disturbance fields are not directly specified for the resilience quantification as a single disturbance event in one place.

The study reports a field experiment with factorially crossed perturbation and diversity manipulations and it simulated drought perturbation on constructed grassland ecosystems. Resilience is treated alongside resistance as recovery from perturbation and the text states ecosystems may resist change or exhibit resilience by returning to their original state after perturbation. Resilience was evaluated 9 and 12 months after the drought perturbation by calculating post to pre drought biomass production ratios and Table 1 defines early resilience and late resilience as natural logarithm of production ratios of post drought divided by pre drought. This uses observed above ground biomass production measured at harvests and therefore the measurement is observed. The quantification corresponds to recovery rate because resilience is calculated from post to pre ratios over defined time intervals and the text states the recovery from perturbation was fast and that initial resilience response was faster for low than for high diversity. The target variable used for resilience is above ground biomass production. The disturbance is manipulated drought treatment using roofs for 8 weeks which is a climatic disturbance imposed experimentally. The disturbance is a pulse because roofs remained in place for 8 weeks from 20 July to 18 September 1998 with a clear start and stop. Observation duration spans from the pre drought harvest 20 June 1997 to the last harvest 17 September 1999. The first author affiliation is Universitaet Zuerich in Switzerland and the experiment was carried out at the Swiss site of the BIODEPTH project. The habitat is experimental grassland communities and the organisms are plant species.

The study defines ecosystem stability as the combination of resistance and resilience with the explicit equation stability = resistance + resilience. It uses monthly MODIS EVI time series from 2005 to 2014 and climate anomaly time series and fits an AR1 model where Vt is the standardized EVI anomaly and Vt-1 is the one month lagged EVI anomaly. Resilience is quantified as 1 − δ where δ is the coefficient on Vt-1 and the text states that a larger δ means the ecosystem recovers more slowly and resilience refers to the speed to return to its original equilibrium state. Because resilience is derived from temporal autocorrelation and interpreted as recovery speed it matches recovery rate. The target variable used for resilience quantification is EVI described as a proxy of aboveground biomass and vegetation greenness and used to evaluate vegetation stability at large spatial scales which is treated here as an ecosystem function proxy. The approach is field observation because the data are satellite observations and not an imposed experiment. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic because the analysis is of short term climate anomalies in temperature water availability and cloudiness as a substitute for radiation. The disturbance is treated as press because the study analyzes continuous climate variability across the full 2005 to 2014 period rather than discrete events with a defined recovery window. The study area is the Taihang Mountains in north China and the first author affiliation is in Beijing China. The map axes show the region spans about 35 to 41 N and 109 to 118 E so the average coordinate is approximately N38.0 E113.5 and the range is coded as 0 because the paper does not provide exact site coordinates beyond the regional map. Vegetation types include forests shrubs steppes meadows and crops so the habitat source is multi. The resilience concerns vegetation so the taxon is Plantae. The observation duration is 2005 to 2014 which is 10 years or 3650 days.

The study states that it assesses resilience of seedlings to topkill and seedlings were clipped at 1 cm at about 2, 3, 4 and 5 months old in a common garden experiment. It monitored survival and growth and key plant traits for the following 2.5 years and measured regrowth and recovery after clipping. The methods define time to recovery as the first date after clipping when the difference between the treatment and control was not significant and this is a recovery time metric. Recovery was evaluated using total stem length as the best indicator of above ground biomass and recovery was assessed over time after the browse event. Resilience is not defined as part of an explicit equation framework and is discussed as resilience to topkill and recovery. The target variable used for resilience quantification is regrowth and recovery of above ground biomass operationalized as stem length which is a growth measure. The work is a field experiment at the Wits Rural Facility with vegetation cleared and seedlings planted and clipped. The first author affiliation is University of the Witwatersrand Johannesburg South Africa. The field site is in Limpopo Province South Africa with coordinates given as about 31 degrees 06 E and 24 degrees 30 S. The disturbance is simulated herbivory by clipping which represents browsing and is a manipulated biotic disturbance. The clipping is a once off event per treatment and recovery is tracked afterward which matches a pulse disturbance. The observation window for resilience spans from planting on 14 October 2016 through monitoring for 2.5 years which is about 912 days.

The paper develops and analyzes a mathematical model of semi arid savanna grazing systems with state variables grass biomass G and woody vegetation biomass W and fixed herbivore biomass H. Resilience is discussed in terms of multiple stable states domains of attraction and the separatrix with the Discussion stating that resilience emphasizes the existence of more than one domain of attraction and maintenance of that global structure. The Discussion proposes resilience measures as distances in H G W space from a reference point to the separatrix or to the saddle point D which corresponds to the magnitude of disturbance the system can withstand without shifting to the alternative stable state. The disturbances considered in the model and discussion include increases in H and decreases in G and leftward movement of the grass zero isocline induced by drought. Grazing pressure is treated as sustained intense grazing and fixed moderate stocking and persistent intense grazing which matches a press type driver rather than a discrete pulse in the core analysis. The target variables used in the model and resilience discussion are biomasses of grass and woody vegetation and herbivore biomass. The first author affiliation shown is Institute of Animal Resource Ecology University of British Columbia Vancouver Canada. The examples and parameterization are based on southern African experience and also include a mesquite grass system in the south western United States. No explicit coordinates are provided in the text so the location is coded as NA but the narrative centers on southern Africa and the model is not tied to a single sampled site.

The study used an experimental heat stress treatment where heated corals were ramped from 29 to 35 C over 3 h held at 35 C for 1 h and then returned to 29 C in about 2 h. Resilience was treated as transcriptome resilience defined as return to normal gene expression levels after stress and the paper states that fast return to normal levels was associated with low bleaching. This is a direct quantification from the system response over time because gene expression was measured at 5 h and 20 h and compared to controls to assess return to control levels. The framework is presented as transcriptome resilience linked to bleaching resistance because the discussion states transcriptome resilience may therefore be important to the resistance of corals. Quantification matches recovery rate because the key signal is faster decline and more rapid return to control levels by 20 h. The target variable used to quantify resilience is gene expression level at 20 h and its return to control levels which is a physiological indicator. The sampling location is given as the south lagoon of Ofu Island American Samoa with coordinates 14 11 S 169 36 W. The first author affiliation is Stanford University Hopkins Marine Station Pacific Grove CA USA. The habitat is a reef lagoon which is a shallow nearshore marine environment. The disturbance is manipulated heat stress which is a climatic disturbance and it is a pulse because it has a defined ramp peak and return within the experiment. The observation window for resilience spans from 5 h to 20 h which is 15 h or 0.625 days and is reported here as 1 day using whole days.

The study derived dendrophenotypic measures from dated annual growth rings and quantified resistance resilience and recovery using tree ring index time series which is direct observation. The resilience concept is explicitly applied as resistance resilience and recovery following Lloret et al. 2011. Resilience is defined as the ratio of TRI after vs before the event which is a comparison of post event state to pre event state and therefore matches recovery degree. The target variable used in these ratios is standardized tree ring index derived from tree ring width which is an individual growth measure. The work is based on wood cores collected from adult silver fir trees at two field sites and therefore is field observation. The first author affiliation is Philipps University Marburg in Germany. The sampling sites are in the Bavarian Forest National Park Germany at Filzwald 48.929 13.406 and Rachelsee 48.975 13.400 and the average coordinate is reported. The habitat is mixed mountain forest where silver fir grows with beech and Norway spruce. The study organism is silver fir Abies alba which is Plantae. The stress episode is attributed to air pollution particularly SO2 emissions and the study also analyses the drought year 1976 so disturbances are chemical and climatic and both are unmanipulated. The depression period 1974 to 1983 and the drought year 1976 are treated as discrete historical events with before during and after windows consistent with a pulse framing. The resilience related observation window for the depression analysis uses ten years before 1974 and ten years after 1983 which is 20 years or 7300 days.

The paper defines demographic resilience as the ability of a population to prevail after a disturbance and states that demographic resilience can be captured by three key components resistance compensation and recovery time. It quantifies these components from matrix population models using transient dynamics after a disturbance that changes population structure displacing it away from equilibrium structure. Resistance is calculated as the lowest population density that can be reached in the first time step after disturbance and compensation as the fastest population growth that can be realised in the first time step after disturbance. Recovery time is calculated as the time required to reattain a stable structure after a disturbance using eigenvalue damping. The matrices are from non captive populations in unmanipulated control conditions but the disturbance itself is treated as an imposed punctual event affecting population structure in the transient analysis. The target variables used to quantify resilience are demographic quantities derived from the MPMs including population growth or decline and return to stable stage structure. The study uses global demographic information from COMADRE and COMPADRE for 910 natural populations of animals and plants so the field country and location are global and habitat type spans multiple habitats. The first author affiliation is Oxford University in the United Kingdom. The methods specify annual dynamics and first time step measures implying a one year time step for the disturbance response calculations.

The study used a canopy removal experiment that pushed the system toward a tipping point with canopy trimmed to 0% 25% 50% 75% and 100%. Spatial early warning indicators were assessed after 1 and 2 yr following this perturbation by visually estimating percentage cover in a grid and using 100 minus turf cover as understory cover. Resilience was not quantified as recovery time rate or degree but as spatial early warning signals including standard deviation coefficient of variation Moran correlation spatial DFT at low frequency and spatial skewness. These indicators are based on spatial variance and related statistics so the quantification corresponds to invariability as the inverse of variance. The target variable used for these indicators was understory cover derived from percentage cover estimates. The work was a field experiment conducted along the rocky shore of Capraia Island with coordinates given as 43.048 N and 9.828 E. The disturbance was manipulated canopy removal maintained over time described as a press perturbation experiment. Sampling occurred in July 2014 and July 2015 which are 12 months apart so the observation duration is 365 days. The first author affiliation is University of Pisa in Italy and the field site is in Italy. The system is an intertidal rocky mid shore macroalgal canopy and turf assemblage which fits a coastal habitat and the focal organisms are macroalgae including Cystoseira amentacea.

The study develops and analyzes a reduced 2D dynamical model where the variables are the average pollinator and plant abundances and it compares these with abundances from the original high dimensional ODE system for 59 empirical networks. Resilience is operationalized through resilience functions defined as relationships between average species abundance and parameters reflecting environmental deterioration including the fraction of removed pollinators fn and the pollinator decay rate ϒ. The tipping point is defined as a critical point past which the whole network collapses with all species populations simultaneously becoming zero and the study evaluates whether the reduced model predicts the correct critical parameter value. This corresponds to quantifying resilience as the magnitude of deterioration the system can withstand before shifting to the collapsed extinction state. The target variables used in the resilience functions are average pollinator abundance and average plant abundance. The approach is modeling and simulation because the dynamics are computed from ODE models and artificial random networks and noise is added in simulations. The empirical networks are described as extracted from real data recorded over the world so the field country and location are global and the habitat type is multiple. The taxa involved are pollinators and plants corresponding to Animalia and Plantae. Disturbances include modeled structural removal of pollinators and links and modeled environmental deterioration represented by increasing decay rate and described as global warming and environmental deterioration. The disturbance is treated as press because parameters are continuously increased or removal fraction is gradually increased toward a critical point with no recovery interval. Observation duration is not reported because results are steady state calculations and parameter sweeps without a stated time span between first and last measurement.

The study uses a comparative analysis of species resilience to anthropogenic disturbance using values from Isaac and Cowlishaw 2004 which quantified disturbance effect size as abundance in disturbed habitat divided by abundance in undisturbed habitat and states increasing values reflect greater resilience to disturbance. This treats resilience as a proxy attribute derived from paired surveys rather than measuring recovery dynamics through time. The paper does not define a resilience framework such as resilience equals resistance plus recovery and instead discusses resilience as one variable among others in dilution effect assumptions. Because resilience is operationalized as a ratio of abundance between disturbed and undisturbed areas the quantification corresponds to inferred resilience rather than recovery rate time degree or invariability. The target variable used to quantify resilience is primate abundance in disturbed and undisturbed habitat. The resilience metric is at the population level because it is based on population surveys and abundance. The approach is field observation because the resilience data come from paired population surveys in disturbed and undisturbed areas. The first author affiliation is Harvard University in the United States. The paper draws on multiple datasets and studies including a global parasite database and IUCN range maps and does not specify a single country or coordinates for the resilience data source so field country and location cannot be determined from the text. Disturbance types considered for resilience are hunting agriculture and forestry but because measurement is inferred the disturbance fields are NA by instruction. No observation duration for resilience is provided because the resilience metric is not based on a time series and the paper gives no start and end dates for the paired surveys.

The study quantifies resilience from time series of satellite based vegetation indices by retrieving the 1 lag temporal autocorrelation from kNDVI derived for the 2000 to 2020 period at the global scale. The Methods state that they quantified the resilience of forest ecosystems their ability to recover from external perturbations by the use of the 1 lag TAC and that increased temporal autocorrelation reflects a decline in recovery rates due to critical slowing down. Therefore the measurement is observed and the quantification corresponds to recovery rate via critical slowing down indicators based on autocorrelation. The state variable used is kNDVI which is described as a robust proxy for ecosystem productivity and used as a metric to represent the state of forest ecosystems so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The analysis is based on Earth observations from MODIS and other gridded products so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation is European Commission Joint Research Centre Ispra Italy so the institution country is IT. The analysis is at the global scale using global gridded satellite and reanalysis products so field country and location are Global. The habitat is forest as the paper focuses on forest ecosystems and masks forest pixels using forest cover fraction. The disturbance is treated as environmental perturbations and climate change with water limitations and climate variability and the Methods use climate drivers from ERA5 Land so the disturbance type is unmanipulated climatic and it is treated as a press driver over the observation window. The observation period is 2000 to 2020 which is 21 years so 21 times 365 equals 7665 days.

The study evaluates resilience as the capacity to return to a forest vegetation type described as physiognomic recovery or resilience. The Methods state they assess potential for recovery using a minimum tree density threshold and classify stands into high moderate low potential for physiognomic recovery by comparing densities of trees and potential juvenile successors to the threshold. This is an observed measurement because it uses field surveyed tree densities and juvenile browsing and growth data collected in summers of 2016 and 2017 and reconstructs pre outbreak conditions by adding trees killed within the past 15 years. The quantification corresponds to latitude because resilience is operationalized as whether stands remain above a defined threshold for maintaining the forest state versus transition to low density forest below regional stocking levels. The target variable used to quantify resilience is tree and juvenile density in stems per hectare and juveniles per hectare. The level is ecosystem because the target variable is stand level forest cover recovery potential irrespective of individual identity and is used to infer recovery to a forest vegetation type. The approach is field observation because plots were surveyed in the San Juan Mountains and no experimental manipulation was imposed. The first author affiliation is University of Colorado Boulder in the United States. The field country is the United States because the study area is the San Juan Mountains southern Colorado USA. Disturbances are unmanipulated biotic disturbances because the study analyzes bark beetle outbreaks and ungulate herbivory occurring in the landscape. The disturbance pattern is press because ungulate browsing is treated as persistent post outbreak browsing and the analysis does not define an end followed by a recovery interval. Observation duration is 4380 days because pre outbreak reconstruction includes trees killed within the past 15 years.

The study used a nitrogen and phosphorus addition experiment in a temperate semi arid grassland that experienced a severe natural drought. Plant communities were surveyed annually from 2006 to 2008 and total plant cover and community composition were used to quantify stability dimensions. Resilience was explicitly defined as the rate of return to the pre disturbance state after disturbance and quantified as the slope of change following disturbance with cover change between 2007 and 2008 and Bray Curtis dissimilarity between drought and post drought communities. The disturbance was the natural drought in 2007 described as the driest year on record since 1953 with comparisons across pre drought 2006 drought 2007 and post drought 2008 years. The first author affiliation is Georgia Institute of Technology in the United States. The field site is Duolun Restoration Ecology Research Station Inner Mongolia China with coordinates 42o02′N 116o17′E. The observation window for disturbance response spans 2006 to 2008 which is two years or 730 days.

The study states that it modeled potential beaver dam-building capacity and associated surface water storage and fire resilience in California’s Sierra Nevada. Fire resilience was quantified by overlapping a static wildfire hazard potential raster with modeled dam capacity and water storage was estimated from BRAT dam capacity outputs using geometric assumptions. The wildfire hazard potential is described as a static map that does not reflect actual fire risk for any specific day or season and the analysis does not measure recovery dynamics after a disturbance. The drought component uses Normalized Deficit Cumulated as a proxy for drought risk and not observed system recovery. Because resilience is derived from modeled capacity and proxy risk layers rather than observed post-disturbance trajectories the measurement is inferred. No explicit equation framework defining resilience is provided and resilience is discussed as a benefit rather than decomposed into components. The first author affiliation listed is University of California Berkeley in the United States. The study region is California’s Sierra Nevada and parts of western Nevada are mentioned but the modeled region is defined as Sierra Nevada in California using 31 HUC-8 watersheds. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to the Sierra Nevada region center. Vegetation is described as including pine forest and oak forest and the dominant habitat for the region is forest. The resilience quantified is landscape level fire resilience area and water storage potential rather than a taxon response so taxon is not applicable. Disturbance type and pattern are not applicable because the study does not observe or impose a disturbance response trajectory.

Framework: resilience = ability to return to the initial state after a temporary perturbation
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the ability to return to the initial state after a temporary perturbation and also as recovery of the original community state following antibiotic exposure. The results quantify resilience by whether the community returned to its original stable state after removal of the antibiotic or instead transitioned to the alternative stable state. The response variable used to track recovery is species abundance measured as CFU per culture and CFU per mL in time series and state maps. Antibiotic exposure is experimentally imposed for one daily cycle and then removed which matches a pulse disturbance. The methods state that community exposure to antibiotics occurred by supplementing the media for day 3 and the time series in the figure description shows a 24 h cycle under antibiotic exposure. The observation window for the chloramphenicol time series spans day 0 to day 4. The study also uses in silico analysis and numerical simulations of a modified Lotka Volterra model with temporary antibiotic associated death rates. The first author affiliation is Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge Massachusetts which is in the United States. The experiments are laboratory cocultures in plates and no field sampling country or geographic coordinates are provided. The disturbance is chemical because the perturbations are antibiotics such as chloramphenicol and other drugs and the model also simulates antibiotic induced death rates.

The study analysed field collected species cover data from 2001 to 2022 and quantified plant community recovery following six wildfires. Plant cover was measured in plots using point intercept techniques and the response variable in the mixed effects random forests was the proportion of each plant community association at each plot derived from structural topic modelling. The introduction defines resilience as the capacity to recover after disturbance and the paper repeatedly refers to resistance and resilience together as R&R without defining a higher level variable. Recovery was evaluated by comparing post fire plant community outcomes across years since fire and across monitoring years rather than by a proxy only. The disturbance was wildfire and the sites were areas that had previously burned between 2001 and 2015 which is treated as discrete fire events. Monitoring began the year after fire and continued between 5 years post fire for Soda and 14 years post fire for Murphy and the field data span 2001 to 2022. The first author affiliation is US Geological Survey in Boise Idaho USA. The study area is sagebrush steppe of the western USA and Great Basin USA. The habitat is sagebrush steppe including mountain big sagebrush communities and lowland salt desert which is best matched by Shrubland. The taxa quantified are plant communities including sagebrush steppe grasses forbs and shrubs. No explicit coordinates were provided in the text for plot locations or fire perimeters so location and range cannot be reported.

The paper defines resilience as part of stability by stating that stability is defined via three fundamental properties constancy resilience and persistence. Resilience is not quantified directly because the study uses temporal stability as a proxy for estuarine stability and temporal stability is defined as mean macroinvertebrate biomass divided by the standard deviation of community biomass production through time which represents lower temporal variation around the mean. The target variable used in the temporal stability equation is macroinvertebrate biomass. The data are field collected macroinvertebrate samples from two Portuguese estuaries Mondego and Mira with repeated sampling across years and seasons. The disturbance comparison is based on different human induced impact levels with Mondego described as impacted under permanent anthropogenic pressure including dredging activities and nutrients runoff and Mira described as relatively undisturbed though with some agriculture and aquaculture farms. These pressures are treated as ongoing conditions rather than discrete events so the disturbance pattern is press. The longest observation window used for temporal stability is stated as a 10 years period from 1998 to 2007. The first author affiliation is IMAR Institute of Marine Research Coimbra Portugal. The study sites are the Mondego estuary on the northwestern coast of Portugal and the Mira estuary on the southwest coast of Portugal and the provided coordinate is an average location for these two estuaries.

The study quantifies resilience using a spatially extended model with plant density P and toxin concentration T governed by Eq. 1 and it analyzes how solutions persist as mortality is increased. The text states that the system is driven through regimes by a slow sustained increase of the mortality rate and that traveling pulses persist beyond the tipping points of the homogeneous vegetation cover. The key resilience claim is that traveling pulses are stable for values of mortality above the tipping point of the homogeneous system and survive until they are destroyed at a fold at !SNTP. This corresponds to a threshold limit beyond which the populated state cannot be maintained which matches the latitude definition. The variable used to represent system state in the resilience comparison is plant population density P and figures report maximum plant density and mean plant density and the discussion states mean biomass density of spatiotemporal solutions is greater and persists far beyond tipping points. The approach is modeling and simulation because methods describe numerical simulations using Julia FourierFlows and continuation methods. The disturbance is treated as increasing mortality over time due to climate change and sea temperature above 28 C increasing mortality which is a climatic driver and it is implemented as a sustained increase in mortality steps. The first author affiliation is in Palma de Mallorca Spain. Aerial image coordinates in Fig. 1 include locations in Spain the Netherlands and Libya and the average coordinate is computed from the four provided points. The habitat is seagrass meadows and sediment toxins in coastal shallow marine settings which fits Marine.Neritic. The focal organism is Posidonia oceanica which is a plant.

The introduction states that the paper uses amphipod functional parameters as a proxy because it will "use an amphipod’s functional parameters (e.g. productivity, density) as a proxy for the system’s resilience between periods". This means resilience is not quantified from a direct recovery trajectory of the system state variable after a defined disturbance within the study design but is inferred from proxy population parameters compared across periods. The framework in the introduction presents resilience alongside resistance as "the system’s own resistance and resilience to disturbance" without defining a higher level variable. The target variables used as the proxy are explicitly given as "productivity, density" and the methods quantify density and secondary production and biomass. The study is based on monthly sampling in the Mondego estuary with stations shown in Fig. 1 and described as field sampling from April 2009 to March 2010. The first author affiliation is University of Coimbra in Portugal. The study area provides coordinates "(40◦08′N, 8◦50′W)" for the Mondego estuary. The organism is Echinogammarus marinus an amphipod so the taxon is Animalia. Because measurement is inferred the disturbance type pattern and observation duration for disturbance response are coded as NA per instructions.

The study states it used standardized ring width index RWI from 4072 sites distributed globally and calculated indices of tree resistance and resilience to short term and prolonged droughts. Droughts were identified from SPEI time series and treated as single year drought or consecutive drought of two or more years with resilience calculated using RWI two years after the drought RWId+2. Resilience is quantified as Rs = (RWId+2 − RWId) / RWIn and the discussion explicitly interprets the effect as reflecting a slower recovery rate. The framework is presented throughout as resistance and resilience together as components of tree growth stability and the paper repeatedly evaluates effects on resistance and resilience in parallel without defining a higher level equation. The target variable used in the resilience formula is tree ring width derived growth RWI which is an individual growth metric aggregated to site level chronologies. The approach is field observation because the tree ring data are obtained from the International Tree Ring Data Bank and described as field sampling. The first author affiliation is Peking University Beijing China. The field data are from 4072 sites distributed globally and the paper maps global patterns so field country and location are global. The habitat context is forest ecosystems and global forests. The subjects are trees including gymnosperms and angiosperms which are Plantae. The disturbance is drought identified from climate index SPEI so it is unmanipulated climatic disturbance. The disturbance pattern is pulse because drought years are discrete events and resilience is measured two years after drought with a requirement of no new droughts within two years after the end of last drought. The observation window for drought identification and RWI is 1951 to 2021 which is 71 years or 25915 days.

The paper defines resilience as the speed with which a system returns to equilibrium state following a perturbation and states that the faster the perturbed system returns back to the equilibrium point the shorter its recovery time TR and the greater its resilience. It explicitly takes 1/TR as a measure of system resilience and uses return time to equilibrium TR defined as 1.0 divided by the real part of the eigenvalue with the least negative real part. The work investigates resilience for food web energy models and nutrient cycling models and verifies conclusions analytically for general ecosystem models and also reports Monte Carlo simulations of Lotka Volterra equations. The introduction defines relative stability as a measure of both the resistance of the system to perturbations and the speed with which it returns and identifies the latter property as resilience. The examples describe models being subjected to a standard perturbation and recovery towards equilibrium followed through time with the perturbation assumed to occur at time t equals 0 which is a pulse event. The analysis and simulations concern energy or biomass flux and nutrient cycling in compartment models which are ecosystem level state variables rather than individual organisms. No specific field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the study is theoretical and simulation based and uses hypothetical and abstract models and parameter sampling.

The study simulated altered rain regimes in intact soil monoliths and then quantified resistance and recovery using measured soil microbial variables at the end of the stress period T1 and after a recovery period T2. The Introduction defines overall resilience as decomposed into resistance and recovery and states recovery is the capacity to recover after the end of the stress. Methods state resistance and recovery indices were calculated for microbial biomass and activities using Orwin and Wardle 2004 and for community composition using Bray Curtis similarity based indices. The recovery index is described as impact normalized and based on the difference between altered rain regime and control and a value of plus one indicates full recovery meaning identical value between control and stressed soil which matches recovery degree. Target variables used in these indices are microbial biomass from PLFA microbial biomass C enzyme activities summed as EEA and microbial community composition from relative abundance of 27 PLFAs. The experiment used terrestrial model ecosystems extracted from field sites and exposed to controlled rain regimes which is a field experiment approach. The first author affiliation is University of Grenoble Alpes in France. Field sites are in Switzerland France and Portugal with coordinates given in Table 1. The dominant habitat is managed grasslands and grassland in rotation within agroecosystems which fits Agricultural. The disturbance is manipulated altered rain regimes including dry wet and intermittent simulating severe drought and heavy rain which are climatic. The rain regimes were maintained for 263 days and recovery was observed during 89 days under normal regime so the disturbance is treated as press. Observation duration for resilience spans from the end of acclimation when treatments began through the end of recovery which is 263 plus 89 days.

The objective states it was to determine long-term resilience of aquatic communities to prolonged drought in mediterranean-climate streams. The introduction defines resilience as the time to return to a reference state following a disturbance. The study used 19 to 20 years of annually collected fish and benthic invertebrate data and the study period encompassed a 5-year prolonged drought period and several wet years. Invertebrate recovery was evaluated using community composition trajectories and the results state no recovery from drought was evident and no return to a pre-drought community composition. Fish recovery was evaluated using abundance time series and the discussion states roach recovered relatively rapidly 1 to 2 years in the permanent sites. The disturbance was drought identified from precipitation records and described as a five-year consecutive drought. Sampling was annual from 1984 to 2003 for invertebrates and 1984 to 2002 for fish which supports an observed field observation approach. Study sites were in Napa and Lake Counties California USA at Knoxville Creek and Hunting Creek within the University of California Donald and Sylvia McLaughlin Natural Reserve. The first author affiliation listed is University of California Berkeley in the USA. The drought is treated as prolonged multi-year conditions rather than a discrete event which matches a press disturbance pattern.

The study defines resilience as the capacity to recover from disturbances by returning to previous physical and functional levels and it states that estimates of GC reflect stony coral resilience. GC is calculated as a point in time estimate from calcification rate times live colony surface area minus erosion rate times dead colony surface area and the paper states this provides a snapshot or point in time estimate. Because GC is not measured as a recovery trajectory through time and the paper states GC does not predict accretion or erosion and is calculated for a single point in time the resilience measurement is inferred rather than observed. The framework is resilience = recovery because resilience is explicitly defined as capacity to recover by returning to previous levels. Quantification is inferred because the measurement is inferred. The target variable used to quantify resilience is skeletal growth capacity in kg CaCO3 per year which is a carbonate accretion minus erosion functional metric tied to reef framework and ecosystem services. The level is ecosystem because the indicator is used for species and community values and is linked to reef ecosystem processes and services rather than individual performance alone. The approach is field observation because colonies were documented during two field surveys in the U.S. Virgin Islands and demographic survey data were used. The first author affiliation is U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in the United States. Field data are from the U.S. Virgin Islands specifically St. Croix and St. Thomas and St. John shallow coral reefs. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated as the average of the named islands. The habitat is shallow coral reefs at 1 to 12 m depth which fits Marine.Neritic. The subjects are scleractinian stony corals which are animals. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not specified for the resilience quantification because GC is a point in time indicator and disturbances are only mentioned as examples such as hurricanes or disease without being used as an analysed event window.

Framework: system stability = ecosystem structure and resilience
Justification: The study used a customised ecosystem model Ecopath with Ecosim and compared scenarios over a 15 year time span from 2017 to 2032. Resilience was treated as part of system stability because the paper evaluated ecosystem services including ecosystem structure and resilience with the parenthetical label system stability. Resilience was quantified using modelled changes in state variables rather than only static proxies because the methods state that performance was assessed by the absolute change of each indicator between 2017 and 2032. Coral cover is explicitly listed as an indicator for ecosystem structure and resilience and the model simulates coral bleaching mortality and coral recovery to 80% of pre mortality cover in 10 years. This makes the resilience quantification a recovery degree based on the change in coral cover between the start and end of the forecast period. The approach is modeling and simulation because pressures and bleaching events were simulated and scenarios were run in Ecosim. The first author affiliations are in Honolulu HI USA so the institution country is US. The study site is Puako on the west coast of Hawaii Island so the field country is US and the habitat is a fringing coral reef in shallow marine waters which fits Marine.Neritic. The disturbance types included simulated bleaching related coral mortality driven by thermal stress which is climatic and simulated fishing which is biological resource use and simulated land based sources of pollution described as nutrients and bacteria which is chemical. The disturbance pattern is press because the model projects multiple simultaneous pressures across the forecast window and includes an annual increase in fishing effort and continued land based pollution rather than a single bounded event. The observation duration is 15 years which is 5475 days.

The study quantifies resilience using early warning statistics computed from observed ecosystem state variables including chlorophyll a and phycocyanin fluorescence and dissolved oxygen saturation and pH measured daily and at 5 min intervals and also spatially with FLAMe. The methods state that early warning statistics are based on critical slowing down where the recovery rate of a system following perturbations declines as it loses resilience. The analysis uses rolling window standard deviation and lag 1 autocorrelation and applies quickest detection to generate binary alarms of a switch from a baseline high resilience state to an alarm low resilience state. Autocorrelation is treated as an early warning statistic and the text links it to recovery rate via critical slowing down so the quantification corresponds to recovery rate. The target variables used for resilience indicators are chlorophyll a and phycocyanin as proxies for algal biomass and dissolved oxygen saturation and pH as variables affected by algal metabolism which are ecosystem level functional state variables. The experiment is a whole lake fertilization experiment with nutrients added daily to Peter Lake while monitoring an adjacent reference lake so the approach is field experiment. The first author affiliation is University of Virginia in the United States. The field site is at the University of Notre Dame Environmental Research Center in the Upper Peninsula of Michigan United States. No coordinates are provided in the text. The disturbance is nutrient addition described as phosphoric acid and ammonium nitrate added daily with increasing loading rates which is a manipulated resource disturbance. The disturbance is maintained daily from day of year 161 to 237 so it is treated as a press disturbance. The observation duration for the main experiment spans 2018 and 2019 with baseline data collected in 2018 and manipulation in 2019 so the duration between first and last measurement is two years which is 729 days.

The study states that it used demographic modelling of 531 post-fire sagebrush populations across the western US and that sites had burned and were subsequently reseeded by the BLM. It also states that population height distribution data were collected once at sites 2 to 36 years after seeding and that seeding occurred after wildfire. The paper explicitly says that it quantifies resilience based on recovery times of population growth rate density and plant cover following disturbance. Therefore the quantification is recovery time and the target variables are population growth rate density and cover. The target variables are population level because they are population density and population growth rate for sagebrush populations. The approach includes field observation because size structure data were measured at field sites and includes modeling and simulation because it used inverse modelling and matrix population models and simulated annual observed population growth rate for 38 years after seeding. The first author affiliation is U.S. Geological Survey in Flagstaff Arizona so the institution country is US. The field country is the western US and Great Basin sites so the field country is US. No coordinates are provided in the provided text so location and location range are NA. The habitat is sagebrush dominated Great Basin shrubland so habitat type is Shrubland. The focal organism is big sagebrush Artemisia tridentata which is Plantae. Disturbance includes wildfire and the reseeding restoration action described as seeding after wildfire so disturbance types are unmanipulated fire and unmanipulated biological resource use. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event followed by recovery over years since seeding so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The maximum time since seeding to measurement is 36 years so the observation duration is 36 years which is 13140 days.

The study used field experiments and time series observations to evaluate resilience including a lagoon pulse manipulation described as a resilience test. The resilience test applied simulated pulse disturbances of different magnitudes to lagoon patch reefs and then assessed whether bommies returned to the predisturbance Turbinaria state. The response variable used to evaluate recovery was the abundance of Turbinaria and the paper also reports macroalgal biomass density and biomass of macroalgae at the end of experiments. The resilience outcome was classified by whether manipulated reefs fell within the 95% prediction intervals of the no disturbance relationship which represents return to the Turbinaria state. This corresponds to recovery degree because it evaluates whether the system returned to its original state rather than estimating a recovery rate parameter. The disturbance was manipulated and described as simulated storm events removing adult and juvenile Turbinaria which is a direct alteration of community structure. The disturbance was treated as a pulse because it was a discrete event and reefs were then followed for 26 months with an additional resample at 37 months. The methods give the study site as Moorea at 17.54 degrees south and 149.83 degrees west in French Polynesia. The first author affiliation is University of California Santa Barbara in the United States. The focal organism for the resilience test is the brown macroalga Turbinaria ornata which is a brown alga in class Phaeophyceae.

The introduction defines community resilience as the extent to which affected communities can achieve pre event states and as the capacity to achieve pre disturbance levels of structure and function. The methods quantify resilience by direct comparisons between pre drought and post drought levels of system properties using observed community data. Absolute resilience in total cover is defined as Rst = post drought covert divided by pre drought cover. Relative resilience in total cover is defined by weighting recovery by the damage during the drought using post drought cover and drought cover and pre drought cover. Resilience in composition is quantified from NMDS ordination distances as RRct = 1 minus Dpre−t divided by Dpre−dist which measures return toward pre drought composition. The target variables used to quantify resilience are total vegetation cover and vegetation taxonomic composition. Cover is measured from contacts with branches along transects within 18 permanent plots and composition is based on species relative abundance and Bray Curtis dissimilarity. The study system is a shrubland community in Doñana National Park in southwest Spain. The disturbance is the 2004 to 2005 hydrological year drought combined with an episode of extreme cold described as an extreme climatic event and treated as a discrete event with post event sampling. The plots are at Raposo Marqués and Ojillo with coordinates given and the average coordinate is N37.0060 W6.5200. The first author affiliation includes CREAF in Cerdanyola del Vallès Spain. Post drought sampling times are 2008 2010 2013 2017 and 2019 compared to pre drought 2005 and disturbed state 2007 and the duration from 2005 to 2019 is 14 years which is 5110 days.

Framework: resilience = elasticity + amplitude + hysteresis + malleability + damping
Justification: The study states that ordination trajectories or vectors through time were used to quantify components of resilience using 13 yr of annual records from six experimental fire treatments and a control. Resilience is defined using Westman as elasticity amplitude hysteresis and malleability with inertia as a separate property and Fox and Fox added damping and the paper directly examines elasticity amplitude malleability and damping. The methods quantify resilience from year to year vectors toward the 1969 origin and explicitly treat elasticity as the rate of recovery in ordination axis units on a year to year basis. The core data used for resilience quantification are point contacts within horizontal strata for the 30 most common species and the vectors derived from DCA ordination axes. These are community vegetation composition and structure data rather than a proxy index. The disturbance is experimentally imposed prescribed burning at different seasons and frequencies on 50 m2 plots. Fires are discrete events with recovery observed in subsequent annual censuses which matches a pulse disturbance framing. The experiment location is given as Puech du Juge 10 km north of Montpellier in southern France and the affiliated institution listed is in Montpellier France. The observation window is from 1969 before the initial burn through 1981 with annual sampling which is 12 years or 4380 days.

Framework: resilience-based management = functional response diversity + functional redundancy + functional connectivity
Justification: The study states that it assessed changes in future resilience using indicators and refers to functional response diversity redundancy and connectivity as suitable indicators for evaluating landscape level ecological resilience. It also states it used resilience proxy indicators such as functional response diversity redundancy and connectivity. Resilience was evaluated by rescaling the three indicators as a percentage using initial conditions 2010 as a reference rather than by measuring recovery after a disturbance. The functional response diversity was quantified using the abundance weighted functional dispersion index and functional connectivity was computed as the probability of connectivity index PC and functional redundancy was measured as a functional redundancy index. The approach is simulation because the authors simulated future forest landscape dynamics using LANDIS II and computed the functional network at each time step of the simulations. The first author affiliation is Universite du Quebec a Montreal in Quebec Canada. The study area is the entire Centre du Quebec in southern Quebec Canada with coordinates 45 35 to 46 34 N and 72 59 to 71 22 W and the landscape is forested and described as northern hardwood forests to mixedwood with southern boreal conifers. Because the measurement is inferred the disturbance fields are NA by instruction.

The study states that resilience was assessed using a composite resilience indicator and that fifteen indicators were developed and combined into composite resilience indices using normalisation weighting and aggregation. The data come from a structured questionnaire survey of 330 households from 15 villages in five coastal sub-districts and the results are reported as a resilience score and percentile values of composite resilience indices rather than as observed recovery trajectories of an ecosystem state variable. The paper explicitly describes resilience assessment as indicator based and indices based and notes that composite indices based measurement is incapable of providing a deeper understanding of system dynamics. The framework is explicitly defined as including three capacities absorptive adaptive and transformative and Table 2 presents these capacities as the structure of the resilience assessment. Because resilience is quantified from indicators and indices rather than direct time series recovery of a biophysical state variable the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The target of the resilience index is farmers and households and their abilities and capacities across social economic ecological physical and institutional dimensions which are socio economic in nature. The approach is field observation because the core data are collected by household survey in coastal Bangladesh. The first author affiliation is Universiti Sains Malaysia which is in Malaysia. The field data are from Bangladesh and the study concerns coastal agricultural systems. The paper provides a map and names sub districts but does not provide coordinates so location and location range are NA. Disturbances are discussed as shocks and stresses such as flash flood waterlogging salinity intrusion and cyclones but because the measurement is inferred the disturbance fields are NA per instructions.

The introduction defines resilience as the ability of the system to return to its original state after perturbation and presents temporal stability resistance and resilience as three stability dimensions. The methods identify drought years at Duolun and along the transect using precipitation percentiles and SPEI thresholds and require a post drought typical climatic year for resilience calculation. Resilience of productivity is quantified as ln(Ppost-drought/Pdrought) which uses observed productivity in the drought year and the following year. Productivity is measured as plant cover at Duolun and as MODIS EVI used as a proxy of aboveground biomass along the transect which are observational data. The disturbance is drought which is climatic and is not experimentally imposed in the control plots and is analysed as discrete drought years followed by post drought years. The Duolun site coordinate is given as 42°02′ N 116°17′ E and the study region is semi arid grasslands of Northern China and Inner Mongolia. The first author affiliation is Georgia Institute of Technology in the United States. The observation window for the transect productivity time series is 2000 to 2022.

The study used a space-for-time subrogation with a natural meadow and meadows farmed for 30 yr and then abandoned for 1 10 and 20 yr to determine whether the system could be reversed to the natural vegetation state. Aboveground vegetation was measured using species richness abundance and cover and species composition and seed bank was measured using seed density species richness and composition. The paper states that after 20 yr of natural regeneration aboveground vegetation composition and properties had recovered to the natural meadow state. This indicates resilience was quantified from observed recovery toward the natural meadow state rather than from a proxy only. The framework is not given as an explicit equation that includes resilience. The quantification aligns with recovery degree because recovery is assessed as whether composition and properties recovered to the natural meadow state. The target variables used for this recovery assessment are vegetation composition diversity abundance and cover. These variables describe a plant community so the level is community. The work is based on field sampling at Gannan on the northeastern Tibetan Plateau with coordinates 34 degrees 55 minutes N and 102 degrees 53 minutes E in Gansu Province China. The disturbance is agricultural activities described as long term arable farming and its cessation which is a land use disturbance treated as long term. The disturbance is treated as a long term driver over decades so it is press. The observation window spans from abandonment age 1 yr to 20 yr so the duration is 21 years which is 7670 days.

The study defines resilience as the ability to recover after a drying and rewetting perturbation and resistance as tolerance to low moisture levels. It states that resilience can be quantified by following the microbial response to a rewetting event. In methods it says soils were air dried and subsequently rewetted to 50% water holding capacity and responses of bacterial growth fungal growth and respiration were followed during 1 week. It states that the time that bacterial growth and fungal growth needed to recover to the 50% of the moist control levels were calculated as an index of resilience. This is a direct measurement of recovery dynamics after an imposed perturbation so the measurement type is observed. The quantification is recovery time because resilience is indexed by time needed to recover to 50% of control levels. The target variables used for resilience quantification are bacterial growth and fungal growth which are measured as proxies for growth. These growth measures represent microbial groups rather than individuals so the level is community. The approach includes field sampling across 17 sites in central Texas and laboratory tracking of responses so it is field observation. The first author affiliation is Lund University in Sweden. The rainfall gradient is in central Texas USA across the Edwards Plateau spanning about 400 km so the field country is the United States and the location is approximated to the Edwards Plateau in Texas with a multi site range.

The paper develops a theoretical consumer resource framework using the Rosenzweig MacArthur equations and defines BCR as K divided by R* which is a ratio of resource biomass without the consumer to resource biomass with the consumer. It explicitly states that the framework is used to determine food web stability including equilibrium resilience and rates of recovery from perturbations. It quantifies resilience conceptually via the Jacobian dominant eigenvalue described as the rate that the system returns to equilibrium following a perturbation and plotted as minus one times the dominant eigenvalue. This is a direct recovery rate measure derived from a model response around equilibrium rather than a proxy. The target variables used in the resilience quantification are consumer and resource biomass dynamics and equilibrium biomass terms K and R*. The organization level implied by consumer resource biomass across trophic levels is community. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study uses theoretical equations and numerical solutions and eigenvalue analysis rather than field data. The first author affiliation is University of Toronto in Canada. No empirical field site or coordinates are provided because the work is a general theory paper. The disturbance is a modelled perturbation around equilibrium described as small perturbations and perturbations to the system and the recovery is evaluated after the perturbation which is treated as a pulse. Observation duration is not reported because no empirical time window is given.

The study proposes transcriptomic resilience as the return to control levels of gene expression and states that it is analogous to ecological resilience which describes the return of species abundance and performance to predisturbance conditions. The Methods describe a simulated heat wave where water temperature was increased to 26 C maintained for 3 wk and then decreased back to 19 C with samples taken in the middle of the heat wave and 1 d after return to control values. The Discussion states that gene expression 1 d after termination of the heat wave differed strongly between populations and that the southern population rapidly returned to control expression levels while the northern population failed to recover. Resilience is therefore quantified from observed gene expression trajectories relative to control values after the disturbance. The target variable used for this quantification is gene expression patterns and transcription profiles measured by 454 cDNA sequencing and validated by quantitative real time PCR. The experimental plants were collected from Denmark and Italy with coordinates given for Doverodde Denmark and Gabicce Mare Italy. The disturbance is a manipulated realistic heat wave mimicking the 2003 event and is treated as a discrete event with a termination followed by recovery sampling. The observation window spans from the start of temperature increase to the recovery sampling which is 5 d ramp up plus 21 d at 26 C plus 5 d ramp down plus 1 d recovery.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the magnitude of a perturbation a community can withstand and remain in a given state. The methods state that the study uses a dynamic model with alternative stable states and compares resilience under conventional management versus reserve management. The methods define deterministic resilience as the fraction of initial predator and competitor abundances that reach the predator dominated equilibrium which is the size of the basin of attraction. The methods define stochastic resilience as the probability of being in the desired state at the end of a finite time horizon under climate variability that causes stochastic recruitment. These resilience metrics quantify how much perturbation in initial conditions the system can withstand without shifting to the competitor dominated alternative state which matches latitude. The model tracks population sizes in numbers for adult predators juvenile predators and competitors so the target variable is abundance. Because the model includes multiple interacting species and resilience is defined for the predator dominated community state the level is community. The approach is modeling and simulation because resilience is calculated from deterministic computations and stochastic simulations over annual time steps. The first author affiliation is University of California Davis in the United States. The system is based on rocky reefs in the eastern North Pacific and parameterised with data for yelloweye rockfish and pygmy rockfish and observations off the US West Coast so the field country is the United States. No explicit sampling coordinates are provided so the location is set to the coordinates of the first author institution. Fishing mortality is imposed in the model with reserves and effort displacement so the disturbance is model biological resource use and it is treated as a continuous mortality rate so the pattern is press. The stochastic simulations use a time horizon of 5000 annual time steps so the observation duration is 5000 years converted to days.

Target variable type: Diversity; Abiotic parameter; Socio-eco parameter; Ecosystem function
Justification: The paper constructs an assessment indicator system for national-scale ecological resilience and evaluates 1434 ecological function areas in China using a multi-criteria model. It states it focuses on general resilience and does not target a specific disturbance but evaluates the overall state of ecosystem resilience. Resilience is quantified as a resilience index derived from indicators rather than measured from recovery dynamics because indicators are biodiversity natural condition index infrastructure GDP per area and ecosystem service value. The introduction defines ecological resilience as ability to recover after being disturbed plus total amount of disturbance the system can absorb plus ability to self-organize and also states resilience is related to ability to resist disturbance and reorganize. The methods show indicators are standardized and weighted by an entropy method and combined by a weighted sum method to measure general resilience level. The target variables used in the resilience quantification are vascular plant biodiversity topographic rainfall and soil organic carbon for natural condition index GDP per area traffic accessibility and ecosystem service value. The approach is field observation because it uses GIS raster datasets and survey derived statistics for China for 2015 rather than experiments or simulations. The first author affiliation is China University of Mining and Technology in Xuzhou China. The field data are national scale China ecological function areas. The study spans multiple land types across China and discusses wetlands coral reefs and forests as examples of spatial resilience so the dominant habitat type cannot be single and is coded as Multi. Taxon is Plantae because biodiversity indicator is Chinese vascular plant distribution data. Disturbance is not specified for quantification because the study explicitly does not target a specific disturbance and uses general resilience indicators without imposed or observed disturbance response.

The study states that vegetation changes were studied for 21 yr in two clearcut logged and slash-burned Pseudotsuga forests. It quantifies resistance and resilience using Euclidean distances between pre- and postdisturbance samples in ordination space. Resistance is defined as inversely proportional to the maximum Euclidean distance between pre- and postdisturbance samples. Resilience is defined as inversely proportional to the Euclidean distance between predisturbance and final samples which is a degree of recovery toward initial composition. The target variable used in these distances is species composition represented by ordination positions derived from species cover data. The data come from permanently staked 2 x 2 m plots sampled repeatedly from 1962 through the final sampling year shown as 1983 which yields 21 years. The approach is field observation because plots were established in the H. J. Andrews Experimental Forest and resampled through time. The first author affiliation is Oregon State University in the United States. The field sites are Watersheds 1 and 3 of the H. J. Andrews Experimental Forest 80 km east of Eugene Oregon in the western Cascade Range. The disturbance includes clearcut logging and slash burning with soil disturbance classes and these are treated as discrete events followed by recovery sampling.

The introduction defines resilience as encompassing recovery or recovery status and recovery rate and resistance. The methods state that subplots were experimentally disturbed in April 2016 by clipping and removing all above-ground vegetation to the soil surface and that all plots were then monitored over time for 12 months until April 2017. The methods state that n-dimensional hypervolumes of plant community biomass and structure were simulated using field data collected from disturbance-recovery experiments. The methods quantify recovery as centroid distance between undisturbed and disturbed marsh hypervolumes one year after disturbance and quantify resistance as centroid distance between disturbed marsh hypervolumes and the alternative state mudflat hypervolume. The results report lower post-disturbance total recovery and recovery rates and resistance with greater pre-disturbance abiotic stress and report a threshold where plots flooded more than about 52 percent of the time transitioned to an alternative unvegetated state after disturbance. The target variables used to build the hypervolumes are plant community biomass and structure and the recovery metric is based on distances in that multivariate biomass and structure space. The study sites are Spartina alterniflora dominated salt marsh in southeastern Louisiana USA with plot coordinates provided. The first author affiliation is U.S. Geological Survey Western Ecological Research Center Davis CA USA.

The study states that it estimates vegetation resilience by calculating the residence and recovery time of various biomes over the last 3.5 Myr. This uses the system trajectory derived from a continuous pollen record from lake sediment cores and therefore uses observational data rather than proxies without dynamics. Resilience is operationalized with recovery time where shorter recovery time indicates higher resilience and longer recovery time indicates lower resilience. The biome state used for residence and recovery time is the reconstructed megabiome category forest meadow steppe shrubland and desert steppe derived from pollen assemblages via biomization. This target variable is therefore vegetation composition expressed as biome identity through time. Because the biome reconstruction is based on multiple plant taxa pollen assemblages the level is community. The approach is field observation because the core was retrieved from the Zoige Basin and pollen was measured from sediment samples. The drilling site coordinates are given as 33 degrees 58 minutes 03 seconds north and 102 degrees 20 minutes 09 seconds east which converts to N33.9675 E102.3358. The first author affiliation is the Institute of Geographic Sciences and Natural Resources Research Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing which is in China. The field data come from the Zoige Basin on the eastern Tibetan Plateau in China. The discussion links resilience shifts to temperature change and global warming thresholds and treats climate as the driver without experimental manipulation so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic and it is treated as long term forcing rather than discrete events. The record covers the last 3.5 Myr and the sampling resolution is multicentennial so the observation duration is 3.5 million years which is 1277500 days. The modern and reconstructed dominant system is alpine meadow and meadow dominated ecosystem on the Tibetan Plateau so the major habitat type is grassland.

Approach: Modeling and simulation; Field experiment; Field observation
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the capacity of an ecosystem to reorganize and recover processes. The methods state that the study used a modelling framework and simulated community change over the following 500 years after introducing an invader at low density. The model output tracked the number of individuals for Avena and the number of seedlings and adults for Stipa at peak growing season in spring which are population sizes. The analysis evaluated whether communities represent separate states and whether dynamics exhibit hysteresis under three nitrogen scenarios and it states that the dominant community state was determined by initial starting conditions under moderate nitrogen. This use of basins of attraction and alternative states corresponds to latitude because it concerns whether the system shifts to an alternative stable state under the same environmental scenario. Nitrogen was experimentally manipulated with soil amendments including table sugar to decrease soil nitrogen and calcium nitrate to increase nitrogen and the moderate treatment was ambient. Nitrogen deposition is treated as an input level maintained as a scenario rather than a discrete event which matches a press disturbance framing. The empirical nitrogen manipulations were applied from 2009 to 2011 which spans about three years. The study system is California grasslands and the focal species are grasses Stipa pulchra and Avena spp which are plants. The first author affiliations listed are University of Montana and University of California Riverside which are in the United States. Field sites are in Irvine California Browns Valley California and Livermore California which are in the United States. No coordinates are provided in the text for these sites.

The study compared annual benthic macroinvertebrate surveys from August 2009 to August 2011 before the floods with surveys in September 2011 November 2011 March 2012 and August 2012 after the floods which uses the system’s own observed data through time. The objective states they document community response as resistance to impact and ascertain the timing of recovery as resilience which presents resilience alongside resistance. Resilience is quantified as the timing of recovery because the paper reports that most community metrics rebounded in 3 to 7 months and that full recovery was evident in 12 months and it cautions analysis within the 12 months following a flood event. The variables used to evaluate recovery include bioassessment metrics such as species richness EPT richness HBI PMA and BAP and community composition analyses using Bray Curtis distances ANOSIM and nMDS and a density surrogate percent of sample sorted. The work is based on field sampling at five sites in the Upper Esopus Creek watershed in the Catskill Mountains of New York USA. The disturbance is the extreme flooding from Tropical Storms Irene and Lee described with peak discharges and rainfall and treated as discrete events in late August and early September 2011 followed by post flood sampling. The first author affiliation is New York State Department of Environmental Conservation in Troy New York USA. The watershed is described as over 95 percent forested land which supports the Forest habitat type. The subjects are benthic macroinvertebrates which are animals. The observation window spans from August 2009 to August 2012 which is three years.

The study uses a probabilistic cellular automaton and reports simulated time series and steady states of forest area fraction [F] versus fire ignition rate. It states that it uses the cellular automaton to observe emergent structure and bistability in simulations and then derives a balance equation and an ODE for d[F]/dt. Resilience is operationalized as sensitivity to perturbations defined as lambdaF based on the change in P[F] which is the right hand side of Eq. 9 and it is interpreted as local stability and local resilience. This sensitivity is not recovery time or recovery rate after a pulse disturbance but a stability feedback metric based on perturbations so quantification fits Other. The target variable used in the resilience indicator is forest area fraction [F] which is a cover fraction. The analysis concerns a landscape on a lattice and uses perimeter quantities and forest area so the level is Landscape. The approach is modeling and simulation using a Gillespie algorithm for the cellular automaton. The first author affiliation is University of Exeter United Kingdom. No empirical field dataset country or coordinates are provided because the work is simulation based. The disturbance is fire represented by ignition and spread rates in the model so it is model fire. Fire ignition occurs repeatedly at a rate and is treated as a continuous driver parameterized by ignition rate rather than a single bounded event so the disturbance pattern is Press. Example simulations run for centuries and one figure states canopy closure reached after 300 y so observation duration is 300 years converted to 109500 days.

Framework: resilience = speed of return to the initial state
Justification: The study states that it studied resilience of forest floor vegetation to experimental disturbance with five treatments and controls replicated 10 times. Resilience is defined as the speed of return to the initial state and is quantified by the rate of change in species composition.
