The methods estimate successional rate as Bray Curtis compositional dissimilarity between successive years and also use DCA ordination displacement vectors as successional rates in ordination space. These are based on repeated vegetation recordings every year from 1993 to 2000 which provides direct observed recovery dynamics after the disturbance performed 23 to 25 August 1993. The target variable used for resilience quantification is species composition because Bray Curtis dissimilarity and DCA are applied to the full species by observations matrix. The level is community because the composition metrics are calculated across multiple species in the forest floor vegetation. The approach is field experiment because treatments were performed manually in plots in a nature reserve and vegetation was monitored annually. The first author affiliation is University of Oslo in Norway. The field site is in éstmarka Nature Reserve southeastern Norway with coordinates 11 8029E and 59 8509N. The habitat is Picea abies forest. The disturbance is manipulated removal of vegetation and soil layers which is a structural manipulation of the system. The disturbance is a pulse because treatment was applied on specific dates and recovery was then observed for seven years. Observation duration is from first measurement window 20 June 1993 to last measurement window 2 July 2000 which is 2555 days.

The study quantified resilience using tree ring width time series from a common garden and calculated resistance recovery and resilience indices from growth before during and after drought years. The Introduction defines growth resilience as achieving pre disturbance growth levels and states it includes two components growth resistance and growth recovery. The Methods quantify resistance as growth during drought compared to pre drought recovery as growth after drought compared to during drought and resilience as growth after drought compared to before drought. Drought years were identified from SPEI and treated as discrete drought years with recovery assessed in the following two years. The target variable used in the resilience indices is stem growth measured as tree ring width at 1.3 m height. The work is based on sampled trees in the Schovenhorst Estate Putten the Netherlands at 52.25 N 5.63 E. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University and Research Wageningen the Netherlands. The disturbance is drought derived from climate data and not experimentally imposed. The observation window for the main tree ring series is 1974 to 2017 which is 44 years.

Framework: resilience = magnitude of disturbance that can be absorbed before the system redefines its structure
Justification: The study reconstructs long term ecosystem dynamics using fossil pollen and macrofossil charcoal with geochemistry and diatoms from sedimentary sequences and it compares vegetation change and recovery through time at two sites. Resilience is explicitly defined as the magnitude of disturbance that can be absorbed before the system redefines its structure. The key resilience evidence is a threshold ecosystem switch from forest types to Erica–Myrica heath when marine surges and drought occurred together and the contrast that the littoral forest maintained composition during the 1400 cal yr bp surge while the open Uapaca woodland shifted. The target variable used to evaluate resilience is vegetation composition from pollen percentages and associated community shifts. The disturbances are unmanipulated marine surges linked to sea level rise inferred from Sr Ca and diatom salinity changes and unmanipulated drought aridity inferred from the diatom water depth index. Sea water influx is described as occurring in pulses and the vegetation switch is described as occurring within less than 100 years supporting a pulse framing. The first author affiliation is Oxford University in the United Kingdom. The field sites are in Mandena on the south east coast of Madagascar with coordinates given for both basins and the average is reported. The observation window spans from 6455 cal yr bp to the present in the Erica matrix sequence which is about 6455 years converted to days.

The study states that it performed a 5-year rainfall reduction treatment under field conditions and assessed the taxonomic functional and phylogenetic diversity of annual plant communities each year for 5 years. Resilience is evidenced by the statement that after a rainy season with above average rainfall all of the diversity values recovered completely even under the rainfall reduction treatment and in the discussion that decreased diversity recovered rapidly in rainy years. This uses observed community data from repeated annual measurements of diversity indices rather than proxy scoring. No explicit equation framework defining resilience is provided and resilience is discussed as the ability to recover diversity after drought. The quantification corresponds to recovery degree because the text says diversity values recovered completely. The target variables used to quantify resilience are diversity indices including richness inverse Simpson evenness multi-trait Rao MPD and MNTD. The study is at the community level because these indices are calculated for assemblages in subplots. The approach is a field experiment using rain shelters and paired control plots. The first author affiliation is Universidad Rey Juan Carlos Madrid Spain. The study area is given as El Espartal experimental station central Spain with coordinates 40°11′11.5′′N 3°37′47.0′′W. The habitat is described as a gypsum steppe dominated by gypsum specialist shrubs with annual plants seasonally among shrubs which best matches shrubland. The disturbance is manipulated drought via rainfall reduction which is a climatic disturbance and it is maintained across the 5-year experiment so it is press. Annual sampling occurred each year across 5 years so the observation duration is 5 years.

The study defines resilience as the ability of a population to recover following disturbance and resistance as the ability to withstand disturbance. It quantifies recovery dynamics using modeled population size Nt as a function of time since a flood event t and flood magnitude Q including statements such as population recovered post-flood and recovery was evident between 150 and 200 days. It explicitly interprets r as a measure of resilience and uses population size 50 days after a flood across r and h parameter space. The target variable used in the core analyses and figures is population size or relative population size Nt. The approach includes modeling and simulation because the authors derived and parameterised a disturbance logistic model and forecasted population abundance for 50 years in the fire example. The approach also includes field observation because they compared model predictions to observed population sizes after prescribed flood events on the Bill Williams River and used observed relative population sizes and sampling times. The first author affiliation is Oregon State University in the United States. Field data for the empirical test are from Arizona USA and the fire analysis is parameterised from tropical South America literature without a specific country stated. Disturbances include floods and fire in the model and floods in the empirical comparison and these are treated as pulse events with defined post-event recovery intervals. The results and methods describe post-disturbance evaluation windows up to 200 days following sequential flood events which provides the observation duration.

The study defines variability from stationary fluctuations in species abundance time series with Var(Ni(t)) and constructs invariability as I = 1/(2V). It models stochastic perturbations in equation 1 as white noise forcing and analyses the resulting community responses which is a direct quantification of system response in a model. Resilience is discussed but no explicit equation framework including resilience is defined and the paper instead focuses on stability quantified by variability and invariability while comparing to asymptotic resilience. The target variable used in the variability and invariability definitions is species abundance Ni(t). The analysis is at the community level because community wide variability is defined as the average variance across species. The approach is modeling and simulation using Lotka Volterra community assembly and analytical formulas. The first author affiliation includes CNRS and Paul Sabatier University in France. The paper states this work does not use data so field country and coordinates are not applicable. The perturbations are imposed in the model as stochastic forcing and species specific perturbations so the disturbance is model structural. The perturbations are treated as ongoing stochastic forcing producing stationary fluctuations so the disturbance pattern is press. Observation duration is not reported because the analysis is based on stationary distributions and no start and end measurement window is given.

The study reconstructs floodplain dynamics from 74 cloud-free Landsat scenes acquired between 2000 and 2024 and uses daily water levels from the Xingzi Hydrological Station. Vegetation is quantified as Landsat-derived vegetated area and proportional vegetation cover in land-cover maps and figures. The methods define flood and drought timing descriptors as duration and interval of high-water and low-water spells and combine them into composite indices that capture persistence and recovery. The results quantify suppression and recovery using vegetation-area difference Δ across duration and interval thresholds and state that recovery is promoted by dry intervals exceeding about 100 days. Because resilience is evaluated from observed vegetation cover responses to observed hydrological extremes and sequences it is observed rather than inferred. The paper discusses resilience alongside timing and recovery concepts but does not define an explicit equation framework that includes resilience. The resilience-related quantification is expressed as whether vegetation cover remains lower or rebounds relative to other conditions using Δ and described as suppressed or promoted recovery which corresponds to recovery degree. The target variable used for this quantification is vegetation cover or vegetated area derived from remote sensing land-cover classification. The study area is Poyang Lake with coordinates 28 22 to 29 45 N and 115 47 to 116 45 E and the average coordinate is N29.0583 E116.2667. The habitat is described as floodplain wetlands and the study focuses on floodplain vegetation. The disturbance is hydrological because it is based on water-level fluctuations and flood and drought events derived from gauge records. The disturbance is treated as press because the analysis uses continuous daily water-level records and repeated flood and drought alternations across the full 2000 to 2024 period. The observation duration spans 1 January 2000 to 31 December 2024 which is 9131 days.

The study assessed biomass production stability using 14 years of NDVI temperature and drought index time series and modelled vegetation response to temperature and drought anomalies. It defined stability via resistance and resilience where resistance refers to the ability to withstand a perturbation and resilience expresses the speed at which the system returns to its average state. Resilience was derived from the ARx model lagged NDVI anomaly coefficient which is associated with the speed of return to average biomass production and could thus be interpreted as a resilience term. NDVI was used as a biophysical indicator of greenness or biomass and NDVI anomalies denote higher or lower vegetation biomass or greenness than average so the target variable is an ecosystem function proxy. The disturbance was periods of drought and temperature anomalies between 2000 and 2013 which are climatic anomalies not experimentally imposed. The data were MODIS NDVI and KNMI climate grids for the Netherlands and the study focuses on the Netherlands. The first author affiliation is KU Leuven in Belgium. The NDVI period is 2000 to 2013 and monthly time series were used so the observation window spans 14 years which is 5110 days. The disturbance is treated as climate anomalies across the whole time series rather than a single defined event with a recovery interval so it is press. No explicit coordinates were provided in the text beyond national and regional descriptions so location information is not available.

Framework: resilience = threshold infiltration of peatlands; restoration resistance; critical flux
Justification: The study states that it presents a dynamic simulation model constrained and validated by a wide set of observations to quantify resilience of peatlands and forests. It defines peatland resilience as capacity to absorb climate natural and artificial disturbances while maintaining functioning as carbon store. It then says it selected three key resilience aspects and lists threshold infiltration of peatlands TIP restoration resistance TEF minus TIP and critical flux based on critical slowing down quantified by 3 y temporal autocorrelation AR3 of peatland biomass. The tipping points are described as threshold groundwater flux of the tipping points from peatland to forest and from forest to peatland and the study links raised bog disappearance to a dryness index boundary and climate change. Because resilience is quantified through tipping point thresholds in groundwater flux and bistable range it matches a threshold to state shift which corresponds to latitude. The variable used for critical slowing down and bistability diagrams is peatland biomass and aboveground biomass trajectories after 800 to 1000 y. The model is run for 1000 y with daily water fluxes and yearly carbon fluxes and is forced by resampled climate data and a groundwater flux gradient so the approach is modeling and simulation. The first author affiliation is Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam in the Netherlands. The analysis is for all of Europe south of the tree line but it also uses a global soil carbon inventory and validates with Canadian datasets so the data sources are global and no single field country or coordinates are specified for resilience quantification. The focal systems are peatlands and forests and the peatland habitat is wetland. The resilience indicators are based on vegetation biomass of forests and peatlands so the taxon is plants. Disturbances considered in the quantification are changes in groundwater flux and climate forcing so they are model hydrological and model climatic and they are treated as ongoing conditions rather than a single event. The simulation duration is 1000 years which is 365000 days.

The study analysed a large dataset of phytoplankton abundance time series with samples collected every 2 weeks over 20 years at 10 sites along the French coastline which is direct observational time series data. Resilience is quantified from the interaction matrix estimated by MAR(1) models using the maximum modulus of the eigenvalues which the Methods state measures resilience and the Discussion states quantifies the return time to a point equilibrium. The target variable used for this resilience quantification is the interaction matrix and its eigenvalues which are interaction network properties rather than abundance itself. The level is community because the interaction matrix describes effects between multiple phytoplankton genera within communities. The approach includes field observation because the data come from a long term coastal monitoring programme and includes modeling and simulation because MAR(1) models are fitted to estimate interaction matrices and stability metrics. The first author affiliation is University of Bordeaux in France. The field data are from sites along the French coastline. The habitat is coastal marine waters along the French coastline which corresponds best to the shallow nearshore Marine.Neritic category. The taxon is phytoplankton because phytoplankton abundance time series and phytoplankton genera are the focal organisms. No disturbance is explicitly imposed or analysed as a defined event for resilience quantification because resilience is derived from eigenvalues of the fitted interaction matrices rather than response to a specified disturbance. The observation duration is 20 years which equals 7300 days using 365 days per year.

The study defined resilience as the capacity of the system to persist after a disturbance such that variables and processes controlling ecosystem functions remain intact. It also stated that resistance is an attribute of ecosystem resilience and is measured by the degree of change in ecosystem variables before and after a disturbance. The Methods quantified change before and after the mountain pine beetle outbreak using 2005 before outbreak and 2012 after outbreak measurements of basal area density quadratic mean diameter and species dominance. Table 1 specified resilience measurement criteria as whether treatments remain dominated by larger ponderosa pine after the outbreak and trajectories in multivariate analysis. The disturbance was a naturally occurring mountain pine beetle outbreak approximately 5 yr after treatment. The resilience related target variable is species dominance and associated structure and composition shifts between ponderosa pine and Douglas fir which is community composition. The work used replicated thinning and burning treatments established in 2000 to 2002 which is a field experiment and it observed the natural outbreak response which is field observation. The site was on Lubrecht Experimental Forest in western Montana USA with coordinates 46 degrees 53 minutes N and 113 degrees 26 minutes W. The first author affiliation was USDA Forest Service Rocky Mountain Research Station in Missoula Montana USA. The outbreak was treated as a discrete event between 2005 and 2012 with before and after comparisons. The observation window for outbreak response spanned from 2005 measurements to 2012 measurements which is seven years or 2555 days.

The study used over five decades of fish monitoring data from the San Francisco Estuary to evaluate resistance and resilience to prolonged drought events. Resilience was defined as the increase in species occurrence from a drought to a subsequent wet period and resistance was defined as the lack of decline in species occurrence from a wet to a subsequent drought period. Occurrence was modeled from presence and absence data from two long term field monitoring programs the Fall Midwater Trawl and the Delta Juvenile Fish Monitoring Program beach seine survey. The resilience coefficient quantified return of occurrence to wet period levels following a drought period which is a recovery degree definition. The target variable used in the resilience model was species occurrence rate which is a population level abundance related measure. Drought periods were identified from hydrologic classification and treated as multi year periods which matches a press disturbance framing. The disturbance was climatic because it was drought and it was unmanipulated because it was based on observed drought cycles. The first author affiliation is United States Bureau of Reclamation in Sacramento California. The field data were collected in the San Francisco Estuary California USA and the map shows sampling locations across the estuary and delta. The time series spans 1967 to 2017 which is 51 years or 18615 days.

Framework: resilience = recovery rate + return to equivalent ecosystem state
Justification: The study used the spatially explicit individual-based forest landscape model iLand and analyzed 500-year recovery trajectories after a large high-severity wildfire. Resilience was defined as disturbance resilience described as rate of recovery and return to equivalent ecosystem state. Recovery trajectories and recovery rates were evaluated for total ecosystem carbon stocks rumple index and presence of late-seral species. The disturbance was a simulated large high-severity wildfire in year 1500 with additional scenarios of subsequent fire frequency. The approach is modeling and simulation because all resilience results come from iLand simulations with 25 replicated runs. The institution country is Austria because the first author is affiliated with University of Natural Resources and Life Sciences Vienna Austria. The field data context is the H J Andrews Experimental Forest in Oregon USA with coordinates 44.28 N 122.28 W. Habitat is forest because the study landscape is a forest watershed dominated by Douglas-fir and other conifers. Taxon is Plantae because indicators include tree species and late-seral tree species presence. Disturbance pattern is pulse because the analysis starts from a discrete landscape-level high-severity fire event around year 1500 followed by recovery trajectories. Observation duration is 500 years which is 182500 days.

The abstract defines stability as resilience by stating community structure and stability are i.e. resilience to disturbances. The study does not quantify recovery dynamics from observational time series and instead uses network analyses to provide measures of network specificity and stability and modularity and parasite competition and vulnerability to extinction. The Methods state community stability was estimated using nestedness and modularity and nestedness was used to estimate community stability. Extinction slope is calculated from repeated removals of host species from the network which is a simulated procedure rather than an observed disturbance response. Communities are defined as 5 by 5 degree global grid cells and the Location is stated as Global. The first author affiliation is Department of Zoology University of Otago Dunedin New Zealand. Major taxa studied are avian malaria and malaria like parasites and the host species are birds so the kingdom is Animalia. Because resilience is treated via network stability metrics rather than direct recovery after disturbance the measurement is inferred and quantification is inferred. Habitat within 5 by 5 degree cells is acknowledged to encompass diverse habitats so the dominant habitat type cannot be single and is coded as Multi.

The study states that it calculated a soil resilience index and that it used soil organic carbon stock and arylsulfatase activity to build indices including SRI CMI and BAI. The soil resilience index is defined as a ratio using SOC stock values with CC-TBC as the lower reference and FR-3 as the higher reference level which is an index based on land use categories rather than a direct time series of recovery after a disturbance. The land use systems include tobacco cultivation pastureland eucalyptus reforestation and natural ecological successions with 10 20 and 35 years of regeneration and the analysis compares these systems using SOC pools and enzyme activity rather than tracking the same site through time. The methods describe soil sampling in triplicate across land use systems and depths and laboratory measurements of SOC pools and arylsulfatase and then index calculations. Resilience is not defined as part of an explicit equation framework such as stability equals components and the paper presents indices rather than a conceptual component framework including resilience. The target variables used in the resilience index are SOC stock and related SOC pools and arylsulfatase activity which are soil carbon pools and soil biological activity. The study location is given as Faxinal Taquari dos Ribeiros in the State of Parana southern Brazil with altitude approximately 856 m and Fig 1 shows the study area. The first author affiliation is State University of Ponta Grossa Brazil. The work is based on field collected soil samples so the approach is field observation. No explicit disturbance event is imposed or observed for recovery dynamics and the disturbance is described as land management history so disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery are not directly quantified.

The study uses time series data on coral percentage cover and macroalgae percentage cover and fish community biomass from 2006 to 2017 across 57 sites around Mo’orea. It explicitly tests resilience using postdisturbance dynamics by calculating coral return rate as the average annual change in coral cover between 2010 and 2017 following the COTS outbreak and Cyclone Oli. The Methods state that return rate was calculated as the average annual change in coral cover between 2010 and 2017 and the Results describe this as how sites recovered from disturbances. This is a direct quantification of recovery rate in the system’s state variable rather than a proxy. The Introduction defines stability as the tendency to maintain in a given state and then equates increasing resilience with increasing the ability to absorb disturbances while maintaining structure and function. The field site is given as Mo’orea with coordinates 17.541 S and 149.833 W and the disturbances are described as a crown-of-thorns starfish outbreak from 2006 to 2009 and Cyclone Oli in winter 2010. These are discrete events within the study period and are treated as disturbances followed by recovery which matches a pulse pattern. The first author affiliation is University of Michigan in the United States. The habitat is nearshore coral reefs around Mo’orea which corresponds to shallow marine neritic environments. The resilience target variable is coral cover which is a cover metric at the ecosystem scale. The focal coral is described as reef-building corals Scleractinia which are animals but the resilience quantification is based on coral cover as benthic cover and the paper also analyses macroalgae cover and fish biomass.

The study states that it will assess resilience by “assessment of the relative resilience of natural communities using theoretically important measures of functional potential i.e. functional diversity and functional redundancy that are based on field data” and that it will “infer the relative vulnerability of different subregions of the Great Barrier Reef to phase shifts based on functional characteristics of the local herbivorous fish communities” which indicates inferred rather than directly measured resilience. The Methods define the functional measures used as “Functional diversity the number of functional groups” and “Functional redundancy the average probability of occurrence for species in each functional group” and also use “Abundance the total number of individuals in each functional group” as measures of herbivore function used to assess vulnerability. The Introduction links resilience to these measures by stating resilience is likely highest when “both functional diversity and functional redundancy are high” and the Abstract says “we explore the idea that resilience is positively related to the diversity within and among functional groups of organisms.” The approach is field observation because reefs were “surveyed annually” using “underwater visual census” and benthic cover from “video or still cameras” and water clarity estimates. The first author affiliation is “Australian Institute of Marine Science Townsville Queensland 4810 Australia” which is AU. The field system is the “Great Barrier Reef Australia” with study reefs “between 14 S and 24 S” and on the continental shelf so Marine.Neritic. A representative average coordinate is taken from the stated latitudinal span and the Great Barrier Reef longitude near Townsville. Disturbance and recovery dynamics are discussed for phase shifts but resilience itself is not quantified as recovery rate time or degree of a state variable and is instead inferred from herbivore functional characteristics so disturbance fields are NA and observation duration is NA under the instructions for inferred measurement.

The paper defines that soil stability includes both resistance and resilience and states that resistance is the ability to withstand a perturbation or stress and resilience is the ability to recover to pre perturbation levels. The methods state that functional resistance was evaluated after 4 days as percentage of enzyme activity change relative to pre perturbation condition and functional resilience was assessed by evaluating the shift in enzyme activities from the pre perturbation condition along 360 days after PAH perturbation to detect when each activity is able to return to values comparable to the pre perturbation. The resilience calculation is given as Resilience = D x / C0 x 100 where C0 is the variable measured in the unperturbed soil at time 0 and D is the variable measured in perturbed soil at different times after perturbation. The target variables used for resistance and resilience are the three enzyme activities total hydrolase laccase and peroxidase which are soil functional indicators. The study is a mesocosm incubation under controlled conditions in the laboratory with soils spiked with PAHs. The first author affiliation is University of Sannio in Italy. Soil sampling locations are on Matese Mountains southern Italy with coordinates given for holm oak black pine and beech sites and the average of these coordinates is reported. The disturbance is PAH spiking with phenanthrene pyrene or benzo[a]pyrene which is a chemical contamination perturbation applied by the researchers. The disturbance is treated as a spiking event with measurements before time 0 and after 4 52 108 213 and 360 days which matches a pulse disturbance. The observation window for resilience spans from time 0 to 360 days.

The methods state that a dendrochronological analysis measured yearly radial tree growth and that they estimated beech growth resistance recovery and resilience to two drought episodes 1991 and 2006. Resilience is defined as the ratio of the mean growth during the 3 years following the drought event to the mean growth during the previous 3 years which uses observed growth data before and after drought. The study explicitly uses the three metrics proposed by Lloret et al. 2011 resistance recovery and resilience and presents them together as resilience components. The target variable used to quantify resilience is tree growth because the resilience metric is a ratio of growth values across time windows. The focal measurements are on dominant trees and focal trees indicating an individual level response. The study area is Catalonia NE Spain and the sampling design selected forest patches there so the field country is Spain. The first author affiliation lists CREAF Bellaterra Spain so the institution country is Spain. The drought events are classified as drought events based on climatic variables and are not experimentally imposed so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic. The drought events are treated as discrete years 1991 and 2006 with pre and post windows so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The resilience calculation uses 3 years before and 3 years after each drought year so the observation window spans 7 years which is 2555 days.

The introduction states the study asks whether restoration leads to plant pollinator communities with increased robustness to future species losses and later says they modeled secondary coextinction cascades and compared predicted extinction cascade sizes when plants were removed. The methods state they constructed plant pollinator networks for six prairie fragments and followed them for 4 years and then modeled secondary coextinction cascades using stochastic coextinction models. The resilience related outcome is network robustness operationalized as secondary extinction cascade size after imposed plant species loss scenarios and cascade size was divided by network size. Because resilience is quantified from simulated responses to imposed removals it is observed rather than inferred. The quantification corresponds to resistance because it measures how much secondary extinction occurs following a disturbance rather than recovery rate time or degree. The target variable used to quantify resilience is the interaction network response specifically secondary extinction cascades in plant pollinator networks. The level is community because networks include multiple plant species and multiple insect taxa. The approach includes field observation because they quantified floral resources and pollinator visitation and built networks from observations and includes modeling and simulation because they simulated species loss scenarios and extinction cascades. The first author affiliation is Quamash EcoResearch Olympia Washington USA and the University of Arizona is also USA so the institution country is US. The field data are from southern Puget Sound Region Washington State USA so the field country is US. The habitat is prairie fragments described as Pacific Northwest prairies and grassland restoration so Grassland is the major habitat type. The taxa whose resilience was quantified are plants and insect pollinators so Plantae and Animalia. The disturbance is model imposed loss removal of plant species in simulations so Model_Structural and it is treated as discrete removals so Pulse. The observation window for networks is 2017 to 2020 which is 4 years or 1460 days.

The study defines ecological resilience as the ability of an ecosystem to return to the same state following a perturbation. Resilience is evaluated with a structured dynamical model of a temperate rocky reef and the authors vary fishery harvest of lobsters and urchins and recruitment facilitation strength. The methods state that simulations run for 200 time steps and equilibrium biomass is recorded at the end of each simulation while incrementally increasing and then decreasing fishing mortality to test for hysteresis and alternative stable states. The results state that increasing urchin harvest increases resilience by reducing the range of predator harvest rates at which alternative stable states are possible and that this reduces the parameter space in which an external perturbation could shift the system from one alternative stable state to another. This operationalization quantifies resilience as the size of the bistable region and the thresholds for shifts between states which corresponds to latitude. The state variables tracked and used in the hysteresis figures are biomasses of kelp algae sea urchins and spiny lobsters. The disturbance is fishing harvest represented as fishing mortality rates FL and FU which is a modelled biological resource use pressure. The model system is described as rocky reefs in southern California and the first author affiliation is San Diego State University in the United States.

The introduction states a resilience framework where stability is partitioned into resistance defined as the change during disturbance and recovery defined as the capacity to return to the original state after the disturbance. The methods describe a common garden experiment where half of the plants received no water for a severe 4-week drought followed by a 2-week recovery period and weekly measurements tracked responses. The results and discussion report declines during drought and that within 7 to 14 days after rewatering Amax gs and leaf health recovered to nearly the levels of controls and that drought treated plants recovered to the gs levels of controls by recovery week 2. These are direct measurements of system response and recovery dynamics so measurement is observed. Resilience is quantified through the speed of return toward control or baseline after rewatering which corresponds to recovery rate. The variables used to track recovery include maximum net photosynthesis and stomatal conductance and proportion of healthy leaves which are physiological indicators and cover type condition. The study subjects are vascular epiphytes which are plants. The disturbance is an imposed absence of rain described as meteorological drought so it is manipulated climatic and treated as a discrete 4-week event followed by recovery so it is pulse. The first author affiliation is Franklin and Marshall College in the United States. Epiphytes were collected in Monteverde Cloud Forest Reserve and Curi-Cancha Reserve in Costa Rica with coordinates given and the average coordinate is reported with a small range so location range is zero. The observation window spans from 7 April to 7 May plus a 2-week recovery period which totals 42 days.

The study imposed an extreme drought in microcosms described as 2 weeks at 3% water holding capacity and then rewetted soils to 60% water holding capacity and measured responses 1 15 and 60 days after rewetting. Resistance and resilience were quantified using indices including Orwin and Wardle 2004 and Bray Curtis similarity and resilience was defined as the proportion of changes caused during the extreme drought event that was recovered between the end of such disturbance and each sampling time during recovery. This uses observed measurements of bacterial and fungal community composition richness and abundance and a multifunctionality index based on eight soil functions. The introduction states ecosystem stability has two key components resistance and resilience during and after the drought event respectively which defines the framework stability = resistance + resilience. Because resilience is quantified as recovery of the drought induced change toward the control it matches recovery degree rather than recovery rate or recovery time. Target variables used to quantify resilience include microbial community composition richness and abundance and multifunctionality derived from eight soil functions related to soil C N and P cycling. The work combines soils from a long term manipulative field climate change experiment in Mostoles Spain with a laboratory drought and rewetting incubation. The first author affiliation is Universidad de Alicante in Spain. The field site is given as 40 degrees 20 minutes 37 seconds N and 3 degrees 52 minutes 00 seconds W. The disturbance is manipulated climatic drought and rewetting and also manipulated resource water availability via soil moisture levels. The drought is a discrete 2 week event followed by recovery so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The observation window for drought plus recovery spans 14 days plus 60 days which is 74 days.

The study reports a perturbation and recovery experiment in which predators were excluded and then reintroduced and the response was tracked through time. Plant species percent cover was sampled once per month between May and September each year from 1999 to 2003 and the monthly values were averaged by year. The paper states that temporally staggered predator reintroductions failed to restore the ecosystem and calls this a loss of resilience. The recovery assessment is based on whether Solidago rugosa abundance returned to levels matching the baseline natural state after predator restoration and whether plant species evenness matched the three level control. This corresponds to recovery degree because it evaluates whether the system returned to the original state rather than estimating a recovery rate or recovery time. Resilience is not defined as part of an explicit equation framework in the introduction methods or discussion. The target variables used in the recovery and state comparison are Solidago rugosa relative abundance measured as percent cover and productivity estimated as dry plant biomass production. The work is a field experiment with predator exclusion barriers and pesticide treatments and also includes field observation of vegetation cover and biomass conversion. The first author affiliation is Yale University in the United States and the field site is in north eastern Connecticut USA. The habitat is described as a 1.5 ha meadow surrounded by hardwood forest which corresponds best to grassland. The disturbance is manipulated predator removal and reintroduction which is a biotic disturbance. The disturbance is treated as a discrete exclusion period followed by restoration attempts which fits a pulse framing. The observation window for the time series used to evaluate recovery spans 1999 through 2003 which is five years or 1825 days.

Framework: resilience = ability of a stand to quickly recover its basic structure and function following disturbance
Justification: The study synthesizes data from 1538 field sites across boreal North America and evaluates compositional changes in tree species following 58 recent fires from 1989 to 2014. It defines resilience as the ability of a stand to quickly recover its basic structure and function following disturbance. Resilience and loss of resilience are evaluated using a postfire trajectory metric based on changes in stem density and species dominance comparing prefire tree stem density with postfire seedling stem density measured 2 to 13 years following fire. This uses observed field measurements of species specific postfire seedling densities and prefire tree composition and relative density. The quantification is based on whether the prefire dominant taxon maintains dominance and whether absolute stem density is maintained reduced or becomes zero which is a recovery degree classification rather than a rate or time. The target variables directly used are stem density and species dominance which correspond to density and composition. The focal subjects are tree species including black spruce jack pine and broadleaf trees which are Plantae and the level is community because dominance and composition are assessed across multiple taxa. The disturbance is wildfire treated as stand replacing fires and analyzed as discrete events with postfire measurements which matches a pulse disturbance. The first author affiliation is Wilfrid Laurier University in Canada. The field region is boreal North America including Canada and the United States. The observation window spans from the earliest fire year 1989 to the latest postfire measurement year 2014 plus up to 13 years postfire which yields 4745 days.

The study uses MODIS derived GPP and ET to compute WUE and analyzes WUE from 2000 to 2020 which is direct observational time series data. Resilience is quantified using the index Rd defined as WUE during drought years divided by WUE during non drought years which measures how much WUE changes under drought relative to other years. The introduction defines ecological resilience as the ability to return to its normal state after experiencing a disturbance but the method operationalizes resilience as the ratio Rd rather than a multi component framework. Because Rd compares performance during drought years to remaining years it captures deviation under disturbance which aligns with resistance rather than recovery rate or recovery time. The target variable used in the resilience calculation is WUE which is a functional metric defined as GPP to ET and thus is ecosystem function. The analysis is global and uses remote sensing and gridded climate datasets so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation is Northwest AF University in PR China so the institution country is CN. The disturbance is drought characterized by SPEI and treated as a hydroclimatic disturbance without experimental manipulation so it is unmanipulated climatic. The study evaluates drought effects across the 2000 to 2020 period without a defined recovery interval for resilience calculation so the disturbance is treated as press. The observation window for WUE time series spans 2000 to 2020 which is 21 years or 7670 days using 365 days per year.

The introduction defines resilience as the ability to resist and recover from disturbances and states that resilience encompasses at least two separate processes resistance and recovery. The methods state that resilience was defined following Hodgson et al. 2015 where resilience is defined as the result of two processes resistance to and recovery from disturbance. The study uses Living Planet Database population time series with repeated monitoring surveys of population abundance and estimates population growth rate r from abundance data. Resistance time series are negative r values and recovery time series are positive r values and trends in these are estimated with state space models. Recovery is explicitly defined as the rate at which a population returns at its previous state after a disturbance and positive r values represent proxies of the recovery of the population with more positive r meaning faster recovery. The target variable used to quantify resilience components is population growth rate r derived from abundance which is a demographic parameter. The data are vertebrate populations so the level is population and the taxon is Animalia. The approach is field observation because the database compiles monitored population abundance time series from surveys. The first author affiliation is University of Bristol in the UK. The data cover all continents and are described as globally distributed so field country and location are Global. Threats are defined as human activities or processes impacting populations for at least 50 percent of surveyed years and include climate change invasive species habitat loss or degradation exploitation pollution and diseases so the disturbance is unmanipulated and treated as ongoing press. The time series duration varies between five and 63 years and covers 1951 to 2017 so the maximum observation duration is 63 years which is 22995 days.

The study frames resilience in terms of postfire forest recovery and states that it used seedling density thresholds as a broad approach to resilience and asked what percentage of burned area has recovered to meet thresholds consistent with historical tree densities. Resilience is therefore quantified from observed postfire ponderosa pine and Douglas-fir seedling densities compared against historical density thresholds which is a recovery degree assessment. The target variable used in the resilience assessment is conifer seedling density expressed as seedlings per hectare. Data come from field surveys of regeneration plots across 15 wildfires and spatial predictions within fire perimeters so the approach is field observation. The disturbance is wildfire and it is treated as discrete fire events from 1988 to 2010 so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The first author affiliation is University of Colorado Boulder in the United States. The study area is southern Colorado and northern New Mexico in the United States with fires selected in the southern portion of the Southern Rocky Mountains Ecoregion and Table 1 gives an elevation range of 1861 to 3605 m. The average coordinate is approximated from the stated latitude constraint of less than 38.5 N and the regional description of southern Colorado and northern New Mexico. The observation window for postfire recovery spans from the earliest fire year 1988 to seedling establishment data through 2015 which yields 27 years or 9855 days.

The study states that it assessed resistance and resilience of anuran amphibians to an abrupt change in weather conditions in 2010 to 12 the Big Wet following the Big Dry 1997 to mid 2010. It defines resistance as a measure of the degree to which a species can maintain aspects of its viability in relation to an applied pressure such as drying or warming and resilience as the rate of recovery once the pressure is released. The resilience assessments are based on statistical comparisons of among period reporting rates of breeding calling males. Calling male counts and reporting rates are observational survey data collected by aural surveys and visual searches across repeated survey rounds in 2006 and 2007 and 2011 and 2012. Because resilience is defined as rate of recovery it matches recovery rate. The target variable used for resilience is reporting rates of breeding calling males which is an abundance related measure at the population level. The approach is field observation because the data come from repeated visits to waterbodies with day and night surveys. The first author affiliation is Monash University Clayton Victoria Australia. The field region is north central Victoria Australia across a 30000 square kilometre box and ironbark region. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to central Victoria near Bendigo and the range is set to zero because only an average point is reported. The dominant habitat described is box and ironbark eucalypt forests and woodlands even though agricultural landscapes are also included. The taxon is anuran amphibians which are Animalia. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic drought and wet period and it is treated as a prolonged period Big Dry and Big Wet so it is press. The observation window for resilience spans from the first surveys in 2006 to the last in 2012 which is about 6.25 years or 2280 days.

The study imposed prescribed fires in treatment pastures while control pastures remained unburned and all pastures were grazed by cattle. Vegetation structure variables were measured in the field from 21 May to 16 June in 2006 to 2008 and compared across time since fire classes from 0.5 to 5 years. The discussion defines resiliency as the amount of time required to return to a state following disturbance which approximates the pre-disturbance state. The results state that all measurements of vegetation structure returned to levels that were not significantly different from unburned control pastures 4 years after being burned and that affected measures recovered within 1 to 4 years. This directly quantifies resilience as time to recovery after a discrete fire event. The response variables used to assess recovery include percent cover variables and vegetation structure measures reported in Table 1. The study site is Cooper Wildlife Management Area in Woodward County Oklahoma USA with coordinates 36 32 10 N and 99 30 05 W. The first author affiliation is Oklahoma State University in the United States. The habitat is described as an Artemisia shrubland dominated by Artemisia filifolia. The focal measured variables are vegetation structure components so the taxon is plants and the level is community because cover and structure are measured across vegetation categories rather than individuals.

The study states that it evaluates secondary forest resilience along a gradient of land use intensity in a swidden cultivation system. It defines resilience assessment using recovery by stating that resilience was assessed as the recovery level of forest structure and species diversity achieved by secondary forests 5 years after abandonment. It also states that the sampling of same aged secondary forests allowed evaluation of differences in recovery rate by directly comparing the recovery level achieved by same aged secondary forests. The response variables used to represent recovery include stand total basal area and mean canopy height and species richness and Shannon diversity and inverse Simpson diversity. Basal area and canopy height are measures of forest structure and are quantified from field measurements of woody individuals including diameter at breast height and estimated height. Species richness and diversity indices are calculated from field sampled trees shrubs palms and lianas identified to species or morphospecies. The approach is field observation because the work sampled 38 early secondary forests in five communities and recorded vegetation and soil and landscape variables in the field. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University in the Netherlands. The study site is in the municipalities of Tefe and Alvaraes in the Central Amazon Brazil. The text reports that geographical location was recorded with GPS but it does not provide coordinates in the provided text. The habitat is secondary forests in the Amazon and agriculture is in non flooded terra firme areas so the dominant habitat type is forest. The focal organisms are woody plants including trees palms shrubs and lianas so the taxon is Plantae. The disturbance context is swidden slash and burn agriculture described as slashed and burned for crop cultivation and then left to fallow and intensification is treated as increasing cycles and weeding and decreasing fallow so it is unmanipulated land use and infrastructure development and unmanipulated fire. The disturbance is treated as ongoing intensification gradient rather than a single event with a defined recovery interval within the study window so the pattern is press. The recovery level is evaluated at 5 years after abandonment so the observation duration is 5 years which is 1825 days.

The introduction defines stability as two concepts with the phrase that the stability of ecosystems can be described in two concepts one is resilience and the other is resistance. The methods state that resilience was evaluated using measures of functional performance and observed recovery time. The methods specify that recovery time was calculated and that continuously recorded current was selected as default recovery time for further analysis. The results report recovery time values in hours for Group A Group B and Group C and describe recovery to stable performance after pH disturbance. The disturbance is a pH shock applied by changing the feeding with PBS of pH 4.0 for four cycles and then changing back to pH 7.0 which is a manipulated chemical disturbance treated as a discrete event. The target variable used for resilience quantification is the continuously recorded current which the methods describe as reflecting reactor status and biofilm community performance and it is a functional performance measure. The study system is microbial biofilm communities in microbial electrolysis cells and the dominant taxa include Geobacter and Methanobrevibacter which correspond to Bacteria and Archaea. The activated sludge inoculum was collected from Beixiaohe Reclaimed Water Plant in Beijing China and the first author affiliation list includes Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing China. The observation window for disturbance and recovery includes approximately 14 cycles predisturbance plus four cycles disturbance plus nearly 14 cycles recovery with each cycle being 24 hr which totals about 32 days. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated from Beijing China as the site of the reclaimed water plant and the institution city.

The study aims to identify thresholds that lead to state change and discusses resilience as something decreased by chronic drivers and potentially lost stepwise but it does not define resilience in an explicit equation framework. The methods state they use a space for time substitution approach and therefore cannot delineate the trajectory of individual reefs. Thresholds are identified as nonlinear change points in the variance or relationship between fish biomass and ecosystem metrics rather than recovery after a disturbance. Total fish biomass is used as a proxy for the driver fishing pressure and the analysis quantifies threshold biomass levels and biomass ratios associated with risk of state change. Because resilience is not directly measured as recovery dynamics but is discussed in relation to thresholds and risk of shifts it is treated as inferred. The approach compiles monitoring and literature data from 2001 sites across 26 Caribbean countries from 1993 to 2011 which is field observation. The first author affiliation listed is Environmental Defense Fund in San Francisco USA. Data are from Caribbean coral reefs across the listed countries and regions including Florida Keys and Puerto Rico and US Virgin Islands and Venezuela and others. The habitat is coral reefs which are shallow nearshore marine environments consistent with Marine Neritic. No coordinates are provided in the text for an average location. Disturbance type and pattern are not directly used to quantify resilience because the analysis is based on spatial thresholds along a biomass gradient rather than observed responses to discrete disturbances.

The methods state that a plant removal experiment was conducted with treatments control shrub removal selective cutting of subordinate arbour tree species and shrub removal plus selective cutting. The methods define a nematode resilience index RL(tx) using the absolute difference between control and disturbed soil at time x and state that a value of 1 indicates full recovery and lower values indicate a slower rate of recovery. This is a direct quantification of recovery dynamics from observed nematode community data so the measurement is observed. The study presents stability indices as including resistance and resilience and the discussion defines stability as including resilience or resistance under perturbation. The resilience index is based on change in the difference between disturbed and control through time which corresponds to recovery rate rather than recovery time. The resilience index is calculated from nematode community data identified to genus or family and grouped into trophic and c p guilds so the target variable type is composition and the level is community. The work is a field experiment because treatments were applied in natural mixed bamboo forests in Yanling County Hunan Province China. The site coordinates are given as N26 30 and E113 59. Treatments were applied at the end of November 2008 and shrubs were removed again in late November 2009 indicating a maintained disturbance during the study window so the disturbance pattern is press. The disturbance is manipulated removal and selective cutting of plants which is a form of biological resource use. Soil sampling for resilience related indices occurred in May 2009 and March 2010 giving 305 days between first and last measurement.

The study states that it used community models and a Lotka Volterra competition model influenced by external perturbations and immigration. It defines ecosystem stability as the response of community biomass to external perturbations including invariability resistance and resilience. It defines resilience as the recovery rate or the reciprocal of the time needed for the community to reach a half recovery with Sresil defined from BTt relative to BTpert and BTeq. The target variable used in the resilience formula is total community biomass BT. The perturbations are simulated as continuous environmental stochasticity where Ej represents Gaussian white noises and as a catastrophic perturbation Ej equals minus 0.5 that lasts for a short period of time 0.5 time unit. The continuous stochasticity is treated as a press disturbance and the catastrophic perturbation is treated as a pulse disturbance. The first author affiliation is Peking University Beijing China. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work is based on simulations. The disturbance duration for the pulse perturbation is explicitly given as 0.5 time unit and the recovery metric uses the time to half recovery so the observation window must extend beyond the perturbation and the only explicit duration stated is 0.5 time unit.

The study defines resilience as ecosystem capacity to recover following disturbance and evaluates recovery of function structure and composition independently and collectively. It introduces a spatially explicit Ecosystem Resilience Index that integrates vegetation function structure and composition recovery metrics. Recovery is quantified from remotely sensed time series and a postfire comparison including NPP from 1986 to 2020 and fractional cover from 1986 to 2020 and canopy height in 2020 with a modeled unburned potential canopy height. Percent recovery is calculated as RM 2020 minus RM minimum postfire divided by RM prefire minus RM minimum postfire for NPP and composition and as observed canopy height divided by predicted unburned potential canopy height for structure. The disturbance is the Crazy Horse Fire that occurred on August 10th 2003 and recovery is assessed over 17 years after wildfire. The approach uses remote sensing and Google Earth Engine extraction and spatial statistical models which are field observation rather than experiments. The first author affiliation is University of Montana in the United States and the study domain is in northwestern Montana in the Mission Wilderness. The habitat is mixed coniferous forest and the subjects are vegetation functional types and forest canopy. The fire is treated as a discrete event with postfire recovery trajectories. The observation window for the temporally continuous recovery metrics spans 1986 to 2020 which is 34 years or 12410 days and the disturbance to last measurement span is 2003 to 2020 which is 17 years or 6205 days.

The study imposed simulated deer browse by clipping saplings with fixed percentages of leaf bearing branches removed. Resilience was quantified from the system response after this imposed disturbance using regrowth dynamics including the time required for saplings to recover their former canopy size following clipping. The paper states that recovery times were calculated as approximately percent clipping divided by percent shoot growth and it reports predicted recovery times such as half a year to more than 3 years after 60% clipping. The experiment measured shoot growth diameter growth and mortality with repeat measurements after 12 and 24 months and mortality recorded 1 and 2 years after clipping. The framework in the Introduction and aims explicitly treats resistance and resilience together as a resistance resilience trade off and tests hypotheses about both. The target variable used to quantify resilience is shoot regrowth expressed as shoot growth and used to compute recovery time. The work was an in situ field experiment on saplings in regenerating mixed beech conifer forest in the Rowallan Forest Southland New Zealand with coordinates given as 2082975 E 5437950 N. The first author affiliation is the Department of Plant Sciences University of Cambridge UK. The disturbance corresponds to browsing by introduced deer and is treated as a discrete clipping treatment applied in January February and March 2002 with subsequent recovery observations. The observation window for resilience spans from clipping to the last measurements at 24 months which is 730 days.

The study proposes a remote sensing based ecosystem health index using the pressure state response framework and reports that ecosystem health increased from 2000 to 2016. Resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics of an observed state variable after a defined disturbance but is computed as an ecological resilience index using landscape diversity area ratios and resilience score values for landscape types derived from literature. The methods state that the resilience score value is derived from literature and shown in Table 2 and the ecological resilience index is calculated as ERI = sum of D times Si times Ei. This makes resilience an inferred property based on land cover composition and assigned scores rather than observed recovery. The introduction quotes Myneni that ecosystem health is a measure of system resilience organization and vigor which provides the framework ecosystem health = resilience + organization + vigor. The resilience related target variable is landscape type and its area ratio which is land use. The indices are derived from MODIS NDVI Landsat based land cover and gridded population data which is field observation via remote sensing. The study area is in Guangxi Province China with coordinates 104 degrees 28 minutes to 109 degrees 09 minutes E and 22 degrees 51 minutes to 25 degrees 37 minutes N and the average is approximately N24.2333 E106.7833. Dominant vegetation biomes are subtropical mixed evergreen and deciduous forests and shrublands and forests are a major land cover type so the habitat type is Forest. The analysis concerns ecosystem health indices and ecosystem services and resilience at pixel and county scales so the level is Ecosystem. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not applicable.

The study used an unplanned 2006 wildfire and compared vegetation in patches exposed to low medium and high fire severities. Fire severity was quantified with dNBR from pre and post fire Landsat images and vegetation was surveyed in 2017 to 2018 which is 11 years after the fire. The introduction states they hypothesized a fire severity threshold at which open forest resilience is lost leading to an abrupt transition to a persistent thicket state. The results estimate breakpoints in dNBR where tree density loss and small woody plant density increase changed and the discussion suggests an operational threshold of dNBR about 600 corresponding to a forest shrub thicket transition. This is a threshold beyond which the original forest state cannot be maintained so the quantification matches latitude. Resilience was evaluated using observed vegetation structure metrics including changes in density of trees at least 25 cm and density of woody plants at most 3 m. The focal organisms are woody plants including Prosopis caldenia and other shrubs so the taxon is Plantae. The field site is a caldén forest near Santa Rosa La Pampa Argentina with coordinates given as 36 26 46 S and 64 44 04 W. The first author affiliation is Universidad Nacional de La Pampa Santa Rosa Argentina. The disturbance is a discrete wildfire event in September 2006 so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The observation window for disturbance response spans from the prefire 2005 image to the 2017 to 2018 field measurements which is about 11 years or 4015 days.

Framework: resilience = ability to withstand disturbances before shifting to alternative states
Justification: The study states that it quantifies boreal forest resilience using a resilience indicator derived from the temporal autocorrelation of kNDVI from 2000 to 2020 and it also uses VOD to construct TAC. It defines resilience as the ability of a system to withstand disturbances before shifting to alternative states and it defines resilience on the basis of critical slowing down theory. It explains that TAC can quantitatively assess resilience because the autocorrelation coefficient reflects recovery rate and when autocorrelation approaches 1 the recovery rate decreases to 0. It quantifies changes in resilience as the difference in TAC between 2018 to 2020 and 2000 to 2002 and it regresses ΔTAC on ΔFC to estimate sensitivity. The state variables used for TAC are vegetation indices kNDVI and VOD and the text describes VOD as closely related to vegetation biomass and NDVI as an indicator of vegetation health and greenness which are ecosystem functioning proxies. The approach is remote sensing observation using MODIS NDVI MOD44B tree cover VOD and ESA CCI land cover products rather than experiments or imposed model disturbances. The first author affiliation is Southern University of Science and Technology Shenzhen China. The boreal region is defined as snow and polar zones north of 50 N and the analysis spans the entire boreal region across continents so the field extent is global. The disturbance is treated as forest cover change in tree cover percentage and the study states it considers solely forest cover changes without distinguishing or excluding any specific disturbance types which corresponds to an unmanipulated structural change in system components. Forest cover change is analysed over two decades without a defined end of the driver within the observation window so it is treated as press. The observation window is 2000 to 2020 which is 20 years or 7300 days using 365 days per year.

The paper derives an operational index of short term retrospective resilience from remotely sensed change detection in NDVI for grassland habitats in the Baganza stream watershed in northern Italy. It states that the normalized difference vegetation index was used as the ecological response variable and that differences of NDVI between two different times at the same location were considered as disturbances. It defines retrospective resilience operationally by stating that it used 1 over the median absolute change intensity as an approximate estimate of five year retrospective resilience. Because resilience is quantified from observed NDVI change between 1990 and 1995 it is an observed measurement based on system response over time. The text discusses resilience together with resistance and states resistance is a key complementary aspect of resilience and also frames resilience as proportional to resistance per unit of external pressure. The quantification is not recovery time rate degree resistance latitude or invariability because it is an inverse of detected change intensity rather than a return to baseline metric. The target variable used for change detection is NDVI which the paper links to vegetation disturbance and dynamics and to ecological interest such as health and stress conditions of vegetation cover. The study area is the Baganza watershed on the Emilian side of the northern Apennines and the authors are affiliated with the University of Lecce in Italy. The disturbance is treated as unmanipulated detectable change in land use and land cover affecting NDVI between two image dates and the change detection uses two discrete dates five years apart which is a pulse framing. The observation window is five years between August 11 1990 and July 24 1995.

The study states that it simulated a drought event on intact plant soil mesocosms extracted from mountain grasslands along a management intensity gradient. It reports that ecosystem processes and properties were quantified before during and after drought to characterize ecosystem resilience. It defines resilience quantification using resistance and recovery indices with formulas Ra(Y)i = (YT1i − YT0i) / YT0i * 100 and Rec(Y)i = (YT2i − YT1i) / YT1i * 100. It states that ecosystem resilience of plant and microbial communities was quantified based on two key ecosystem properties microbial biomass N and total forage N. The disturbance is imposed by stopping watering for 13 days and then re watering for 48 days which is a discrete event with a recovery interval. The observation window spans from the start of the pre drought greenhouse period through the end of recovery which is 13 days watering plus 13 days drought plus 48 days recovery. The study area is Val d Autrans in the northern Vercors range of the French Pre Alps with coordinates 45 07 N 5 31 E. The first author affiliation is Université Grenoble Alpes CNRS LECA Grenoble France. The habitat is described as pastures and grasslands and the title specifies mountain grasslands. Taxon is not specified for resilience because the quantified properties are microbial biomass N and total forage N which are ecosystem properties rather than a single kingdom.

The study states that it proposed and calculated a spatial resilience index based on landscape indicators and landscape metrics related to the abundance of Bactrocera oleae. It explicitly says it assumed spatial resilience could be quantified from certain indicators and that the spatial resilience index was calculated from scrubland area landscape diversity index Euclidean nearest neighbour distance landscape shape index patch density olive grove area and mean patch size. These are static spatial structure variables rather than observed recovery dynamics of the system after disturbance. Therefore resilience measurement is inferred from proxy landscape metrics. No explicit equation or statement defines resilience as a component within a larger framework and resilience is discussed conceptually so the framework is none. The approach is field observation because the analysis uses a land use map from 2007 and applies Fragstats landscape metrics to real regions municipalities and circular areas. The first author affiliation is Universidad Complutense de Madrid in Spain. The study areas are two olive regions of Andalusia Spain Estepa and Sierra de Segura. Figure 1 locates them in the provinces of Sevilla and Jaén so the field country is Spain. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated as the midpoint between Estepa and Sierra de Segura. The habitat is agricultural because the focal system is olive grove socio ecological landscapes and the index includes olive grove area as a key component. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is not quantified from observed disturbance response trajectories.

The study quantifies resilience using model simulations of vegetation and soil water dynamics and it also compares model patterns to empirical aerial and drone observations from western Namibia. Resilience is explicitly defined as “resilience to a drier climate quantified by the tipping point to bare soil” and the Discussion reiterates that “the tipping point to bare soil is shifted to lower precipitation values” under different response mechanisms. This matches the Latitude definition because it is a threshold beyond which the system shifts to an alternative state namely “collapse to bare soil.” The target variable used in the resilience quantification is “total biomass spatial integral of b1+b2” shown in Fig. 4 while the tipping point is identified by collapse points at specific precipitation values. The approach includes modeling because the core analysis uses “integro-partial differential equations” and simulated scenarios with changing precipitation. The approach also includes field observation because the paper uses “empirical images” and an “empirical image… using a drone” and performs O-ring statistics on observed plant locations. The first author affiliation is “Ben-Gurion University of the Negev… Israel” so the institution country is IL. The field data are from “western Namibia” including “NamibRand Nature Reserve” and “Girib es” so the field country is NA and the location is approximated as the midpoint of these Namibian sites. The habitat is described as “grasslands” for fairy circles so the habitat type is Grassland. The study subjects are grasses in the genus “Stipagrostis” so the taxon is Plantae. The disturbance used for resilience quantification is decreasing precipitation representing “a drier climate” imposed in simulations so it is model climatic and treated as a continuous driver in time. The precipitation decline in Fig. 4 runs until collapse times up to 238 years so the observation duration is 238 years converted to days.

The introduction defines resilience as the ability to resist and recover from disturbance. The study uses a 20 year time series from 1993 to 2013 on 46 Great Barrier Reef reefs and quantifies temporal change in community composition using Bray Curtis dissimilarity between years. Disturbances are observed and classified as coral bleaching Acanthaster planci outbreaks coral disease or storms based on diver assessments. Resistance is represented by smaller changes in community composition from the pre disturbance state and recovery is represented by return toward partial effect equals 0. The methods state that predicted time to recovery partial effect equals 0 was estimated using loess regression and the results report recovery times such as MPAs about 6.6 years and non MPAs about 8.7 years after A planci outbreaks. Because resilience is quantified from observed post disturbance trajectories and recovery time is explicitly estimated the measurement is observed and the quantification is recovery time. The target variable used for resilience quantification is community composition of fish and benthic assemblages. The work is based on field monitoring surveys and not an experiment or simulation. The first author affiliation is the Australian Institute of Marine Science in Townsville Australia. The field system is the Great Barrier Reef Australia extending between 9 and 24 degrees south and Figure 1 shows the reef locations around about 20 degrees south and 145 degrees east so the average coordinate is approximated from that map. The habitat is shallow coral reef slope in the Great Barrier Reef which is a neritic marine habitat. The assemblages include fish species and benthic growth forms including corals and algal turfs so the kingdoms Animalia and Plantae apply. The disturbances are unmanipulated bleaching climatic A planci outbreaks biotic coral disease chemical and storms treated as hydrological events and they are analysed as discrete events with lags up to 5 years so the pattern is pulse.

The study uses Kansas field trial sorghum data collected from 1985 to 2014 and matches observed yields with daily temperatures and precipitation which is direct observational data. Resilience is operationalized as heat resilience estimated as the yield impact from an additional degree day above 33 C and the paper repeatedly uses resistance language such as most resistant cultivars and ability to resist heat stress. The cultivar specific resilience estimates are defined as the regression coefficient for extreme heat which measures the percentage impact on yield from an additional degree day above 33 C and smaller absolute values indicate more heat resilient cultivars. This is a measure of reduced deviation in yield under heat exposure rather than recovery after a discrete event so quantification aligns with resistance. The target variable used in the resilience quantification is sorghum yield and yield is treated as productivity in the text so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The biological organization level corresponding to yield as a system functional output is ecosystem. The approach is field observation because the core data are field trial and on farm observational yield and weather records and the analysis is regression modeling without imposed experimental warming. The first author affiliation is Kansas State University in the United States. The field data are from 11 sites in Kansas and county level Kansas data so the field country is the United States. No coordinates are provided for the 11 Kansas locations so location and location range cannot be determined. The habitat is dryland sorghum production under nonirrigated conditions which is agricultural. The taxon is sorghum Sorghum bicolor which is Plantae. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic warming and extreme heat exposure above 33 C derived from observed temperatures. The disturbance is treated as press because warming scenarios are uniform temperature increases applied across the growing season without a defined end for recovery. The observation duration spans 1985 to 2014 which is 30 years or 10950 days.

The study imposed a simulated drought using rain-out shelters and compared responses to ambient and roofed control treatments. Resistance and recovery were quantified using percentage change indices calculated as (P0 − C0)/C0 × 100 described as indices of resistance and recovery. The paper reports resistance of CO2 fluxes to drought measured as ecosystem respiration and also reports recovery of ecosystem respiration after rewetting. The core resilience-related outcome emphasized in the title and conclusions is increased resistance of carbon fluxes to drought. CO2 fluxes are ecosystem-level carbon cycling functions measured repeatedly with static chambers before during and after drought. The experiment was conducted at Colt Park Meadows Ingleborough National Nature Reserve northern England with coordinates latitude 54°12′N longitude 2°21′W. The first author affiliation is Centre for Ecology and Hydrology Lancaster Environment Centre Lancaster UK.
