The drought shelters were in place from 5 June to 10 July 2013 and CO2 fluxes were measured from 17 May to 16 August 2013 giving 91 days between first and last flux measurement. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event with shelter installation and removal and subsequent rewetting so it is a pulse disturbance.

The study is a long term pulse perturbation experiment featuring heavy grazing and shrub removal to determine if critical thresholds can be demonstrated in Chihuahuan Desert grasslands. Vegetation was measured before manipulation in spring 1996 and after manipulation in fall 2002 and again in fall 2009 using line point intercept to estimate foliar cover for each plant species in each paddock. Resilience is evaluated as vegetation recovery because the authors ask whether cover values or patch size metrics could predict vegetation recovery and they model change in Bouteloua eriopoda cover from 2002 to 2009. The paper explicitly states that recovery occurred at the same rate irrespective of grazing history and that grass cover predicted variation in recovery rates. The target variable used in the recovery analysis is Bouteloua eriopoda foliar cover and the predictor is cover and patch size metrics observed in 2002. The disturbance is manipulated grazing by cattle with 65 to 80 percent removal of aboveground perennial grass biomass applied yearly from 1996 to 2000 and shrub removal using glyphosate to prevent resprouting. The disturbance is treated as a pulse perturbation and no grazing had occurred since spring 2000. The experiment was conducted on the USDA ARS Jornada Experimental Range near Las Cruces New Mexico USA and the centre coordinates are given as 32 35 21.20 N 106 51 03.00 W. The observation window for perturbation and recovery measurements spans from spring 1996 to fall 2009.

Framework: resilience = functional response diversity + functional redundancy + connectivity + modularity + centrality
Justification: The study states that it uses a simulation approach and that it simulates management strategies and three simulated disturbances drought pest outbreak and timber harvesting. It defines resilience as the capacity to absorb environmental changes and cope with disturbances without losing functionality. It quantifies five resilience related properties and calls them indicators namely functional response diversity functional redundancy connectivity modularity and centrality. Functional response diversity is measured as the FDis diversity index of the whole metacommunity across the landscape and functional redundancy is calculated as proposed by Ricotta et al. Connectivity is quantified as the probability of connectivity index PC and centrality is quantified as generalized betweenness centrality BCPC and modularity is Q. The approach is static simulation and it explicitly says it does not account for regeneration and establishment processes after disturbance so recovery time or recovery rate are not measured. The target variables used to quantify resilience are functional diversity indices and network properties which correspond to diversity and interaction. The study area is Central Quebec region southeastern Canada with coordinates 45.350 to 46.340 N and 72.590 to 71.220 W and the average coordinate is reported. The first author affiliation includes Universite du Quebec a Montreal in Canada. The disturbances are imposed in the model as drought induced mortality pest outbreak mortality and clear cut harvesting. The disturbances are treated as discrete simulated episodes designed to simulate a 25 percent loss of tree canopy.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the capacity of ecosystems to recover from stresses and disturbances and defines resistance as the ability of ecosystems to limit the population growth of invasive species. The methods state that the study applied published indicator models that used random forests to classify indicators of both resilience and resistance using 19 predictor variables representing climate ecological drought and soil moisture availability. The methods further state that a process based ecohydrological model SOILWAT2 was used to project these predictor variables under ambient observed conditions and projected climatic conditions and then the published R and R prediction models were applied to obtain projected R and R values. The study therefore does not quantify resilience from observed recovery dynamics of a state variable after a disturbance and instead produces resilience indicators from predictor variables and model outputs. The continuous resilience index is explicitly defined as a linear combination of category probabilities from the random forest model which are derived from the predictor variables rather than from observed system recovery. The study area is within the sagebrush region of the Western United States and is dominated by big sagebrush Artemisia tridentata which supports rangelands and woodlands. The resistance model is described as resistance to invasion by Bromus tectorum which is a plant. The first author affiliation is U S Geological Survey in Flagstaff Arizona USA. The data sources used for predictors and projections are gridded products and soil databases for the study area but no explicit latitude longitude coordinates are provided in the text.

The introduction explicitly defines resilience as resistance and recovery in the phrase natural ecosystem’s resilience (i.e. resistance and recovery) to drought. Resilience is quantified using direct system responses because the study reports vegetation cover and biomass before during and after the 2011 drought and compares trajectories under crab exclusion and controls. The results state vegetation cover was 60 to 80 percent pre drought and dropped to less than 10 percent after 2011 and that in crab exclusion plots plants reached a cover of about 90 percent and thus fully recovered within just a year of drought subsidence. This supports recovery degree because recovery is expressed as return to high cover relative to pre drought conditions and as fully recovered. The target variable used repeatedly for the core resilience claim is vegetation cover as shown in Figure 1a and Figure 1b and described as vegetation cover was visually estimated and used to show collapse and recovery. The level is community because the target variable is plant community vegetation cover of the marsh dominated by Suaeda salsa rather than individual performance. The approach includes field experiments because the methods describe grazer exclusion cages and planting experiments and field observation because long term monitoring and remote sensing NDVI time series were used. The first author affiliation is Beijing Normal University in China. Field data were collected in protected salt marshes in temperate China including Fuqiaobei in the Yellow River Delta with coordinates 37 46 N 119 09 E and additional sites listed in the Yellow River Delta and Liao River Delta. The disturbance includes an unmanipulated climatic drought described as the 2010 to 2011 China drought and a manipulated biotic disturbance because crab access was manipulated via exclusion and cage removal. The drought is treated as a discrete event with a defined period between November 2010 and February 2011 followed by an extremely dry spring and then drought generally subsided after 2011 which matches a pulse framing. Observation duration is from the start of monitoring in 2009 to the last remote sensing year 2015 which is six years or 2190 days but the core pre during post experimental window is 2010 to 2015 and the long term monitoring since 2009 supports using 2009 to 2015 which is 1825 days between late 2009 and late 2015 as the study period reported.

The study used simulation experiments with the honey bee colony model BEEHAVE and a landscape generator and it states that it explored colony viability and resilience under different cropping system scenarios. Resilience is treated as a system response to forage gaps because the methods state that they assessed whether the colony can maintain basic functionality after a disturbance in this case foraging gaps. The disturbance is explicitly described as forage gaps and these are driven by spatiotemporal nectar and pollen supply in the modeled landscapes. Resilience is indicated by brood mortality because the abstract states resilience indicated by brood mortality and the methods quantify percent larval losses pollen lack and other colony dynamic responses. This is a direct response variable from the modeled system so the measurement is observed within a model with imposed disturbance. The quantification aligns with resistance because the analysis focuses on mortality and impairment during forage gaps rather than time to recovery and it describes resilience being critically reduced by prolonged forage gaps. The target variables used to quantify resilience include larval mortality and egg laying reduction and worker numbers which are demographic parameters at the colony population level. The approach is modeling and simulation because all results come from BEEHAVE simulations run for five years or until extinction. The first author affiliation is Helmholtz Centre for Environmental Research UFZ in Leipzig Germany. The farmland maps used are from Cornwall Trenwheal and Lincolnshire Nocton Heath in England and Table 2 provides their coordinates so the field country is the United Kingdom and the average coordinate is the mean of the two map coordinates. The habitat is agricultural because the study uses farmland maps and cropping systems. The taxon is Apis mellifera which is Animalia. The disturbance is model resource because the disturbance is lack of nectar and pollen supply and forage gaps in the model. The disturbance pattern is pulse because forage gaps are treated as discrete periods with start and end such as three weeks in April and two months in June to July. Observation duration is five years because simulations ran for five years or until extinction.

The study defines resilience as community recurrence by stating resilience is the recurrence of arthropod communities to pre disturbance communities. It quantifies resilience using community similarity by stating similarity in the community composition of windthrows and intact forest was used to test the resilience of arthropod assemblages after windthrows. Similarity is calculated from Bray Curtis distances and trajectories are analysed over time which uses observed community composition data from trap catches. The disturbance is windthrow from storms Vivian and Lothar described as stand replacing disturbances and as windstorms. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event with post disturbance years and sampling period 2 to 20 years post disturbance. The target variable for resilience is arthropod community composition because Bray Curtis dissimilarities and similarities are computed between windthrow and control forest communities. The approach is field observation because arthropods were sampled on permanent plots using traps over growing seasons. The first author affiliation is in Switzerland at WSL Birmensdorf. Field data are from Switzerland with sites in multiple cantons and Table 1 provides coordinates. The average coordinate is computed from the six site coordinates in Table 1. The location range is computed as the sum of the maximum latitude difference and maximum longitude difference relative to the average. Habitat is forest because all plots are in coniferous and broadleaf forests and control forest is intact forest. Taxon is Animalia because the focal organisms are arthropods including insects spiders and woodlice. Observation duration is 20 years because each study covered a period of 20 years and sampling period is 2 to 20 post disturbance years.

The study quantified resilience using the drought recovery period which it states indicates the resilience of plants and ecosystems to drought. Drought was monitored with MODIS based VHI during 2000 to 2020 and drought years 2000 2001 and 2008 were identified from the VHI time series. DRP was estimated using gross primary productivity and drought recovery was defined as when GPP exceeds the long term average after the drought. This is an observed measurement because it uses MODIS time series of GPP and VHI to quantify recovery dynamics after drought. The quantification is recovery time because DRP is reported in days and described as the time it takes for GPP to reach above average after the drought. The target variable is GPP which is described as carbon uptake and photosynthetic activity so it is an ecosystem function variable. The analysis compares DRP across land use and land cover types across Iran so the level is ecosystem. The approach is field observation because it uses remote sensing observations and no experimental manipulation. The first author affiliation is Tarbiat Modares University Tehran Iran so the institution country is IR. The study area is Iran so the field country is IR and the coordinates are given as 25.5 to 39.78 N and 44.10 to 63.11 E with the average reported. The habitat type is multi because resilience is quantified for cropland forest grassland and shrubland. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic drought identified from VHI and linked to low precipitation and high temperatures. The disturbance pattern is pulse because drought events are defined to begin when VHI is less than 0.3 and end when VHI goes beyond 0.3. The observation duration is 2000 to 2020 inclusive which is 21 years or 7665 days.

The study states that the final output was a composite index composed of ecosystem exposure sensitivity and resilience to climate thermal change. Resilience was defined as a composite variable integrating climate zone life form and successional stage with scores in Table 1 and calculated as Ri = (Rclim i + Rlf i + Rss i) / 3. The discussion states that accurate resilience data were not available and the approach used indicator based definition leaving knowledge gaps which shows resilience was not measured from recovery dynamics. The indicators used for resilience are vegetation attributes rather than observed time series response so the measurement is inferred and quantification is inferred. The resilience indicators include Raunkiaer life form regarding perennating bud location which is a plant trait so the target variable type is physiological indicator. The resilience is defined for vegetation community types and vegetation types so the level is community. The data sources are gridded climate datasets and a vegetation map and natural reserve map and the work is a national level assessment using spatial datasets rather than experiments so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation is Peking University Beijing China so institution country is CN. The assessment is for natural terrestrial vegetation types in China using data extracted within the whole of China so field country is CN. No explicit coordinates are provided for the study area beyond China wide maps so location and range are NA. The vegetation types include forests wetlands alpine tundra deserts and mangroves so the habitat type is multi. The subjects are vegetation types so the taxon is Plantae. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA.

The study experimentally imposed drought by excluding precipitation and maintaining mesocosms at predefined soil water deficits and then terminated drought by watering all pots to field capacity which is a manipulated pulse drought. Resistance was determined at peak drought and recovery was analyzed through repeated measurements in the weeks following drought and also with aboveground biomass in the subsequent year. The paper states that resistance and recovery were mapped into a bivariate resistance recovery framework and that predicted values were normalized to the corresponding baseline value to calculate normalized resistance and recovery. The productivity variables used for this normalization include gross primary productivity NDVI vegetative height and aboveground biomass production which are direct observations of system state and function. The first author affiliation is the University of Innsbruck in Austria and the experiment was located at the Botanical Garden in Innsbruck with coordinates 47.267628 N and 11.380426 E. The drought treatment lasted 23 days and measurements extended to peak biomass in the subsequent year which supports an observation window of about one year. The study species are Dactylis glomerata and Plantago lanceolata which are plants. The setting is a common garden mesocosm experiment rather than a natural IUCN habitat category so the habitat type is coded as Other.

The introduction states that the authors developed landscape scale assessments of belowground biomass and applied these as a measure of the resilience of salt marshes to sea level rise. The study quantifies trends using monthly Landsat based estimates from 2014 through 2023 and reports that belowground biomass decreased on average by 0.94% per year while aboveground biomass increased by 0.66% per year. The methods define annual trend as the slope of a linear model of predicted pixel biomass versus month number divided by mean biomass which is not recovery rate recovery time resistance latitude or invariability. Resilience is not defined as part of an explicit equation framework and is discussed as an indicator measure so the framework is none. The target variable used as the resilience measure is belowground biomass and aboveground biomass is also estimated but belowground biomass is described as the primary indicator of marsh resilience or vulnerability. The focal organism is Spartina alterniflora which is a plant. The study area is US Georgia coast salt marshes and a specific mapped example is given near Blackbeard Creek with coordinates −81.2261 31.4614. The disturbance context is sea level rise driven increased inundation intensity treated as increasing through time with no defined end which matches a press disturbance. The observation window for the time series is January 2014 through December 2023 which is 10 years or 3650 days.

The study objective was to detect declining resilience in a lake approaching a regime shift using automated sensor time series. Resilience was assessed using leading indicators computed from time series including standard deviation and autocorrelation time which the text states are indicators of declining resilience near thresholds. The indicators were computed from daily averages of directly measured variables chlorophyll a pH and dissolved oxygen and from calculated metabolism rates gross primary production respiration and net ecosystem production. Autocorrelation based indicators are treated as slowing down and thus recovery rate. The manipulation was a whole lake trophic cascade induced by slowly adding piscivorous bass with multiple stocking events over four years while a reference lake was unmanipulated. Sensor measurements were made every five minutes from 2008 to 2011 which supports a field experiment with long duration observation. The first author affiliation is University of Wisconsin Madison in the United States. The lakes were Peter and Paul Lakes and the work was conducted at a lake field setting in the United States but no coordinates were provided. The variables used for resilience quantification were ecosystem metabolism related measures and water chemistry variables so the target variable types are ecosystem function and abiotic parameter. The disturbance is biotic because it involved adding fish to alter the food web and it was treated as a slow ongoing driver toward a threshold rather than a single discrete event.

The abstract states that resilience is assessed as the rate at which perturbations to a stable ecological system decay and the introduction defines resilience or its reciprocal return time as measuring how rapidly a stable system returns to its original state after a perturbation. Resilience is defined as resilience = -2Re(l1(A)) which is an asymptotic approximation of the decay rate of perturbations to the linear system. The study quantifies responses to perturbations using imposed perturbations in models including linear compartment models for phosphorus cycling through a lake ecosystem and flow of elements through a tropical rain forest and a nonlinear predator prey model. The disturbance is treated as a perturbation event with dynamics following the perturbation and the paper explicitly refers to pulse perturbations in the key words. The target variables are model state variables representing amounts of material in compartments and densities in the predator prey model which are used to compute the magnitude of deviation from equilibrium and its decay rate. The approach is modeling and simulation because calculations use transfer matrices and matrix exponentials and integration of model equations. The first author affiliation is Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution in Massachusetts USA. The examples use a Panamanian tropical forest and Tuesday Lake in Wisconsin USA. The tropical rain forest example discusses perturbations remaining after a century and shows r(t) over t in (0,100 yr) so the observation window is 100 years which is 36500 days. Figures and tables used include Fig 1 to Fig 8 and Tables 1 to 6 which define and compute resilience and related transient measures from the models.

The study uses a chronosequence with plots that vary in time since abandonment and compares them to mature woodland reference plots to gauge the extent to which carbon stocks and biodiversity recover. Resilience is evaluated using observed field measurements including aboveground woody carbon stocks and soil carbon stocks and tree species composition and diversity across 40 years of succession. The paper states the objective was to examine the rate and extent to which carbon stocks in trees and soils recover after cultivation and it models changes with time since abandonment. Aboveground carbon recovery is quantified as an accumulation rate in Mg C per ha per year from regression of AGC against time since abandonment. Soil carbon is assessed for change over time since abandonment and is described as largely unaffected indicating resistance but the main quantified resilience metric is the recovery rate of AGC. Species composition recovery is assessed by increasing Bray Curtis similarity to mature woodland and predicted time to convergence but this is not expressed as a rate metric in the same way as AGC. Disturbance is shifting cultivation involving cutting and fire and manual cultivation followed by abandonment and regrowth and sites were selected to avoid major cutting or burning post abandonment. The disturbance is treated as a discrete clearance and cultivation phase followed by a recovery interval across the chronosequence. The study area is Kilwa District southeastern Tanzania bounded between 38.8400 to 39.8420 E and 8.8160 to 9.8560 S. The first author affiliation is the University of Edinburgh in the United Kingdom.

The study states that plant community response to perturbation can be separated into resistance and recovery and it says it incorporated the framework of Ingrisch and Bahn into its characterization of resilience and that this framework integrates resistance and recovery. Resistance is defined as baseline normalized biomass of flooded monoliths directly after a 2 week flood and recovery is defined as baseline normalized biomass of flooded monoliths 5 weeks after the flood. Figure 1 states resistance and recovery are above ground biomass in flooded monoliths relative to control monoliths with a dynamic baseline at 100%. The flooding treatment imposed two floods with water added on day 9 and day 64 and water levels maintained for 2 weeks then removed and monoliths recovered for 5 weeks. Above ground biomass was harvested directly after each flood to quantify resistance and after each recovery period to quantify recovery. Because resilience is quantified from observed biomass responses of the system to imposed floods it is observed. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University and Research in the Netherlands. Monoliths were taken from a field experiment in Wageningen the Netherlands with coordinates 51.9884 N and 5.6616 E. The system is intensively managed grassland with fertilization and thus agricultural habitat. The subjects are plant communities of grass and legume species. The disturbance is manipulated flooding created by adding water and maintaining surface water for 2 weeks which is a hydrological disturbance treated as discrete events. The observation window for resistance and recovery spans from day 1 to day 113 which is 112 days.

The study states it used a simulation approach with an integral projection model calibrated with National Forest Inventory data at the European scale. It says each species assemblage was disturbed from equilibrium using species specific storm disturbance mortality probabilities. It quantified resistance as inverse of immediate basal area loss recovery as slope of post disturbance increase in basal area and resilience as inverse of the cumulative deviation of basal area from the undisturbed state. The introduction states resilience encompasses both the forest ability to persist during the disturbance hereafter resistance and its ability to bounce back and return to an equilibrium hereafter recovery. Resilience is therefore treated as a combination of resistance and recovery. The resilience metric is not recovery degree recovery rate recovery time resistance latitude or invariability because it is defined as inverse of the integration over time of the difference in basal area from the undisturbed state. The target variable used for resistance recovery and resilience is total stand basal area. The disturbance is a storm disturbance applied in the model and applied as a single disturbance event with delayed effects over 3 years. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event followed by post disturbance trajectories over 3000 years which matches a pulse disturbance. The first author affiliation is Université Grenoble Alpes INRAE in France. The model calibration data come from NFI from Spain France Germany Sweden and Finland. No plot coordinates are provided so the location is approximated as a European average and the range reflects the broad European extent. The habitat is forest because the study concerns European forests and uses forest inventory plots. The taxa are trees and the listed species are tree species so Plantae. The observation duration for disturbance response is the 3000 years of post disturbance simulation expressed in days.

The study uses GIMMS NDVI bimonthly time-series acquired between July 1981 and December 2006 and models NDVI anomalies as a function of past NDVI anomalies and monthly SPEI drought index and temperature anomalies. Resilience is treated as engineering resilience and is quantified via the ARx coefficient alpha where large alpha indicates anomalies are strongly determined by the anomaly at time t minus 1 and the ecosystem recovers slowly and small alpha indicates ecosystems tend to recover quickly. This makes the resilience quantification a recovery rate type because it is based on autocorrelation and memory effects in a time series. The target variable used for resilience quantification is NDVI anomalies and NDVI is described as correlating with vegetation biomass and fAPAR and as an estimator of vegetation health so it is used as an ecosystem function proxy. The framework is presented as resistance and resilience together as measures of vegetation stability and the paper develops indicators of short-term vegetation resilience and resistance. The approach combines field observation via satellite remote sensing time series with modeling via fitting the ARx model and extracting metrics from model coefficients. The location is global and the paper states Location Global and uses global gridded datasets. The first author affiliation is KU Leuven in Belgium. The disturbance is short-term climate anomalies including drought and temperature anomalies and these are not experimentally manipulated so they are unmanipulated climatic disturbances. The disturbance is treated as ongoing anomalies across the full time series rather than discrete events so it is press. The observation window spans from July 1981 to December 2006 which is 25 years and 6 months or 9280 days using 365 days per year and 30 days per month.

The abstract states that when the invasion operator has a positive eigenvalue then recovery and resistance to extinction resilience are implied which defines resilience as recovery plus resistance. In the seagrass case study the paper describes flooding in 2017 leading to poor light conditions and widespread decline in seagrass meadow health and then notes that many meadows returned to their prior cover within a number of years and that some have not fully recovered. The text explicitly links resilience to recovery time by stating that restoration is intended to improve the recovery time i.e. the resilience of the network when subject to disturbances in the future. The target variable used in that recovery description is seagrass cover because it says meadows returned to their prior cover and have not fully recovered. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event flooding in 2017 so it is a pulse and it is unmanipulated climatic. The monitoring duration is described as over a decade of monitoring and the disturbance and recovery window includes 2017 to as of 2022 so five years equals 1825 days. The approach for the resilience analysis is modeling and simulation because the persistence operator uses a connectivity matrix estimated using numerical modeling and the study applies SVD of the propagator. The first author affiliation is CQUniversity in Australia so the institution country is AU. The field system is Port Curtis and Rodds Bay in northeastern Australia so the field country is AU. The habitat is intertidal mudflats and coastal seagrass meadows in a harbor which fits shallow nearshore marine environment Marine.Neritic. The focal organism is the seagrass Zostera muelleri which is Plantae. Coordinates are not provided in the text so location and range are NA.

The methods state that phylogenetic diversity is used to assess resistance and resilience and that resistance and resilience are also assessed by Bray–Curtis dissimilarity values. For community resilience they compared Bray–Curtis dissimilarity values between Nature and ecological groups G S-G and T-S-G rather than tracking recovery through time after a defined disturbance. This makes resilience an inference from compositional dissimilarity relative to a reference state rather than an observed recovery trajectory. The introduction and results discuss resistance and resilience together and Figure 1 caption states the value in Soil indicates resistance and in ecological restoration it indicates resilience supporting the framework resistance + resilience. The target variable directly used for resilience is Bray–Curtis dissimilarity which is based on microbial community composition. The study is a 4-year field experiment with plots planted in June 2020 and soils sampled in July 2024 on Zeduo Mountain in Kangding County Sichuan Province China. The first author affiliation is Chengdu Institute of Biology Chinese Academy of Sciences in Chengdu China. No explicit WGS84 coordinates are provided so the location is approximated from the named site Kangding County Sichuan. The disturbance is described as infrastructure construction but it is not used as a quantified disturbance-response in the resilience metric so disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not explicitly defined.

Framework: resilience = size of the basin of attraction
Justification: The study states it used replicate laboratory populations of the budding yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae and reports direct observation of critical slowing down before population collapse. It defines ecological resilience as size of the basin of attraction and says loss of ecological resilience is manifested as the shrinking basin of attraction between the stable and the unstable fixed points. It experimentally mapped a bifurcation diagram by growing populations with daily dilutions and identifying stable and unstable fixed points from population density between subsequent days. It tested resilience by applying a salt shock of sodium chloride for 1 day and reports that populations at low dilution factors were able to recover whereas those at higher dilution factors went extinct. The quantification aligns with the capacity to recover without shifting to an alternative state and with the shrinking basin of attraction which is a threshold to extinction. The target variable used throughout the figures and analyses is population density in cells per microliter. The approach is a laboratory experiment with daily dilutions and imposed salt shock. The first author affiliation is Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge Massachusetts USA. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work is laboratory based. The disturbance includes a manipulated chemical perturbation sodium chloride salt shock and a manipulated resource related mortality pressure via daily dilution into fresh media. The salt shock is applied for 1 day which is treated as a pulse. Population density time series shown and described span day 1 to day 6 so the observation duration is 6 days.

The paper states that it estimated social-ecological resilience at the county level using an indicator evaluation system built from 12 indicators including DEM meteorological data NPP NDVI population GDP soil erosion nighttime light and land ratios. It explicitly says direct measurement of resilience is difficult and therefore resilience-related indicators need to be selected for evaluation. Social-ecological resilience is computed by linear weighted regression of the 12 standardized indicators to obtain SER for each county. This means resilience is inferred from proxy indicators rather than measured as recovery dynamics of a state variable after disturbance. The study discusses resilience conceptually with stability and thresholds but does not present an explicit equation style framework that includes resilience. The analysis uses multi-source remote sensing and statistical datasets for China and reports results for 547 coal-distributed counties. The first author affiliation is Zhejiang University Hangzhou China. The study scope spans China with many regions and multiple environments so the dominant habitat type cannot be reduced to a single IUCN category. Because measurement is inferred the disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery are not applicable.

Approach: Modeling and simulation; Field experiment; Field observation
Justification: The study states that it used experimental and modeling approaches to investigate woody cover effects and their implications on ecological stability and resilience. The modeling framework quantifies system behavior as normalized woody plant cover Sn and identifies equilibrium states under varying background minimum temperature Tb and browsing pressure c. The discussion states that browsing affects the basin of attraction hence the resilience or the capacity of the system to withstand disturbances without changing the basin of attraction. The results describe critical thresholds in Tb where the system shifts between stable states and report that a small increase in minimum temperature can induce critical transitions from grass to woody plant dominance and that the model predicts a threshold temperature of -7C for mangrove salt marsh ecotones. This corresponds to a threshold limit beyond which the original state cannot be maintained which matches latitude. The target variable used in the resilience related modeling is woody plant cover Sn. Field observations include paired temperature monitoring from November 2018 to March 2019 under shrub canopy and in adjacent grassland. Field experiment includes the seedling transplant experiment at Arroyo Hondo Preserve. Disturbances considered include climatic freezing and warming through Tb and Tmin and biotic browsing pressure c and observed browsing damage. The disturbance is treated as a continuous driver through background temperature and browsing pressure rather than a discrete event. The first author affiliation is University of California Berkeley in the United States. Field sites are in California USA and the model is also applied to the Atlantic coast of Florida USA. Coordinates are given for Arroyo Hondo Preserve 34.78N 120.23W and Point Reyes National Seashore 37.92N 122.73W and the reported location is their average. The monitoring period from November 2018 to March 2019 gives 135 days between first and last measurements.

The study quantified resilience from field data on ant communities sampled across a chronosequence of sites with 1 to 34 years of regeneration time and compared to old-growth forest. The paper explicitly defines resilience as a recovery rate and states that resilience is the rate of recovery relative to its loss and that the slope for regeneration time depicts the resilience. The framework is presented as two independent components resistance and resilience and the study compares them directly. Resilience was quantified using linear mixed-effects models on standardized similarity values and extracted slopes for regeneration time. The target variables directly used for resilience quantification were community properties including species richness and species composition similarity to old-growth forest. The biological level is community because the quantified properties are community species richness and community composition. The approach is field observation because ants were collected in plots at Reserva Río Canandé using Winkler extraction hand sampling and tree trunk sampling. The first author affiliation is Technical University of Darmstadt in Germany. The field data were collected in Ecuador at Reserva Río Canandé with coordinates given as 0.5263 N and 79.2129 W. The disturbance is forest conversion to agriculture described as deforestation and subsequent agriculture and treated as land use legacy with ongoing agriculture defined as regeneration time zero which indicates a continuous press context rather than a discrete pulse recovery experiment. Observation duration spans from the start of agricultural use within the last 50 years to the oldest regeneration age of 34 years which totals 34 years and equals 12410 days.

The study states it used Integrated Population Modelling along with Bayesian population forecasting and resilience analysis to evaluate demographic resilience. It defines two key components of population resilience as recovery time and population inertia and refers to Figure 1 for these metrics. Recovery time is quantified as the number of years required for the breeder population to again reach its size from 2021. The target variable used for resilience metrics is the breeder population size expressed as number of breeding pairs and compared between scenarios with and without outbreak years. The disturbance is HPAIV H5N1 outbreaks described as a wildlife disease panzootic affecting the population during 2021 to 2024 and coinciding with declines in survival and breeding pairs. The disturbance is not experimentally manipulated and is treated through scenario comparison and projections that include outbreak years versus pre outbreak dynamics. The disturbance is considered over multiple consecutive years with duration annotated as T = 4 years in Figure 4 which indicates a sustained period rather than a single discrete event. Data sources include mark resight recovery data and breeding pair counts and productivity surveys collected in the Netherlands indicating field observation. The projections and resilience metrics are produced by simulating future population dynamics until 2034 indicating modeling and simulation. The first author affiliation is Swiss Ornithological Institute in Sempach Switzerland. The study area is stated to cover the entire Netherlands but no coordinates are provided. Habitat is described as lowlands coasts and urban farmland mosaics indicating multiple habitat types. The observation window for demographic estimation spans mark resight recovery 1993 to 2024 which is 42 years or 15330 days.

The paper defines resilience as the capacity to absorb disturbance and retain structure and function and it then tests two components described as resistance to outbreak and post-outbreak successional trajectories. Resistance is quantified directly from observed survival outcomes of susceptible lodgepole pine using tree-scale survival probability and stand-scale survival proportion of susceptible density and basal area. The disturbance is a severe mountain pine beetle outbreak that occurred in the early 2000s and is treated as a discrete outbreak event with post-outbreak measurement about eight years later. Data were collected by field measurements in July through August 2018 in plots at the Fraser Experimental Forest in Colorado USA. The study location is given as 39 degrees 53 minutes north and 105 degrees 53 minutes west which converts to N39.8833 W105.8833. The first author affiliation is the University of Washington in the United States. The focal variables for resistance are survival probability and survival proportion which are demographic outcomes for lodgepole pine and thus correspond to a population level target. The observation window for disturbance and resilience spans reconstructed pre-outbreak 2004 to post-outbreak 2018 which is 14 years or 5110 days.

The paper develops and applies an ecosystem functional stability metric defined as the ratio of average T to F dissimilarity and it is described as a core indicator for evaluating aquatic ecosystem stability. Resilience is not directly quantified from recovery dynamics after a defined disturbance and the study instead parameterizes stability using functional variability and taxonomic variability and EFS derived from dissimilarity matrices. The introduction states ecosystem stability can be defined multidimensionally by several metrics such as resilience resistance and recovery. Box 1 discusses resistance and recovery trajectories and states that interconnected multi level stable states mediate ecological resilience but the methods quantify EFS rather than measuring recovery rate time or degree after disturbance. The approach is a large scale field survey and a temporal survey with in situ measurements and sequencing. The first author affiliation is Tianjin University in China. The location is China with a stated spatial range from 21.105 N to 45.767 N and from 99.647 E to 126.592 E and the provided coordinate is the midpoint of these bounds. The temporal sampling is described but the task requires NA for observation duration because measurement is inferred. Wetland is selected as the major habitat because the only explicitly named focal site for the temporal survey is the Qilihai Wetland and wetlands are repeatedly highlighted in results and discussion.

The introduction defines resilience as the capacity of an ecological system to return to the pre-disturbance condition. The methods state that they evaluated ground-layer resilience to fire by assessing temporal changes in community structure composition and species populations after fire and compared it with the pre-fire condition and with unburned plots. Data collection occurred before the experimental fires in April 2015 and again after the fire in April 2016 which was 8 months after prescribed burning with additional bimonthly sampling in burned plots for 8 months after fire. The results state that eight months after fire species abundance did not differ from pre-disturbance values for 86% of the species demonstrating resilience and that the system quickly recovered the pre-fire state. The quantified variables used in the core analyses include species richness density species abundance community turnover metrics and percent ground cover. The disturbance is prescribed fire applied to burned plots in winter 2015 June which is a manipulated fire event. The study area is Santa Bárbara Ecological Station in Southeastern Brazil with coordinates given as 22°46ʹ33ʺ to 22°50ʹ3ʺS and 49°10ʹ27ʺ to 49°15ʹ36ʺW and the vegetation is described as a grass-dominated savanna campo cerrado. The first author affiliation is Universidade Estadual de Campinas in Brazil.

The paper defines resilience as the capacity of an ecosystem to recover from disturbance and evaluates it using repeated vegetation surveys in paired fenced and unfenced plots. It states that they monitored plant species composition during the first 5 years following land use exclusion and evaluated resilience as the rate of change of vegetation towards the primary forest considered as the reference ecosystem. Resilience is quantified from fenced plot change minus unfenced plot change using Sørensen distance Bray Curtis distance and differences in aggregate cover of woody and grass growth forms. This uses observed time series data from 2014 to 2019 and directly measures recovery trajectories relative to a reference. The framework is not expressed as an equation combining resilience with other components and resilience is treated as a standalone concept. The target variables used for resilience are floristic composition dissimilarity and growth form cover differences. These variables describe plant communities rather than individuals or single populations. The approach is a field experiment because fencing excludes logging and livestock grazing and trampling and it is also field observation because vegetation is monitored over time. The first author affiliation is in Córdoba Argentina. The study system is in Chancani central Argentina with coordinates given as about 31 degrees 15 to 31 degrees 44 south and 65 degrees 16 to 65 degrees 40 west and the average is reported. The habitat is the Gran Chaco forest described as an open xerophytic forest. The disturbance removed by fencing is land use involving logging and livestock grazing and trampling which correspond to biological resource use and biotic disturbance and it is treated as ongoing until exclusion which is a press disturbance. The monitoring spans 5 years which is 1825 days.

The introduction states that a type II perturbation stresses the community and that a rapid recovery after such a stress is a measure of the resilience of the system which defines the framework as resilience = recovery. The methods state that once constant population levels were established the systems were frozen to -1C for 7 d and sampling continued after the freeze for up to 100 d which is a manipulated pulse climatic disturbance. Resilience is quantified from the system response after freezing using recovery dynamics including time to return to prefreeze equilibrium such as bacteria took longer to recover 17 d and A avenae was 67 d before populations recovered and by recovery indexes based on deviations from prestress level. Autocorrelation is not used and the recovery index combines deviation and time so the closest quantification category is recovery rate. The target variables used for resilience quantification are population abundance and ammonium nitrogen and carbon dioxide as nutrient cycling measures. These variables are measured repeatedly in microcosms which makes the measurement observed and the approach an indoor experiment. The first author affiliation is Broadlands Long Ashton Bristol England which is GB. The soil is from northeastern Colorado and organisms include UCR sources and University of Georgia so the field country is US and the habitat source is shortgrass prairie soil which is grassland. No coordinates are provided for the soil location so location and range are NA.

Framework: pressure-state-response = pressure resilience + state resilience + response resilience
Justification: The paper states it developed an index system for evaluating resilience to rural disasters based on the PSR model and calculated a Rural Disaster Resilience Index DRI by weighted summation. The PSR structure is explicitly described as pressure state response and Table 1 groups indicators into pressure resilience state resilience and response resilience. Resilience is quantified as an index constructed from static and spatial indicators including GDP electricity consumption medical assistance capability slope rainfall NDVI based vegetation coverage and other variables rather than direct recovery trajectories of an ecosystem state variable after a disturbance. The methods describe AHP entropy weighting with expert scoring and entropy correction and then DRI calculation which matches inferred resilience from proxy indicators. Although NDVI is included it is used as one indicator within the index system and not analyzed as a time series recovery metric. The study area is the upper reaches of the Minjiang River and the centroid coordinates are reported as 103.407 E and 31.946 N for 2020. The first author affiliation is Chengdu University of Technology in China. Data sources include multiple land use types and the discussion reports resilience rankings across forest wetland grassland farmland other and urban ecosystems indicating multiple habitats. Because the measurement is inferred the disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience response are not applicable under the instructions.

The study uses four periods of Landsat remote sensing images from 2007 2013 2017 and 2021 to build land use transfer matrices and networks which are observational data. Resilience is discussed as maintaining resilience of the land ecosystem and enhancing the resilience of the ecosystem but no explicit equation framework defining resilience is provided. Resilience related analysis is performed via a cascading failure model where land use types are attacked by scaling down their area with attack intensity from 5 to 100 percent and the response is quantified using network efficiency and network eco efficiency. Network efficiency and network eco efficiency are network level metrics so the target variable type is a regional network parameter. The disturbance is an intentional attack representing land use conversion damage so it is a modelled landuse and infrastructure development disturbance. The attack is applied as discrete scenarios with defined intensities rather than a continuous driver so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The study area is the southeastern part of Chengdu Pidu District Sichuan Province China with coordinates 30 45 13 to 30 47 7 N and 103 59 9 to 104 3 5 E so the average coordinate is reported and the range is under two degrees. The first author affiliation is Chengdu University of Technology Chengdu China. The observation window spans from 2007 to 2021 which is 14 years or 5110 days using 365 days per year. Habitat includes multiple land use types including forest land grassland dryland paddy river and urban residential land so the dominant habitat type cannot be single and is coded as multi. No organism taxon is the subject because the analysis is on land ecosystem network properties rather than species.

The study used 10 years of English wheat yield data and derived three resilience metrics from the yield time series which makes the measurement observed. The paper defines crop yield resilience as maintaining yields despite environmental perturbation and states resilience has multiple facets derived from the capacity to resist recover from and adapt to environmental change. The metrics include yield stability which incorporates the aspects of resistance and recovery and a resistance metric based on the 2012 extreme rainfall event. Resilience was quantified using relative yield across the time series yield stability around a moving average and resistance to a specific event which does not match a single listed quantification option so it is coded as other. The target variable used to quantify resilience is wheat yield which is an ecosystem function in this context. The analysis level is ecosystem because yield is a system level functional output irrespective of species identity. The approach is field observation because it uses a national survey of farms with annual yields aggregated to 10 km by 10 km grid cells. The first author affiliation is in Wallingford UK so the institution country is GB. The yield data are from English farms so the field country is GB. The disturbance is exceptionally heavy spring and summer rainfall in 2012 so it is unmanipulated climatic and treated as a discrete event. The observation duration spans 2008 to 2017 which is 10 years or 3650 days. The dominant habitat context is arable agricultural landscapes and the focal organism is wheat which is plantae. Coordinates are not provided in the text beyond being located to 1 km so location and range are NA.

The study states it analyzed long term data collected from 1991 to 2012 on physicochemical variables and the structure of the phytoplankton community and it used NMDS and diversity indices to analyze changes with respect to recovery over time. The disturbance is described as highway construction in the watershed beginning in 1996 and completed in 2005 which was associated with input of anthropogenic pollutants and significant alteration in water quality and increased eutrophication. Resilience is discussed alongside resistance in the introduction with the phrase resisting damage and the text also equates resilience with recovering to a new state of balance similar to pre disturbance status. Recovery time is explicitly quantified with the statement it took approximately 5 years for the reservoir to recover to a state quasi similar to that pre disturbance on the basis of diversity indices. The target variable used for that recovery time estimate is phytoplankton community diversity indices H and MI. The approach is field observation because water and phytoplankton sampling took place monthly at the reservoir sampling site and the study uses those observations. The first author affiliation is Academia Sinica in Taipei Taiwan. The study site is Feitsui Reservoir in Taiwan with coordinates given as N24 54 prime and E121 34 prime. The habitat is a freshwater reservoir which fits the wetland category. The taxon is phytoplankton. The disturbance is unmanipulated landuse and infrastructure development because the highway construction occurred in the watershed and was analyzed as the disturbance. The disturbance pattern is press because the construction and associated pollutant input persisted across 1996 to 2005. Observation duration is from 1991 to 2012 which is 21 years or 7665 days using 365 days per year.

The study states that it quantifies how forests resist drought events and recover after them and determines their growth resilience. It uses a large tree ring database and selected 775 tree ring width chronologies with years of extremely dry conditions defined as drought. For every dry year and site it calculated three indices representing components of growth resilience to drought namely resistance recovery and resilience. The indices are computed from observed mean tree ring width during the dry year and mean growth during the three years before and after the dry year. Resilience Rs is defined as Gpost divided by Gprev which compares pre drought and post drought growth values and measures the capacity to recover the growth rates observed before the drought stress. This makes the measurement observed because it uses the system growth time series around drought events. The framework is explicitly presented as growth resilience decomposed into resistance and recovery with resilience also calculated alongside them. The quantification for resilience is a pre versus post comparison of growth which corresponds to recovery degree. The target variable used in the indices is tree growth quantified as mean tree ring width which is a growth variable. The chronologies represent average growth series of trees of the same species at the same site so the level is population. Drought years are identified from climate data and treated as single drought events so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic and the pattern is pulse. The period considered for chronologies is 1948 to 2008 and drought events are analysed during 1949 to 2008 so the observation window spans 7 years plus 60 years which is 2555 days. The first author affiliation is in Zaragoza Spain. The location is North America and Europe with conterminous USA and Europe mentioned but specific coordinates are not provided in the text excerpt. The results and figures describe forest sites and tree species so the habitat is forest and the taxon is plants.

Framework: resilience = frequency + intensity + type of disturbance an ecosystem can withstand while still returning to its original state
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the frequency and intensity and type of disturbance an ecosystem can withstand while still returning to its original predisturbance state. The study quantified compound disturbance effects using observed stand scale and stem scale structural and demographic variables measured in the field. The methods state that impact and early response were quantified 1 year after the wildfire and field assessments were undertaken 1 year after the fire. Stand scale response metrics were change in quadratic mean diameter and stem density and live canopy height calculated as change from all trees to live trees after the fire. Stem scale response metrics were probability of stem survival and proportion of the stem resprouting analyzed with mixed effects models. These quantify how much the system deviated after disturbance rather than time to recovery because measurements were taken at one postfire time point. The study area is the Northern Jarrah Forest in southwestern Australia with coordinates given as 30.8 to 33.5 S and 115.8 to 117.8 E and the first author affiliation is Curtin University in Western Australia Australia. Disturbances were an acute drought and heatwave in 2010 to 2011 followed by a lightning ignited wildfire on 6 January 2016 and the study treats these as discrete events.

The study assesses ecosystem health using the Vigor Organization Resilience model and states that ecosystem health is based on three aspects including resilience. Resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics but from ecosystem elasticity using coefficients assigned to land use and land cover types with Resilience Coefficient and Resistance Coefficient values and the equation ER = 0.7×Resil + 0.3×Resist. Vigor is assessed using NDVI as related to primary productivity and organization is assessed using landscape metrics such as SHDI SHEI AWMFDI IJI DIVISION and CONTAG and PARFAC. Because resilience is constructed from static LULC based coefficients rather than observed recovery after a disturbance the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The approach is field observation because the core data are Landsat satellite images for 2000 2010 and 2019. The first author affiliation is University of Gour Banga Malda West Bengal India. The study area is Kolkata Metropolitan Area India with coordinates given as 22•00∕19∕’N to 23•00∕01∕’N and 88•00∕04∕’E to 88•00∕33∕’E and the reported location is the midpoint. The habitat is a rapidly urbanizing metropolitan area and the analysis emphasizes built up expansion so the dominant habitat type is urban. Taxon is Plantae because vigor is based on vegetation index and vegetation cover is a core LULC class used in the resilience related coefficients.

The study used a chronosequence design to collect data on herbaceous and woody cover at unburned reference plots and plots that burned between 1972 and 2010 and it analysed years post fire classes up to 21 to 40 years post fire. In the data analysis the authors state that resilience in vegetation structure and the potential for the development of a grass fire cycle could be measured using the log ratio of woody to herbaceous cover and total woody and herbaceous cover and total and relative cover of invasive annual grass. These are direct observations of vegetation cover used to evaluate whether burned plots were similar to unburned reference conditions after decades which matches observed resilience measurement. Resilience is discussed alongside resistance in the introduction and predictions including the phrase low resilience of native desert vegetation and low resistance to invasion and the paper does not define a higher level variable that combines them. The results and discussion interpret resilience as whether woody cover and the woody herbaceous ratio return to levels similar to unburned sites by 40 years post fire depending on burn severity which is recovery degree relative to reference conditions. The target variables used for resilience measures are cover variables including woody cover herbaceous cover and invasive grass cover. These cover measures are community level because they aggregate across multiple plant species grouped into woody and herbaceous categories and invasive grasses. The approach is field observation because vegetation was sampled in 807 plots and burn severity was estimated from Landsat derived dNBR without experimental manipulation. The first author affiliation is U S Geological Survey in California USA. The study region is the Mojave Desert USA and the plots were within the Mojave Desert in the southwestern United States. The disturbance is fire treated as unmanipulated because plots burned historically and were sampled after burning. The disturbance pattern is pulse because fires are discrete events and the analysis uses years post fire intervals. The observation duration spans from the earliest fire year 1972 to the latest sampling year 2013 which is 41 years or 14965 days.

The study states that it applied a suite of 20 indicators of resilience of tree secondary growth and that Basal Area Increment was calculated as a proxy of secondary tree growth from dendrochronological time series. Table 1 defines RS resilience as capacity to reach pre disturbance performance levels and as a ratio between performance after and before disturbance and it also lists resistance and recovery indices which the Discussion describes as integrating resistance resilience and recovery capacity. The resilience quantification is therefore based on recovery to pre disturbance performance levels which matches recovery degree. The disturbance events used for resilience calculations are described as major periods of drought detected in each decade in 1980 to 2019 corresponding to summers of 1982 1999 2006 and 2015 and the study also measured an extreme summer heatwave event in late June 2019. These are unmanipulated climatic disturbances and they are treated as discrete events which matches a pulse pattern. The study also quantifies herbivore damage by roe deer and wild boar and discusses recruitment limitation by increased herbivory pressure which is an unmanipulated biotic disturbance. The approach is field observation because it used field sensors transects and tree cores and analysed observed time series. The study area is within the Natural Park of Sant Llorenc del Munt i lObac in Catalonia NE Iberian Peninsula Spain and the first author affiliation is University of Barcelona Barcelona Spain. The location is not given as coordinates so it is approximated from the named park location near Barcelona. The observation window for resilience indices spans the dendrochronological series shown as 1960 to 2019 which is 59 years or 21535 days.

The introduction defines resilience as the ability to resist or recover from disturbance and further decomposes it into resistance and recovery. Resistance is operationalized in the methods as whether points reburned at high severity with high severity defined as greater than 75% canopy mortality and the response variable high_severity is a binary indicator of whether a point burned at high severity in the second fire. The study uses observational data from a natural experiment of the 2000 Storrie Fire and the 2012 Chips Fire plus LiDAR and field plots rather than inferred proxies. The main resilience quantification used in the core models is resistance to high severity reburn which is a measure of deviation avoidance after disturbance. The target variable for this resistance analysis is burn severity class based on percent canopy cover mortality derived from RdNBR and classified into low or moderate versus high severity. The study system is vegetation communities and forest versus early seral stages so the level is community. The approach is field observation because it leverages LiDAR remote sensing and field plot data without experimental manipulation. The first author affiliation is University of California Berkeley in the United States. The study area is on the Plumas and Lassen National Forests in the northern Sierra Nevada of California United States and the inset map indicates northern California. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to northern Sierra Nevada near the Plumas and Lassen National Forests and the range is set to 0 because a single average coordinate is reported. The disturbance is wildfire and it is unmanipulated because the fires are described as a natural experiment. The disturbance pattern is pulse because the analysis concerns discrete fires in 2000 and 2012 with a reburn interval. Observation duration spans from the initial fire in August 2000 to the last measurement time used for recovery indicators in September 2015 LiDAR which is 5479 days.

The study states that it used data collected prior to during and after a severe growing season drought to compare resilience. It quantified resilience using aboveground net primary productivity ANPP measured by harvesting biomass from quadrats at peak biomass and drying and weighing it to estimate ANPP. It defined resilience as recovery to average or higher than average functioning following drought and operationalized this as drought legacy effect calculated as ANPP 2019 minus pre drought average ANPP 2014 to 2017 divided by the pre drought average. The disturbance was a natural growing season drought in 2018 described with Palmer Drought Severity Index values and analyzed as a discrete drought year followed by a post drought year. The work combined a long term restoration experiment with soil heterogeneity manipulations and unmanipulated control plots in two remnant prairie experiments so the approach includes field experiment and field observation. The site is Konza Prairie Biological Station in Riley County Kansas with coordinates given as 39.1405 N and 96.1435 W. The first author affiliation is University of Kansas in the United States. The focal system is tallgrass prairie dominated by perennial grasses and forbs and the resilience target variable is ecosystem productivity ANPP which is an ecosystem function derived from plant biomass.

The study states that it uses portfolio theory to characterize resilience and it evaluates portfolio performance using the Sharpe ratio defined as mean portfolio return divided by the standard deviation of portfolio variance. The data used are salmon total run and spawner abundance time series assembled into watershed portfolios and analysed for 1985 to 2005. Resilience is not quantified as recovery time recovery rate recovery degree resistance latitude or invariability after a defined disturbance and instead is treated as reliability risk adjusted performance based on variance and covariance of returns. The paper links higher performance to lower anthropogenic impact such as dams and land use change but does not quantify resilience as a response to a specific disturbance event. The first author affiliation listed is University of Washington in the United States. Salmon data are described as coming from California Washington Alaska and the Canadian Yukon and British Columbia indicating the United States and Canada. The study spans western North America across many watersheds and does not provide coordinates in the provided text. The focal organisms are Pacific salmon Oncorhynchus spp which are animals. The target variable used in the Sharpe ratio is salmon return or spawner abundance which is an abundance type variable aggregated as a portfolio level ecosystem service metric. The approach is based on compiled observational monitoring data rather than experiments or simulations.

The study develops an integrative coupling index and integrated rate of change indices by combining multiple socioeconomic and biophysical indicators in a coupling model and it uses these indices to assess transient social ecological dynamics and decoupling. Resilience is not quantified as recovery time recovery rate recovery degree resistance latitude or invariability of a focal state variable after a defined disturbance and instead is discussed conceptually such as loss of resilience inferred from maximum rates of change and as part of sustainability and critical transition framing. The methods quantify rates of change using generalized additive models and then compute integrated indices using entropy weighted integration and a coupling model where CI is the ratio of ecological degradation rate to socioeconomic development rate. Because resilience is not directly measured from system recovery dynamics after a disturbance and is instead inferred from proxy indicators and constructed indices the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The core analysis focuses on socioeconomic and coupled system indices rather than a single organismal variable so the target variable type is socio eco parameter and the level is landscape. The approach is modeling and simulation because the main quantification uses numerical and model analyses including GAM based RoC estimation and a coupling model. The first author affiliation is in Nanjing Institute of Geography and Limnology Chinese Academy of Sciences in China. The field data are from Lake Taihu watershed China with the coring site in Meiliang Bay at N31 28 52 E120 10 44 converted to decimal degrees. The study area is a freshwater lake and watershed so the dominant habitat type is wetland.

The introduction states that the study tests which fungi “recover quickly from this environmental perturbation” and the results quantify response to benomyl by changes in AM fungal sequence type abundance and frequency in treated versus control monoliths. The methods describe a field manipulation where monoliths were “raised regularly for immersion in a bath of fungicide” with treatments “applied monthly from 18 March 1997 to 18 June 1998” at Pretty Wood near York UK. Resilience is operationalized in the results as “Sequence types with wide host range were on average more resilient to this treatment” based on their persistence and increased abundance under benomyl relative to controls which is resistance to the imposed chemical perturbation rather than a measured return time. The target variable used for this resilience statement is the AM fungal community response measured as clone based relative abundance and presence or absence of diagnostic tRFLP fragments which are community composition and interaction network related measures. The disturbance is manipulated chemical because benomyl fungicide is applied to disrupt AM fungi. The disturbance is press because benomyl was applied repeatedly across most of the study period with no defined recovery interval. Observation duration is from first treatment 18 March 1997 to last harvest 20 July 1998. The habitat is a natural woodland under a mature canopy of Quercus petraea and Acer pseudoplatanus. The first author affiliation is the University of York in the United Kingdom. The field location is Pretty Wood near York with grid reference SE 732 867 which corresponds to approximately N53.983 W1.050.

The paper defines stability as what Leigh and Holling call resilience and contrasts it with steadiness which refers to numerical fluctuations under less disturbed conditions. Resilience is defined as the ability of the system or community species to persist in time given a major natural perturbation and the author states interest is whether the population will go extinct or remain in the game. The resilience test uses a natural perturbation in Panama described as the introduction of a predatory fish Cichla ocellaris. The results are based on fish census data from time distance counts repeated from 1972 to 1976 and figures show changes in prey fish abundances and local extermination outcomes. Resilience is quantified by whether species persist versus local extermination with 13 of 17 common diurnal fishes exterminated locally at the Cuipo station in the lake and none disappearing from the Chagres River. This corresponds to resistance because it is the ability to deviate less from the original state after disturbance and the river shows minimal changes and no local extermination while the lake shows strong deviation and extermination. The target variable used for resilience is persistence or extinction of fish species which is a demographic outcome at the community fauna level. The study is field observation because it uses repeated censuses in natural lake and river stations and the perturbation is inadvertent rather than experimentally imposed. The first author affiliation is University of Washington Seattle Washington USA. The field system is the Chagres River and Gatun Lake basin in Panama and the map and text place the work in the Panama Canal area. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to the Gatun Lake and Barro Colorado Island area. The disturbance is biotic and unmanipulated because it is an introduced piscivore acting through predation in the field and the study treats Cichla as present throughout the 5 year period at several stations. The disturbance is press because Cichla is described as always present for the duration of the 5 year study at BCI and Chagres stations and effects are evaluated over years without a defined end. The observation window spans 1972 to 1976 which is 5 years.

The study used data from 46 experiments that manipulated grassland plant diversity and measured productivity across Europe and North America. It classified years as extremely dry moderately dry normal moderately wet or extremely wet using SPEI and defined climate extremes as events occurring less frequently than once per decade. It quantified resistance and resilience using proportional changes in productivity from one year to the next within each plot for each observed climate event. Resilience was defined as a rate of return towards normal productivity levels after a climate event using Yn Ye and Ye+1. Ecosystem stability was quantified as the ratio of the mean to the standard deviation of productivity across years and the paper states biodiversity increased ecosystem stability and then quantified resistance and resilience as its components. The target variable used in the resilience equations was ecosystem productivity measured as g m-2 yr-1. Climate events were not experimentally imposed but identified from historical water balance so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic. The recovery interval was the year after a climate event and the paper also tested resilience 2 years after climate events. The first author affiliation is University of Minnesota Twin Cities in the United States. The field countries include the United States Germany Ireland Poland Spain and the Czech Republic as named in the experiments list in the text. The habitat is grassland plant communities. The observation window for resilience spans from the climate event year to the year after which is one year and the paper also reports analyses using two years after events.

The study defines drought resilience using remotely sensed vegetation indices and ground plot demography and it states that it used satellite indices of photosynthetic capacity EVI and activity GOSIF and analysed vegetation index anomalies during drought. It explicitly defines drought resilience as a forest’s ability to increase or relatively better maintain photosynthetic capacity or activity during a perturbation by more positive or less negative anomalies in vegetation indices during drought and it notes this can also be characterized as resistance. The resilience metric is therefore based on observed EVI and GOSIF anomalies during drought rather than on a proxy constructed from static attributes. The disturbance is drought defined from MCWD anomalies for the 2005 2010 and 2015 to 2016 events and it is not experimentally manipulated. The droughts are treated as discrete events with defined onset and end months and drought duration is calculated within hydrological years which matches a pulse framing. The main resilience target variable is photosynthetic function inferred from vegetation indices described as proxies for photosynthetic capacity or activity and linked to GPP which fits ecosystem function. The analysis combines field observation via remote sensing and ground plots with statistical modelling using GAM and SCM. The first author affiliation is the University of Arizona in the United States. The field data are from the Amazon basin including plot sites along the BR-319 road between Manaus and Porto Velho and other pan Amazon sites and the paper is about Amazonia which is in Brazil. The only explicit coordinates provided are the BR-319 range from S5.9 W62.5 to S4.4 W60.9 so the average is S5.15 W61.70 and the farthest point differs by 0.75 latitude degrees and 0.90 longitude degrees giving 1.65. The observation window for the remote sensing record is stated as from 2000 to 2020 so the duration is 20 years or 7300 days and the EVI and GOSIF period used is 2001 to 2019 which is 19 years or 6935 days and the drought definition uses 2000 to 2020 so 7300 days is used.

The paper develops the Bioclimatic Ecosystem Resilience Index which assesses the capacity of ecosystems to retain biological diversity under climate change. BERI is calculated from habitat condition and modeled compositional turnover and connectedness to surrounding habitat projected to support similar species composition under climate change. The method uses correlative compositional turnover models and climate scenarios rather than observing recovery dynamics of an ecosystem state variable after disturbance. Habitat condition is used as an input filter and resilience is represented by connectedness of compositionally similar habitat under scenarios. The framework section does not define resilience as a component of a larger equation and instead describes an indicator focusing on one aspect of ecosystem resilience. The example application uses the Moist Tropical Forest biome but the modelling framework and datasets are global and results can be aggregated to countries or ecoregions. The first author affiliation is CSIRO in Australia. Because the resilience quantification is based on modeled connectivity and compositional similarity across landscapes the level is landscape and the target variable type aligns with interaction network style connectedness.
