Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because the measurement is inferred from modeled indicators rather than observed disturbance response.

The study quantified resilience using measured soil moisture and precipitation data from three sites and three land use types with repeated measurements across years. The Methods define resilience as Ri = WUg/Pg and state that higher variation of WU during the growing season indicates higher soil moisture resilience. Resistance is defined as Rs based on the magnitude of change between soil moisture at the beginning and end of the growing season. Soil moisture stability is explicitly defined as SSM = 0.691Ri + 1.375Rs and described as mainly reflected by resistance and resilience. The disturbance context is precipitation variability because the study compares dry wet and normal years and analyzes sensitivity to precipitation. The disturbance is not experimentally imposed and is treated as ongoing climatic variation across sites and years. Soil moisture was sampled at the beginning and end of the growing season and precipitation was used for growing season calculations. The observation window for the precipitation year comparison is 2017 to 2019 which is three years. The first author affiliation is Lanzhou University in China and the field sites are in Gansu province China with coordinates given for Tianshui Quzi and Shishe.

The study states that optimized networks are less resilient and that resilience is measured as the maximum real part of the eigenvalues of the community matrix and as a longer recovery timescale from perturbations. This is a direct dynamical stability based measure derived from the modeled system response and therefore the measurement is observed within a simulation. The paper does not define resilience as part of an explicit equation style framework and it discusses stability and persistence separately while focusing on resilience as a property of optimized stationary networks. The quantification corresponds to recovery rate because the eigenvalue based metric and the stated recovery on a longer timescale describe the rate of return after perturbations. The resilience metric is computed from the community matrix which is derived from the interaction network and thus the target variable type is interaction. The system is a mutualistic community of plants and pollinators and the resilience is a community level property. The methods describe controlled numerical experiments Monte Carlo simulations and analytical calculations so the approach is modeling and simulation. The first author affiliation is Universita di Padova in Italy so the institution country is IT. No empirical field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work uses simulated networks and dynamics so field country and location are NA. The disturbance is a modeled perturbation to the stationary system and the optimization involves rewiring links which is a structural manipulation in the model so the disturbance type is model structural. The perturbations are treated as events after which recovery timescale is compared so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The text does not provide a start and end time span for the perturbation response measurements so observation duration is NA.

The study defines resilience as recovery when conditions improve by stating that a population must absorb drought resistance and recover fully if conditions improve resilience. It quantifies resistance and resilience by estimating temporal trends in reporting rates of terrestrial birds during extended drought 1998 to 2009 and in the post drought period 2010 to 2013. Reporting rates are derived from Atlas of Australian Birds survey data and are modelled as the probability of observing a species during a single survey with annual trends estimated using a logistic hierarchical Bayesian model. Resilience is operationalized as species that declined during the drought but had positive temporal trends in the post drought period. This uses observed time series data of species reporting rates rather than proxy indices. The framework is presented as resistance and resilience in parallel with explicit definitions for each. The quantification corresponds to recovery rate because it uses annual trend slopes in the post drought period rather than time to recovery or degree of return. The target variable is species reporting rate which is an occurrence based abundance proxy and is analysed per species across surveys. The biological level is population because reporting rates represent population level occurrence for each species. The approach is field observation because it uses standardized field surveys from BirdLife Australia. The first author affiliation is Monash University in Australia. The field data are from the Murray Darling Basin Australia and the figure shows the basin wide distribution so a single coordinate is not provided and the average is approximated at S25.0 E135.0 with range coded as 0 because no explicit coordinate spread is given. The dominant habitat referenced for the focal comparison is floodplain forest and adjacent upland ecosystems so Forest is selected. The taxon is birds which are Animalia. The disturbance is the Big Dry drought which is an unmanipulated climatic disturbance treated as a prolonged period so it is a press disturbance. The observation window spans January 1998 to December 2013 which is 16 years or 5840 days using 365 days per year.

The study imposed a simulated drought using rainout shelters for 41 d between 1 June and 13 July 2015 and measured soil functions before the shelters were erected in May and immediately after in mid July and again eight weeks after in mid September to determine recovery. The Methods state that resilience comprises resistance to and recovery from a perturbation and that these were calculated using the metrics presented by Orwin and Wardle 2004. Resistance was calculated in July by comparing the roofed drought treatment to the unroofed control. Recovery was calculated by comparing values in September with those obtained shortly after the roofs were removed in July and the recovery metric uses differences between drought and control values. These calculations use observed soil function variables including ecosystem respiration mineralization nitrification and nutrient concentrations and therefore the measurement is observed. The resilience framework is explicitly given as resilience comprises resistance to and recovery. The resilience metric is based on the difference between drought and control values after the recovery period relative to the initial difference at the end of drought which corresponds to recovery degree rather than recovery rate or recovery time. The response variables used for resilience include CO2 fluxes decomposition and nutrient cycling and availability which are ecosystem functions. The experiment is described as a 3 yr field experiment on degraded ex arable land at Winklebury Hill Wiltshire southern England with coordinates 50.5988 N 2.0709 E. The first author affiliation is The University of Manchester United Kingdom. The drought is a manipulated climatic disturbance and it is treated as a discrete event with a defined start and stop. The observation window for disturbance and resilience spans from May 2015 to September 2015 which is about 120 d.

The study defines return time as the time it takes a system to recover to its reference state once a perturbation dissipates or is removed and states that return time is the inverse of resilience or elasticity. Sensitivity and return time were calculated from observed dynamics of total chlorophyll a in pulsed mesocosms compared to reference distributions derived from pre pulse dynamics or control mesocosms. The nutrient pulse was a one time addition of available nitrogen and phosphorus applied after the 4 August 1999 and 21 August 2000 sampling periods. Return time was estimated as the number of days chlorophyll concentrations were outside the uncertainty intervals using linear interpolation between sample dates. The experiments were field mesocosm experiments conducted in 1136 L cattle tanks in Hanover New Hampshire USA. The primary response variable was phytoplankton chlorophyll a which is used as an ecosystem level productivity indicator. The paper focuses on freshwater plankton communities and nutrient pulses in aquatic systems which fits Wetland as the closest IUCN habitat category among the provided options. The first author affiliation is Dartmouth College in the United States. The field site is Hanover New Hampshire and the coordinate provided is an approximate town center location because the paper gives no explicit coordinates. The disturbance is manipulated chemical because nutrients were added as Na2HPO4 and NH4NO3. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because it is described as a one time pulse and sampling increased for two weeks after each pulse. The observation window spans summers 1999 and 2000 and the paper reports both years so the duration is taken as one year between the first and last measurements across the study period.

The paper defines resilience as capacity of ecosystems to reorganize and regain fundamental structure processes and function following stressors or disturbances and defines resistance as capacity to remain largely unchanged despite exposure to stressors such as disturbances or invasive species. The study uses generalized resilience to disturbance and resistance to annual grass invasion index values high moderate and low extracted from a map developed by Maestas et al. 2016 and describes these as underlying soil temperature and moisture regimes. These RR index values are used for post hoc comparison in the pinyon juniper removal example and direct integration in the post wildfire restoration example rather than measuring recovery dynamics from time series of ecosystem state variables. The methods simulate land cover changes given estimated fire severity and underlying ecosystem processes influencing resilience to disturbance and resistance to invasion by annual grasses and simulate scenarios for land cover transitions after at least 15 yr post fire which indicates modeling rather than observed recovery. Because resilience is represented by mapped RR classes based on soil regimes it is inferred from abiotic proxies rather than observed system recovery. The first author affiliation is U.S. Geological Survey Western Ecological Research Center Dixon Field Station in California USA. The study area spans the border of Nevada and California in the Bi State area at the interface of the Sierra Nevada Mountains and the Great Basin. Specific coordinates are not provided so the location is approximated from the described California Nevada border Bi State region near Mono County and Bodie Hills. The habitat is described as sagebrush ecosystems dominated by Artemisia tridentata subspecies and other shrubs which corresponds best to shrubland. Taxon is NA because resilience quantified is sagebrush ecosystem RR index rather than a focal organism response.

The study states that it conducted annual disturbance–recovery experiments and that disturbed plots were cleared each July and allowed to recover for 12 mo before being photographed and recleared. It explicitly interprets resilience as recovery dynamics because it reports that in disturbed communities overall average recovery rates declined and that recovery rates slowed and became more variable over time. The Methods define the response data as annual percent cover of all taxa estimated from photographs and percent cover of each species estimated after 1 y of recovery. Recovery rate was operationalized using increasing ordination distance between intact control and recovery removal plots through time because the authors reasoned that if the rate of recovery was slowing the ordinal distance should increase. The disturbance is a complete clearance of all macrobiota in plots which is a manipulated structural disturbance. The disturbance is treated as a discrete annual event with a defined recovery interval of 12 mo which matches a pulse pattern. The work is a field experiment in the low intertidal zone at six sites within three Oregon coast capes spanning about 260 km. The first author affiliation is Oregon State University in the United States and the field sites are on the Oregon coast in the United States. The location is approximated as the central Oregon coast average because the text provides only a coastwide span and named sites without coordinates. The observation window for the resilience experiment spans 2011 to 2019 which is about eight years or 2920 days. The taxa quantified in resilience metrics include macrophytes such as algae and surfgrass and sessile invertebrates such as mussels and barnacles so the kingdoms include Plantae and Animalia.

The paper states that it established an overground underground analysis framework and model to quantitatively assess the degree of mining impact on the ecosystem and its resilience and it calculated a comprehensive value of each grid by the linear weighted method using 12 indicators. The indicators include land use terrain relief water distribution the proportion of construction vegetation coverage desertification rate rainfall population density mining depth mining thickness the ratio of buried depth versus mining thickness and mining strength and these are treated as an index system rather than a measured recovery trajectory. The study divides the comprehensive value into four ecological resilience partitions by the natural breakpoint method which indicates a static spatial classification. The introduction defines function as the capacity of an ecosystem to resist disturbance and still maintain a specified state and it presents ecological resilience as the capacity to absorb shocks and still maintain function which links resilience with resistance and function. The data sources are Landsat 8 OLI August 2015 MODIS vegetation cover data from 2015 meteorological station datasets and literature derived mining parameters and the analysis is GIS based interpolation weighting and correlation rather than time series recovery after a disturbance. The study area is Shendong coalfield China at 110 05 to 118 14 and 39 17 to 39 26 37 and the average coordinate is within this range. The land use classes include farmland forest grass water bodies residential and others and the region includes desert transition and river systems so multiple habitat types are involved. The first author affiliations are Zhejiang University Hangzhou and China University of Mining and Technology Beijing which are in China.

The study states that resilience comprises resistance and recovery and defines resistance as structure and function upheld following a disturbance and recovery as partial or full return to a pre disturbance condition. It assessed seagrass resilience by calculating resistance times and recovery times using comparisons of biomass and leaf density data prior to and during the event and recovery times through assessment against historical condition. Resistance and recovery were quantified from repeated measurements at six seagrass sites sampled monthly prior to the flood in December 2016 and January 2017 and after the flood from February 2017 to March 2018 with no sampling in June and July 2017. Recovery was defined as the time taken after the flood for the seagrass metric to be equal to or greater than the median in the background period. Because the framework explicitly includes resistance and recovery and the question requires one quantification the recovery time metric is selected. Biomass is a target variable because resistance and recovery were calculated using seagrass biomass and leaf density and recovery time is reported for biomass in Table 4. The study is at the ecosystem level because biomass and leaf density are meadow scale measures expressed per square meter. The approach is field observation because it used a seagrass monitoring program and additional field samples and used salinity and water quality monitoring data. The first author affiliation is Edith Cowan University in Australia. The field site is the Swan Canning Estuary in southwestern Australia and Figure 1 states it flows through Perth. The disturbance is an unseasonal summer rainfall event in 2017 resulting in an excess discharge of freshwater and hyposalinisation and is treated as an event with a defined start and end based on salinity differing from background by at least five percent for five or more consecutive days. The observation duration is from December 2016 to March 2018 which is 456 days using 365 days per year.

The study states that it used aerial PhotoMaps between 2009 and 2014 to track kelp patch area perimeter and perimeter to area ratio through time and it also used in situ field experiments in 2014. The discussion states that perimeter to area ratio of kelp patches was found to predict phase shift to urchin barrens with patch extinction occurring when ratios exceed a critical threshold of about 0.7 and the abstract states patch extinction occurring when ratios exceeded a critical ratio of about 0.7. This is a threshold beyond which patches are unlikely to persist which matches a tipping point framing and therefore Latitude. The resilience indicator used in the synthesis is monitoring dynamics of this critical ratio using aerial imagery which is a spatial patch metric and therefore a regional network parameter. The introduction and conclusions discuss resilience and resistance together including the title and the statement that restoration will increase kelp patch resilience and provide increased resistance to establishment of undesirable turfs and invasive kelp which supports resilience + resistance as parallel concepts. The first author affiliation is University of Tasmania in Australia. The study location is given as adjacent to Williamstown Beach in Port Phillip Bay Australia with coordinates S 37 52 10.5564 and E 144 53 36.4884 converted to decimal degrees. The habitat is subtidal temperate rocky reef in a shallow coastal bay which fits Marine Neritic. The disturbance driving the phase shift is destructive overgrazing by sea urchins which is a biotic disturbance and it is observed rather than experimentally imposed for the aerial time series. The decline events are tracked from one time period to the next available image in the time series over 6 years and the disturbance is treated as ongoing over the observation window so Press. The observation duration for the aerial imagery spans 6 years which is 2190 days using 365 days per year.

The paper states that it assesses resilience by using the diversity and dominance of species within energetic pathways as a measure of resilience to loss of species. It defines an energetic Shannon–Wiener index ESWI where low ESWI indicates high dependence on a few species and hence potential vulnerability and it reports ESWI comparisons among old-growth forest logged forest and oil palm. This is a proxy based inference about resilience rather than a direct measurement of recovery dynamics after a disturbance. The study does not quantify recovery time recovery rate recovery degree resistance latitude or invariability of a state variable following a disturbance event. The approach is based on field collected data including camera traps live trapping bat harp traps bird point counts and NPP plots in Sabah Malaysia. The first author affiliation is University of Oxford in the United Kingdom. The logged forest data are collected in the SAFE Project landscape with coordinates 4 degrees 43 minutes N and 117 degrees 35 minutes E in Sabah Malaysia. The habitat is lowland tropical forest with comparisons to oil palm plantations but the dominant natural habitat for the resilience proxy is forest. The resilience proxy is computed from energy flow partition across bird and mammal species so the taxon is Animalia and the target variable type is interaction because it is an index of redundancy within energetic pathways.

The study states that it aimed to determine how diet affects the resilience of the gut microbiome to antibiotic treatment and it treated mice with a triple antibiotic cocktail for 72 h and then collected faecal samples for 4 weeks. It explicitly says it quantified resilience in terms of microbial biomass by counting colony forming units. It reports that CFU counts for mice on regular chow had recovered to baseline levels by day 4 post antibiotics whereas CFU counts for mice on Western diet did not recover to baseline up to at least day 7. This is a direct measurement of recovery dynamics using the system state variable microbial biomass so the measurement type is observed. The disturbance is experimentally imposed antibiotic treatment described as a triple antibiotic cocktail in drinking water so it is manipulated and chemical. The disturbance is time bounded for 72 h and then recovery is tracked so the pattern is pulse. The quantification corresponds to time to return to baseline because recovery is described as recovered to baseline by a specific day. The target variable used for that resilience quantification is microbial biomass operationalized as CFU counts. The work is an in vivo mouse experiment with imposed treatment so the approach is field experiment. The first author affiliations list The University of Chicago and Argonne National Laboratory in Illinois USA so the institution country is US. The reporting summary states no field collected samples were used so field country and location are NA. The observation window for the resilience measurement described spans from day 0 to day 28 post antibiotics which is 28 days plus the 72 h treatment period giving 31 days between the start of disturbance and the end of the 4 week sampling period.

The study uses satellite derived vegetation indices from Landsat to quantify cyclone induced canopy loss and recovery dynamics across years. It states that it examined canopy cover loss and recovery dynamics using remote sensing data and that it quantified canopy loss and recovery. Resilience is discussed alongside resistance with statements that resistance of mangrove canopy cover to cyclone disturbance varies and that resilience is inherent to the magnitude of canopy damage. Resilience is operationalized as canopy recovery time because it states that the main predictor of recovery time was the degree of canopy loss and that resilience of mangrove forests to cyclone disturbance is canopy recovery time. Recovery time is estimated as the time until canopy recovery in years based on when post cyclone vegetation index values become greater than or equal to pre cyclone values. The target variables used for resilience quantification are EVI and NDII vegetation indices which are used as metrics of changes in forest canopy cover and are treated as vegetation indices rather than species level measures. The work is based on field observation via remote sensing with no experimental manipulation. The first author affiliation is National Taiwan Normal University in Taipei Taiwan. The location is described as global tropics with 56 mangrove sites across the globe so the field country and location are global. The habitat is mangrove forests at the terrestrial marine ecotone which fits coastal habitat. The major taxa studied are mangrove trees. The disturbance is tropical cyclones which are unmanipulated climatic events and are treated as discrete events with pre cyclone and post cyclone images and subsequent recovery monitoring. Images were acquired for up to 9 years after events and the study period is 2000 to 2020 which is 21 years or 7665 days and the maximum recovery window described is 9 years so the observation duration for resilience recovery assessment is 3285 days.

The study defines forest response to drought using resistance as sensitivity to drought and resilience as post-drought recovery rate. It quantifies resistance and resilience from tree ring width index time series from 2935 sites in the International Tree Ring Data Bank. Resilience Rs is calculated using RWI in a drought year and RWI two years after a drought and the Methods state they selected two years of unperturbed forest recovery to measure resilience. Drought years are identified from SPEI thresholds and the study focuses only on individual droughts while ignoring consecutive drought years and droughts within two years of an extreme drought. Because resilience is explicitly described as post-drought recovery rate and computed from post-drought RWI it is an observed recovery rate measure. The target variable used in the resilience calculation is standardized ring width index which is a tree growth metric. The disturbance is extreme drought identified from SPEI which is a climatic disturbance and it is not experimentally manipulated. The disturbance is treated as discrete drought years with recovery assessed after the event which matches a pulse pattern. The tree ring series cover at least 25 years between 1901 and 2015 and the study analyses 1901 to 2015 which is 116 years or 42330 days. The first author affiliation listed is Peking University in Beijing China. The tree ring sites are described as global and the analysis is global so field country and location are global. The system studied is forests and the subjects are trees including gymnosperms and angiosperms which are plants.

The study reports zooplankton community recovery following the experimental acidification of Little Rock Lake Wisconsin USA and it states sulfuric acid was added to the northern basin from 1985 to 1990 and all acid additions ceased in 1991. Resilience is quantified from observed time series because the methods compare annual mean biomass time series in the acidified basin to the reference basin envelope and conclude recovery once annual mean biomass was consistently within the 10th and 90th percentiles of the reference basin. The study also quantifies community composition trajectories using correspondence analysis and states that by 1996 community composition had essentially returned to an assemblage characteristic of the reference basin and that recovery was complete by 1997 for copepods. Recovery lag is defined as a time delay between chemical recovery and biomass recovery and Table 1 reports recovery years and recovery lags in years which grounds a recovery time quantification. The target variables used for recovery are biomass of total groups and individual taxa and multivariate community composition from correspondence analysis. The level is community because the core resilience metrics are community composition and aggregated community biomass across multiple zooplankton taxa. The approach is field experiment because the lake was experimentally acidified and field observation because long term sampling from 1984 to 2000 measured pH and zooplankton. The first author affiliation is University of Wisconsin in the USA. The field site is Little Rock Lake Wisconsin USA and the habitat is a lake which fits Wetland. The disturbance is manipulated chemical because sulfuric acid additions altered pH. The disturbance is treated as press because acid additions were maintained across multiple years with target pH levels each maintained for two years. The observation duration spans 1984 to 2000 which is 16 years or 5840 days.

The introduction states that land degradation in non equilibrium rangelands may be defined in terms of loss of resilience. The methods state that variations in ecosystem resilience are dealt with in the model through changes in water use efficiency and tree and shrub cover. The study uses simulation techniques with a simple herbage production and consumption model and it models a 50 year rainfall sequence. The model is calibrated from remotely sensed data using Landsat Multispectral Scanner data and the PD54 vegetation index and it reports a good fit to observed data. The core outputs analysed are modelled herbage production consumption and standing biomass and the paper reports percent reductions in production and consumption under degraded versus undegraded conditions. Because resilience is represented by parameter changes in water use efficiency and tree and shrub cover rather than a direct recovery metric the quantification does not match recovery rate recovery time recovery degree resistance latitude or invariability. The field setting is central Australia with rainfall and evaporation data from the Alice Springs and Owen Springs recording stations adjacent to the study area and the author affiliation is CSIRO in Alice Springs Australia. The paddocks are described as shrubby grassland and open woodland but the dominant described pasture is grassland and herbage. The disturbance driver analysed through time is rainfall variability and grazing effects represented in the model. The rainfall and grazing influences are applied throughout the 50 year run so the disturbance is treated as press. The observation window for the main modelling sequence is 50 years which is 18250 days.

The abstract states the study examines ocean warming and its long-term consequences for population resilience and integrates experimental results into stochastic density-dependent integral projection models. The methods describe simulating an 80% increase in mortality as a major disturbance and then projecting populations 100 years to estimate recovery. The methods define recovery as the minimum time required to achieve a stable density threshold of large individuals greater than 10 cm defined as 0.8 large individuals per m2. The results report mean recovery times of about 46 years at 16 C and about 54 years at 20 C and no recovery at 24 C. This is a direct quantification of resilience as recovery time following a simulated disturbance so the measurement is observed via model response. The target variable used for recovery is the density of large individuals per m2 which is an abundance measure at the population level. The approach includes an indoor laboratory experiment measuring settlement and survival and field observation from five natural populations used to parameterize vital rates and modeling and simulation via IPMs. The first author affiliation listed first is Universitat de Barcelona Barcelona Spain. Field data and collections are from NW Mediterranean sites including Cap de Creus Montgri Medes Islands Baix Ter and Columbretes Islands all in Spain and the experimental collection site is given as 42 2 34.026 N 3 13 36.358 E. The three temperature logger locations are given with coordinates and their average is approximately N41.999 E2.390 with the farthest site Columbretes producing a summed max deviation of about 3.1 degrees. The habitat is rocky bottoms at 15 to 50 m depth in the Mediterranean Sea which fits the shallow nearshore marine environment. The disturbance types in the resilience quantification are modelled warming and modelled mortality events from storms and ghost fishing nets and the mortality events are treated as discrete events in simulations.

The study uses measured above-ground biomass from experimental plots and states that the analysis is based on data from 2005 to 2007. It defines resistance as the difference in biomass between 2006 and 2005 and recovery as the difference in biomass between 2007 and 2006. It defines resilience as the ratio of post-drought 2007 to pre-drought 2005 biomass and states that this ratio measures the extent to which biomass production after drought has recovered to the pre-drought level. Because resilience is quantified from observed biomass before and after a natural drought it is an observed measurement. The framework is explicit because the text states that the ratio combines resistance and recovery. The quantification corresponds to recovery degree because it is the extent of return to the pre-drought level expressed as a post to pre ratio. The target variable used for resilience is above-ground biomass. The focal level for this resilience metric is the plant community because it is calculated for community biomass in plots. The approach is a field experiment because the authors established replicated 1 m2 plots in an arable field and maintained planted communities. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University and Research Centre in the Netherlands. The field site is in Wageningen the Netherlands and climate data are from a meteorological station in Wageningen about 2 km from the site. The drought is described as a summer heatwave with very low rainfall and is treated as a natural drought so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic. The disturbance is a pulse because it is a discrete event in the last 2 months of the 2006 growing season followed by post-drought measurement in 2007. The observation duration for resilience spans from the 2005 harvest to the 2007 harvest which is two years or 730 days.

The study used demographic data from monitored populations and then projected population dynamics with matrix models. It simulated disturbances by introducing at year 10 a storm that caused 80% mortality and by simulating mortalities caused by fishing nets. Recovery was evaluated in the population projection where after the disturbance the size structure converged back to a stage similar to that at equilibrium after more than 20 years. This is a direct recovery dynamic from an imposed disturbance so the measurement is observed. The framework is none because resilience is discussed without an explicit equation defining resilience as a component of a larger variable. Quantification is recovery time because the text specifies the time until convergence back to equilibrium structure. The target variable is population density by stage because the projection figures and quasi extinction threshold are based on density and the threshold is defined as 10% of the initial population density. The level is population because density and population growth rate are population level outputs. The approach includes field observation because individuals were monitored in permanent transects and includes modeling and simulation because population projection and disturbance scenarios were simulated for up to 100 years. The first author affiliation is Universitat de Barcelona in Spain. The survival and growth data used for parameterization came from Columbretes Islands Marine Reserve Castellon Spain with coordinates given for Columbretes I and Columbretes II and the average of those coordinates is reported. The habitat is sublittoral rocky substrate at 15 to 50 m depth which is shallow nearshore marine so Marine.Neritic. The organism is a brown macroalga in Ochrophyta so Chromista. Disturbances are modelled storms and lost fishing gear with storms treated as extreme events and fishing nets as mortality events so pulse. Observation duration is 100 years because the simulations projected the population for up to 100 years.

The study states that it investigates forest resilience by comparing the time required for each pixel to recover a leaf area index value of three with the observed mean fire interval between 2003 and 2016. It defines a site as resilient when the time required to recover LAI equals three is shorter than or equal to the current mean fire interval and non resilient otherwise. This makes the resilience quantification a recovery time metric based on canopy recovery. The target variable used for this resilience calculation is leaf area index which is a canopy cover measure. The approach combines remote sensing products including MODIS LAI and MODIS burned area derived fire return intervals with the CARLUC Fire ecosystem model. The disturbance includes a modelled high intensity fire during an extreme drought and modelled logging imposed as an additional 60 percent LAI loss in areas within three kilometres of forest edges. Fire return intervals are derived from observed satellite products so fire is also treated as an unmanipulated disturbance in the observational data layer. The study location is the Amazon Basin but the climate projections and remote sensing products are global datasets and the paper does not specify a single country for the data sources used in the resilience calculation. The time period for the present analysis spans 1981 to 2017 which is 37 years corresponding to 5110 days using 365 days per year.

The study used seven replicated field experiments with Pteridium control treatments including cutting and asulam spraying against an untreated experimental control. Vegetation was monitored annually for 10 years and the cover of all vascular plant bryophyte and lichen species was recorded in quadrats. Resistance and resilience were operationalized using principal response curves where resistance meant no significant temporal change in species composition relative to controls and resilience meant a significant change that then reverted to the experimental control line by the end. Resilience was therefore assessed from observed temporal trajectories of community composition relative to controls after one off treatments ceased. The framework is presented as overcoming resistance and resilience of the invaded community. The resilience quantification corresponds to whether the community returned to its original state or remained significantly different which matches recovery degree. The target variable used for this resilience assessment was species composition derived from cover data analyzed with PRCs. The work was conducted in Great Britain at Cannock Hordron Edge Carneddau and Sourhope. The first author affiliation is University of Liverpool in the United Kingdom. Treatments included asulam herbicide application and cutting which are chemical and structural manipulations. One off treatments are discrete events applied at the start so the disturbance pattern is pulse. Monitoring occurred from 1994 to 2003 which is 10 years or 3650 days.

Framework: resilience = resistance + recovery rate + recovery time
Justification: The abstract states that the paper analyses resilience at individual tree level using a network of tree ring collections from 22 sites. The methods define resilience components as resistance recovery rate and recovery time and state that they were used simultaneously. The resilience indices are computed from detrended tree ring width index TRWI and growth reduction GR following identified drought events. Resistance is defined as the inverse of total GR 1/GR. Recovery time is defined as the time needed to reach pre drought growth levels after the disturbance with a maximum length of 10 years. Recovery rate is calculated as total GR divided by the length of the recovery period. Drought events are identified from SPEI and WBAL and selected when TRWI was lower than mean growth in the target year or the following one. The three growth response indices were computed for drought events at different sites from 1940 to 2017. The study area spans 47 to 64 N and 10 to 27 E and includes 22 sites in 10 countries. The first author affiliation is Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences in Sweden. The disturbance is drought which is treated as discrete drought events and followed by a recovery period which matches a pulse framing. The target variable used to quantify resilience is tree growth via tree ring width index and growth reduction at individual tree level.

Framework: resilient = able to recover after disturbance
Disturbance type: Unmanipulated_Fire; Unmanipulated_Biotic; Unmanipulated_Climatic; Manipulated_Biological resource use
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as able to recover after disturbance and contrasts it with resistance as able to avoid disturbance impacts. The study treats salvage logging as a secondary disturbance that is superimposed on already disturbed ecosystems and it compares disturbed plots with disturbed plus salvage logged plots. The response variables used in the meta analysis are the density and height of regenerating trees and effect sizes are calculated as Hedges g from plot level means. The discussion states that salvage logging effect sizes on regeneration density became more positive with time elapsed after the logging disturbance and that negative impacts tend to diminish over time which is a recovery dynamic. Time since logging is explicitly modelled as a moderator with a positive slope indicating the effect became less negative with time after logging. This corresponds to recovery rate because resilience is inferred from the change in effect size with time after logging rather than a single end state difference or a time to return. The target variables are tree density and tree height which correspond to density and growth. The biological level is population because density and height are aggregated at plot level for tree species regeneration. The approach is field observation because the meta analysis synthesizes field studies of natural disturbance and subsequent salvage logging rather than an indoor or field experiment imposed by the authors. The first author affiliation is University of Granada in Spain. The results mention 35 study locations in Mediterranean temperate and boreal forests but do not provide countries or coordinates in the provided text. The habitat is forest because the study concerns forest plots and forest regeneration. The taxon is Plantae because the subjects are tree species regeneration. Disturbances include wildfire windthrow and insect outbreak as natural disturbances and salvage logging as an anthropogenic logging disturbance. The disturbance pattern is pulse because the study records time elapsed between disturbance and logging and time since logging implying discrete events with subsequent observation. The maximum time since logging range is 0 to 20 years which gives an observation duration of 20 years or 7300 days.

The study states an objective of maximizing average resilience of coral reef systems measured as mean herbivore biomass achieved in the reef system. The methods define herbivore biomass h on each reef and model its decline under fishing and recovery under protection using continuous time differential equations and equilibrium solutions. Resilience is therefore quantified directly from the modeled system state variable herbivore biomass rather than inferred from static proxies. The approach is deterministic modeling with analytic solutions and supporting stochastic simulations rather than field observation. The first author affiliations are University of Queensland and The Nature Conservancy in Australia. The case study parameterization uses recovery rates measured in fully protected areas in the Western Indian Ocean and cites reef fish recovery in marine protected areas in Kenya as the data source context. The habitat is coral reef systems which are shallow nearshore marine environments. The focal organisms are herbivorous fishes described as typically teleost fish. The modeled disturbance driving loss of biomass is fishing pressure on unprotected reefs which is a biological resource use disturbance. Fishing is treated as constant on unprotected reefs and decline occurs during unprotected periods which corresponds to a press disturbance. No explicit start and end dates for observations are provided and the analysis focuses on equilibrium outcomes so observation duration cannot be determined. No coordinates are provided for the Western Indian Ocean or Kenya sites so location and range cannot be determined.

The study quantified short term growth responses to severe droughts using the resilience indices proposed by Lloret et al. (2011). It explicitly defines Resistance (Rt) Recovery (Rc) and Resilience (Rs) and provides formulas where Rs equals BAIpostD divided by BAIpreD. This uses observed tree ring derived basal area increment time series and compares pre drought and post drought growth around selected drought events 1995 2005 and 2012. The target variable for resilience is tree growth expressed as basal area increment derived from tree ring width. The disturbance is severe drought identified using SPEI18.Jun and treated as discrete drought events with defined pre and post windows. The observation is based on field sampled increment cores from silver fir trees in two Spanish Pyrenees forests. The first author affiliation is Instituto Pirenaico de Ecología (IPE CSIC) Zaragoza Spain. Field coordinates are given in Table 1 for the two sites and the reported location is the average of those coordinates. The observation window for drought resilience uses 3 years preceding and following each drought and the earliest and latest drought windows span from 1992 to 2013.

The introduction states that analyses of nematode communities provide insight into sensitivity or resilience but the methods quantify ecological indices and diversity indices from nematode community parameters rather than measuring recovery dynamics through time. The methods list indices including BI which indicates soil food web resilience and MI which reflects soil ecosystem stability and SI which indicates changes in food web structure following disturbance or restoration. Sampling was conducted between July-September 2016 and the design compares management practices grazed mowed cultivated and control that were established for at least six years but the study does not measure trajectories of recovery after a defined disturbance. Therefore resilience is inferred from ecological indices rather than observed as recovery rate time or degree. The target variables used to represent resilience are nematode ecological indices especially the basal index described as indicating soil food web resilience which are food web and interaction metrics. The work is based on field sampling of soils and nematodes at three grassland sites in Inner Mongolia Hebei and Qinghai China with coordinates given for each site. The average coordinate is calculated from the three site coordinates shown in Fig 1 and Table 1. The location range is based on the farthest site from that average using the stated latitude and longitude span. The habitat is grassland because the study region covers meadow steppe typical steppe and alpine meadow. The study subjects are soil nematodes which are animals.

The paper presents a spatially explicit nucleation theory and uses numerical simulations of patch dynamics where patches below a critical radius collapse and patches above it expand. It states that geometrical principles can be used to derive a critical patch radius and that this provides a framework to measure resilience. It defines resilience in terms of overcoming resistance thresholds and promoting ecological recovery and it discusses resilience and recovery together with resistance thresholds. The resilience related quantity is the critical patch size or critical patch radius which is an unstable equilibrium point separating basins of attraction and it determines whether a disturbance patch can trigger a transition between alternate stable states. This corresponds to a threshold magnitude for state shift rather than a recovery trajectory metric. The target variable used in the simulations is the spatial state of the system as alternative stable states represented as patch types rather than a single species abundance. The disturbance is represented as introducing a patch of the desired or alternative state into a landscape which is a modeled structural perturbation. The disturbance is treated as a discrete introduction event followed by dynamics. The simulation figure shows time steps from 0 to 50 for patch size trajectories.

The study quantified resilience using aboveground biomass measured repeatedly across harvests from 2012 to 2015. The Methods define resistance as the difference between pre flood biomass and biomass two months after the flood and recovery as the difference between biomass after recovery and biomass two months after the flood. The Introduction states together resistance and recovery determine ecosystem resilience as we define it here. The Methods define resilience as the difference between the average biomass of the three harvests before the flood and the average biomass of the three harvests after recovery which is a pre vs post difference in state. The flood event is described as field site flooded in June 2013 because of sustained heavy rains with standing water in the experimental plots. The field site is given as Jena Thuringia Germany with coordinates 51 N 11 E. The first author affiliation lists University of Zurich Switzerland. The approach is a field experiment because seedlings were transplanted into plots at the Jena Experiment field site with soil treatments and biomass was harvested in the field. The observation window for disturbance response spans from the first harvest May 2012 to the last harvest May 2015 which is four years or 1460 days using 365 days per year. The habitat is grassland because the study is a long term grassland biodiversity field experiment and the communities are grassland plant species. The taxon is Plantae because the subjects are plant communities and plant species.

The study states that it monitored coral and fish assemblages over 14 years on fixed sites and compared communities prior to the disturbance with those present in 1992 and subsequent changes through to 1998 which is direct observation of system trajectories. The Introduction defines a stability framework where a system is stable if it retains similar structure resistance or returns to similar pre disturbance structure resilience. The disturbance is described as a large scale decline in live coral cover sometime between November 1987 and October 1989 probably as a result of storm damage and the Discussion refers to major storm activity. Resilience is evidenced by return to pre impact levels such as hard coral cover becoming indistinguishable from pre impact levels by 1998 and most fish taxa recovering to pre impact levels by 1998 which matches recovery degree. Target variables used in the main resilience evidence are live hard coral cover and soft coral and turf algae cover plus fish abundance and fish species richness for multiple families. The work is field observation using manta tow video transects line intercept surveys timed swims and transect counts on reefs. The first author affiliation is Australian Institute of Marine Science in Townsville Australia. The field sites are reefs in the southern Great Barrier Reef Australia including Capricorn Bunkers and Swains sectors and the figure shows their location. The habitat is coral reef in shallow nearshore waters consistent with marine neritic. The taxa quantified include corals and algae in benthic cover and reef fishes in abundance and richness. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event between 1987 and 1989 with recovery tracked afterward indicating a pulse. The observation window for disturbance and resilience spans 14 years which is 14 times 365 days.

The study used remotely sensed Landsat estimates of vegetation cover collected every 2 to 5 years from 1985 to 2015 and modeled changes in sagebrush cover on 375 former oil and gas well pads. It states that it used modeled relationships to predict recovery time across the landscape as an indicator of resilience where faster recovery indicates a greater capacity to recover when similarly disturbed. Resilience was therefore quantified as time to recovery to a reference condition because it iteratively predicted yearly sagebrush cover until it exceeded cover estimated in 2015 or until 100 years elapsed. The target variable used for resilience quantification was percent sagebrush cover because recovery was defined by reaching the 2015 sagebrush cover estimate. The focal organism was sagebrush Artemisia spp which is a plant. The analysis combined field observation via remote sensing time series with modeling using generalized additive models and landscape prediction. The disturbance was oil and gas well pad construction and associated infrastructure which the paper describes as destroying vegetation and removing and replacing upper soil layers and it was not experimentally manipulated by the authors. The disturbance was treated as a discrete event with start and end dates based on drilling initiation and termination of production and recovery was modeled after termination. The study area was the Wyoming Landscape Conservation Initiative area in southwestern Wyoming USA and the first author affiliation is in Fort Collins Colorado USA. The figures locate the study in southwestern Wyoming and the average coordinate provided reflects that region. The habitat is described as semi arid big sagebrush communities and mosaics dominated by sagebrush which corresponds to shrubland. The observation window for the remote sensing time series spans 1985 to 2015 which is 30 years or 10950 days.

The study used long term monitoring data from 1980 to 2019 collected fortnightly throughout the ice free season at three stations in Lake Simcoe Ontario Canada. Resilience is explicitly linked to the rate of recovery to equilibrium after perturbation and the analysis uses early warning indicators that signal a decline in an ecosystem’s rate of recovery to equilibrium after perturbation. The early warning indicators quantified were standard deviation autocorrelation at lag 1 skewness and kurtosis calculated within rolling windows from decomposed time series and evaluated for monotonic trends prior to breakpoints. These indicators were calculated directly from observed state variable time series including dissolved organic carbon phosphorus chlorophyll a Secchi depth dissolved oxygen and temperature and from PCA axis score time series. The target variables used for resilience related quantification are lake water quality state variables which are abiotic parameters. The work is based on field observation from a monitoring program rather than an experiment or simulation. The study area coordinates are given as latitude 44.4167 and longitude 79.3333 for Lake Simcoe. The first author affiliation is Trent University in Canada. The paper describes multiple ongoing stressors including human activities such as urban development and agricultural practices and invasive species establishment and treats them as long term changes rather than discrete events which matches a press pattern. The observation window spans 1980 to 2019 which is 39 years or 14235 days.

The study states that it used an age structured population model and simulations to examine stability resilience and productivity. It defines resilience as the ability of a population to return to an equilibrium state after disturbance. It quantifies resilience as time to recover from disturbance and as the number of years to rebuild a population above mean spawning stock biomass after five poor recruitment years. The target variable used for resilience is spawning stock biomass because rebuilding time is defined relative to mean spawning stock biomass. The focal subject is a white perch fish population so the level is population and the taxon is Animalia. The approach is modeling and simulation because it performed 500 stochastic model runs over a 150 year time period. The disturbance is imposed in the model as a sequence of five poor recruitment years so it is a model structural disturbance treated as a discrete event. The first author affiliation is University of Massachusetts Dartmouth in the United States. The modeled system and empirical parameterization are from the Patuxent River sub estuary of Chesapeake Bay USA. The habitat is an estuarine system within Chesapeake Bay consistent with shallow nearshore marine waters. The simulation duration is 150 years which equals 54750 days.

The study uses numerical simulations of a discrete multispecies population dynamics model and states that it employed numerical simulations to study the effect of phenology on network topology community stability and biodiversity. Resilience is computed directly from the dynamical system as the return rate to a stable equilibrium after a small perturbation using the leading Eigenvalue k1 of the Jacobian matrix and resilience as minus log k1. This is a direct model based quantification of system response to an explicitly stated small perturbation so the measurement type is observed. No equation style framework defining resilience as a component of a larger construct is provided and resilience is treated as a metric alongside stability and diversity so the framework is none. The target variable underlying the Jacobian and equilibrium is species population sizes Pi and Aj and the paper describes population dynamics and community diversity based on extinctions so the target variable type is abundance and the level is community. The first author affiliation is University of Groningen The Netherlands so the institution country is NL. The work is not based on field collected resilience data and uses simulated communities so field country and location are NA and habitat type is NA. The model community consists of plant and animal species so the taxon is Plantae and Animalia. The perturbation is described as a small perturbation around equilibrium in the model so disturbance type is model structural and no pulse or press pattern is specified. The simulations run for 3500 years and resilience is determined from the last 500 time steps so the observation duration is 3500 years which is 1277500 days.

Framework: ecosystem stability = resistance + resilience + temporal stability
Justification: The study states it assessed stability including resistance resilience and temporal stability and defines ecosystem stability as evaluated through indicators of resistance resilience and temporal stability. Resilience is quantified as Resilience after drought = Ln(NEEafter dry/NEEdry) which uses observed NEE measured repeatedly from 2020 to 2023. NEE is measured using a static assimilation chamber with measurements every 10 days during peak growth each year and GEP is calculated from ER and NEE. Because resilience is calculated from the ratio of NEE in the year following drought to NEE during the dry year it represents recovery rate rather than recovery time or recovery degree. The target variable used in the resilience formula is NEE which is a carbon exchange flux and therefore an ecosystem function variable. The work is a manipulated field experiment with water addition and nitrogen addition treatments applied in plots at field sites in Inner Mongolia. The first author affiliation is in Beijing China and the study area is in Inner Mongolia China. The methods list four sites with coordinates and the average of those coordinates is approximately N44.374 E116.123 with all sites within two degrees so the location range is 0. Drought is treated using dry years 2020 and 2022 and resilience after drought uses the year following drought so the disturbance is climatic and the manipulation is water addition. Water addition is applied every 10 days during the growing season for multiple years so the disturbance pattern is press. The observation window for resilience spans 2020 to 2023 which is four years or 1460 days.

The study states that it quantified resilience using spatial early warnings and that these early warnings reflect recovery because they reflect the speed required by the system to recover from perturbations and that resilience loss means recovery capacity decreases. The Methods specify spatial early warnings as spatial variance near neighbor correlation and spectral density ratio computed on presence absence vegetation matrices from satellite images. These are treated as indicators expected to increase as a dynamical system approaches a transition which is described as critical slowing down meaning longer recovery from small perturbations. This matches recovery rate because autocorrelation based measures fall under recovery rate and the text links early warnings to recovery speed. The target variable used for these resilience metrics is the presence absence vegetation matrices derived from remote sensing images which represent vegetation cover. The study also runs simulations from two cellular automaton models and computes the same spatial metrics on simulated landscapes so the approach includes modeling and simulation as well as field observation via remote sensing and field sites. The disturbance is increasing aridity described as increasing abiotic stress and water shortage along an aridity gradient which is a climatic driver and it is analyzed as a continuous gradient rather than discrete events so it is press. Observation duration is supported by the NDVI time window described as mean annual NDVI for each year between 2000 and 2015 which spans 16 years or 5840 days. The first author affiliation is Institut des Sciences de l Evolution de Montpellier in France. The field dataset is said to be located in 13 countries but the countries and coordinates are not listed in the provided text and the map figure does not provide coordinates so field country and location are NA. The paper refers to global drylands and includes examples of grasslands and mentions other dryland types so a single habitat type cannot be identified from the provided text and is coded as Multi. Because the level is landscape based on spatial metrics of vegetation cover across plots the taxon is NA by the rule.

The abstract states that sites with good condition were associated with higher directed connectance and modularity indicating higher resilience compared with fair condition sites. The methods describe trophic network analysis using directed resource to consumer interactions and calculating directed connectance and modularity. The discussion states that beta diversity directed connectance and modularity are all higher in good sites meaning these good sites are expected to be more stable and indicates higher resilience in good condition sites. No recovery dynamics after a disturbance are measured and resilience is not quantified from a system response trajectory. Resilience is therefore inferred from network properties rather than observed recovery. The framework is presented as resilience discussed alongside stability in phrases such as parameters corresponding with ecosystem resilience and stability and assess ecosystem stability and the conclusion indicates higher resilience. The target variables used to infer resilience are network properties directed connectance and modularity which are interaction network measures. Sampling was field observation because kick net samples were collected from Grand River tributaries across four study sites in Waterloo Ontario in November 2019. The first author affiliation is University of Guelph in Canada. The field country is Canada because sites are in Waterloo Ontario. The location is approximated as Waterloo Ontario because the text provides the city but no coordinates and the sites are within that area. Habitat is freshwater river tributaries and streams and rivers lakes wetlands and ponds are discussed and sampling was in riffles so the closest provided IUCN option is Wetland. Taxon is NA because the resilience inference is based on network metrics rather than resilience of a specific kingdom. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA because the study compares site condition categories and does not define a disturbance event window for resilience measurement.

Framework: general resilience = diversity and redundancy + connectivity + controlling variables and feedbacks
Justification: The paper defines general resilience as adaptive capacity and applies three general resilience principles described as diversity and redundancy connectivity and controlling variables and feedbacks. The study develops ten indicators and states that this set of indicators provides a simple description of the adaptive capacity and general resilience of four reaches. The indicators are calculated from habitat classification and inundation model outputs fish functional diversity and redundancy connectivity metrics and controlling variable metrics such as water surface elevation ranges total suspended solids nutrient concentrations sedimentation rates and invasive fish species counts. The analysis rescales indicator values from 0 to 1 and visualizes reach specific indicators using radar plots and interprets higher indicator contributions as higher general resilience. This does not quantify recovery dynamics of a focal state variable after a disturbance and instead infers resilience from indicator values and assumptions about their relationship with resilience. The approach uses existing long term monitoring and geospatial datasets and an inundation model described as empirically evaluated and suitable for ecological applications which is consistent with field observation based indicator construction. The first author affiliation is U.S. Geological Survey in La Crosse Wisconsin USA. The study system is the Upper Mississippi River System and the Illinois River which are in the United States. The habitat is a large floodplain river ecosystem with floodplain inundation and backwater areas which fits Wetland. No coordinates are provided in the text for the data used in the resilience indicators. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience response are not applicable.

The study uses cross-sectional data to classify lake states and estimate total phosphorus thresholds and steady state relationships between total phosphorus and chlorophyll a. It states that managers can rank lakes in terms of resilience using time series metrics like autocorrelation and variance but that these require long-term time series and the paper instead develops a framework using cross-sectional data. Resilience is described as measurable as the distance between the stable and unstable state and as proximity to total phosphorus thresholds or distance to the unstable line or critical chlorophyll a threshold. These are proxy distance based indicators derived from a fitted bifurcation diagram rather than direct measurement of recovery dynamics from observational time series. The empirical data are from 130 shallow lakes surveyed once in July during each of three consecutive years 2009 to 2011 in Minnesota USA. The first author affiliation is University of Minnesota in St Paul Minnesota USA. The approach includes data simulated from a stochastic differential equation model and application to field collected lake data. The habitat is shallow lakes and wetlands are explicitly referenced via the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources Wetland Wildlife Populations and Research Group and the system is shallow lakes. No explicit disturbance event is imposed or analyzed for resilience quantification in the cross-sectional framework and the disturbance related examples are contextual rather than used as the quantified disturbance in the resilience metric. No coordinates are provided for the lakes.

The introduction defines resilience as how quickly the system returns to its prior state after a perturbation. The methods define resilience mathematically as the negative of the real part of the dominant eigenvalue of the linearized system which is a recovery rate measure. The study quantifies resilience from a deterministic differential equation ecosystem model coupled to a genetic algorithm rather than from field time series so the approach is modeling and simulation. The state variables used in the model are autotroph and herbivore biomasses A and H and nutrient mass N expressed as nutrient concentrations so the target variable type is biomass and the level is ecosystem. Perturbations are explicitly imposed in the transient selection pressures where all perturbations removed half of each of the autotroph and herbivore steady state biomasses which is a pulse disturbance. The discussion also treats nutrient enrichment as a perturbation and simulations examine addition of nutrient which is a resource disturbance in the model. The first author affiliation is Griffith University in Queensland Australia so the institution country is AU. The parameter values are stated to be averages for a 20 m deep mixed layer oceanic environment and the model is a mixed layer plankton system so the habitat type is Marine.Neritic. No specific field sampling country or coordinates are given because the work is simulation based and uses literature values rather than a described field site so field country and location are NA. Observation duration is not reported because resilience is computed analytically from eigenvalues and transient windows are described without a stated time span.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the ability of a system to withstand disturbance. The methods state that the long term monitoring measured responses of benthic macroinvertebrate communities from 1989 to 2017 and the mesocosm experiments exposed communities to metals and simulated a spill of diesel fuel. The diesel experiment explicitly used a single pulse of diesel and measured abundance after 7 days of exposure. The metals experiments compared control versus metals treatments and attributed differences at the end of the experiment to mortality. The results report reductions in total macroinvertebrate abundance and shifts in community structure after exposure to metals and reductions in abundance after diesel exposure. These are direct measurements of system response to imposed disturbances so the measurement type is observed. Resilience was quantified through the ability to withstand disturbance which in the experiments is reflected by smaller reductions in abundance under treatment relative to control which corresponds to resistance. The focal response variables used in the main analyses include abundance of major insect orders and total abundance and these are abundance type variables at the community level. The study site is the Arkansas River near Leadville Colorado USA and the institution is Colorado State University in the United States. The disturbance types include unmanipulated chemical contamination history and manipulated chemical exposure in mesocosms including metals and diesel. The observation duration for the long term monitoring spans 1989 to 2017 which is 28 years.

The abstract states that ecosystem resilience depends on functional redundancy and response diversity and the study explores how land use change impacts these attributes in plant communities. The introduction says they follow a hierarchical effect response functional trait framework to measure response diversity based on effect traits and response traits and they quantify functional redundancy as species richness within each functional effect group and response diversity as multivariate within group dispersion in response trait space. The methods describe computing Gower dissimilarity matrices from traits and classifying species into functional effect groups using Ward clustering and then measuring functional redundancy as number of species within an effect group and response diversity as functional dispersion in response trait space. The discussion interprets reductions in functional redundancy and response diversity as reducing ecosystem resilience to future disturbances. Because resilience is not measured as recovery dynamics of an ecosystem state variable over time but is inferred from trait based redundancy and dispersion metrics across land use intensity gradients the measurement type is inferred. The first author affiliation is School of Forestry University of Canterbury Christchurch New Zealand. The methods state the 18 gradients are in nine countries Australia Canada China Costa Rica Laos New Zealand Nicaragua Portugal and the United States of America. The paper references Fig 1 as a map of study regions but provides no coordinates in the provided text so location and range are not available. The gradients span five biomes including tropical rainforest sub tropical rainforest temperate forest temperate woodland and temperate grassland so the habitat type is multi. The vegetation surveys included all vascular terrestrial species so the taxon is Plantae. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable here because resilience is inferred from land use intensity gradients rather than quantified from observed disturbance response trajectories.

The study states that it conducted numerical simulations of a vegetation water model and numerical disturbance experiments. Disturbances are applied by reducing biomass with homogeneous disturbance defined as P2(x,t) = (1 − β0) P1(x,t) and heterogeneous disturbance defined by setting selected vegetation patches to 0. Resilience is quantified using recovery time because the paper reports that recovery time is negatively correlated with blr and defines recovery time as days to recover a unit of mean biomass. The target variable used for the recovery calculation is mean biomass because the methods state they simulate until the mean biomass recovers to predisturbance mean biomass and in another case until mean biomass and blr recover. The approach is modeling and simulation because the methods describe MATLAB numerical simulation and disturbance experiments. The institution country is China because the first author affiliations are Second Institute of Oceanography Ministry of Natural Resources Hangzhou China and East China Normal University Shanghai China. Field pattern locations are explicitly given for Sudan United States Argentina and Australia in Fig 1 and southern Niger is mentioned for aerial photos. The average coordinate is computed from the five explicit locations 11.4461 27.8938 38.1738 −109.7099 −23.4363 −65.9768 −23.4484 119.8529 and southern Niger without coordinates is not used for averaging. The disturbance is model structural because the disturbance is a modeled biomass removal that changes system structure rather than a directly observed real event. The disturbance pattern is pulse because disturbances are applied as discrete biomass reductions and then the system is simulated until recovery. Observation duration is 5000 days because Fig 5 shows recovery trajectories up to 5000 days and the methods describe simulating recovery in that experiment.

The study states that it quantified response diversity and that this diversity of yield responses to weather patterns is a proxy for climate resilience because it says the diversity of yield responses to weather patterns critical to yield is the proxy for the climate resilience of crops. Resilience is therefore not measured as recovery after a disturbance but is inferred from response diversity. Response diversity is quantified using the annual Shannon diversity index for the clusters and interpreted as true diversity exp(H) which is a diversity metric. The response diversity is derived from cultivar yield responses to agroclimatic variables and clustering of cultivars into weather response clusters rather than from observing recovery dynamics after a defined disturbance. The target variable used to quantify resilience is response diversity which is a diversity measure. The focal system is wheat cultivars considered together as a set within countries and trials so the level is community because the diversity index is calculated across multiple cultivars. The approach is field observation because the data are long term cultivar trials and farmers fields and the paper uses observational yield and weather data rather than an imposed experimental disturbance. The first author affiliation is LUT University in Lappeenranta Finland so the institution country is FI. The data come from nine countries listed in the data description Finland Denmark Germany Belgium Czechia France Slovakia Italy and Spain. The trial coordinate bounds are given as latitudes 37.21 to 61.34 and longitudes minus 6.02 to 26.24 so the average location is N49.275 E10.11 and the farthest differences sum to 19.2 degrees. The habitat is agricultural because the study concerns wheat cultivar trials and farmers fields. The taxon is Plantae because wheat is a plant. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA because the measurement is inferred and the study treats weather variability as the context for response diversity rather than quantifying resilience from a disturbance response trajectory.

The study states that it used experimental disturbances to measure resilience defined as the rate of recovery after disturbance. Recovery was measured using canopy cover percent cover and percentage recovery calculated as percent cover disturbed divided by percent cover control times 100. The rate of recovery was derived as percentage recovery per month using regression with time in months. Disturbances were applied in the summer of 2006 and canopy cover was measured five times from September 2006 to October 2007. The focal organism was the dominant salt marsh grass Spartina alterniflora. The work was a disturbance recovery experiment with clipping and herbicide applied to field plots at two Louisiana salt marsh restoration sites. The first author affiliation is Louisiana State University in the United States. Field locations are given for Venice at 29 degrees 12.31 minutes north and 89 degrees 26.23 minutes west and Fourchon at 29 degrees 10.58 minutes north and 90 degrees 14.23 minutes west and the average is reported. The habitat is described as intertidal salt marsh in the Mississippi Delta Plain along the Northern Gulf of Mexico. The disturbance is manipulated removal and herbicide mortality applied to vegetation plots which is a structural disturbance. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event with recovery tracked afterward. The observation window spans from the first post disturbance measurement in September 2006 to the last in October 2007 which is about 395 days.

The study uses laboratory yeast populations and records population densities daily by measuring optical density which is direct observational data. It defines resilience as the distance between the stable and unstable fixed points which measures the maximal pulse perturbation a population can tolerate without going extinct and corresponds to the one sided size of the basin of attraction. This is a tolerance threshold style definition so the quantification matches latitude. The framework explicitly distinguishes stability as recovery rate and resilience as size of the basin of attraction and discusses their relation as stability resilience. The target variable for resilience is population density because resilience is defined in terms of the number of individuals lost and the state variable is population density. The work includes indoor experiments with serial dilutions and variable sucrose and salt and also includes simulations of a phenomenological model with tuned parameters. The first author affiliation is Massachusetts Institute of Technology in the United States. The data for resilience quantification come from laboratory yeast experiments rather than a field site so no geographic coordinates are provided. The organism is Saccharomyces cerevisiae which is a fungus. Disturbances are manipulated by increasing dilution factor described as mimicking a rising death rate and by decreasing sucrose concentration and by osmotic stress caused by salt and in the model by tuning parameters as drivers. The environmental deterioration is applied gradually over days which is treated as a slowly deteriorating environment without a defined recovery interval so it is press.
