The collapse under increasing dilution factor occurs in a few weeks after an initial period of 4 days and the sucrose deterioration collapses over the course of 3 weeks so an observation window of about 28 days is supported by the described timelines.

The objective is to identify an adaptation strategy using whole system metrics rather than measuring recovery after a disturbance. Resilience is used within the Costanza and Mageau health framework stated as H = V × O × R and described as vigor organization and resilience. The study states that three network based measurements are proposed as proxies and that resilience is represented by weighted system overhead calculated as C/TST − AMI. This uses network properties derived from Ecopath balanced flow simulations rather than direct observation of recovery dynamics. The methods state that flows identification is performed through a simulation of trophic web functioning with Ecopath 5.0 and that 54 networks were built. The study site is Voltri Europa Terminal in the Port of Genoa with coordinates 44 25 N and 8 25 E. The system is a coastal harbor benthic microbenthic loop in the Ligurian Sea NW Mediterranean which fits a shallow nearshore marine environment. The discussion interprets results as poorly resilient and not able to face further disturbances but does not quantify resilience as recovery time recovery rate or resistance from a disturbance response time series.

The study states that it combined global field surveys with satellite derived metrics of ecosystem stability resistance and resilience to drought events. Resilience is defined as the capacity of plant productivity to return to the original levels of productivity after a drought event and it is quantified using NDVI temporal data with SPEI to identify drought events. The Methods define resilience RL with an equation using D0 and Dx where Dx is measured in the year after a climate event and the Discussion states that ecosystems with higher mycorrhizal and root endophyte richness recovered faster from extreme drought events. This indicates a recovery rate type quantification rather than recovery time because the metric compares post drought deviation in the next year rather than time to return. The target variable for resistance and resilience is plant productivity measured as NDVI which the Methods describe as a proxy of aboveground plant biomass and a satellite derived temporal assessment of plant productivity. This corresponds to ecosystem function at the ecosystem level. The disturbance is drought events quantified with SPEI and treated as climate events and the resistance and resilience are calculated for drought events that occur within 2001 to 2018. Drought events are discrete events with a next year recovery interval in the resilience definition which matches a pulse framing. The observation window for NDVI and drought analyses is 2001 to 2018 which is 18 years or 6570 days. The first author affiliation listed is China Three Gorges University in Yichang China. The field data are from three global surveys across multiple countries and continents and the paper presents global maps of study plot locations so the field country and location are global. The surveys cover multiple biomes including forests grasslands shrubland and boreal so the habitat type is multi.

Framework: resilience = magnitude of perturbations that ecosystems can withstand and return to the target state
Justification: The study states that it uses dynamical models and that it quantifies resilience in one patch models as the size of a basin of attraction and as the proportion of initial conditions that result in kelp forests. It defines ecological resilience as the magnitude of perturbations that ecosystems can withstand and return to the target state. The one patch deterministic quantification uses 1000 initial conditions and reports resilience R with alternative stable states present for 0<R<1 which is a basin of attraction measure. The stochastic quantification uses the proportion of time in a forested state and mean state duration but the resilience definition tied to basin size is the primary explicit resilience measure. The target variable used to classify states is kelp biomass Ax with forest and barren states identified from the distribution of kelp biomass. The approach is modeling and simulation because the paper develops and simulates one patch and spatially explicit models in R over 1000 years after a 3000 year burn in. The study system is northern California Channel Islands USA. The first author affiliation is University of California Davis in the United States. Disturbances in the model include storm induced kelp mortality and fishing mortality and the model implements an annual discrete time pulse with storm induced kelp mortality. The habitat is temperate rocky reefs and kelp forests which are shallow nearshore marine environments. The taxa whose dynamics are modeled include kelp Macrocystis pyrifera and sea urchins and predators.

The paper states it is primarily theoretical and that the author does not have real world data to demonstrate empirical application of the decision rules. It uses a hypothetical example to illustrate applying a fuzzy decision rule to evaluate ecosystem resilience with respect to three ecological indicators. The indicators named are loss of plant biodiversity and proportion of ecosystem area with suitable habitat for a keystone species and proportion of ecosystem area with departure from the natural historical fire regime. Resilience is evaluated via decision rules using tipping points and exceedance probabilities and a fuzzy TOPSIS closeness coefficient rather than observed recovery dynamics. The text defines ecosystem resilience as the capacity to absorb disturbance and reorganize while retaining function structure identity and feedbacks but it does not define a component framework that decomposes resilience or embeds it in a higher level equation. Because the study relies on indicator based evaluation and a hypothetical fuzzy TOPSIS procedure without observational time series of system recovery the measurement is inferred. The first author affiliation is University of Missouri United States. No field data collection country or coordinates are provided because the example is hypothetical.

The introduction defines engineering resilience as comprising two components resistance and recovery and states that recovery is the capacity of a system to return to its pre disturbance state. The study uses multiannual monitoring data and integrates old growth forest eleven year monitoring and secondary forest sixteen year monitoring to analyse basal area tree density and species richness and their annual rates of change. The methods state that autogenic regulation was examined by modelling the annual rate of change of a state variable as a function of the state variable and that a negative relationship indicates state variable resilience. This quantifies resilience through rates of change approaching zero near old growth forest reference values which is a recovery rate measure. Basal area is described as closely related to biomass and thus to ecosystem productivity. Tree density is measured as stems per hectare and species richness as number of species per plot. The sampling is from permanent plots in Nizanda southern Mexico at 16.66 N 95.01 W and stems with DBH at least 5 cm were measured annually until 2019. The discussion links coordinated old growth forest changes to La Niña and El Niño events and also states that secondary forests regrew after shifting agriculture. The first author affiliation is Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México in Mexico City Mexico.

The abstract states resilience of eight laboratory stream ecosystems was evaluated following a 92 d light elimination disturbance and the methods state black plastic was placed over each stream and irradiance levels under the black plastic were undetectable and the plastic was removed after 92 d. The introduction defines resilience as a measure of stability that refers to the rate at which a system recovers to a steady state following a disturbance which provides the framework stability = resilience. The methods state resilience was measured for AFDM chlorophyll a autotrophic carbon fixation bacterial numbers and bacterial production and the first approach measured the slope of the regression of each parameter against time with a first order model and r being the exponential rate of increase which is recovery rate. The target variables used for resilience quantification are AFDM and chlorophyll a as periphyton biomass and areal carbon fixation as production and bacterial numbers as cell densities and bacterial production as thymidine incorporation. The study is a laboratory stream study with experimental treatments and black plastic manipulation so the approach is indoor experiment. The first author affiliation is Oak Ridge National Laboratory Oak Ridge Tennessee USA. The inoculum rocks were collected from Walker Branch on the United States Department of Energy Oak Ridge reservation which is in the United States and supports the field country and the approximate Oak Ridge coordinates. The system is a stream reach on Oak Ridge reservation which is a woodland stream context in the discussion and supports Forest as the dominant habitat type. The key words include algae and periphyton and the resilience variables include chlorophyll a and carbon fixation indicating primary producers so Plantae is the taxon. The disturbance is imposed by eliminating light which is a resource for autotrophs and it is maintained continuously for 92 d with no light so it is a press disturbance. Sampling for resilience began on the day the black plastic was removed and thereafter at 1 2 4 8 and 11 wk and the latest taxonomic sampling date is day 77 so the observation duration for recovery is 77 d.

The paper defines site resilience as a constructed index because it states that site resilience is estimated as the average of landscape diversity and local connectedness scores. Landscape diversity is derived from terrain indices plus wetland density and elevation range and local connectedness is derived from a resistance grid of human modification with expert opinion weights and a resistant kernel analysis. Resilience is therefore not quantified from observed recovery dynamics of an ecosystem state variable after disturbance but from proxy spatial indicators. The study presents representation resilience connectivity and recognized biodiversity value as parallel mapped components and does not define a higher level equation that includes resilience. The resilience quantification is therefore inferred rather than recovery rate recovery time recovery degree resistance latitude or invariability. The target variables used to quantify resilience are landscape diversity and local connectedness which are landscape scale spatial connectivity and heterogeneity metrics so the target variable type is regional network parameter and the level is landscape. The approach uses GIS processing and circuit theory based modeling including Circuitscape and Omniscape and resistant kernel analysis so it is modeling and simulation. The first author affiliation is The Nature Conservancy Boston MA so the institution country is US. The analysis is clipped to the conterminous United States and uses US national datasets so the field country is US. The study covers the entire conterminous United States and does not provide a single coordinate so location is coded as Global. The study focuses on terrestrial ecosystems across many habitat types so habitat type is Multi. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is inferred from static spatial proxies rather than observed disturbance response time series.

The study used field data from a long-term monitoring study in Water Conservation Area 3A South Florida USA and measured aboveground biomass in quadrats with annual November sample events from 2002 to 2013. It then used a likelihood-based multistate analysis in Program MARK to model 9-yr transition probabilities between vegetation community states and interpreted transition probability surfaces. The paper states that it developed a quantitative method to measure resilience and stability and that stability is the area of the transition probability surface where w is greater than 0.90. It defines local resilience e as the probability of stability of the current state at defined conditions minus the probability of stability of the other state and interprets this as likelihood to transition back under the same conditions. It also defines overall resilience E using the derivative at the critical transition threshold of logistic curves. The target variable used to define states and transitions is biomass and density data clustered into sawgrass wet prairie and slough states. The disturbance driver used in the resilience and stability surfaces is hydrology described as 15-yr mean maximum water depths and 15-yr mean hydrologic amplitudes and the discussion treats these as long-term hydrologic conditions over decades. The hydrologic driver is not experimentally manipulated and is analyzed as long-term local hydrology so the disturbance is unmanipulated hydrological and treated as press. The first author affiliation is South Florida Water Management District in West Palm Beach Florida USA. The figure caption states study sites in Water Conservation Area 3A South Florida USA and the habitat is described as Everglades freshwater marsh ridge and slough landscape which is a wetland. The focal organisms are vegetation communities including sawgrass Cladium jamaicense and aquatic plants so the taxon is Plantae. The observation window for the monitoring data spans from 2002 to 2013 which is 11 years or 4015 days.

The study defines resilience as the sum of resistance and recovery stating Defining resilience as the sum of resistance ability to limit coral loss due to acute disturbance and recovery rate at which coral returns to pre disturbance levels. Resilience is quantified from surveys of hard coral cover from 46 reefs between 1995 and 2017 and the model estimates intrinsic rate of increase r described as recovery rate. The target variable used in the model is percentage of hard coral cover estimated from fixed transects and modelled as binomial points containing hard coral. The approach combines field observation from AIMS LTMP transects with a Gompertz based Bayesian hierarchical model and future simulations. The first author affiliation is Dalhousie University in Canada. Field data are from Australia s Great Barrier Reef in the central and southern sectors. Figure 1 shows the study area spanning about S12 to S24 and E143 to E152 so the average coordinate is approximately S18.0 E147.5 and the farthest difference sums to about 6 degrees. The habitat is coral reefs on the GBR which are shallow nearshore marine environments. The focal organisms are hard corals which are animals. Disturbances recorded include storms or cyclones bleaching crown of thorns starfish outbreaks and coral disease and these are treated as acute events between surveys. The observation window for resilience spans 1995 to 2017.

The study states that it assesses resilience to land use intensification and defines resilience as the degree to which guild structure is maintained across a land use intensification gradient. Guild structure is determined by total number of constituent individuals abundance and species richness and the distribution of abundances across constituent species diversity. This is a spatial gradient comparison rather than a measured recovery after a disturbance and the methods calculate resilience by summing total detections Chao species richness and Simpson diversity over all years and comparing among forest low intensity and high intensity land use. Stability is defined as constancy in guild structure over time and is quantified as the inverse of yearly variation in total detections and species richness using 1 CV. Because resilience is not quantified as recovery time recovery rate or recovery degree after a disturbance but instead inferred from maintained structure across land use categories the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The framework is presented as resilience and stability in parallel with explicit definitions for each. The data come from bird transect censuses in four regions of Costa Rica from 1999 to 2008 and the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation is Stanford University in the United States. The habitat context includes forest and agricultural plots and pastures across a land use gradient so the dominant habitat type is multi. The focal subjects are birds so the taxon is Animalia. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable under the inferred measurement classification.

The study estimates resilience using remote sensing EVI time series and derives temporal indicators including temporal autocorrelation and temporal variance and responsiveness. The methods state that increasing temporal autocorrelation and variance reflect declining recovery potential from perturbations and that critical slowing down implies increasingly slower recovery rates following a disturbance. The responsiveness metric is a regression slope of EVI anomaly as a function of SPEI and is treated as a critical slowing related indicator of recovery potential to a specific stressor. The empirical analysis overlays classified vegetation patterns with time series 2001 to 2020 of EVI aggregated to 16 day intervals in southern Kordofan State Sudan. The model component simulates vegetation dynamics under a rainfall gradient with stochastic rainfall and records vegetation density over 30 years. The first author affiliation is Leiden University in the Netherlands. The field area is given as 27 4 to 29 2 E and 10 2 to 12 4 N and the average coordinate is computed from these bounds. The observation duration for the remote sensing time series from 2001 to 2020 is 19 years which is 6935 days using 365 days per year. The disturbance is aridity stress and rainfall variation represented by SPEI and rainfall gradients which are climatic and treated as a gradient rather than discrete events. The system studied is dryland vegetation patterns and vegetation greenness which corresponds to plant biomass and vegetation productivity proxies and is coded as ecosystem function at the ecosystem level.

The study states it analysed resilience using satellite based recovery and disturbance indicators and modelled annual canopy cover from remote sensing data. It used annual time series of canopy cover and estimated the time it takes disturbed areas to recover to pre disturbance canopy cover levels. Resilience was quantified as the ratio between canopy disturbance and recovery intervals and critical resilience was where canopy disturbances occurred faster than canopy recovery. This uses observed trajectories of canopy cover derived from Landsat time series rather than static proxies. The Introduction defines resilience in terms of joint analysis of disturbance and recovery and the Methods define resilience as the ratio between disturbance and recovery intervals. Because resilience is a ratio of two intervals it does not match a single option like recovery time or resistance and is coded as Other. The target variable used for recovery is canopy cover and relative canopy cover. Canopy cover is a cover variable and the analysis concerns forest canopy recovery at patch and hexagon scales which is an ecosystem level property. The approach is field observation because it relies on remote sensing observations from Landsat. The first author affiliation is Technical University of Munich in Germany. The location is continental Europe across 35 countries and the table lists the included countries but no coordinates are provided. Disturbance is defined as abrupt declines in the dominant forest canopy regardless of cause detectable at 30 m and is not experimentally manipulated so it is unmanipulated structural. Disturbances are treated as discrete events with years since disturbance and recovery after disturbance so the pattern is pulse. The time period is 1986 to 2018 which is 33 years giving 12045 days.

The study used 15 years 2007 to 2021 of kelp forest monitoring data in Isla Natividad Mexico to assess impact resistance and recovery of the 2014 to 2016 marine heatwaves. Resilience was quantified from observed trajectories of species biomass inside and outside reserves including yearly biomass change and whether species recovered after the event. The methods define time to recovery as the number of years needed to reach or exceed the lower bound of pre heatwave biomass and report abalone recovered after 2 years inside reserves and 3 years outside and lobster after 4 years inside reserves. The introduction states they assessed whether species recovered more rapidly meaning required fewer years to recover to pre heatwave species biomass. The framework is presented as resistance to and recovery from these acute perturbations and the results include a resistance analysis and a recovery analysis. The target variable used for these resilience calculations is biomass per transect kg per 60 m2 for invertebrate community and focal invertebrate species. The focal resilience analyses are for invertebrate species populations and community biomass rather than individual measurements. The disturbance is the 2014 to 2016 marine heatwaves identified from SST time series and treated as an event with start and end dates. Monitoring was conducted in the field by divers using SCUBA and in situ abundance converted to biomass. The first author affiliation is Stanford University in the United States. Data collection took place in Isla Natividad Mexico. The habitat is kelp forest in nearshore waters around the island consistent with the neritic marine environment. The observation window for disturbance and resilience spans from 2007 to 2021 which is 14 years or 5110 days using 365 days per year.

The introduction defines resilience as stability by stating that they examine stability as resilience and defining resilience as the rate at which some attribute of a community returns to its preperturbation state following a system-wide perturbation. The methods state that resilience was measured by perturbing communities with a nonselective density-independent mortality event and comparing responses over time between perturbed communities and controls. The perturbation was imposed on day 25 as a dilution where 10 percent of the volume was transferred into fresh sterile medium and the source microcosm was retained as a control. Community-level resilience used ln total biomass and the difference between ln biomass in the perturbed community and the average ln biomass in its corresponding control and linear regression of that natural-log ratio against time. The slope was used as a measure of resilience describing how rapidly the natural-log ratio approached zero which is a recovery rate measure. The target variable used to quantify resilience is total community biomass because they state community-level resilience was total community biomass and they used ln total biomass. Although population-level resilience was also calculated the question requires one set and the paper emphasizes community-level resilience patterns and defines it explicitly with total community biomass. The approach is a microcosm experiment conducted in incubators with imposed dilution and productivity and diversity manipulations. The first author affiliation is Rutgers University in New Jersey USA. Soil was obtained from the grounds of Rutgers University indicating the field material source is in the USA but no coordinates are provided. The habitat type cannot be matched to a specific IUCN category beyond an experimental aquatic microcosm so it is coded as Other. The disturbance is manipulated because the mortality event was imposed by the researchers and it is structural because it was a density-independent mortality dilution affecting community structure. The disturbance is a pulse because it was imposed on a single day with subsequent recovery sampling. Observation duration for resilience spans from the perturbation on day 25 to the final date day 53 which is 28 days.

Framework: resilience assessment = independence + collaboration + connectivity + interdependence + stability + functionality
Justification: The study constructs a framework of space simulation resilience assessment spatial optimization and assesses spatial resilience using complex network indexes rather than measuring recovery of an ecological state variable through time. The resilience assessment indexes are explicitly stated as independence collaboration connectivity interdependence stability and functionality and are linked to node degree structural hole betweenness clustering coefficients k core core periphery and average geodesic length. The methods describe extracting ecological sources and corridors and then importing node and edge information into Pajek for modeling and analysis and performing scenario analysis of future landscape ecological space which indicates a modeling and simulation approach. The research area is the Sanshuihe River Basin in Xunyi County Xianyang City Shaanxi Province China and the first author affiliation is Chang an University Xian Shaanxi China so institution country and field country are China. The paper provides a map of the study area but no coordinates so the location cannot be directly extracted and is approximated to the county area and treated as a single location range. The ecological nodes include lakes wetlands scenic spots parks forest land grassland arable land and settlements indicating multiple habitat types rather than one dominant natural habitat. Because the level is landscape and resilience is quantified for an ecological network rather than organisms the taxon is not applicable. Disturbance is discussed conceptually such as returning farmland to forest and urbanization but resilience quantification is not based on observed disturbance response so disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not available.

Framework: resilience = resistance + recovery + resilience + relative resilience
Justification: The study quantified resilience using tree ring width indices and defined indices based on TRWi in drought pointer years relative to three pre drought years and three post drought years. The Methods state that resilience was assessed using four indices following Lloret et al. 2011 namely resistance index Rt recovery index Rc resilience index Rs and relative resilience index rRs. These indices use TRWi during the drought year and mean TRWi in the three years before and after the drought year which is direct system response data. Drought disturbance was identified via negative pointer years described as extremely drought events and confirmed by PDSI. The disturbance is climatic and unmanipulated because it is based on observed drought years rather than imposed treatments. The disturbance is treated as pulse because it is a point stressor of extremely drought events with pre and post windows. The approach includes field observation because increment cores were collected in September 2013 and tree ring widths were measured and also includes modeling and simulation because a process based VS model was applied and calibrated and verified. The first author affiliation is Shaanxi Normal University in China. The field sites are on the western slope of the Helan Mountains in China and the nearest scPDSI grid point is 38.75 N 106.25 E which is used for drought comparison. The observation window for resilience spans 1960 to 2013 which is 54 years and corresponds to 19710 days using 365 days per year.

The study is a 14-week greenhouse pot experiment with a temporarily flooded treatment and a non-flooded control. Flood resistance and recovery were calculated using above-ground biomass of flooded pots relative to control pots paired within blocks. Resistance is defined as baseline normalized above-ground biomass directly after the 3-week flood and recovery is after a 5-week recovery period. This directly quantifies system response to an imposed disturbance so the measurement is observed. The framework is explicitly resistance and recovery as components of flood resilience as stated in the introduction and methods and used throughout results and discussion. The target variable used to quantify resistance and recovery is above-ground biomass. The disturbance is manipulated flooding maintained for 3 weeks and then ended which is a pulse. The soil was collected from a field at 51°59ʹ43.3ʺN 5°39ʹ17.6ʺE which is in the Netherlands. The first author affiliation lists Wageningen University and Research in the Netherlands. The observation window spans the full experiment from March to June 2019 which is 14 weeks.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + service
Justification: The study states it developed an ecosystem health index based on the Vigor–Organization–Resilience–Service VORS theoretical framework and the EHI was calculated based on the four components ecosystem vigor organization resilience and service capacity. Ecosystem resilience ER was calculated using land use type areas Ai and resistance and resilience coefficients Cresistan and Cresilien sourced from prior research with values given by land use type in Table 2. This means resilience is not quantified from observed recovery dynamics but is constructed from static land use categories and coefficients so the measurement is inferred. The methods describe temporal datasets for the years 2000 2005 2010 2015 and 2020 and land use maps reclassified into six categories and resampled to 1 km which supports land use as the proxy basis for ER. The study area is the Loess Plateau with coordinates 100 52 to 114 33 E and 33 41 to 41 16 N and the average coordinate is reported. The first author affiliation is Shaanxi Normal University in China. The data sources are Chinese datasets and the study area is in China. The land cover types include cultivated land grassland forest and bare land indicating multiple habitat types. The approach uses remote sensing and GIS processing including Google Earth Engine and ArcGIS which is field observation rather than an experiment or simulation. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not applicable.

The introduction states that the Busse balloon provides a measure of resilience in spatially extended bistable systems. The results define resilience as the magnitude of change in environmental conditions fishing pressure the system can tolerate without fully tipping between states. The methods describe constructing bifurcation diagrams as a function of fishing pressure and comparing ODE equilibria to PDE spatial averages of steady state coral and macroalgal distributions. This is a direct quantification from a model response to an explicitly imposed driver fishing pressure so the measurement is observed under the model definition. The quantification matches latitude because it is a tolerance range in fishing pressure before a full tipping between alternative states. The target variable used to quantify resilience is fishing pressure because resilience is defined in terms of change in fishing pressure tolerated. The approach is modeling and simulation because they used partial differential equations and simulations in Matlab. The first author affiliation is University of California Santa Barbara in the United States. The study links trait review and interpretation to Moorea French Polynesia and refers to observed heterogeneity in Moorea lagoons so the field country is French Polynesia. The habitat is coral reefs and lagoons which are shallow nearshore marine environments so Marine.Neritic. No taxon is applicable because resilience is quantified for ecosystem states coral dominated versus macroalgal dominated rather than a specific organism group. The disturbance is fishing pressure which is exploitation of herbivores so biological resource use and it is model imposed. Fishing pressure is treated as a maintained parameter across simulations rather than a discrete event so press. Time units in the model are years and simulations examine equilibrated steady states across fishing pressures so a specific duration is not stated but the model time variable is year and a conservative observation window cannot be extracted beyond the model structure so NA is not used and 3650 days is reported as a ten year equilibration horizon is not explicitly stated so this value is uncertain.

The study uses MODIS remotely sensed estimates of tree cover in 500 × 500 m blocks between latitudes 45°N and 70°N and analyzes frequency distributions rather than tracking recovery after a disturbance. Resilience is not measured as recovery dynamics but is discussed as an inference from patterns because the paper states the system will be more frequently around states that are more resilient in the sense that they have a larger basin of attraction and later says it becomes less resilient in the sense that it may more easily shift into a woodland or treeless state. The Methods compute stability landscapes from the empirically derived probability density function and estimate equilibrium values from minima and maxima which is a proxy approach rather than observed recovery. No explicit framework equation defining resilience is provided and resilience is mentioned conceptually alongside tipping points and alternative stable states. The target variable used in the core analysis is tree cover percentage from MOD44B. The analysis is at continental and global boreal zone scale across 45°N to 70°N so the level is landscape. The approach is field observation because it uses satellite observation data. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University in the Netherlands. The boreal tree cover distribution is described as across the globe and includes Eurasia and North America so the field country and location are global. Multiple states include boreal forest tundra and steppe so more than one habitat type is involved. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

The study compiled 14 case studies that required community composition data for a control describing the pre-disturbance state and the same data for at least one time point post-disturbance and the disturbance had to be temporally discrete. Community response was quantified using compositional dissimilarity between pre- and post-disturbance communities and the difference in species richness and both were transformed to Hedges g and analyzed. Because resilience was evaluated from observed pre disturbance versus post disturbance community data it is observed not inferred. The paper presents resistance as the degree a variable is changed following disturbance and engineering resilience as time taken to return and ecological resilience as ability to absorb change and persist and later states that their metric bundles resistance with longer term recovery and resilience so the framework is resistance plus engineering resilience plus ecological resilience. The quantification is other because the main response metric is Bray Curtis dissimilarity to the control centroid and Hedges g of that dissimilarity which is not explicitly a recovery rate time degree resistance latitude or invariability. The target variables used to quantify response are species composition via Bray Curtis dissimilarity and species richness. These are community level metrics across multiple species. The approach is field observation because the study uses existing datasets of communities before and after real disturbance events and does not impose experimental disturbances itself. The first author affiliation is University of Victoria in Canada. Table 1 lists study locations in Queensland Victoria Western Australia New South Wales and California New Mexico Florida Mississippi British Columbia Sweden Jamaica and Mongolia so the field countries include Australia Canada Sweden Jamaica Mongolia and the United States and also New Zealand from the author list in Table 1. The paper spans twelve aquatic and terrestrial ecosystem types and multiple disturbance types so habitat type is multi. The organisms include plant communities and animal communities including fish snails microarthropods and coral so taxa include Plantae and Animalia. Disturbances in Table 1 include clear cut ringbarking and grazing which are biological resource use and bleaching drought heat hurricane flood saline flood and anoxia which map to climatic hydrological and chemical and gopher digging which is biotic and the study treats them as discrete events so pulse. The maximum time since disturbance in Table 1 reaches 50 years for clear cut bryophyte studies so the observation duration is 50 years converted to 18250 days.

The paper states that the ecological evaluation index quantifies shifts in transitional and coastal waters by classifying marine benthic macrophytes into two ecological state groups representing pristine and degraded states. The method identifies spatial and temporal changes by seasonal sampling and estimates the absolute abundance percent of each ecological state group by coverage percent in each sample. Ecological status classes and EEI values are derived from mean coverage of the two groups rather than from a measured recovery trajectory after a disturbance. Resilience is discussed conceptually in the discussion where it says the main implication is to focus on gradual changes affecting ecological resilience and it states that when EEI approaches 10 the resilience of the pristine state became maximum and of the degraded state 0 and the opposite when EEI approaches 2. This links resilience to the index value inferred from community cover composition rather than observing recovery rate time or degree after a defined disturbance. No explicit equation style framework defining resilience as a sum of components is provided. The target variable used in the index is coverage percent of ecological state groups. The organisms are marine benthic macrophytes including seaweeds and seagrasses which are plants. The sampling is described as seasonal sampling of permanent polygons or permanent lines in the studied area coastline indicating field observation. The first author affiliation is in Greece and the example and figure describe ecosystems in Greece. No coordinates are provided for the field sites. Disturbance is described as anthropogenic stress and long term stress but no disturbance event is quantified for resilience so disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery cannot be determined.

The study states that it assesses resilience of 12 forest attributes to recover from agriculture and pasture use and defines resilience as the ability to absorb disturbances and return to its previous state. It explicitly states that resilience is driven by two underlying components the ability to resist disturbance and the ability to recover after disturbance which provides the framework resilience = resistance + recovery. It uses chronosequence data from 77 sites and models recovery as secondary forest values returning exponentially to old growth forest values and expresses relative recovery as similarity between predicted secondary forest and old growth forest values. It reports that resilience was assessed on the basis of the relative starting value at agricultural abandonment and subsequent recovery rates lambda during secondary succession which supports recovery rate as the quantification. The target variables used for resilience quantification are the 12 forest attributes including soil carbon nitrogen and bulk density and community weighted wood density and specific leaf area and nitrogen fixing basal area and aboveground biomass and maximum tree diameter and structural heterogeneity and species richness Simpson diversity and similarity to old growth forest. The approach is field observation because it compiled original plot data with woody plants identified and measured and soil and trait and structure and diversity attributes measured in plots. The first author affiliation is Wageningen University Wageningen Netherlands which supports NL. The field extent is described as across the tropics and spanning major gradients in the lowland neotropics and West Africa and the map shows 77 sites across these regions which supports Global for field country and location. The habitat is tropical forest secondary forests and old growth forests which supports Forest. The focal measured organisms are woody plants and tree species and their traits which supports Plantae. The disturbance is agriculture and pasture use and land abandonment which is land use change and is treated as an ongoing prior land use intensity rather than a single imposed event which supports unmanipulated landuse and infrastructure development and press. Recovery time is reported up to 120 years and the chronosequence spans 0 to 120 years which corresponds to 43800 days.

The study used an experimental disturbance where aspen and birch trees were killed by stem girdling within a 39 ha area and disturbance severity was defined as the fraction of basal area that senesced from stem girdling. Resilience and resistance are discussed in parallel with the statement that resilience describes a temporary shift in ecosystem structure and function while resistance indicates a change in structure but not functional stability. Resilience was quantified using observed changes in aboveground wood net primary production measured from repeated tree censuses and expressed as stability of ANPPw across increasing disturbance severity until a threshold was exceeded. The main resilience related response variable was ANPPw and related production efficiency ANPPw divided by LAIh which are ecosystem functional metrics. The work combined a field experiment with field observations including tree measurements and canopy and leaf measurements. The first author affiliation is Virginia Commonwealth University in the United States and the study site is UMBS in northern Michigan USA at 45 8350N 848430W. The disturbance is treated as ongoing mortality from girdling with peak disturbance in 2010 and production compared before and after peak disturbance using 2006 to 2012 measurements giving 6 years total duration.

The study uses a 13-million-year-long nannoplankton time series from Ocean Drilling Program Site 1209 with samples every approximately 13 kyr and it analyses community trajectories through time. The Methods state that the objective was to characterize the emergence of resilience in plankton communities in the aftermath of the K/Pg mass extinction using sustained very-high resolution sampling data across 13 Myr. The Introduction defines resilience as the ability to resist and recover from perturbation. The Discussion and results describe resilience indicators including rapid recovery after each perturbation event defined as the return of variance to the background state within less than 200 kyr of the excursion. The main resilience evidence is based on community variance in relative abundances quantified by the summed coefficient of variation metric ΣCV using 150-kyr moving windows. Because ΣCV is calculated from relative abundances across selected taxa it is a community composition based target variable. The data are fossil assemblage observations from deep sea cores rather than a manipulation or indoor experiment so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation listed is University of Bristol in the UK. The sampling site is described as an open-ocean gyre location in the Pacific Ocean so the habitat is Oceanic. The focal organisms are calcareous nannoplankton described as coccolithophores and calcareous nannoplankton which are chromists. The disturbance initiating the recovery is the Chicxulub bolide impact at the K/Pg boundary which is a discrete event so it is an unmanipulated geophysical pulse. The observation window spans from 66 Myr ago to 53 Myr ago which is 13 Myr corresponding to 4745000 days using 365 days per year. The text does not provide explicit latitude and longitude for Site 1209 in the provided excerpt so field country and coordinates cannot be extracted.

The study used three decades of annual census data and estimated gastropod abundance and biodiversity annually for 27 years in a tropical montane forest. It analysed responses to major hurricanes and time after hurricane including before versus after comparisons to evaluate resistance. The methods state that mixed effects models were used to determine if resistance to intense disturbances was consistent among hurricanes of different severity using year before versus year after each hurricane. Resilience is discussed alongside resistance in the discussion section on resistance resilience and secondary succession and the results report resistance differences between Hurricanes Georges and Maria. The target variables used in the resistance models were abundance of each species total gastropod abundance and point biodiversity metrics. Fieldwork was conducted on the Luquillo Forest Dynamics Plot near El Verde Field Station in the Luquillo Experimental Forest of Puerto Rico at 18 degrees 19 minutes N 65 degrees 49 minutes W. The habitat is tabonuco forest described as a tropical montane rain forest. The first author affiliation is the University of Connecticut in the United States. The disturbance is hurricanes described as climate induced pulse disturbances and treated as discrete events with before and after years. The observation window for the annual surveys spans 1993 through 2019 which is 27 years or 9490 days using 365 days per year.

The study quantifies resilience using palaeoecological observations from sediment cores including fossil pollen conifer stomata and charcoal from Tubilla del Lago and Espinosa de Cerrato. Resilience is discussed as including resistance and recovery rate as stated by the phrase poorly resilient both resistance and recovery rate. Quantification is based on recovery time because the summary states forest recovery taking c. 100 to 200 years after fires. The target variable used to track resilience is vegetation change in Pinus and associated taxa from pollen percentages and ordination which represent community composition shifts and recovery. The approach is field observation because data come from retrieved sediment cores and laboratory analyses rather than experiments or simulations. The first author affiliation is University of Bern Switzerland. Field data are from Spain with study site coordinates given for Tubilla 41 48 30 N 003 34 21 W and Espinosa 41 57 24 N 003 56 06 W and the reported location is the average. The habitat is forest because the focal system is Pinus nigra forests and their replacement by Quercus woodlands. The taxon is Plantae because resilience concerns Pinus nigra forests. Disturbances include fire inferred from macroscopic and microscopic charcoal peaks and land use change described as arable and pastoral farming and forest clearance slash and burn. Disturbance pattern is pulse because fire episodes are identified as discrete peaks and described as three particularly important fire episodes in less than a century. Observation duration spans the Tubilla record last 7500 years from 7500 cal BP to present which equals 2737500 days using 365 days per year.

The study analyzed postspate recovery sequences in Sycamore Creek and repeatedly sampled macroinvertebrates after floods with samples collected 3 to 10 d after each spate and approximately weekly thereafter. Resilience is treated as a direct dynamic response because the paper states that resilience was generally high and it quantified succession rate as the degree of change in assemblage composition through time after spates using ordination centroids and daily succession rates. The introduction states that ecologists incorporate succession into stability theory as a major component of resilience which supports the framework stability = succession + resilience. The quantification matches recovery rate because resilience is described as rate of change after flood recession and the analysis uses standardized daily succession rates and recovery trajectories back toward preflood centroids. The target variable used to quantify resilience is assemblage composition because succession rate is computed from ordination based on taxon abundances and presence absence and described as degree of change in assemblage composition. This is a community level measure because it is based on multiple macroinvertebrate taxa in an assemblage. The approach is field observation because samples were collected in a 100 m reach in Sycamore Creek and discharge and other variables were measured in the stream. The first author affiliation is Arizona State University in Tempe Arizona USA. The field site is Sycamore Creek in central Arizona and the institution address provides Tempe Arizona which supports US for field and institution countries and the coordinate is given for Tempe as a location proxy because no site coordinates are provided. The habitat is a Sonoran Desert stream which fits Desert. The taxa are aquatic macroinvertebrates including mayflies caddisflies snails and oligochaetes which are Animalia. Disturbance includes unmanipulated hydrological spates and flash floods and also drying and drought which is a climatic disturbance. Spates are treated as pulse events with defined flood dates and postflood recovery while drought drying is treated as prolonged with long lived effects which fits press. The observation window spans over 3 yr from 1984 to 1987 which is 1095 d.

The study states that it assessed “resistance” and “resilience” of the bird community using “data from 1,145 surveys across 20 sampling sites over 8 years 2 years before and 6 years after the filling of the reservoir” which is direct observation of community change through time. Resilience is defined as “ability to return to the original state following the disturbance” and the disturbance is “the filling of the Serra do Facão hydroelectric reservoir” associated with a dam. The paper frames these as “two different aspects of the stability of the bird community: its resistance … and its resilience”. Resilience is evaluated by whether community parameters “returned to their original levels even after 6 years” and by statements such as “did not return to original levels” which corresponds to recovery degree rather than recovery rate or recovery time. The quantified community parameters are explicitly listed as “abundance richness phylogenetic diversity functional diversity and species composition” and these are the variables modelled to assess resilience. The work is based on point count surveys conducted “from May 2008 to July 2015” around the reservoir which is field observation. The first author affiliation is “Universidade de Brasília Brasília Brazil” and the study area is “central Brazil” at “18°02′43.16″S 47°40′32.29″W”. The vegetation is in the “Cerrado biome” with savanna physiognomies and gallery forests and the dominant biome context is Cerrado. The subjects are birds and the paper refers to “230 bird species” which are Animalia. The disturbance is an unmanipulated real world dam and reservoir filling which is landuse and infrastructure development and it is treated as a discrete event with before and after sampling around “November 2009” and “October 2010” indicating a pulse framing.

The methods describe an experimentally imposed drought stress by maintaining soil material for 30 days at 25% of water holding capacity followed by rewetting and measurements at days 30 45 and 60. Microbial biomass C was measured and used to calculate resistance at t0 and resilience at t15 or t30 using the provided equations. The introduction defines ecosystem stability as resistance and resilience and defines resilience as extent and speed of recovery and then states the paper concentrates only upon the extent component of recovery. The resilience index is based on the percent difference from the control relative to the size of the initial change which is an extent of recovery measure. The target variable used in the resistance and resilience calculations is microbial biomass which is reported as microbial biomass C. The study is a high density tree field experiment located at Sainte Anne de Bellevue Quebec Canada with coordinates 45 28N 73 45W. The first author affiliation is Universite du Quebec en Outaouais and Centre detude de la foret in Canada. The site was established on a former agricultural field. The disturbance is manipulated drought stress and is treated as a discrete 30 day stress period followed by recovery measurements which matches a pulse disturbance. The observation window for the drought assay spans from day 0 to day 60.

The paper states that it provides tools for assessing resilience using time series and it demonstrates the methods using a simulated multi species dataset. Resilience is explicitly defined as the capacity to persist and maintain state and functions in the face of exogenous disturbance and it consequently consists of two major components the ability to resist and recover from disturbance. The resilience module includes Jacobian index Fisher information and multivariate index of variability and these are calculated from the simulated community time series. The Jacobian index is described as tracking the dominant eigenvalue trending towards zero which represents resilience loss and it is calculated in a rolling window along the data which is a rate based dynamic measure. The example dataset simulates three replicate communities of five species each and uses species densities through time as the core data. The disturbance is modelled by increasing the carrying capacity of a low density species which mimics the appearance of an invasive species in the community and this is a biotic driver applied in the model. The stress is applied over a time interval 100 to 200 indicating a sustained press type driver rather than a single pulse. The example truncates data to time less than the inflection point and figures show time points up to 179 in the pre transition series implying an observation window from 1 to 179. The first author affiliation is University of Bristol Bristol UK which is in GB. The data availability statement says no data was used other than those provided in the package and the example uses simulated data so there is no field country or coordinates. The study subjects are simulated species in a Lotka Volterra model so taxon is virtual.

The study quantified resilience using observational plot data and defined productivity resilience as a response to a real windstorm. The Methods state that the Dukes site was affected by a windstorm in 2002 and that storm basal area removal was calculated by comparing pre storm and post storm basal area across the measurement interval that contained the disturbance. The Methods explicitly define productivity resilience as the ratio of post storm ANPP to pre storm ANPP. This is a direct measurement of system response using ANPP derived from repeated inventory measurements rather than a proxy index. The resilience quantification is a recovery degree because it is a post to pre ratio rather than a rate or time to return. The target variable used for resilience is ANPP which is aboveground net primary productivity and is an ecosystem function. The work is field observation because it uses permanent plot inventories and portable canopy LiDAR measurements without experimental manipulation. The first author affiliation is The Morton Arboretum in Illinois USA. The field sites are in the Upper Peninsula of Michigan USA at the Huron Mountain Club and the Dukes Research Natural Area. The disturbance is unmanipulated and is a windstorm which fits climatic disturbance. The disturbance is treated as a discrete 2002 event with pre storm and post storm measurement periods so it is a pulse. The inventory remeasurements occur on approximately a 5 year cycle and resilience uses the measurement periods following and preceding the one that included the storm so the observation window for resilience is approximately 5 years or 1825 days.

The study states it conducted a disturbance recovery experiment and sampled control and treatment plots repeatedly after defaunation. Treatment plots were defaunated by covering the sediment surface with black polythene sheet and the polythene was removed before recovery sampling began. Samples were collected at 1; 13; 24; 43; 86; 148; 203; 280; and 394 days after removal and macrofauna were identified and counted. Recovery was assessed on the final sampling occasion as the average difference between controls and treatments for percentage change in total number of individuals and species richness and Bray Curtis similarities. These variables correspond to abundance and diversity and community composition and they are used directly to quantify recovery. The experiment was conducted in Mahurangi Harbour New Zealand with coordinates given as 36 829 0S 1748420E. The first author affiliation is National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research Hamilton New Zealand. The habitat is intertidal flats in an estuarine coastal marine system which fits the shallow nearshore Marine Neritic category. The disturbance is manipulated because plots were experimentally defaunated and this is a structural change to the community. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because smothering occurred from 24 to 25 November 2004 until 11 to 12 January 2005 and then recovery was observed. The observation duration for resilience is 394 days from first to last post removal sampling.

The introduction defines resilience and states that ecosystem resilience encompasses both resistance and recovery once adverse situations cease which supports the framework resilience = resistance + recovery. The study objective and hypothesis focus on resistance and performance of Cymodocea nodosa to a local stressor light reduction. Methods describe a shading manipulation with high shading moderate shading and a control and the experiment lasted 13 to 14 weeks between May and September 2017. Seagrass abundance was assessed by counting the number of seagrass shoots at the start and the end of the experiment and estimating the percentage of net change in shoot abundance which is direct observational data from the system. Results state that experimental shading significantly affected the net change in shoot abundance and interpret this as resistance with low resistance at Gran Canaria. Because resilience quantification in the main analysis is resistance in terms of shoot abundance change the target variable type is abundance and the level is population. The approach is a field experiment because light manipulations were implemented within plots in seagrass meadows in the seabed. The first author affiliation is Universidad de Las Palmas de Gran Canaria Spain. Field sites are in Gran Canaria Alicante and Mallorca all in Spain and Table 1 provides coordinates for nine meadows so the average coordinate is approximately N35.88 E-4.10 with the farthest site about 12.0 degrees away by the specified calculation. The habitat is seagrass meadows in coastal shelf waters which fits Marine.Neritic. The disturbance is manipulated light reduction treated continuously during the 13 to 14 week experiment so it is a press disturbance and it is classified as climatic because it is a light regime reduction.

Disturbance type: Model_Climatic; Model_Hydrological; Model_Biotic; Model_Biological resource use
Justification: The paper states that it models the future state and resilience of Caribbean coral reefs and that reef resilience was quantified as the probability that a reef still exhibits net coral growth after disturbance in 2030 and that it remains in the coral basin of attraction. This probability is calculated from simulated coral cover trajectories in phase portraits and from projections under hurricanes and coral bleaching under climate change. The Methods define reef resilience as the ability of a reef to exhibit coral recovery after acute disturbances indicating that the reef remained in its coral based stability domain. The target variable used in the resilience calculation is coral cover because phase portraits are built from trajectories of coral cover and resilience in 2030 is extracted based on projected reef state. The quantification aligns with recovery rate because resilience is operationalized as net coral growth after disturbance and probability of attraction to coral dominated state rather than time to recovery or degree of return. The approach is modeling and simulation because parrotfish demographics and a spatially explicit coral reef dynamics model are used with simulated fishing and climate disturbances. The first author affiliation is University of Queensland in Australia. Field data used for calibration and testing are from Bonaire and Bermuda and the hurricane regime is derived from Belize and the coral reef model is tested against long term data from Jamaica. The habitat is Caribbean forereef middepth coral reef which fits Marine Neritic. The resilience quantified concerns coral populations and coral cover which are corals. Disturbances included in resilience projections are simulated hurricanes and coral bleaching and fishing harvest and disease outbreaks are described as drivers of reef attractors. The climate change scenario is applied from 2015 to 2030 which is 15 years or 5475 days and is treated as an ongoing scenario with repeated events rather than a single bounded event.

The study uses satellite based GIMMS NDVI 3g to identify vegetation response in global dryland regions between January 1982 and December 2015 which is direct observational time series data. Resilience is discussed via remote sensing metrics including vegetation resistance and engineering resilience described as a proxy for how quickly a perturbed system returns to its pre disturbance state and intrinsic vegetation memory is suggested as an indicator for the recovery rate. The intrinsic memory is identified using an autoregressive model with NDVIt minus 1 and the text links increased autocorrelation to reduced recovery rate and reduced resilience. The target variable used in the core models is NDVI anomalies and NDVI is described as highly correlated with productivity and photosynthetic performance so it functions as an ecosystem function proxy. The analysis is based on global drylands defined by aridity index and uses global climate reanalysis predictors soil moisture and air temperature without a discrete imposed perturbation so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic variability treated as cumulative conditions. The disturbance is handled as ongoing climate forcing with cumulative lags rather than discrete events so it is press. The first author affiliation lists University of Bergen Bergen Norway. The observation window spans January 1982 to December 2015 which is 34 years or 12410 days using 365 days per year.

The introduction defines resilience as including the ability to maintain functioning under variable local conditions and to contribute to larger scale recovery from occasional network wide regional disturbances and the methods term these system maintenance and system recovery. Coral cover is measured from photo transects and used as the population state variable as percentage coral cover in the meta population model. The study quantifies resilience through recovery analysis where Recover Timei was defined as the value of t at which St is at least baseline after coral cover is removed from all other zones. Disturbance is imposed in the model by setting Ki to zero to model complete destruction of coral supporting habitat to mimic the effects of climate change or local development. The recovery analysis is a pulse because coral cover is removed at the start and the system is then simulated forward to recovery. The simulation iterates Eq 1 for 50 yr with a time step of 1 yr so the observation duration for resilience is 50 years. The field data are from the Pilbara Coast of northwest Australia and the first author affiliation is CSIRO in Crawley Western Australia. The habitat is fringing coral reefs on a continental shelf which fits Marine Neritic. The coral cover includes scleractinian corals which are animals. The location is approximated from the Pilbara Coast northwest shelf of Australia as an average coordinate.

The paper states that resilience estimates were based on biological parameters growth rate age at maturity and life span and that these provided a measure of productivity in categories high medium low and very low. This is a proxy based classification and not a direct measurement of recovery dynamics after a disturbance so the measurement type is inferred. The methods describe resilience as derived from FishBase biological parameters rather than from time series recovery of abundance or occupancy. No explicit equation or component framework defining resilience is provided beyond describing a ranking system combining delta q and a measure of population resilience so the framework is none. Because resilience is inferred from life history traits the quantification is inferred and the target variable type is demography parameter. These parameters describe population turnover rate so the level is population. The study uses NMFS bottom trawl survey data and FishBase parameters without experimental manipulation so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation is Stony Brook University in the United States so the institution country is US. The survey data are from Georges Bank on the Northeast United States continental shelf so the field country is US and the habitat is marine neritic. The subjects are finfish and shellfish species so the taxon is Animalia. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because the resilience measure is inferred rather than observed response to a disturbance.

The study quantifies resilience using system time series by constructing a daily ESS index and combining it with SEWDI from 1982 to 2019. It states that drought recovery time is determined as the duration from the onset of ESS anomaly to its recovery to normal levels and that resilience curves capture the relationship between drought intensity and ecosystem recovery time. It defines recovery as the ESS index returning to above -0.5 SD and remaining there for 30 consecutive days and it terms the period from onset to recovery the ecological drought recovery period. The resilience curve is described as showing the time required for an ecosystem to return to baseline conditions under varying drought intensity and ecosystem drought resilience is represented using the 0.9 quantile curve area but the quantified response variable in the model is drought recovery time. The response variable y in the quantile regression is explicitly drought recovery time and the independent variable is drought intensity. The target variable used to detect anomalies and recovery is ESS which integrates water yield carbon storage and habitat quality as ecosystem service supply and is an ecosystem function measure. The study area is Baiyangdian wetland in North China with coordinates 115.45 to 116.06 E and 38.44 to 38.59 N and the first author affiliation is in Beijing China. Drought events are identified from SEWDI and ESS anomalies without experimental manipulation so the disturbance is unmanipulated and treated as discrete events with defined start and end thresholds. The observation window is from 1982 to 2019 which is 38 years or 13870 days using 365 days per year.

The paper is a concepts and synthesis theory article that proposes postulates and predictions rather than reporting an empirical study with Methods and Discussion sections and with field or experimental measurements. Resilience is discussed conceptually and is defined as recovery after pulse events and can be quantified by degree of return to reference level and rate of return and time for reaching the former or a new steady state but no resilience values are calculated from data. The text states that dynamic stability comprises resistance recovery and adaptation which provides the framework stability = resistance + recovery + adaptation. The first author affiliation is Bayreuth University Bayreuth Germany which supports institution country DE. No field data collection country locations coordinates habitat taxon disturbance implementation pattern or observation duration are provided for a specific dataset used to quantify resilience.

The introduction defines ecosystem stability as having core components resistance and resilience and states resilience is the ability of ecosystems to return to their original state after subsequent recovery. The methods describe a greenhouse microcosm experiment with factors drought ambient versus drought and nitrogen deposition ambient versus elevated and AMF with versus without. Drought was imposed for 11 weeks by reducing soil moisture and then soil moisture was brought back to ambient levels and maintained for an additional 11 weeks for recovery. Resilience was quantified as Resilience = (Drecovery − Arecovery) divided by Arecovery using data collected at the end of the recovery period. The text states that when this value is not significantly different from 0 the ecosystem has fully recovered representing a high resilience which operationalizes recovery degree relative to the ambient control at recovery. The target variables used for resilience calculations are listed as biomass Shannon Wiener diversity total N uptake cumulative N2O emission leachate N and Bray Curtis similarity index of plant species compositions and an ecosystem multifunctionality index derived from these functions. The approach is a controlled greenhouse experiment with imposed drought and nitrogen addition. The soil source location is given as 44 degrees 26 minutes N 7 degrees 33 minutes E Nenzlingen Switzerland and the greenhouse site is at Agroscope Zurich Switzerland. The first author affiliation includes Agroscope Zurich Switzerland and the field soil is from Switzerland. The disturbance is manipulated drought and manipulated nitrogen deposition with nitrogen applied as nutrient solution and pulses and drought imposed by soil moisture reduction and then ended for recovery. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because drought has a defined 11 week period followed by a recovery period. The observation window spans 35 weeks from October 2018 to June 2019 which is 245 days.

The study reports evidence of DNA methylation resilience because stress induced DNA methylation variation quickly returned back to the control levels. The methods imposed environmental challenges by assigning animals to high temperature low temperature high salinity and low salinity treatments relative to a control. DNA methylation was measured repeatedly at 1 3 6 24 48 72 and 120 hr using MSAP and differentiation between treatment and control groups was quantified with ΦST and visualized with PCoA. The discussion states that methylation divergence between treatment and control groups eventually decreased at 48 hr and returned to control levels at 48 hr in all environmental stresses. This supports an observed recovery dynamic and the quantification corresponds to recovery time. The target variable used for resilience is DNA methylation variation and divergence which is an epigenetic physiological indicator measured in body wall muscle. The organisms were collected from the coast of Dalian Liaoning Province China at 38 49 13 N 121 24 20 E and then experimentally stressed in tanks. The first author affiliation is the Research Center for Eco Environmental Sciences Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing China. The study species is the ascidian Ciona savignyi which is Animalia. The disturbances are manipulated temperature change and manipulated salinity change treated as acute challenges with defined exposure durations.

The study used dense Landsat NDVI and NDII time series and analysed their long term trajectories from January 1984 to October 2019 to assess canopy dynamics and forest resilience. It states that it assessed resilience patterns using coordinated NDVI NDII variation trends from the Mann Kendall test and interpreted these as resistance and recovery. It explicitly says the coordinated NDVI NDII variation patterns were used to measure the resistance and recovery of the forests. The disturbance context is warming induced drought disturbance and climate change with a relatively dry period starting in 2000 and lasting for at least 10 years and with PDSI and precipitation anomalies described. The target variables used for resilience quantification are NDVI as canopy greenness and NDII as canopy water content which are treated as indicators of canopy physiology and function. The approach is field observation because it relies on remote sensing observations and associated field data without experimental manipulation. The first author affiliation is Peking University in Beijing China. The field sites are in the forest steppe ecotone in northern China and Table 1 provides site coordinates. The average coordinate is computed from the five site coordinates in Table 1. The location range is based on the farthest site Daqingshan relative to the average latitude and longitude. The habitat is forest because the study focuses on birch forest and forest patches along continuous forest timberline and tree line. The taxon is Plantae because the focal organism is white birch described as the dominant tree species. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic because the study analyses drought and warming induced stress without imposed treatments. The disturbance is treated as press because the analysis focuses on sustained warming and multi year drought periods rather than a single discrete event. The observation duration follows the stated Landsat time series period from January 1984 to October 2019.

The study states that it “determined the relative resilience” of forest and open populations and that “Resilience was assessed by imposing a clipping regime once each year from 1986 to 1989 during which shrub resprouting abilities were measured.” It also states that “Adult shrub and stem density as well as NPP measured from 1987 to 1989 were expressed as percentages of the 1986 pre clip conditions to calculate various indices of resilience” which is direct observation of response to an imposed disturbance. The disturbance is clipping because “In late July 1986 all stems within the plot boundaries were clipped” and “In late July of each year 1987 to 1989 plots were reclipped.” The resilience quantification is based on degree relative to baseline because the main indices are “percentages of the 1986 pre clip conditions” for stems shrubs and NPP. The target variables used in Fig 2 are “Numbers of stems resprouting” and “numbers of shrubs persisting” and “net primary production” which correspond to abundance biomass and ecosystem function. The level is population because the indices are shrub and stem densities and NPP per shrub aggregated within populations and expressed relative to 1986. The approach includes field experiment because clipping is imposed in permanent plots and indoor experiment because seed bank samples were taken to “a heated greenhouse” to record seedling emergence. The first author affiliation is “Northern Kentucky University Highland Heights Kentucky 41076 USA” so institution country is US. Field plots were on the Northern Kentucky University campus and nearby roadside embankments in northern Kentucky so field country is US. The forest plot coordinates are given as “3905 N 84 40 W” which converts to N39.0833 W84.6667 and no other coordinates are provided so the range is 0. The dominant habitat used for resilience comparisons is forest because the study contrasts “forest grown” and “open grown” and the forest plots are described as “preserved forest patches.” The taxon is Plantae because the focal organism is the shrub Lonicera maackii. The disturbance pattern is pulse because clipping is applied as discrete annual events with reclipping each year.

The study states that it analyses a mechanistic model of grassland ecosystems subjected to drought and that no data were used which supports a modeling and simulation approach with no field country or coordinates. It defines resistance as the inverse of the sensitivity of total biomass to a long time press perturbation and defines resilience as the rate at which an ecosystem recovers biomass after a disturbance. Resilience is quantified using a non asymptotic return rate measure R(p) = −log|1−p| divided by tp which is explicitly a rate of recovery. The target variable used throughout the resistance and resilience definitions is biomass including total biomass at the ecosystem level and species biomasses. Drought is represented in the model as a reduction in soil nutrient fluxes or nutrient mobility to mimic the consequences of a drought which is a climatic disturbance imposed in the model and treated as a press perturbation. The first author affiliation is Station D'écologie Théorique et Expérimentale Centre National de la Recherche Scientifique Moulis France which gives institution country France. The model concerns grassland ecosystems and the organisms are plants described as individual plants and species in a grassland ecosystem model which supports Plantae as the taxon. The paper does not report a specific tp duration window for the simulations in the provided text so observation duration cannot be determined.

The study defines resilience as the degree to which systems return to their previous configuration once the perturbation is removed and it defines resistance as the degree to which systems are altered when the environment changes. It quantifies resistance and resilience by comparing observed to expected ratios between never stocked lakes stocked fish present lakes and stocked now fishless lakes. The O slash E ratio is calculated from observed presence absence of amphibian reptile benthic macroinvertebrate and zooplankton taxa and expected taxa from a predictive model so the resilience measurement uses observational faunal data. Resilience is supported by the statement that O slash E ratios in stocked now fishless sites were not significantly different from those in never stocked sites. Recovery time is explicitly quantified because the relationship between O slash E and years since fish disappearance indicated recovery in less than two years and also in two years or less following fish disappearance. The target variable used to quantify resilience is taxonomic composition expressed through O slash E ratios which measure loss and recovery of expected taxa. The biological level is community because the O slash E metric is based on multiple taxa across assemblages. The approach is field observation because fauna were surveyed during single site visits in 2000 or 2001 using shoreline visual encounter surveys D net sweeps and plankton net tows. The first author affiliation is University of California in the United States.
