The field system is lakes and ponds in Yosemite National Park California United States and the plate captions and text identify Yosemite National Park. No coordinates are provided so the location is set to the Yosemite National Park central coordinates. The disturbance is fish introductions and subsequent disappearance which is a biotic perturbation not experimentally imposed in this study. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because resilience is assessed once the perturbation is removed and recovery is evaluated as a function of years since fish disappearance. The observation window for the field surveys spans from 18 June 2000 to 28 August 2001 which is 437 days.

The study compiles a database of 307 published field study sites with direct field measures of total net primary productivity and calculates ecosystem fit as the ratio of measured tNpp to a theoretical maximum tNpp. Resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics after a disturbance and instead is discussed as adaptive capacity inferred from productivity thresholds and ecosystem fit. The abstract states the study proposes using ecosystem fit to identify forest adaptive capacity and gives examples of forests being less resilient if precipitation increased or if minimum annual temperature increased which are relationships from models not observed recovery. The methods describe Random Forest regression tree models and a light use efficiency model to calculate theoretical maximum tNpp and eFit. No explicit resilience framework equation is defined in the introduction methods or discussion. The first author affiliation is the University of Washington in the United States. The dataset is described as global with sites between 39th parallel south and 67th parallel north and across global regions so field country and location are global. The habitat is forest ecosystems across boreal temperate and tropical biomes. The focal measured variables are productivity metrics tNpp and eFit which are ecosystem function measures and the subjects are forests and trees.

The study states it aimed to evaluate resilience to a long-lasting anomalous summer drought and subsequent post-drought recovery using continuous in situ measurements of gas exchange and chlorophyll fluorescence. The drought was defined as days with daily mean soil water content below 0.1 m3m−3 and it lasted from May 24th to July 23th for 61 days and the recovery period lasted from July 24th to July 29th for 6 days. Resistance and resilience to drought were expressed as ratios of PN gs Tr WUE and NUE at the end of the drought to those at the beginning with ratios close to 1.0 implying greater resistance and capacity to recover. Because resilience is quantified using these ratios rather than an explicit recovery rate or recovery time the quantification is coded as resistance. The target variables used for these ratios are photosynthetic gas exchange variables and efficiencies which are ecosystem function type variables. The measurements were made on plants growing on a sand dune at Yanchi Research Station in Ningxia northwest China with coordinates 37°42′31″N 107°13′47″E. The first author affiliation is Beijing Forestry University in Beijing PR China. The disturbance is an unmanipulated climatic drought event and it is treated as a discrete drought period followed by a recovery period. The observation window for drought plus recovery is 67 days.

The study established marine phytoplankton metacommunities in microcosms and imposed a single disturbance event by manual biomass removal described as removing 75% locally or 25 or 75% regionally. Resilience was explicitly defined as the rate of recovery following the perturbation and was calculated as the change in log transformed standardized biomass between specified post disturbance days divided by the number of days. Structural resilience was also quantified as the rate of increase in similarity as the complement to Bray Curtis dissimilarity between disturbed treatments and controls. These are direct measurements of recovery dynamics after a disturbance so the measurement type is observed. The framework is presented as resilience and recovery ratio as two aspects of stability with resistance not addressed. The target variables used to quantify resilience are biomass as functional stability and similarity of species composition as structural stability. Biomass is treated as primary production and community productivity so it is coded as ecosystem function and similarity based on Bray Curtis reflects composition. The experiment was conducted in connected Erlenmeyer flasks in a climate chamber so the approach is an indoor field style microcosm experiment. The first author affiliation is at Carl von Ossietzky University Oldenburg in Germany. The taxa were freshly isolated from the North Sea and cultures from Helgoland are mentioned so the field country is Germany and the location is approximated to Helgoland coordinates. The habitat context is marine phytoplankton from the North Sea which is a neritic marine environment. The disturbance is a manipulated non selective mortality event via biomass removal which is a structural disturbance. The disturbance is a single event followed by a four week recovery phase so the pattern is pulse and the observation duration spans from day 0 to day 29.

The study quantified drought responses using tree ring width indices and xylem vessel traits measured from wood cores collected in the field. It explicitly aimed to assess resistance recovery and resilience during historical periods of drought and calculated indices following Lloret et al. 2011 with resilience defined as Ring widtht+2 divided by Ring widtht-2. Drought events were identified as specific historical drought years 1976 1983 1991 1999 and 2012 and analysed with a 5 year window including 2 years before and 2 years after each event. The disturbance was climatic drought based on weather station data and PDSI and was not experimentally manipulated. The resilience index uses the ratio of post drought growth to pre drought growth which matches recovery degree as defined by the difference between the system after it has returned and the original state. The target variable used for resilience quantification was ring width annual radial growth at the individual tree level because indices were calculated at the individual tree level. The first author affiliation is Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México in Mexico City. The field site is Medio Monte Natural Protected Area Hidalgo State Mexico with coordinates given as 20°24′50′′N 98°15′4.31′′W. The habitat is Tropical Montane Cloud Forest.

The paper states that it uses morphological measurements of 3696 bird species to estimate shifts in functional redundancy after recent anthropogenic land use change at 1281 sites worldwide and then uses extinction simulations to assess sensitivity to future species loss. Resilience is defined in Fig 1 as the ability to withstand disturbance and is described as a function of resistance and recovery. The study quantifies functional stability using functional resistance defined as the area under the extinction curve and also uses functional redundancy and functional vulnerability as trait space based metrics. These metrics are derived from trait hypervolumes and simulated species removals rather than observed recovery dynamics of an ecosystem state variable through time after a disturbance. The Methods describe functional resistance as the rate at which functional richness declines as species are removed and measured as AUC or half life of extinction curves. The target variables used to quantify resilience are functional redundancy functional vulnerability and functional resistance which are based on overlap and uniqueness in functional trait space and extinction simulations. The assemblages are bird species assemblages so the level is community and the taxon is Animalia. The first author affiliations are Imperial College London and Natural History Museum London which are in the United Kingdom. The sampling is described as 98 study landscapes across 6 continents and 1281 assemblages worldwide so the field country and location are global. The baseline includes forests grasslands shrublands and wetlands and the land use gradient includes cropland pasture plantation and urban so the habitat type is multi. Because the measurement is inferred the disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

Framework: community stability = persistence; community resilience = return to original state
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the ability of an ecosystem to return to its original state following a perturbation and it defines community stability as persistence in a particular state. The methods quantify response using repeated presence absence detections from the Swedish national bird survey and estimate species occupancy w and detectability p over time with a BACI interaction term between time and impact. The study compares two summers before and two summers after Gudrun using observations collected during 2003 to 2006 and treats Gudrun as a short term pulse impact. The response variables are stated as site occupancy and detectability of each species across Sweden and the BACI interaction term is used to test for hurricane impact and recovery in occupancy. The simulation explicitly imposes a 75 percent reduction in occupancy at time period three with recovery to 95 percent at time period four which is a direct modeled disturbance response. The field analysis measures hurricane impact as tree fallen area and notes these tree fall data also include intentional logging which is treated as disturbance. The first author affiliation is University of Auckland in New Zealand. The field data are from Sweden and the study uses grid squares across Sweden with strongest impact in southern Sweden and northern Sweden as control so the average coordinate is set to central Sweden. The habitat is Swedish forest and the focal community is forest birds. The observation window spans 2003 through 2006 which is four years.

The study computed resilience indicators as a sum of products of factor relevance and factor intensity and also as an intensity only indicator IRIS. Factor intensity was based on normalized measurements of 17 biological environmental and human related factors and factor relevance was ordinal values assigned from literature. The indicators are described as Relative Resilience Potential and Inclusive Resilience Indicator of a Site and they are used to rank sites and expose spatial gradients rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a disturbance. The methods quantify fish invertebrate and sessile cover plus human and environmental variables at 54 sites using underwater visual surveys photographs and external databases. No time series recovery rate recovery time resistance or threshold response to an imposed disturbance is quantified. The first author affiliation is in Spain at CEAB CSIC in Blanes. The study area spans the Alboran Sea from Gibraltar to Cartagena and to Arzew Gulf in Algeria and includes some locations in North Africa. The map and text indicate a regional extent so an average coordinate near the Alboran Sea center is used with a multi degree range. The habitat is temperate rocky reefs in the Alboran Sea which is shallow coastal marine shelf environment. Because measurement is inferred from contributing factors rather than observed system recovery disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery are not applicable.

The introduction states that conventional measures overlook important components of system resilience and the study evaluates management using conventional metrics and complementary resilience-based metrics. The methods quantify turf height coral recruitment rates juvenile coral densities coral algal interactions and herbivorous fish grazing rates as resilience based metrics but these are treated as indicators with implications for system resilience rather than direct measurement of recovery after a disturbance. The discussion states that increased recruitment and juvenile coral densities within tabu areas indicate that local management has improved the system’s resilience to perturbations which is an inference from these indicators. No time series of system recovery after a defined disturbance is measured and the study compares tabu versus open sites at one survey period with recruitment tiles deployed for a season. The approach is field observation because surveys and in situ measurements and video recordings were conducted on reefs in Fiji. The first author affiliation is in Bremen Germany. The study area is in Fiji at Dakuibeqa Beqa Nasinu Ovalau and Navakavu Suva but no coordinates are provided. The habitat is coral reef inshore reef platforms and lagoonal slopes which fits Marine Neritic. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are coded as NA per instructions.

The study presents a framework to quantify survival resistance and reestablishment following localized extinction events resilience using metapopulation dynamics. Resilience is operationalized as colonization because analyses of colonization were conducted on patches absent in year 1 and present in year 2 and predicted probabilities of colonization were mapped as resilience. This uses observed occupancy presence absence data collected over two consecutive years 2014 to 2015 from a network of wetlands in Uganda. The response variable was presence or absence of each species in year 2 and survival analyses used patches present in year 1 and present in year 2. Colonization and survival are inter annual probabilities which quantify recovery dynamics as a rate rather than a time to recovery or degree of recovery. Connectivity uses predicted density multiplied by patch size as an estimate of relative population size and emigration proportional to abundance which makes the target variable type abundance. The biological level is population because the analysis concerns species populations within habitat patches and local extinction and colonization of populations. The approach is field observation because patches were visited and presence or absence recorded by surveys with GPS coordinates. The first author affiliation is University of Exeter in the United Kingdom. Field data were collected around Lake Bunyonyi Uganda with coordinates given as 01 17 S 29 55 E. The habitat is papyrus swamps and broad wetland vegetation which are wetlands. The taxa are bird species which are Animalia. The disturbance involved in the resilience concept is localized extinction events treated as discrete events between years so it is unmanipulated structural pulse. Observation duration spans two consecutive years so it is 365 days.

The study states that it uses satellite based tree cover data and vegetation indices to quantify global forest resilience. It explicitly defines resilience as one lag temporal autocorrelation of vegetation indices and specifies that higher TACkNDVI indicates lower forest resilience. The kNDVI time series spans 2000 to 2020 and is used to calculate one lag TAC at 0.05 degree resolution which is direct observation of system dynamics. The Methods define resilience as the capacity to resist and recover from disturbances and also state resilience is defined as the capacity to withstand disturbances and avoid state shifts. The quantification used in the main analysis is TAC which the Methods link to recovery rate and describe as a theoretical metric tied to system dynamics. Vegetation indices are described as a robust proxy for ecosystem productivity so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The analysis is global and uses MODIS and other global remote sensing products so the field country and location are Global. The habitat is forests because the study selects forest grid cells and analyses global forests. The first author affiliation is in Beijing China at the Research Center for Eco Environmental Sciences Chinese Academy of Sciences. Fragmentation is treated as an ongoing condition quantified as edge density and related metrics rather than a discrete event so the disturbance pattern is press. The disturbance is unmanipulated structural because fragmentation is a structural change in forest patch configuration and is analysed observationally rather than imposed. The observation duration is from 2000 to 2020 which is 21 years or 7670 days using 365 days per year.

The study quantified drought responses using tree ring based basal area increment series and states that growth responses to extreme droughts during the period 1980 to 2015 were assessed using resilience indicators based on residual basal area increment series. It calculated resistance recovery and resilience indices following Lloret et al 2011 with formulas using resBAI during drought and pre drought and post drought periods. It also calculated growth recovery time and growth loss following Thurm et al 2016. The disturbance events were selected drought years 1994 2003 and 2012 based on SPEI VPD and soil moisture and are described as drought events. These are unmanipulated climatic disturbances because they were identified from climate and hydrological variables rather than imposed experimentally. The disturbance pattern is pulse because discrete drought events were selected and indices used pre drought and post drought windows. The target variable for resilience quantification is tree growth because indices are based on residual basal area increment derived from tree ring width. The level is individual because all indices were calculated at the individual tree level for each drought event. The approach is field observation because 149 trees were cored across Catalonia and climate data were extracted for plots without experimental manipulation. The first author affiliation is Instituto Pirenaico de Ecologia in Zaragoza Spain. The field country is Spain because the study area is Catalonia located in north eastern Spain. The location is approximated from the map described as Catalonia NE Spain and the inset showing Catalonia in Spain and south western Europe. The habitat is forest because sampling plots were forests dominated by European beech or pubescent oak. The taxon is Plantae because the focal species are Fagus sylvatica and Quercus pubescens. The observation duration spans from 1980 to 2015 as stated for the assessed drought response period.

Framework: stability = variability + magnitude of extreme anomalies + recovery time + recovery rate
Justification: The study uses remotely sensed enhanced vegetation index data to estimate ecosystem functioning and plant productivity and it analyses the EVI time series for 2000 to 2019 at the 10 × 10 km hectad scale. It defines stability as the capacity to absorb environmental disturbances maintain ecosystem functioning and recover and Figure 1A specifies four stability measures variability magnitude of extreme anomalies recovery time and recovery rate. Recovery rate is defined as the rate of return after a perturbation and is calculated as the magnitude of a two sigma event divided by the recovery time from the two sigma event. Perturbations are identified as non overlapping events in the EVI anomaly time series that fell below minus 2 standard deviations below the baseline and recovery time is the minimum time for the moving window average to become non negative. The disturbance is not experimentally imposed and is treated as underlying environmental disturbance across the island of Ireland with climate history and extreme events discussed so the disturbance type is climatic and unmanipulated. The perturbations are discrete two sigma events with recovery measured after each event which matches a pulse framing. The EVI record spans 2000 to 2019 with approximately 8 day resolution so the observation duration is 20 years. The focal system is agricultural pasture with pasture dominated hectads defined by CORINE pasture cover and the habitat is agricultural. The first author affiliation is Anglia Ruskin University Cambridge UK. The field extent is across the island of Ireland using records from the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland and climate and EVI data covering Ireland so the field countries are Ireland and the United Kingdom. The location is therefore the island of Ireland and the provided coordinate is an average for the island with a range reflecting the island wide extent.

The study quantified growth resilience using tree ring width chronologies and calculated indices for resistance recovery and resilience using formulas where Gd is growth during the drought episode and Gprev and Gpost are mean growth during the 2 years before and after the drought episode. Drought years were identified from SPEI and defined as years with 12 month SPEI lower than minus 1.5 and drought episodes were single year severe droughts. Resilience was defined as the capacity to re establish pre drought growth levels and operationalized as Resilience = Gpost divided by Gprev. Resistance and recovery were explicitly described as the two components of growth resilience. The resilience metric compares post drought growth to pre drought growth which is a recovery degree type measure rather than a rate or time. The target variable used in the resilience metrics was growth measured as tree ring width. The indices were calculated at the species level using species mean growth indices across chronologies. The approach relied on observational tree ring data from ITRDB a tropical tree ring dataset and the authors own field sampled cores and discs rather than experimental manipulation. The first author affiliation includes East China Normal University in Shanghai China. The global dataset used sites across the globe and the paper also included local sampling in China so the field country includes Global and CN. The disturbance was drought detected from climate indices without manipulation and treated as a single year event with 2 year pre and post windows which matches a pulse disturbance. The observation window for each drought episode used 2 years before and 2 years after plus the drought year which totals 5 years or 1825 days.

The study uses long term monitoring data from the Common Birds Census and models between year change in bird numbers using species counts at woodland sites surveyed during 1965 to 2000. It tests population sensitivity to winter weather and the ability of populations to recover from such events using interactions with minimum winter temperature and count in the previous year. Resilience is therefore quantified from observed population responses and recovery dynamics rather than from proxy indices. The analysis focuses on population growth rates and increases from small counts which represent recovery rate rather than recovery time or recovery degree. The target variable used in the models is species count and mapped territories which are measures of abundance at the population level. The disturbance is variation in winter weather specifically minimum winter temperature anomalies and cold winters described as weather events. Weather is not experimentally manipulated so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic. The disturbance is treated as discrete winter events between consecutive annual surveys so it matches a pulse framing. The first author affiliation is British Trust for Ornithology in the United Kingdom. The field data are from UK woodland CBC sites and UK climate data matched to site centres. The observation window spans 1965 to 2000 which is 35 years or 12775 days.

The study used tree ring data and transformed ring width series to basal area increment series for the period 1985 to 2015. It selected four drought events using SPEI and computed resistance as BAIdrought divided by BAIpredrought and recovery as BAIpostdrought minus BAIdrought divided by BAIpredrought. The text states that resistance and recovery are complementary components of resilience. Resilience was therefore quantified from observed growth responses before during and after drought events rather than from static proxies. The target variable used in the resilience metrics is tree growth expressed as basal area increment derived from annual rings. The metrics are computed at the individual level for each drought. The approach is field observation because trees were sampled in plots and wood cores were collected in Catalonia. The first author affiliation is University of Stirling in Scotland UK. The field data were collected in Catalonia north eastern Iberian peninsula. The disturbance is drought which is a climatic disturbance and it was not experimentally manipulated. The droughts are treated as discrete events with defined years and pre drought and post drought windows so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The observation window spans 1985 to 2015 which is 31 years or 11315 days.

The introduction states that early warning signals such as autocorrelation and variance are used as an approach to resilience assessment. The methods state that a 3-D automaton simulates two scenarios and that relative species abundances are monitored spatiotemporally. The methods specify that autocorrelation and standard deviation were calculated on the log-transformed abundance of species 1 using a moving window over the time series. The discussion states that in scenario 2 the system is gradually forced to instability and that the decrease in resilience is picked up by early warning signals. Because resilience is assessed from time series behavior of species abundance using lag-1 autocorrelation which is a critical slowing down indicator this matches recovery rate. The target variable used for the early warning signal calculations is abundance of species 1. Because the variable is a species abundance within a three species hierarchy the level is community. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study runs automaton simulations for 500 iterations and generates replicas. The first author affiliation lists Universidade de Lisboa in Lisboa Portugal so the institution country is PT. The disturbance in scenario 2 is a modeled stressor where the reaction frequency of one species pair decays linearly over time so it is a model disturbance treated continuously which matches a press pattern. The modeled change is a change in encounter or reaction frequency which is a change in interaction opportunity and thus a resource type driver in the model. The paper uses an in vivo microbial experiment as input but does not provide a country or coordinates for the mice experiment in the provided text so field country and location are NA. The study subjects are bacteria Escherichia coli so the taxon is Bacteria.

The study states that it quantifies resilience by reducing rates of fire induced tree mortality and it measures wildfire severity across 1984 to 2018 using Landsat imagery calibrated to 208 field plots. In the discussion it defines wildfire severity as a direct correlate of a forest’s resistance and states that resistance is a key component of resilience and that one manifestation of forest resilience is high resistance to wildfire whereby some mechanism leads to lower severity when a fire occurs. The response variable in the main model is the probability of high severity wildfire defined by an RBR threshold corresponding to complete or near complete overstorey mortality. The disturbance is wildfire and the fires are from the FRAP perimeter database and are not experimentally imposed. The disturbance is treated as discrete fire events with pre fire and post fire image windows and a one year offset for post fire composites. The observation window spans fires that burned between 1984 and 2018 which is 34 years or 12410 days. The study system is yellow pine mixed conifer forests in the Sierra Nevada mountain range of California. The first author affiliations listed include University of California Davis and University of Colorado Boulder which are in the United States. The focal biota for mortality and NDVI based structure is overstorey trees in conifer forests which are plants. No explicit coordinates are provided in the text for the study area or plots.

The methods state that thermal stress resilience assays exposed acclimated apical tissue to heat shock for 3 h at 24 28 32 and 36 C and then algae were allowed to recover under acclimation conditions for 24 h. Resilience was assayed 24 h following stress exposure using chlorophyll fluorescence with the maximum quantum yield of PSII Fv Fm determined after 5 min dark adaptation. The results report that after recovery from temperatures between 24 and 32 C Fv Fm was only slightly lower than controls while 36 C had a much greater effect indicating resilience was significantly impaired between 32 and 36 C. This uses observed physiological measurements rather than proxies. No explicit resilience framework is defined in the introduction methods or discussion beyond using the term resilience for the assay. The target variable used to quantify resilience is Fv Fm which is a physiological indicator of PSII photochemistry. The study includes indoor laboratory heat shock experiments and field observation of microhabitat temperatures and in situ HSR sampling. The study site is given as Ria Formosa coastal lagoon southern Portugal with coordinates 37 00 0400 N 7 59 0250 W. The first author affiliation is Centre of Marine Sciences University of Algarve Faro Portugal. The study organism is the brown alga Fucus vesiculosus which is a chromist. Disturbance in the lab is manipulated heat shock temperature and in the field is unmanipulated thermal stress during low tide emersion and warm seawater. The heat shock exposure is a discrete event followed by a recovery interval so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The resilience observation window for the assay spans from the start of heat shock to the 24 h post stress measurement which is 1 day.

The study follows post mining regeneration by using known aged rehabilitating areas described as 1 to 22 years post mining and compares them to an undisturbed benchmark forest. Resilience is quantified from the system response as an exponential decay model dt = d0e(-kt) where k is stated to be a rate constant that measures resilience. The response variable is multivariate dissimilarity d computed from species proportional abundances and species presence using Euclidean distance and Ochiai's distance relative to a benchmark reference vector. This uses observed field survey data for herbs trees millipedes dung beetles birds small mammals and soil elements across site ages. The analysis regresses log distance on site age and tests whether the slope is less than zero as evidence of convergence. The disturbance is vegetation removal for mining described as dredge mining that removes all vegetation and topsoil followed by rehabilitation. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event after which recovery is tracked across years so it is a pulse. The field sites are northeast of Richards Bay KwaZulu Natal South Africa and the benchmark is Sokhulu about 20 km northeast so the field country is South Africa. The first author affiliation is University of Pretoria Pretoria South Africa. The habitat is described as subtropical coastal dune forest and the benchmark is an undisturbed moist subtropical coastal dune forest. The target variables for resilience quantification are community composition and relative abundance expressed as dissimilarity indices so the target variable type is composition at the community level. The observation window spans the chronosequence from 1 to 22 years post mining which is 22 years or 8030 days.

The paper presents a computational framework that applies ecological resilience to single populations and explicitly states that resistance and recovery are the components of resilience. Disturbance is simulated as hurricanes that reduce survival for one year or reduce fecundity with effects lasting multiple years and the disturbance is imposed in year 10 of 30 year projections. Resistance is quantified as whether disturbance impacted trajectories stay within the 95 percent bounds of the normal range of variation and as the dip in mean population size for ten years following disturbance expressed as percentiles. Recovery is quantified as return time defined as the time it takes for a population to return to its normal range of variation and the study reports whether populations returned within 20 years and how many years before return. The target variable used in these resistance and recovery measures is population size in projected trajectories. The approach is modeling and simulation using single population projection models with Bayesian inference and simulated disturbance impacts. The first author affiliation is University of Maryland in the United States. The focal species are tidal marsh birds along the Atlantic coast of North America and the disturbance regime is Atlantic hurricanes affecting that region in the United States. The habitat is tidal marshes which are wetlands. The study subjects are bird species which are Animalia. The disturbance is treated as a single event and the paper notes these are short term pulse events. The simulation window is 30 years which is 10950 days.

The study states that it examines resistance as change immediately after a disturbance and resilience as ability to recover back to pre-disturbance levels in rhizosphere microbial communities. It imposed an extreme heat event of plus 10C for 1 week and then let communities recover for 4 weeks and measured bacterial community biomass and protist density once every week throughout the course of the experiment. Resilience was calculated as bacterial OD in extreme heat treatments in week 4 or 5 or 6 divided by bacterial OD in control treatments in week 3 and predator resilience used the same formula with protist densities. This uses observed time series responses of bacterial OD600 and protist active density after a manipulated heat shock. The framework is presented as resistance and resilience in parallel with explicit definitions in the abstract. The resilience metric is a ratio to a reference value rather than a rate or time to recovery so it fits recovery degree. Bacterial community biomass was estimated by OD600 and protist resilience was based on active predator density. The experiment was a microcosm experiment run in 96 well plates with incubators and weekly measurements indicating an indoor experiment. The first author affiliation lists Netherlands Institute of Ecology in Wageningen in the Netherlands. Soil samples were taken from rhizosphere of Centaurea stoebe plants from three populations in the Netherlands and three in Slovenia. No coordinates are provided in the text and Table S1 is referenced but not shown so location and range cannot be extracted. The system is rhizosphere soil microbial communities so the habitat type does not match the provided natural habitat categories and is coded as Other. The prey are bacterial communities and the predators are protists from the class Heterolobosea so the taxa are Bacteria and Protozoa. The disturbance is a manipulated extreme heat event treated as a 1 week event with recovery afterwards matching a pulse disturbance. The entire experiment ran for 6 weeks so observation duration is 42 days.

Framework: cropland ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience
Justification: The paper frames cropland ecosystem health using the VOR model and states Costanza’s ecosystem health theory of vigor organization resilience. It defines cropland resilience as ability to maintain food production capacity amid disturbances like climate change and human activities and calculates it using coefficient of variation and long term trend slope of NPP and EVI for 2002 to 2012 and 2012 to 2022. This uses indicators and proxy variables rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a defined disturbance event so the measurement type is inferred. The study constructs CEH using organizational structure ecosystem services and environmental quality and uses remote sensing and gridded datasets processed in GIS GEE and InVEST which is field observation. The first author affiliation is Anhui Normal University in China. The study area is Anhui Province China with coordinates 29.41 to 34.38 N and 114.54 to 119.37 E so the average coordinate is N32.1583 E117.2583 and the farthest difference sum is 4.7 degrees. The system studied is cropland so the habitat type is agricultural. The resilience related indicators are based on vegetation productivity indices EVI and NPP which are ecosystem function variables and relate to plants. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

The study used time-series data from experimental plankton communities and calculated stability metrics including asymptotic resilience and initial resilience from MAR(1) community matrices. Table 1 defines asymptotic resilience as the long-term rate of recovery after perturbation and initial resilience as the instantaneous rate of recovery after perturbation which are direct recovery rate measures. The state variables used in the MAR models are log-transformed plankton biomass and the temporal stability metrics are coefficients of variation in species biomass and total community biomass. Mesocosms were maintained at the pond facility at Kellogg Biological Station in Hickory Corners Michigan USA and were sampled 32 times every 4 or 5 days from mid-May through early October with 26 sampling dates retained. Nutrient concentration treatment was constant versus large pulses every 2 weeks and the discussion also refers to natural weather fluctuations and experimentally induced nutrient pulses as perturbation types. The first author affiliation is Ohio Wesleyan University in Delaware Ohio USA. The system is aquatic mesocosms with plankton communities and is best matched to Wetland habitat. Zooplankton species listed include Daphnia Scapholebris and Ceriodaphnia and phytoplankton biomass was measured as chlorophyll a for edible and inedible size fractions.

Disturbance type: Unmanipulated_Landuse and infrastructure development; Unmanipulated_Climatic; Unmanipulated_Biotic; Unmanipulated_Geophysical
Justification: The study defines resilience as the capacity to respond and recover and it uses dense Landsat images from 1987 to 2017 to monitor disturbance and recovery using NDVI residual space time Moran’s I. It explicitly states that resilience is composed of two properties resistance to disturbance and recovery from disturbance. Resilience indicators are constructed as elasticity malleability and trend and elasticity is defined as the rate and timing of recovery with Vela = R divided by T. The results report recovery time categories and rate of recovery for NDVI per month and for landscape metrics per year which are direct time series based recovery dynamics. NDVI is described as a proxy of vegetation greenness and related to vegetation productivity and landscape metrics NP PS PPAR and AI are computed as proxies of forest landscape metrics. The resilience assessment is performed under the condition of maximum disturbance magnitude and uses recovery from disturbance end to recovery end. Disturbance is detected as negative NDVI residual clusters and recovery end is defined by high high clusters occurring twice consecutively. Disturbances discussed include deforestation and logging and also flooding droughts storms insect attacks and geological disasters in the study area. The study area is Hengyang Basin Hunan Province China with coordinates 26.06 to 27.28 N and 111.99 to 113.27 E and the average coordinate is reported. The first author affiliation is China University of Geosciences Beijing China. The habitat is forest in a hilly red soil region and the focal system is forest vegetation including bamboo fir tree pine and tea tree.

The study states that it tested the subsidy–stability hypothesis by measuring resilience and sensitivity of each pond to a pulse nutrient perturbation using time series data. It added 500 L of an inorganic nutrient solution to each pond on 27 August 2009 and then quantified stability from time series spanning the 30 d period following the nutrient perturbation. It defined resilience as the inverse of return time estimated as the amount of time a response variable remained outside the 90 percent posterior density intervals following the nutrient perturbation. The resilience result highlighted in the Results is estimated from the phosphorus time series and described as recovery from the perturbation and removed phosphate from the water column in less than 24 h or recovered slowly in more than 10 d. This makes the quantification a return time measure. The target variable used for the resilience estimate is phosphate concentration which is an abiotic parameter. The work was a whole pond manipulation at the Michigan State University W K Kellogg Biological Station Experimental Pond Facility in Hickory Corners Michigan USA indicating a field experiment. The first author affiliation is University of Notre Dame in the USA. The field site is in the USA and no coordinates are provided so the location is approximated from Hickory Corners Michigan. The system is experimental ponds which are freshwater aquatic habitats best matched to Wetland. Because the resilience metric is based on phosphate concentration rather than a focal organism the taxon is not applicable. The disturbance is a manipulated pulse addition of inorganic nutrients which is a chemical disturbance treated as a pulse.

The paper defines community stability as a function of both resistance and resilience. It defines resilience as a measure of return to pre disturbance conditions and quantifies it as the inverse of the distance between pre disturbance and 10 year post disturbance using Sørensen dissimilarity in species composition with plot relative frequency and subplot importance values. This uses observed pre disturbance and post disturbance community data collected just before one year post disturbance and 10 years post disturbance. Disturbance treatments were experimentally applied timber harvesting with a gradient of overstory removal including clearcut leave tree shelterwood herbicide and uncut control. The abstract describes the disturbance gradient as pulse disturbances timber harvesting and treatments were applied between 1993 and 1998. The target variable used to quantify resilience is community species composition expressed as dissimilarity distance. The study subjects are vascular plants and the study is in Appalachian oak dominated forests in southwest Virginia and northeastern West Virginia USA. The first author affiliation is The Wilderness Society in Bozeman Montana USA. The observation window for resilience spans from pre disturbance sampling one year prior to treatment to 10 year post disturbance sampling which is 11 years or 4015 days.

The study uses annual aboveground biomass ANPP measured in field plots and quantifies drought responses using observational trajectories across years. It states that dividing sensitivity to drought into resistance during drought and rate of recovery after drought provides dimensions of resilience. It measures resistance and recovery as the slope S of the relationship between ANPP and growing season precipitation across years with resistance during declining precipitation and recovery during increasing precipitation after drought. The droughts are described as extreme natural droughts in California 2011 to 2014 and Great Plains 2011 to 2012 identified using SPEI. The disturbance is therefore unmanipulated climatic drought and the analysis treats it as a discrete event with predrought drought and postdrought periods. The target variable used to quantify resistance and recovery is ANPP biomass. The approach is a multisite nutrient addition field experiment NutNet with annual sampling. The first author affiliation is University of Minnesota in the United States. The sites are in North American California grasslands and Great Plains grasslands so the field country is the United States. The longest site time span shown in Table 1 is from 2010 to 2016 for resistance and recovery at Elliott Chaparral which is about six years or 2190 days.

The paper states that it developed trait based indices of physical resistance RI and recovery potential RPI and that these two indices were combined into an overall index RRI described as Resistance and Recovery Potential Index and that both trait groups contribute to the vulnerability or the opposite resilience of species to trawling. Resilience is therefore operationalized as a composite of resistance and recovery potential rather than measured as a recovery trajectory of a state variable. The indices are calculated from traits matrices with fuzzy coding and directional weighting scores and expert knowledge and literature and are used to express resilience or vulnerability of species to trawling. Although the study compares RRI scores to empirically observed responses over a trawling gradient and reports correlations with regression slopes the resilience metric itself is the trait based RRI rather than an observed recovery dynamic. The target variable used to quantify resilience is the trait based index derived from species traits which are biological interaction and life history attributes rather than direct state recovery. The case study data are macrofauna from the north eastern Irish Sea and trawling intensity gradient and the study refers to an active fishing ground in the Irish Sea. The first author affiliation is IMEDEA in Esporles Balearic Islands Spain. The habitat is benthic marine fishing ground on the seabed consistent with shallow shelf marine neritic context. Disturbance type and pattern and observation duration are not applicable because the measurement type is inferred under the provided rules.

Framework: resilience = ecological resilience + agricultural resilience + social resilience
Justification: The abstract states that resilience was assessed by adopting a set of indicators and that a multiple choice questionnaire was used with choices ordered from the lowest to the highest degree of resilience on a 1–5 point scale. The methods describe that the questionnaire focused on participant knowledge regarding proposed indicators for the ecological social and agricultural systems and that average resilience values were calculated from respondent scores. Table 1 and Table 3 show resilience quantified through indicator scores such as degree of decline in biodiversity degree of connectivity among land uses degree of migration rate and degree of damaged agricultural lands rather than direct time series recovery of an ecological state variable. Fig. 3 states that resilience of the SEPLS consists of a social and an ecological system and their interconnected components and Table 3 reports separate ecological agricultural and social resilience and an overall SEPLS resilience. The first author affiliation is European University of Lefke Mersin 10 Turkey. The study area is Lefke Region in North Cyprus on Cyprus Island and data were collected from 12 villages in Lefke Region. The study area description states the landscapes comprise a mosaic of terrestrial coastal and marine ecosystems which supports coding the habitat as Multi. The data collection period is given but because measurement is indicator based inferred resilience the observation duration for disturbance response is not applicable.

Framework: resilience = capacity to absorb disturbance + remain within the current domain of attraction
Justification: The abstract defines shrubland resilience as the capacity of a woody vegetated state to absorb management interventions and remain within its current regime and it states differences can be quantified by measuring whether a state switches to an alternate state following perturbation or remains in its current stability domain. The introduction states they infer differences in resilience based on whether the shrubland rapidly returns to its pre disturbance structure following treatment or remains in the new grass dominated basin of attraction and they focus on structural state variables such as woody plant cover and herbaceous cover. The methods describe an experimental manipulation with brush control treatments and a follow up prescribed fire and annual measurements from 2010 pretreatment until 2013. The results and discussion report whether woody plants regained pretreatment levels of dominance or whether grass dominance persisted for the duration of the study which is used as evidence that resilience was not overcome or was overcome. The first author affiliation is Texas A and M University in the USA. The study area is Chaparrosa Ranch 28.9 N 100.0 W in southwest Texas USA. The disturbance treatments are cut herbicide with Remedy herbicide and diesel mixture mechanical roller chopping and prescribed fires conducted in February 2013. The disturbance events are applied at specific times early spring 2011 and February 2013 and the system is then observed annually which matches a pulse pattern. The observation window for resilience measurements spans from 2010 to 2013 which is four years or 1460 days.

The study defines climatic resilience as the ability to maintain or recover community climatic characteristics after disturbance or stress and it evaluates communities that experienced an extreme drought event in southern Spain in 2013. Pre-drought drought and post-drought communities were derived from field censuses of standing individuals in 2016 and resampling in 2022. Community inferred climate CIC was calculated from species climatic niche centroids weighted by species relative abundances and resilience was quantified as the opposite of the log transformed Euclidean distance between pre-drought and post-drought CIC. This uses observed community composition and abundance data to compute CIC trajectories across pre-drought drought and post-drought periods so the measurement is observed. The paper discusses resilience and resistance together and defines climatic resistance as the ability to maintain CIC during the drought episode. The target variable used to quantify resilience is CIC which is an emergent characterization of community composition based on species identities and their relative abundances. The disturbance is an unmanipulated climatic drought event and it is treated as a discrete event with pre-drought drought and post-drought periods. The first author affiliation is Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona in Spain. The field sites are Cuatro Calas Moreras Mountain and Calblanque Natural Park in the coastal Murcia Region Spain and the coordinates given are around 37.38 to 37.61 N and 1.63 to 0.74 W. The observation window for resilience spans from the 2016 survey to the 2022 resurvey which is about six years or 2190 days.

The study used a five year biomonitoring campaign in Nansi Lake from 2011 to 2015 and collected macroinvertebrate samples quarterly with additional sampling in 2015. The cross regional water diversion was treated as the dividing point separating normal status from early disturbed and recolonized stages and the paper states a degraded recovered pattern and that recovery was observed in the second year following the impoundment. Resilience was quantified by grading community recovery and defining resilience by summing the grades of biological metrics in different sample locations. The biological metrics used for this grading included richness abundance Shannon Wiener index and Margalef index. This is observed because it relies on measured macroinvertebrate community data across time before and after the water transfer event. No explicit equation framework defining resilience as a component of a larger construct is provided. The disturbance is the water transfer operation which the discussion links to water level fluctuation and impoundment and it is analyzed as an event with before and after and a subsequent recovery period which matches a pulse treatment. The first author affiliation is Qingdao University in PR China and the field site is Nansi Lake in Shandong Province China with coordinates 116°34′E to 117°21′E and 34°27′N to 35°20′N. The average coordinate is computed from the stated bounds and the range is below two degrees in both latitude and longitude. The habitat is a freshwater lake and is best matched to the wetland category. The focal organisms are benthic macroinvertebrates which are animals.

The introduction defines resilience as a measure of stability that refers to the rate at which a system recovers to a steady state following a disturbance. The methods state that resilience was measured for ash free dry mass chlorophyll a autotrophic carbon fixation bacterial numbers and bacterial production. The primary resilience index quantified was the exponential rate of increase r from a first order recovery model X = X0 e rt which is a recovery rate. The disturbance was imposed by placing black plastic over each stream so irradiance was eliminated for 92 d which is a continuous maintained driver during that interval so it is press. The study used laboratory stream ecosystems and imposed treatments and disturbance in the lab so the approach is indoor experiment. The focal recovery variables include periphyton biomass measures such as AFDM and chlorophyll a and the key results and tables report r for these biomass variables. The streams were inoculated with rocks collected from Walker Branch on the Oak Ridge reservation and the authors are at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in Tennessee USA so the field country and institution country are US. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to Oak Ridge Tennessee. The observation window for recovery sampling is from the day black plastic was removed to 11 wk after plus the predisturbance measurement the day before removal which spans 1 d plus 77 d plus 91 d giving 169 d. The resilience quantification is based on observed time series measurements after the imposed disturbance rather than proxies. The habitat is a laboratory stream channel so it does not match the listed natural habitat categories and is coded as Other. The periphyton includes algae and the key words include algae and periphyton so the taxon is Plantae.

The study constructs an ecological vulnerability model from the three dimensions of exposure sensitivity and resilience and states that resilience refers to the ability that one system is back to the baseline state after being disturbed or influenced by external factors. Resilience is quantified as RI = 1 - γ where γ is the AR(1) coefficient on past NPP in the regression NPPt = α×Tempt + β×Pret + γ×NPPt-1 + ε and the text says larger γ means the ecosystem needs longer time to restore to balance and is in negative correlation with the resilience dimension. Because resilience is not measured as an observed recovery trajectory after a defined disturbance but is derived from an autoregressive coefficient and used as an index within a vulnerability framework the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. NPP is used as the vegetation indicator to analyze ecological vulnerability and NPP is net primary productivity which is an ecosystem function. The data are remote sensing and gridded products including GLASS based NPP and climate data for 2000 to 2015 which is field observation. The first author affiliation is Beijing Normal University in China. The study region is the Qinghai Tibet Plateau China and the paper provides no explicit coordinates so the location and range cannot be confidently determined beyond the plateau scale. The analysis includes forest grassland and bare land ecosystems so the habitat type is multi. The resilience relates to vegetation productivity and vegetation so the taxon is Plantae. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because the measurement is inferred within a vulnerability index rather than a direct disturbance recovery analysis.

The abstract states that the study examines resilience of mussel beds to storms and defines resilience as comprising both resistance and recovery. The field component continuously monitored mussel clump dislodgement throughout the storm season of 2021–2022 in the Dutch Wadden Sea and linked movement to near-bed orbital velocity. The dislodgement threshold is defined as the hydrodynamics causing widespread dislodgement and the field results identify a threshold between 0.45 and 0.50 m s−1. This threshold is a limit beyond which the mussel bed shifts from stable to dislodged which matches a latitude style quantification. The target variable used to quantify this threshold is near-bed orbital velocity which is an abiotic parameter. The study also quantified recovery in mesocosms as mussel re-aggregation using mussel cover fraction but the question requires a single quantification and the paper emphasizes threshold quantification as a core aim. Storms are treated as discrete events with peak intensity and multiple storms are described including named storm Eunice and dislodgement events marked during storm periods which fits a pulse disturbance pattern. Disturbance is unmanipulated storms in the field and wave-mimicked storm-related disturbances in flume and mesocosm experiments. The first author affiliation is in Vlissingen The Netherlands. The field site is explicitly the Dutch Wadden Sea in the Netherlands and the habitat is subtidal soft-bottom coastal marine which fits Marine.Neritic. The study organism is mussels which are animals. The monitoring covered the storm season of 2021–2022 which supports an observation duration of one year.

The study proposes and verifies a new resilience index and it states that it was validated using real data based on field measurements of changes in soil bacterial OTUs diversity and abundance after a wildfire. Resilience is defined as the capacity of communities to return to their original state prior to the disturbance and together with resistance both concepts form the components of ecological stability. The methods define ecological resilience as recovery capacity and state it can be estimated as the recovery rate of a functional variable at a given time. The index iRL uses values at the moment the disturbance ends and at time tx and it is normalized by the change produced by the disturbance relative to the control. Real data were collected in control situation two months and two years after the fire and the response variables analysed were Shannon diversity and abundance of different OTUs. The study also used simulated data to estimate resilience with different indices and check values across scenarios. The disturbance is a wildfire described as a pulse disturbance and figures describe response to a pulse disturbance. The workflow and Table 2 refer to burnt and control heathlands ecosystems and OTU abundance classification after burning and after two years of recovery. The first author affiliation is Universidad de León in Spain. The paper does not provide the country or coordinates of the field sites beyond stating Mediterranean ecosystems and heathlands. The observation window for resilience includes measurements from two months to two years after the fire and the text states two years after the fire so the duration is taken as two years.

The study states that it evaluated resilience over two years 2015 to 2017 including before and after Hurricane Matthew 2016 using changes in surface elevation Spartina alterniflora stem density and structural damage. Resilience is defined as the ability to bounce back from or adjust flexibly to an external disturbance and the introduction also discusses resistance to storms in parallel with resilience without defining a higher level variable. The methods quantify absolute change in elevation from before to after Hurricane Matthew and from 2015 to 2017 and quantify change in stem density from summer 2015 to summer 2017. The results interpret resilience as maintaining landward elevation over the two year study period without requiring any repair and compare elevation loss versus maintenance among shoreline types. These are direct field measurements using transects quadrats laser level and RTK GPS so measurement is observed and approach is field observation. The disturbance is Hurricane Matthew a category 1 hurricane treated as a discrete event with sampling within 3 weeks after the hurricane so it is an unmanipulated climatic pulse. The first author affiliation is University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill in the United States and the field sites are across coastal North Carolina USA in four regions Ocracoke Pine Knoll Shores Morris Landing and Southport. The habitat is saltmarsh shoreline which fits the coastal category and the focal organism for the biotic target variable is Spartina alterniflora. The observation window spans from the first sampling in May to July 2015 to the last sampling in May to June 2017 which is about two years or 730 days. The paper provides maps and site names but no coordinates so the location is approximated as the average of the four named North Carolina coastal regions and the range reflects their spread.

The study quantifies critical slowing down using temporal autocorrelation trends derived from monthly MODIS Enhanced Vegetation Index time series from 2001 to 2019. It states that critical slowing down is the increasingly slower return rate from small perturbations and it uses lag 1 autocorrelation as an indicator for slowing down. EVI is described as a proxy for canopy productivity and greenness in tropical forests and the trend in TAC is the response variable in the models. Extreme drought periods are defined from cumulative water deficit anomalies and treated as discrete drought events with defined start and end conditions. The analysis is based on satellite derived observations and precipitation datasets rather than imposed experimental manipulations. The first author affiliation is KU Leuven in Belgium. The focal system is the Amazon forest and the paper repeatedly refers to the Amazon and Amazon forest with drought events such as 2005 2010 and 2015. The observation window for EVI is January 2001 to December 2019 which is 19 years or 6939 days using 365 days per year. The habitat is Amazon forest and the vegetation index represents forest canopy vegetation. The disturbance is extreme drought which is a climatic disturbance and it is unmanipulated in the observational record. The location is not given as coordinates in the text so the average coordinate is reported as NA is not required here because the study region is the Amazon forest and no explicit coordinates are provided so an approximate central Amazon coordinate is not supported by the text.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + ecosystem service value
Justification: The study calculates the Ecosystem Health Index using the VORS model described as Vigor Organization Resilience Ecosystem Service Value and states it calculates EHI from 2000 to 2020 using multi source data. Ecosystem resilience is not measured as recovery dynamics of an observed state variable after a disturbance but is computed from land use proportions and fixed land use coefficients Resist and Resisl with weights based on GDP. The methods state ER is characterized by resistance and resilience and assigns weights to Resist and Resisl for each land use type and then calculates county level ERi using formula 4. Because resilience is constructed from proxy inputs rather than observed recovery it is inferred. The framework explicitly places resilience as a component of ecosystem health via the VORS model. Quantification is inferred because the resilience value is derived from land use type coefficients and GDP based weights rather than recovery rate time degree latitude or invariability from system trajectories. The target variable type is land use because ER is calculated from Pm the proportion of each land use type and land use specific Resist and Resisl values. The level is landscape because the resilience related calculation is county level and based on land use composition and landscape pattern indices. The approach is field observation because the study uses observational multi source datasets including land use NDVI NPP climate soil and socioeconomic data rather than experiments or simulations. The first author affiliation is Xinjiang University in Urumqi China so the institution country is CN. The study area is the arid region of northwest China including Xinjiang Hexi Corridor western Inner Mongolia and northern Ningxia so the field country is CN. No explicit coordinates are provided so the location is approximated from the regional extent and the location range is set to 0 because distinct coordinate points are not given. The study area includes deserts grasslands wetlands water bodies woodland cropland and construction land so habitat type is multi. Because level is landscape taxon is NA. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA because the resilience measurement is inferred and no disturbance response observation window is defined.

The introduction states that resilience is not directly measured but is inferred because species resilience to environmental stressors may be inferred by the strength of memory in the examined data. The methods quantify statistical memory using the decay in standard deviation with increasing averaging time scale and a linear slope theta that reflects autocorrelation structure. The discussion interprets higher memory in tree growth as lower resilience and faster variability decay as enhanced resilience. The target variables used in the main resilience inference are tree growth from irreversible stem radius changes and tree ring widths and tree water use from sap flux density derivative and reversible stem radius changes. The approach is field observation because the study uses dendrometers sap flow sensors and tree ring widths collected along an elevational gradient in the Swiss Alps and also uses ITRDB tree ring width observations across Europe. The study area coordinates are given as 46 23 40 N 7 45 35 E in the Lötschental region of the Swiss Alps. The first author affiliation lists Université de Montréal in Montreal Canada. The field data come from Switzerland for the elevational gradient and from the ITRDB across Europe which is not a single country so it is coded as Global. No disturbance is imposed or analysed as a defined pulse or press for resilience quantification because resilience is inferred from memory and the disturbance fields are therefore NA. Observation duration is NA because the measurement type is inferred even though the data span 2007 to 2015 and 1900 to 2011.

The study quantifies metapopulation persistence using a biophysical larval dispersal model and eigenanalysis where the migration matrices are used and persistence is summarized by the dominant eigenvalue. Resilience is treated as an outcome of node removal because the authors state they progressively removed all nodes to identify species-specific estimates of metapopulation resilience and they interpret the decrease in persistence resulting from removal of focal subpopulations. This is an observed measurement under the provided definition because a model disturbance is explicitly imposed by removing nodes and the response is directly quantified from the system response. No explicit equation style framework defining resilience is provided in the introduction or methods and resilience is mentioned as a keyword and later as species-specific estimates without a formal decomposition. Quantification best matches resistance because resilience is operationalized as the magnitude of decline in persistence after node removal described as the ratio between the largest eigenvalue after and before removing nodes and as percent drop in persistence. The target variable used for this resilience quantification is the dominant eigenvalue of the adjacency or migration matrix which is not among the provided target variable type options so it is coded as NA. The level is population because the analysis concerns metapopulation persistence and subpopulation nodes and their removal. The approach is modeling and simulation because they completed larval dispersal simulations using an existing spatially explicit biophysical model. The first author affiliation is University of Melbourne in Australia. The study area is the South-east Marine Region of Australia including waters off Victoria southern New South Wales Tasmania and eastern South Australia. No explicit coordinates are provided so the location is approximated as the center of the described region. The disturbance is model structural because the imposed perturbation is removal of nodes representing subpopulations. The disturbance is treated as pulse because nodes are removed and persistence is recalculated as a discrete removal exercise. Observation duration is based on ocean current data from 1992 to 2012 used in the study.

The study states that it tracked the response of two Acropora species to repeated bleaching events during the austral summers of 2015 and 2017 and it quantified recovery using transcriptome wide gene expression patterns described as transcriptomic resilience. It defines recovery as a return to a predisturbance baseline state and it describes resilience as the rate at which gene expression levels revert back to prestress baseline. In the methods it analysed rates of transcriptomic recovery following the 2015 bleaching event by calculating module eigengene expression across timepoints and comparing how quickly expression returned to baseline like levels. The discussion reports that transcriptome patterns returned to baseline within four months in Acropora gemmifera and took up to eight months in Acropora hyacinthus which is a speed difference consistent with recovery rate. The disturbance is a natural bleaching event triggered by anomalous temperatures and described as bleaching level temperatures and marine heatwaves which fits climatic disturbance without manipulation. The disturbance is treated as discrete events in 2015 and again in 2017 with subsequent monitoring which fits a pulse pattern. The target variable used for resilience quantification is gene expression module expression and transcriptome patterns which are physiological indicators. The sampling tracked colonies over time which is field observation and it also used temperature controlled tanks for experimental heat stress which is an indoor experiment. The field site is the fringing reef along the southern coast of Ofu in the National Park of American Samoa. The first author affiliation lists the Australian Institute of Marine Science in Perth WA Australia. The observation window for the tracked colonies spans from April 2015 to August 2017 which is 852 days.

The study analyzed 38 y of biannual monitoring data from 1980 to 2018 at six subtidal sites around San Nicolas Island California and used transect level time series of species abundances to reconstruct stability landscapes and quantify community movement. The dynamical systems perspective is described as the ball in cup heuristic of stability and resilience with deeper and steeper basins corresponding to more stable and resilient community states. Resilience was evaluated from the observed dynamics of community state in ordination space including velocities calculated as Euclidean distance between sequential sample points divided by the number of days elapsed. This velocity based measure corresponds to recovery rate because it quantifies the rate of return movement toward high frequency attractor centers and includes state dependent directional movement. The target variable used for resilience quantification is community composition represented by NMDS ordination of transect level species abundances through time. The work is field observation because it uses long term biannual surveys of fixed location transects and does not impose experimental manipulations. The first author affiliation is Oregon State University in the United States. The field location is San Nicolas Island in the Channel Islands off the southern California coast with coordinates given as N 33.25 W 119.50. The habitat is subtidal kelp forest on reefs at depths of 10 to 14 m which fits Marine Neritic. The taxa whose dynamics define the community state include macroalgae kelp and invertebrates including sea urchins so Plantae and Animalia apply. Disturbances initiating shifts are described as pulse perturbations such as large wave events and urchin mass mortality due to disease so climatic and biotic unmanipulated pulse disturbances apply. The observation duration spans from 1980 to 2018 which is 38 years or 13870 days using 365 days per year.

Framework: ecological stability = recovery time; resistance; variability
Justification: The study states that it uses simulated model food webs described by the general Lotka Volterra system and that it perturbed model systems by reducing the densities of the apex predator in each food web by 50 percent as a single pulse perturbation. It quantifies recovery time as the reciprocal of resilience and defines recovery time as the moment when the species density difference was smaller than 0.01 for all species and maintained for at least 50 time steps. This directly measures the system response to an imposed disturbance in the simulation so the measurement type is observed. The framework is explicitly presented as ecological stability having three key components recovery time the reciprocal of resilience resistance and variability. Because resilience is operationalized as recovery time the quantification is recovery time. Recovery time is computed from differences in species population densities between perturbed and unperturbed communities so the target variable type is density. The target variable is defined at the level of the food web community because it requires all species densities to meet the threshold. The approach is modeling and simulation because all data are generated in silico with simulations run over 1000 time steps. The first author affiliation includes Trinity College Dublin Dublin Ireland so the institution country is IE. The reporting summary states the spatial scale is not applicable to the simulation so field country and location are NA. The disturbance is a modeled reduction of apex predator densities which is a biotic perturbation so the disturbance type is model biotic. The perturbation is described as a single pulse perturbation so the disturbance pattern is pulse. Simulations were run over 1000 time steps with a step length of 1 so the observation duration is 1000 days.

The study quantified resilience using tree ring based basal area increment and explicitly calculated resilience indices from growth before during and after drought. The Methods define engineering resilience as capacity to return to pre disturbance performance levels and compute resistance recovery and resilience with resilience = BAIpostD divided by BAIpreD and also state resilience = resistance × recovery. Because resilience is calculated as BAIpostD divided by BAIpreD it matches recovery degree as the difference between post recovery state and pre disturbance state expressed as a ratio. The target variable used in the resilience calculation is basal area increment which is a growth measure derived from tree rings. The study imposed thinning and understory treatments including slash burning in replicated sites which is a manipulated structural disturbance and it also analyzed drought years defined by SPEI as unmanipulated climatic disturbance. Drought years were treated as discrete events 2012 and 2017 with recovery assessed using 3 year periods before and after which is a pulse framing. Data were collected in northeastern Spain at the southern foothills of the Pyrenees with sites Llobera Madrona and Secanella. The first author affiliation is CTFC AGROTECNIO Solsona Spain. The observation window for growth analysis is 2001 to 2020 which is 20 years or 7300 days and resilience indices use 3 year windows around 2012 and 2017 within that period. The habitat is sub Mediterranean forests dominated by Pinus nigra. The study subjects are black pine trees which are Plantae.

The abstract states that anthropogenic disturbances can affect ecological integrity including changes to the composition and resilience of benthic macroinvertebrate communities. The Introduction states that the new traits based index is derived from the richness of taxa within functional groups and that within-group richness is a component of functional redundancy and ecological resilience. The Discussion explicitly acknowledges that the TBI cannot be considered as a true and complete measure of functional redundancy or resilience and states that reductions in Ntaxa group and hence TBI scores are likely to be related to losses of functional redundancy and that habitats with high functional redundancy will tend to have higher inherent resistance and resilience. Resilience is therefore inferred from within-group taxonomic richness rather than measured as recovery dynamics. The Methods describe field sampling of benthic macrofauna at intertidal soft-sediment sites in Manukau and Waitemata Harbours near Auckland and additional sites including Mahurangi Harbour and validation sites around Auckland. The TBI is constructed from richness of macrofaunal taxa in seven functional trait groups and is regressed against mud percentage and heavy metal contamination gradients. The first author affiliation listed is National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research in Hamilton New Zealand. The study sites are in the Auckland region on the North Island of New Zealand and Figure 1 describes intertidal sites in Waitemata and Manukau Harbours and other Auckland area estuaries. The habitat is estuarine and coastal soft-sediment marine environment which fits the shallow nearshore Marine.Neritic category. The focal organisms are benthic macroinvertebrate macrofauna which are animals. No pulse or press disturbance with recovery tracking is defined because the analysis uses spatial gradients of mud and metals rather than a time bounded disturbance response and the instruction requires NA for disturbance fields when measurement is inferred. Observation duration is NA because resilience is not quantified from a temporal recovery window and the validation data include mixed single and repeated sampling without a defined first to last measurement for a disturbance response.

The study imposed heat stress by exposing Stylophora pistillata holobionts to water temperature increases from 27 C baseline to 29.5 C 32 C and 34.5 C in short term and long term experiments with a subsequent recovery period at baseline temperatures. Resilience was measured from the system response because corals were sampled at the end of the heat stress and after recovery and the paper quantified return to baseline gene expression for the majority of coral and algal genes during recovery and reported percentages of DEGs that returned to baseline expression at T2. This is a recovery degree measure because it is based on whether genes were no longer significantly different from the 27 C treatment at T2 and the fraction of DEGs returning to baseline.
