The framework is presented as transcriptomic resilience and transcriptomic acclimation with resilience defined as relaxing the response poststress and acclimation defined as return to a normal state of gene expression during long term thermal stress. The target variables used to quantify resilience are coral host and symbiont algal gene expression and bacterial microbiome composition because changes were determined via RNA Seq and 16S rRNA gene sequencing and recovery was assessed for DEGs and microbiome shifts. These correspond to physiological indicator for gene expression and composition for microbiome community composition. The level is community because the resilience targets include the bacterial microbiome composition and symbiotic algal gene expression within the holobiont rather than a single individual trait only. The approach is field experiment because ramets were collected from the IUI nursery in Eilat Israel and then subjected to controlled CBASS and RSS heat stress experiments. The first author affiliation is EPFL Lausanne Switzerland. The field location is given as 29 30 06.5 N 34 55 04.6 E at the Interuniversity Institute for Marine Science in Eilat Israel which converts to N29.501806 E34.917944. The disturbance is manipulated climatic because the disturbance is experimentally imposed heat stress. The disturbance pattern is pulse because the short term heat stress is a discrete event with ramp up hold and ramp down followed by recovery. Observation duration is 14 days because the long term experiment was performed for 2 weeks from January 18 to February 2 2019.

The paper defines stability as what Leigh and Holling call resilience and it distinguishes this from steadiness which refers to numerical fluctuations under more normal conditions. It states that resilience refers to the ability of the system or community species to persist in time given a major natural perturbation and it links this to whether the population will go extinct or remain in the game and to the rate of extinction. The resilience test uses a natural perturbation in Panama described as the introduction of a predatory fish Cichla ocellaris. The results are quantified as local extermination with the statement that predation caused the local extermination of 13 of 17 native fish species in the lake but caused no local extermination in the river. This uses observed fish census data from time distance counts at stations across years with figures showing changes and absence of species after the first appearance of Cichla. The target variable used to quantify resilience is species persistence and loss which is species numbers and local extermination of species. The study level is community because the resilience outcome is based on multiple fish species in the assemblage. The approach is field observation because the study uses field censuses and a natural introduction rather than an imposed experiment. The first author affiliation is University of Washington Seattle Washington USA. The field system is the Chagres River and Gatun Lake basin in Panama. The disturbance is biotic and unmanipulated because it is described as an inadvertent introduction and a natural perturbation. The disturbance is treated as press because Cichla is described as always present during the entire 5 year period at several stations and effects are assessed while the predator remains present. The observation duration is 5 years as stated for the 5 year study and the figures span 1972 through 1976.

The study used dendrochronological observational data and quantified drought resilience from stand level basal area increment. It defined drought resilience as Drl = BAI post divided by BAI pre where BAI post is average stand level BAI five years following a drought event and BAI pre is the average stand level BAI during the five years prior to the event. This is a direct recovery comparison of post drought growth to pre drought growth so the quantification is recovery degree. The framework is presented as resistance and resilience assessed together as stated in the objective and questions and in the definition section for Drs and Drl. The target variable used to quantify resilience is basal area increment which is a growth metric. The unit of analysis is population level as stated that plot level BAI was summed and used as a population level metric and that population level drought resistance and resilience were calculated. The approach is a long term replicated thinning experiment with repeated thinnings and field coring so it is a field experiment. The first author affiliation is University of Minnesota in the United States. The field site is in northeastern Minnesota USA with coordinates 47 8420N 918560W. The habitat is a Pinus resinosa forest. The droughts are treated as known drought events in 1948 1961 and 1988 so the disturbance is unmanipulated climatic and the pattern is pulse. Resilience uses five years prior and five years following each drought event so the observation window for resilience is ten years which is 3650 days.

The introduction states they investigated population resilience of intertidal limpets using a heat budget model and that they used an extremely hot day as an exemplar thermal event. The methods describe hindcasts of limpet body temperatures from 2009 to 2022 and then spatially explicit modeling for 1 February 2018 to estimate survival by comparing predicted body temperatures to thermal tolerance distributions. The results and discussion repeatedly interpret resilience as survival during the extreme heat event including statements that smoothing topography decreased modeled survival and that microhabitats and behavior increased estimated survival. Because resilience is quantified as estimated survival under the heat event rather than recovery dynamics it matches resistance as the ability to deviate less in mortality under disturbance. The target variable used to quantify resilience is survival which is a demographic parameter and is applied to limpet populations. The study uses modeling and simulation for heat budget hindcasts and uses field observation via long term limpet density data and robolimpet temperature measurements. The first author affiliation is University of Canterbury in New Zealand. The field sites are on the Kaikoura coastline in New Zealand and Figure 1 provides site coordinates and the Kaikoura aerodrome station coordinate. The disturbance is an unmanipulated extreme heat event described as record breaking air temperature during the midday low tide on 1 February 2018 which is climatic and treated as a discrete event. Observation duration is from 1 August 2009 to 31 August 2022 for the hindcast time series used to identify the extreme day.

Framework: risk assessment framework = risk factors + exposure + vulnerability + resilience
Justification: The abstract states the study establishes a multidimensional WLR risk assessment framework integrating risk exposure vulnerability and resilience. The methods state they used the PLUS model and scenario data to predict land use socioeconomic conditions water resources and vegetation indices and then used a WLR risk assessment index model and random forest to analyse drivers. Resilience is not quantified as recovery dynamics of an observed system state after a disturbance but is operationalised as five indicators including water retention capacity ecological landscape recovery index lake and reservoir storage capacity effectiveness and controllability of water resources and vegetation resilience. The WLRRI equation shows resilience is a factor index R combined with other indices rather than a direct recovery metric. The study area is the Yangtze and Yellow River Source Region on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau in China and the affiliations listed are in Beijing Zhengzhou and Nanjing China. The study area description says alpine grasslands dominate the vegetation cover at about 80 percent which supports the habitat type selection. Because the resilience measure is indicator based within a risk index and not a direct observed recovery response the measurement type is inferred and disturbance fields are not applicable under the instructions.

The study used a multiple before–after control–impact design in artificially constructed ponds and sampled zooplankton and water quality repeatedly across three hydroperiods. Resilience was evaluated from observed community recovery trajectories described as recurring deviations from and approaches to reference conditions in NMDS ordination space and from time profiles before and after contamination. The Discussion states that community resilience can be assessed by the rate at which community composition returned to a characteristic background pattern of variation and the NMDS loops were used to show approaches to reference conditions. The disturbance was an experimental contamination with the fire retardant Fire Trol 934 applied to ponds at the end of August 2003 and described as a protracted press disturbance. The disturbance is therefore manipulated and chemical and treated as press in the paper. The main resilience target is zooplankton community composition tracked by NMDS and supported by species abundance data. The work was conducted at Quintos de Mora Range Station in Toledo Central Spain and the first author affiliation is in Toledo Spain. The monitoring spanned from March 2003 to May 2005 which is 790 days.

The study objective was to investigate the resilience of Sargassum beds to physical disturbance and it simulated physical removal of macroalgal biomass from Sargassum beds and monitored recovery for 11 months. Resilience was quantified using observed Sargassum density height and biomass measured repeatedly from immediately prior to and immediately after manipulations and then at 2 month intervals to 11 months post clearance. The key resilience result is stated as Sargassum biomass recovered rapidly in trimmed plots and was indistinguishable from adjacent natural patches after 5 months and in removed plots biomass recovered to 50% of control biomass after 11 months. This is a recovery degree comparison because it reports biomass as a proportion of control after a defined recovery period. The approach is a field experiment because plots on the mid reef flat were manipulated and monitored and herbivore access was manipulated with exclusion cages on the reef crest. The first author affiliation is James Cook University Townsville Qld Australia. The field site is Orpheus Island Australia with coordinates given as 18 degrees 35 minutes S and 146 degrees 20 minutes E. The habitat is coral reef reef flat and reef crest which are shallow nearshore marine habitats. The focal organism is Sargassum spp which is a brown macroalga. Disturbance includes the experimental trimming and removal of Sargassum thalli and holdfasts and the herbivore exposure treatment that reduced holdfast numbers. The physical removal is treated as a discrete clearance event and recovery was tracked afterward which matches a pulse disturbance. The observation duration for the clearance recovery spans from May 2015 to April 2016 which is 11 months or 335 days using 30 days per month.

Framework: resilience = resistance + engineering resilience + state transition + nonequilibrium
Disturbance type: Unmanipulated_Climatic; Unmanipulated_Biotic; Unmanipulated_Biological resource use; Unmanipulated_Geophysical
Justification: The study analyzed long term records of plant species abundance and calculated Bray Curtis dissimilarity of species composition through time as net ecological displacement. It fit linear and nonlinear models to time series of dissimilarity to classify each plot into stability abrupt nonlinear shift reversible change or gradual linear shift. These four trajectories were stated to represent different aspects of ecological resilience and were linked to resilience concepts in Table 1. The target variable used for quantifying resilience was species composition change expressed as Bray Curtis dissimilarity or Sorensen dissimilarity. The approach used field observation because the data were long term vegetation monitoring records from permanent quadrats or transects and it also used modeling and simulation because it tested accuracy with simulated trajectories and used statistical models for classification. The first author affiliation was Indian Institute of Science Bangalore India. Field data came from sites across North America including Alberta Canada and multiple USA sites and Mt St Helens Washington. Disturbances discussed as affecting sites included major droughts prolonged droughts livestock grazing cheatgrass invasion and the Mt St Helens volcano eruption. The text did not provide coordinates and did not specify a single disturbance window or start stop timing for the resilience quantification so disturbance pattern and observation duration could not be determined.

The study quantifies vegetation resistance and recovery using Solar-Induced Chlorophyll Fluorescence which is described as a satellite based proxy for vegetation productivity and is used to characterize vegetation drought. Resistance and recovery are computed from SIF values with Yn defined as baseline SIF during non drought years Ye as SIF during drought years and Ye+2 as SIF two years after a drought. Drought events and degradation states were identified based on their natural occurrence and statistical thresholds within the study period which indicates unmanipulated disturbances. The disturbance is drought characterized by SPEI and land quality degradation characterized by LQI. Recovery is quantified as the difference between SIF during drought years and SIF two years after a drought which corresponds to recovery degree. The framework is presented as a joint assessment of vulnerability resistance and recovery of vegetation. The approach uses multi source remote sensing data and meteorological reanalysis which is field observation. The study area is Northeast China spanning Liaoning Jilin Heilongjiang and the eastern part of Inner Mongolia. The first author affiliation is Xi an University of Technology in China. The observation period is from 2001 to 2020 and the recovery window uses two years after drought so the overall observation duration is 20 years. Vegetation types include forest crop and grassland so the habitat type is multi. The resilience related metrics are for vegetation and the subject is plants.

The study developed a pantropical time series of monthly radar satellite observations from 1992 to 2018 and analyzed drought responses using those observations which is direct observation of system trajectories. It explicitly defines resistance as the ability to maintain predrought condition when drought occurs and resilience as the ability to return to predrought condition after the drought event which presents resistance and resilience in parallel. Resilience was calculated as (Ypost minus Ypre) divided by Ypre where Ypre is predrought radar signal and Ypost is postdrought radar signal which quantifies recovery degree relative to predrought condition after a fixed postdrought window. The target variable used in the resilience formula is radar backscatter signal representing upper canopy condition and interpreted as reflecting canopy moisture and structure and linked to biomass and productivity so it is an ecosystem function proxy. The analysis is based on satellite observations rather than manipulative experiments or simulations so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation includes Laboratoire Evolution et Diversite Biologique in Toulouse France so the institution country is France. The study examines intact tropical rainforests globally across the Americas Africa and Asia so the field country and location are global. The habitat is intact tropical rainforests which is forest. The disturbance is drought identified by MCWD and described as drought events which are climatic and unmanipulated. Droughts are treated as discrete events with resistance and resilience evaluated 2 years before and 2 years after each drought event so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The observation duration spans from 1992 to 2018 which is 26 years or 9490 days using 365 days per year.

The study used a 39-year time series of satellite observations of kelp surface canopy from 1984 to 2022 and analysed kelp canopy coverage inside and outside MPAs. It explicitly assessed kelp resilience to marine heatwaves by analysing resistance and recovery of kelp canopy coverage to severe marine heatwaves between 2014 and 2016. Resistance and recovery were defined as relative kelp coverage during and after the MHWs compared to a pre-heatwave baseline. Recovery was quantified as proportional change in mean annual maximum kelp coverage in 2017 to 2022 relative to the 1984 to 2013 baseline and compared between MPAs and paired reference sites. The target variable used in these resilience metrics was kelp canopy coverage derived from Landsat. The approach was remote sensing and the paper states it did not involve any fieldwork. The first author affiliation is University of California Los Angeles in the United States. The MPAs and kelp data are along the coast of California including the Channel Islands in the United States and the reference search window was centered on each MPA. The disturbance was the 2014 to 2016 marine heatwaves treated as a discrete event window with post-event recovery period. Observation duration spans from 1984 to 2022.

The study introduces and analyzes a generalized reaction diffusion model and states that most results are based on mathematical and numerical analysis of Eq. 3 and that models were solved in MATLAB and PyOpenCL which indicates modeling and simulation. Resilience is treated through model behavior near tipping points and critical thresholds as the paper proposes an indicator framework to signal proximity to ecological tipping points and shows spinodal and binodal regions and tipping points where the system shifts between patchy and uniform states. This matches latitude because resilience is linked to a critical threshold and tipping point beyond which the patchy state is lost and abrupt shifts and hysteresis occur. The target variable used in the resilience indicator framework is the spatial patch size distribution and patch cover and local densities and the methods quantify patch sizes and patch size distributions from the simulated u field which is a spatial composition pattern. Disturbance is represented in the model as a local disturbance that reduces patch size and can push it below a critical size threshold leading to patch loss which is a modeled structural perturbation. The disturbance is treated as a discrete local disturbance event after which the patch may still disappear even when the disturbance has passed which corresponds to a pulse. The observation duration follows the model implementation where dimensionless time t ran from 0 to 2500 with timestep 0.005 giving a total simulated duration of 12.5 time units.

The study developed a metacommunity lottery model and simulated wildfire scenarios by setting to zero the community abundance of a randomly selected proportion of ponds within a burned area. Resilience was quantified from the system response after the simulated disturbance using return rate and diversity recovery metrics. The return rate was defined as the inverse of the number of coalescent iterations required to reach burned ponds and described as the temporal rate of community recovery and the speed at which the system recovers. Diversity recovery was defined from species richness in burned ponds after one and after one hundred recolonization iterations. Because resilience was directly computed from post disturbance recolonization dynamics in the model it matches observed measurement under the provided definition. The target variable used in the resilience metrics was species richness described as average number of species in burned water bodies at T1 and T100. Species richness is a diversity variable and it was used at the local community level of water bodies. The disturbance was wildfire and the simulations were explicitly described as wildfire scenarios and simulated fire size and intensity. The disturbance was treated as a discrete event followed by recolonization iterations which matches a pulse framing. The first author affiliation listed was University of Girona in Girona Spain. The study site was the Albera region on the NE Iberian Peninsula and the wildfire information was from the Catalan Government indicating Spain as the field country. The habitat was a network of water bodies and temporary ponds which fits wetland. The taxa described were pond macroinvertebrates including Gastropoda and Odonata which are animals. The recolonization process was run for a total of one hundred iterations which provides the observation window length used for resilience metrics.

The study uses in silico trait based simulations and applies an ecosystem stress test where a stress is applied whereby a species is lost and post extinction ecosystem function is measured and then biomass compensation is modeled. Ecosystem vulnerability is quantified as the number of species needed to maintain functioning above a defined threshold of initial function which is a direct measure of the system response to imposed species loss. Resilience is discussed in parallel with resistance in the statement that the framework integrates the effect of resistance and resilience on ecosystem function in natural systems. The target variable used in the stress test is total ecosystem function Ta and the proportion of initial ecosystem function remaining. The biological level is ecosystem because the quantified variable is an aggregate ecosystem function irrespective of species identity. The disturbance is model imposed species loss and extinction scenarios which is a structural manipulation in a model. The disturbance is treated as discrete steps where at each time step a species is first lost which matches a pulse framing. The observation duration is 100 time steps because S equals 100 species and the stress test proceeds until after all species are extinct.

The study defines resilience as the time required for an ecological system to return to a steady state following a perturbation and it assumes unburned plots reflect a stable state for ant plant interactions. It quantifies resilience by observing that network metrics in burned plots took up to half a year to return to similar levels from unburned plots and it also states that differences were no longer observed four months post fire. The target variables used for this recovery are interaction network metrics namely interactions frequency per plot and interactions Shannon diversity per plot. These metrics are derived from observed ant plant associations monitored quarterly in 35 plots for one year before an unmanaged fire and for another year after fire. The disturbance is an unmanaged fire event that started in September 2014 and persisted for two months and it is treated as a discrete event with post fire recovery monitoring. The approach is field observation because the study monitored plots in seven campo rupestre sites and recorded interactions in the field. The field location is Morro da Pedreira Environmental Protection Area southeastern Brazil with coordinates 19 17 49 S 43 35 28 W. The habitat is described as a tropical megadiverse mountaintop grassland ecosystem campo rupestre. The focal organisms are ants which are Animalia and resilience is quantified at the community interaction network level.

The paper defines resilience as the forests ability to resist and recover from disturbances. The study uses an individual based model and runs a simulation experiment with a disturbance module where trees are randomly picked and removed until a targeted basal area loss threshold is reached. The disturbance is applied at a given iteration and then the forest is allowed to recover freely for a further 600 years which treats disturbance as a discrete event. Recovery is quantified from simulated trajectories of ecosystem properties by defining Rx(t) as the ratio of disturbed to control values and then computing the Euclidean distance to perfect recovery Req = 1 and integrating this distance over time with the inverse area used as the resilience index IR. This uses the systems own simulated state variable dynamics after an imposed disturbance so the measurement is observed within a model. The resilience index is based on cumulative deviation from the undisturbed baseline which corresponds to recovery degree rather than a single recovery time or rate. The variables used to compute IR include above ground biomass and stem numbers for structure and GPP NPP and respiration for functioning which map to biomass abundance and ecosystem function. The model parameterization and validation and trait data are from old growth forest plots in French Guiana and the first author affiliation lists Kourou French Guiana. No coordinates are provided so Kourou is used as the location reference. The simulated timeline is 600 years pre disturbance plus 600 years post disturbance which is 1200 years or 438000 days.

The study defines functional resistance as reduced changes in biomass production between control and stressed communities after 28 d of atrazine exposure and defines functional resilience as the proportional change in deviation in biomass production between day 28 and day 49. It defines compositional resistance as Bray Curtis dissimilarity of species densities between control and stressed communities at day 28 and defines compositional resilience as the change in Bray Curtis dissimilarity between day 28 and day 49. These definitions quantify resilience from observed changes in biomass production and species densities measured in microcosms at day 28 and day 49. The framework is presented as stability being composed of resistance and resilience because the introduction contrasts functional resistance with functional resilience and the methods operationalize both as components of stability. The resilience quantification corresponds to recovery rate because it is based on the change in deviation between two time points after stress alleviation rather than time to recovery. The target variables used to quantify resilience are biomass production and community composition via Bray Curtis dissimilarity. The organisms are marine diatoms Bacillariophyceae which are phytoplankton and the paper uses diatom strains isolated from a sample near the Thornton bank in the Southern bight of the North Sea. Atrazine exposure is a manipulated chemical stressor applied for four weeks followed by transfer to atrazine free medium for three weeks which is treated as a pulse disturbance with a defined recovery interval. The observation window for resilience spans from the start to day 49 because the experiment compares outcomes after 28 and 49 days. The first author affiliation is Ghent University in Belgium. The field sampling location is near the Thornton bank in Belgian North Sea waters and no coordinate range is provided beyond a single site.

The study uses 80-year fishery data from Lake Qiandao and analyzes temporal dynamics including breakpoints in local fish catch and early warning signals. It states that early warning signals are statistical metrics such as increase in autocorrelation and variance that have been used to quantify temporal resilience. It reports that resilience of local fish populations decreased before breakpoints and was signaled by increasing standard deviation and AR(1) and that composite resilience indicators SD plus AR1 were used. Autocorrelation based indicators are treated as recovery rate measures under the provided definitions. The target variable used for resilience indicators is local fish catch which the paper treats as local fish population abundance and collapse. The work combines long-term field monitoring with a theoretical competition model implemented in STELLA to simulate population dynamics under alien fish input scenarios. The first author affiliation is in Wuhan China at the Chinese Academy of Sciences. The field site is Lake Qiandao in Zhejiang Province China with coordinates given as 29.22 to 29.50 N and 118.36 to 119.14 E and the average coordinate is reported. The system is a freshwater reservoir so the dominant IUCN habitat category is Wetland. The disturbance linked to observed resilience dynamics is continuous human stocking and associated alien carp pressure described as continuously disturbed by humans via input of silver carp and bighead carp and the model also varies alien fish input as a disturbance scenario. The disturbance is treated as ongoing pressure rather than a discrete event. The observation window for the long-term time series spans 1936 to 2016 which is 80 years or 29200 days using 365 days per year.

Framework: resilience = return to a pre-disturbed condition + maintain its essential structure and function
Justification: The study used a linked modeling approach combining the MC1 Dynamic Global Vegetation Model with state-and-transition models and states that it was used to examine forest resilience under different climate change and management scenarios. Resilience is explicitly defined as the capacity of the system to return to a pre-disturbed condition and still maintain its essential structure and function. Resilience was quantified using probability of exceedance to assess the likelihood that future vegetation types and structure under climate change will be similar in area to current vegetation types and structure with thresholds defined as current area and 90 percent and 75 percent of current area. The target variables used in this quantification were areas of vegetation types and forest structures including dry mixed conifer and moist mixed conifer and dry large open and moist large dense which are composition and structure categories. The study area is located in central Oregon east of the Cascade Mountain crest and is shown as a landscape scale analysis of a 1 023 808 ha area. The approach is modeling and simulation with 30 Monte Carlo simulations per scenario run for 90 years using annual time steps. Disturbances included modeled climate change scenarios and modeled wildfire frequency and severity and insect and disease transitions and management actions including thinning prescribed fire planting salvage harvest. The fire suppression scenario and climate change scenarios are maintained across the simulation period and fire probabilities are scaled annually based on MC1 trends indicating a press type treatment of the disturbance drivers. The first author affiliation is Washington State Department of Natural Resources in the United States. The field country is the United States because the study area is in central Oregon USA. The observation duration is 90 years which equals 32850 days.

The introduction states that they cannot know which portions are resilient without waiting years or decades and chronicling recovery and that they aimed to estimate and predict resilience by quantifying ecological and social aspects and modeling them across the study area. The methods describe a simple additive overlay model that sums ecological indicators and manager Likert scale responses to create composite indices of ecological resilience social resilience and total resilience. The ecological resilience layer is built from indicators such as topographic diversity geophysical diversity vegetation diversity water availability habitat connectivity species richness and human modification rather than observed recovery after fire. The social resilience layer is based on manager reported likelihood to meet objectives and change practices plus survey responses about preparedness and past ability to change. Figures 2 3 and 4 show composite indices from 0 to 1 for ecological social and total resilience rather than time series recovery trajectories. Resilience is defined in the abstract as ability to remain in or return to state but the study operationalizes it as predicted likelihood of retaining characteristics and meeting management objectives. No explicit equation framework including resilience is defined beyond narrative definitions so the framework is none. The first author affiliation is Northern Arizona University in Flagstaff Arizona and Conservation Science Partners in California which are in the United States. The study area is the EPA Level III Sonoran Desert Ecoregion of Arizona USA so the field country is the United States. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated as the central Sonoran Desert in Arizona and the range is set to 0 because only a single average point is reported. The habitat is the Sonoran Desert and is described as a desert ecosystem. Because the level is landscape and resilience is a social ecological composite the taxon is not applicable. Disturbance is fire but because measurement is inferred the disturbance fields are NA and observation duration is NA.

The study uses numerical simulations of a dynamic model of mutualistic communities with empirically informed parameter ranges. It states that stability is examined using community resilience defined as increases in return rates to the equilibrium following a perturbation and evaluated by the absolute value of the dominant eigenvalue of the Jacobian matrix at equilibrium. This is a direct quantification of system response to a perturbation within a model so the measurement is observed under the provided definition. The quantification corresponds to recovery rate because return rates and dominant eigenvalue based measures are treated as recovery rate. The target variable type is interaction because resilience is computed from the Jacobian of the interaction network and depends on interaction strengths and network structure rather than a single species state variable. The level is community because the resilience metric is community resilience of the whole mutualistic community. The approach is modeling and simulation because the analyses are simulations and eigenvalue calculations. The first author affiliation is Rice University in the United States. No field sampling location is used for resilience quantification because the work is theoretical and only parameter ranges are informed by empirical estimates. The disturbance is a model perturbation to the equilibrium so it is model structural. The disturbance pattern is pulse because it is described as a perturbation followed by return to equilibrium rather than a sustained driver. Observation duration is not reported because resilience is computed from eigenvalues rather than a time window.

The study states that it assesses drought resistance Rd of ecosystems in the Tibetan Plateau and analyzes ecosystem resistance in terms of change in WUE in drought years. It defines an indicator of ecosystem resilience to drought as the ratio of WUE in the driest year to the mean annual WUE and labels this ratio Rd as drought resistance. WUE is calculated from annual ANPP and ET raster data at the pixel level for 2000 to 2015 and the driest year is selected per pixel using SPEI which uses temperature and precipitation. Because resilience is quantified from observed time series derived from remote sensing and station based gridded climate data across 16 years it is Observed. The paper discusses ecosystem resilience and ecosystem resistance together and operationalizes the analysis as resistance to drought rather than a recovery framework. The target variable used in the resilience indicator is WUE which the paper defines as carbon uptake per unit water loss linking carbon and water cycling which is an ecosystem function. The focal system is Tibetan Plateau grassland ecosystem so the level is Ecosystem and the habitat is Grassland. The disturbance is drought described with SPEI and hydroclimatic disturbances so it is an unmanipulated climatic disturbance. The analysis treats drought as occurring within the 2000 to 2015 window without an imposed recovery interval and uses the driest year relative to the multi year mean so the disturbance is treated as press within the observation period. The first author affiliation includes Zhejiang A and F University in Hangzhou China so the institution country is China. The study region is the Tibetan Plateau in China and Fig 1 shows the geographical extent of the Tibetan Plateau so the field country is China and the location is represented by an approximate plateau center coordinate. The observation duration spans from 2000 to 2015 which is 16 years or 5840 days.

The introduction defines resilience as capacity to respond and recover from fire disturbance and later states we defined boreal forest resilience as the capacity of a post-fire stand to recover its structure composition and function to pre-fire conditions. The methods state we used post-fire tree recruitment density the aboveground biomass the understory shrub cover and the herb cover as indicators of forest resilience and that we used post-fire regeneration performance as a metric of resilience to represent the ability of boreal forest to bounce back after fire disturbance. These are field measured state variables and include time since fire classes of 8 years and 18 years after stand-replacing fires in 2010 and 2000 so resilience is based on observed post-disturbance recovery. The disturbance is wildfire described as stand-replacing fire and burn severity classes derived from dNBR and field burn assessment so the disturbance is unmanipulated fire and treated as discrete past events which matches a pulse framing. Resilience is quantified by comparing post-fire indicators across burn severity time since fire and topographic position rather than estimating a recovery rate or time to return so it aligns with recovery degree relative to pre-fire conditions as stated in the definition. Target variables used in the GLM and comparisons include recruitment density aboveground biomass shrub cover herb cover and ratios of broad-leaf to needle-leaf trees and shrub to herb cover which are density biomass cover and composition. The study area coordinates are given as 51.17 to 51.56 N and 122.42 to 123.18 E in the Huzhong National Natural Reserve and the average is reported. The first author affiliation is Institute of Applied Ecology Chinese Academy of Sciences in Shenyang China. The habitat is larch dominated boreal forest and the measured subjects are trees shrubs and herbs which are plants.

Framework: resilience = elasticity + amplitude + hysteresis + malleability + damping
Justification: The introduction states that resilience is defined as recovery following disturbance and it discusses four components elasticity amplitude hysteresis and malleability and it adds damping and it treats inertia as a separate property. The methods state that plots were burned at 2 yr 3 yr or 6 yr intervals either in spring or fall with a control and that plots were sampled every spring beginning in 1969 before the initial burn and annually through 1981 producing 13 yr of data. The study quantifies components of resilience using trajectories or vectors of sites through ordination space and it uses detrended correspondence analysis and it uses the vectors defined by the first two ordination axes to quantify the components of resilience. Elasticity is quantified as the rate of recovery in ordination axis units on a year to year basis using vector lengths toward the origin. The target variable used for the main resilience analysis is point contacts by stratum for the 30 most common species which the results state consistently showed directional movement that could feasibly be used to quantify components of resilience. This variable represents community composition and structure in ordination space rather than a single species metric. The disturbance is experimental fire treatments so it is manipulated fire and it is treated as discrete burns followed by recovery vectors so it is pulse. The field site is Puech du Juge 10 km north of Montpellier in southern France and the first author affiliation listed is in Montpellier France. The habitat is Quercus coccifera garrigue which is a Mediterranean shrub dominated community. The observation duration from 1969 to 1981 is 13 years which is 4745 days using 365 days per year.

The study defines stress resilience as the ability to continue breeding efforts despite challenges and states that the measure of resilience was nest abandonment during incubation. Nest abandonment was directly observed after experimental manipulations that temporarily reduced flight efficiency using feather restraint and increased perceived predation risk using a mink mount. Because resilience is quantified from the system response to imposed challenges it is an observed measurement. No equation or explicit component framework including resilience is provided beyond the definition so the framework is none. The quantification is not recovery time recovery rate recovery degree resistance latitude or invariability because it is a binary outcome of abandonment probability so it is other. The target variable is nest abandonment which is a reproductive outcome so it is a demography parameter and it is measured across females in the population so the level is population. The work is a field experiment conducted at two field sites in Ithaca New York. The first author affiliation is Cornell University in the United States and the field sites are in the United States with coordinates given as about 42 degrees 29 minutes north and 76 degrees 27 minutes west converted to N42.4833 W76.4500. The feather restraint manipulation is a structural disturbance and the predator mount exposure is a biotic disturbance and both are manipulated. The treatments were applied for 5 days or 5 to 6 days and then removed so the disturbance is treated as a pulse and the observation window for disturbance response spans about 7 days from incubation day 6 or 7 to recapture on day 12 or 13.

The study uses Earth System Model data and states it is “Combining Earth System Model data with a robust detection and attribution framework” and it “leverage[s] data from four state-of-the-art Earth System Models” which makes the resilience-related assessment based on modeled system trajectories. Resilience is defined in Table 1 as “The ability of an ecosystem (e.g., a forest) to recover from any type of disturbance” but the analysis operationalizes resilience loss via thresholds and abrupt nonlinear decline rather than measuring recovery. The paper identifies “a global warming threshold of 2.3 °C beyond which forest decline accelerates nonlinearly” and discusses “critical threshold” and “tipping point” risk which matches a latitude style quantification based on a limit beyond which dynamics change. The target variable used throughout detection and classification is “tree cover fraction” and forest decline is “reductions in tree cover” so the target variable type is cover. Basin-wide forest decline is computed “by summing the local forest decline across the Amazon basin” indicating an ecosystem-scale target. The approach is modeling and simulation because all core analyses are performed on CMIP6 ESM outputs and methods fit models to “time series of tree cover fraction” from ESMs. The first author affiliation is “Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität, Munich 80333, Germany” so the institution country is DE. The study area is the Amazon basin but the data source for resilience quantification is CMIP6 via ESGF and not collected in a single country and the paper also uses global warming levels so field country and location are coded as Global. The dominant habitat is the “Amazon rainforest ecosystem” so forest. The focal biota is forest trees represented by tree cover so Plantae. The drivers treated as disturbances are “land-use change (primarily through deforestation)” and “anthropogenic climate change” and these are applied as scenario forcings in models so Model_Landuse and infrastructure development and Model_Climatic. The disturbances are treated as ongoing forcings across 1950 to 2100 without a defined recovery interval so press. The observation window spans 1950 to 2100 which is 151 years or 55185 days.

The study compares bryophyte assemblages in old forest that had never been clear cut with matching young stands established after clear cutting of old forests 30 to 50 years earlier. It states that it used the magnitude of the difference in assemblages between old and young stands as a measure of change and therefore resilience with large difference implying low resilience. It also states that high resistance to disturbance or high similarity to pre disturbance conditions is interpreted as high resilience and it separately asks about the resilience component resistance to invasion. Resilience is quantified using species composition differences tested with MRPP and ordination and using species richness changes between old and young stands. The target variables used for resilience quantification are therefore species composition and species richness which are community level variables. The approach is field observation because bryophyte data were collected in 72 plots during 1996 to 2000. The first author affiliation is Umea University Sweden and the study area is northern Sweden. The study area is given as northern Sweden between 62 80 and 66 81 N and the average latitude is about 64.44 N and the figure shows the area in northern Scandinavia. The habitat is boreal forest including streamside and upland forests. The focal organisms are bryophytes liverworts and mosses which are plants. The disturbance is clear cutting which is a timber extraction practice and thus biological resource use and it is not imposed by the researchers but analyzed as past clear cuts. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event because stands were clear cut and then assessed 30 to 50 years after. The observation duration for resilience is 30 to 50 years which is 10950 to 18250 days and the maximum span is 18250 days.

The study states that vegetation changes were studied for 21 yr in two clearcut logged and slash-burned Pseudotsuga forests in the western Cascade Range of Oregon. It defines resistance as based on the maximum Euclidean distance between pre- and postdisturbance samples and defines resilience as the degree of community recovery inversely proportional to the Euclidean distance between predisturbance and final samples in ordination space. The target data are repeated visual estimates of projected canopy cover for each understory vascular plant species and the analysis uses DCA ordination and Euclidean distances to quantify compositional dissimilarity and recovery. Resilience is therefore quantified as long-term recovery toward predisturbance composition which matches recovery degree rather than recovery rate or recovery time. The framework is presented as community stability characterized by resistance and resilience and the paper discusses resistance and resilience together without defining a higher-level equation that includes them. The approach is field observation using permanent plots sampled from 1962 through the final sampling year shown as 1983. The first author affiliation is Oregon State University in the United States and the field sites are the H. J. Andrews Experimental Forest in Oregon in the United States. The habitat is forest and the subjects are understory vascular plants. Disturbances are clearcut logging and slash burning treated as discrete events followed by recovery trajectories.

The paper states it is investigating resilience of reef fish assemblages and that nuclear tests caused multiple instantaneous removals of fish over areas of approximately 12.5 km2. Fish assemblages were measured by visual scuba censuses at 16 lagoon sites in October 1990 and again in October 1996 using permanent transects. The study quantifies change in assemblage structure as species composition and relative abundance using Bray Curtis similarity between 1990 and 1996 and treats this as assemblage stability or instability. It reports that assemblages restored assemblage structure within 1 to 5 yr and that effects are scarcely evident as little as 1 to 5 yr after the test which is a recovery time statement. The disturbance is not experimentally imposed by the researchers and is described as underground nuclear tests that instantly removed virtually all fishes within 2000 m which is a pulse event. The observation window for the resilience comparison is from October 1990 to October 1996 which is about six years or 2190 days. The first author affiliation is Universite de Perpignan in France. The field sites are at Mururoa Atoll in French Polynesia and the habitat is coral reef lagoon sites which are shallow nearshore marine environments. The subjects are reef fish assemblages which are animals.

The introduction defines resilience as returning to the reference state after a temporary disturbance and defines resistance as staying essentially unchanged despite disturbance and the study discusses both together as resistance and resilience. The methods state that assessing conversion resistance and resilience requires both pre and post disturbance measures of state variables and the study uses pre Las Conchas field plots from 2003 to 2006 and re measures 102 plots after the 2011 Las Conchas fire in 2013 or 2014. Resilience is quantified as vegetation recovery relative to forests not exposed to recent prior fire and in the analysis as pre to post Las Conchas changes in total vegetation cover change and compositional distance. These are recovery degree measures because they compare post fire state to pre fire state rather than estimating a rate or time to return. The target variables used for these metrics are total vegetation cover and vegetation composition based on coverages of 433 species and four structural strata. The level is community because composition and total cover are calculated across multiple plant species within plots. The approach is field observation because the study re measured field plots and also used satellite derived burn severity but did not impose experimental treatments. The disturbance is wildfire because the Las Conchas fire reburned earlier burns and burn severity was measured for multiple fires and the disturbance is unmanipulated. The disturbance pattern is pulse because the Las Conchas fire is a discrete 2011 event with post fire re measurement 2 to 3 years later. The first author affiliation is Western State Colorado University in the United States and the field site is in the eastern Jemez Mountains of New Mexico in the United States. Figure 1 places the study near Los Alamos New Mexico so the location is approximated at Los Alamos coordinates. Observation duration is from the first pre fire measurements in June 2003 to the last post fire measurements in 2014 which is 11 years or 4015 days using 365 days per year.

Framework: integrated ecological resistance = ecological elements + ecological importance + ecological resilience
Justification: The study constructs an index system for integrated ecological resistance with aspects of ecological elements ecological importance and ecological resilience. Ecological resilience is not measured as recovery after a disturbance but is computed from proxy indicators because the method states that ecological resilience is measured using three indices ecological sensitivity ecological stability and social disturbance. The ecological adaptation resistance R is defined as R = S1 × S2 / S3 where S1 is quantified through the variation amplitude of perennial NDVI S2 is the reciprocal of distance to the nearest key ecological patch and S3 is expressed by spatial population density in combination with GDP per area. These are static or derived indicators and do not show system recovery dynamics after a defined disturbance. The framework explicitly defines IER = ωS × S + ωI × I + ωR × R and states that R is measured by ecological resilience. Data include MOD13Q1 NDVI values from 2001 to 2013 DEM land use vegetation maps precipitation population density and GDP per area processed in ArcGIS at 240 m resolution indicating a GIS and remote sensing field observation approach. The first author affiliation is Peking University Beijing China and Shenzhen Graduate School Peking University Shenzhen China. The study area is Dali Bai Autonomous Prefecture Yunnan Province China with coordinates 98.8667 to 101.0500 E and 24.6667 to 26.7000 N and the average coordinate is reported. Habitat inputs include woodland farmland water shrubland and construction land so multiple habitat types are involved. Because the resilience quantification is inferred and the level is landscape taxon and disturbance fields are not applicable. Observation duration is not applicable because resilience is inferred rather than observed recovery.

The Introduction defines resilience as the ability of a system to return to its original state following a disturbance. The Introduction also states it was assumed that resistance did not factor in the vegetation response and it explicitly defines resistance as the ability of a community to remain unchanged by disturbance. The Methods state field experiments were carried out in Adventdalen Svalbard Norway and disturbance treatments modelled on grubbing by pink footed geese were applied in early June 2006. The Methods state the impact of disturbance on vegetation was expressed as community dissimilarity between treatments and control plots within each block and if a treated plot is similar to the control plot it is deemed to be resilient to that disturbance in the short term. The Methods specify dissimilarity was calculated based on vascular species composition and abundance using the Ruzicka index. The Discussion states disturbance impact was measured by community dissimilarity three growing seasons after disturbance and this measure of impact could be regarded as the inverse of community resilience. Because resilience is quantified from treatment control community dissimilarity after disturbance using observed shoot abundance data it is observed not inferred. Because the metric reflects similarity to the original state it corresponds to recovery degree. The target variable used for resilience quantification is vascular plant community composition and abundance summarized as community dissimilarity. The study level is community because the dissimilarity metric is calculated across multiple vascular plant species within communities. The approach is field experiment because disturbance was simulated and applied to plots in the field. The first author affiliation is University of Aberdeen UK. The field location is Adventdalen Svalbard Norway with coordinates given as 78 10 N 16 05 E. The habitat is tundra communities including wetlands and mires and the most resilient communities were wetlands and mires so wetland is the major habitat type for the resilience result. The disturbance is manipulated grubbing disturbance by herbivores so it is manipulated biotic. The disturbance is a pulse because treatments were applied in early June 2006 and response was measured in July 2008 after the event. The observation duration from early June 2006 to July 2008 is about three growing seasons which is approximately 1125 days.

The study goal is to identify spatial regimes and transitions using Fisher information applied to animal community data rather than to quantify recovery after a disturbance. The terrestrial data are described as a snapshot because the authors state that the bird species abundance data are a snapshot of the 2007 bird community at each route location. The marine data are also a single survey because the authors state zooplankton community surveys were conducted in 2008 from August to September along a transect. Resilience is discussed conceptually as loss of resilience in relation to declining Fisher information but the analysis does not measure recovery dynamics such as return to baseline recovery time or recovery rate after a defined disturbance. The core variable used in the Fisher information calculation is community species composition and abundance because the authors state they used spatially explicit avian and zooplankton community species composition data and created route species abundance and site taxa abundance matrices. Therefore resilience is inferred from an index derived from community composition patterns across space rather than observed recovery. No explicit equation framework defining resilience as a sum of components is provided in the introduction methods or discussion. The first author affiliation is University of Nebraska Lincoln in the United States. The field data come from the central and northern United States for the Breeding Bird Survey routes and from Canada for the Three Oceans zooplankton survey. The paper provides maps but no explicit coordinates so the location and location range cannot be extracted. The habitats span montane forest grassland agriculture matrix northern forest wetland matrix and multiple marine domains so the dominant habitat type is multiple. The taxa are birds and zooplankton which are animals. Disturbance types and patterns are discussed as context such as land use change and climate change but are not used as imposed or analysed disturbances for resilience quantification in the data so disturbance fields are not applicable. Observation duration for resilience is not defined because the data are spatial snapshots rather than a time series of response.

The paper states it evaluates ecosystem health by constructing the measurement model of Vigor Service Resilience and gives the formula EHI = (EV × ES × ER)^(1/3) which defines ecosystem health as a framework including resilience. Resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics after a disturbance but is computed as ER from landscape ecological indices derived from land use data using Fragstats and a moving window method with SHDI AWMPFD PD CONT and COHESION terms. The text says by assessing the stability of ecosystem structure using landscape indices we can portray the ecosystem ability to respond to and recover from external disturbances and it then defines ER as a weighted composite of those indices rather than observed recovery. Therefore resilience measurement is inferred and quantification is inferred. The target variable used to quantify resilience is ER based on patch connectivity fragmentation and diversity indices which are regional network parameters. The approach uses multi source raster datasets including MODIS NPP and land use and climate rasters which are field observation via remote sensing and existing spatial datasets. The study area is Three River Source National Park in southwestern Qinghai Province China and the counties listed place the field country in China. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to the park region around 34 N 96 E and the range is set to 0 because only one average coordinate is reported. The park contains grassland meadows lake wetlands glaciers and snow mountains so the habitat type is multi. Because the level is landscape taxon is not applicable. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is inferred from static landscape structure rather than observed disturbance response.

The study uses simulations based on the structure of 79 empirical mutualistic networks and generates stochastic time series of species biomasses and total community biomass. It quantifies critical slowing down indicators from these biomass time series including variance expressed as coefficient of variation and autocorrelation at lag 1. The text states these indicators are used as indicators of loss of resilience and as early warning signals for an impending transition. Autocorrelation at lag 1 is defined as correlation of the time series to itself shifted by one time step which is a recovery rate type metric under the provided definitions. The target variable used for the indicators is species biomasses and aggregated total community biomass. The resilience related analysis is conducted at species and community level across mutualistic communities so the level is community. The disturbance is imposed in the model by gradually decreasing mutualistic strength for all plant animal interactions described as a scenario of gradual environmental stress. This gradual decrease is maintained across the simulation steps so it is treated as a press disturbance. The first author affiliation is Estacion Biologica de Donana Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Cientificas in Sevilla Spain. The empirical networks are stated to cover a wide geographic range across all continents and climatic zones so the field country and location are global and the habitat type is multi. The study subjects are plant pollinator and plant seed disperser mutualistic communities which correspond to Plantae and Animalia.

The study states that it used an eco-evolutionary simulation model and simulated a coral reef metacommunity response to climate warming. Resilience is evaluated from the system response in the simulations including that corals declined and then recovered to high levels of coral cover and figures report time series of coral cover. The main performance metrics include final year 500 coral cover and minimum coral cover across 500 years and the discussion states supplementation influenced the degree to which corals recovered after interim declines. Because resilience is quantified using simulated trajectories of coral cover it is treated as observed under the rule that model disturbances with quantified response are observed. No explicit equation style framework defining resilience as a component of another variable is provided. The quantification aligns with recovery degree because it uses final coral cover at year 500 and whether coral cover recovered to high levels. The target variable used for resilience quantification is fractional coral cover. This is an ecosystem level state variable describing habitat area occupied by coral rather than an individual or population count. The approach is modeling and simulation not field observation or experiment. The first author affiliation is University of Washington in the United States. The disturbance is climate warming represented as an asymptotically increasing temperature trajectory with anomalies so it is a model climatic disturbance treated as a press driver. The monitoring period after warming began is 500 years which is 182500 days.

The study states it uses a spatially explicit indicator based approach with a coupled social ecological framework and integrates climatic ecological economic societal and governance indicators rather than measuring recovery dynamics of a state variable through time. Ecological vulnerability is defined as sensitivity and recovery potential and these are scored from trait based indicators and management related indicators such as summer fishing moratorium overlap and stock enhancement. Climate hazards are estimated from CMIP6 model anomalies between 1975 to 2004 and 2030 to 2059 and combined with species occurrence probability from AquaMaps to compute hazard scores. Ecological risk is computed as Eriskt equals Ht plus ESt minus ERt and social ecological risk is computed as SEriskr equals Hr plus SSr minus SACr which are constructed indices. Because resilience is operationalized as recovery potential and adaptive capacity derived from traits and indicator scores rather than observed recovery after disturbance the measurement is inferred. The target variable type is interaction because recovery potential includes indicators tied to management and exploitation and the study also uses portfolio diversification and catch composition reliance as exposure which are interaction network like system properties rather than direct abundance recovery. The level is community because the analysis aggregates across multiple species into taxa and evaluates vulnerability and risk for fisheries resources rather than a single population. The approach is modeling and simulation because it uses climate projection model outputs and spatial overlay calculations rather than field experiments or direct time series recovery observations. The first author affiliation is Stony Brook University in the United States. The assessment focuses on China Seas and eleven coastal regions in China so the field country is China. The habitat is marine neritic because the study concerns coastal and shelf marine capture fisheries in the China Seas and uses marine ecoregions of coastal and shelf areas. No explicit disturbance response observation window is defined because hazards are represented by projected anomalies and indicator scores so disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

The abstract defines resilience as the ability of forests to recover from a disturbance and return to a pre-disturbed state and states that the study used three decades of Landsat time series data to quantify forest resilience to spruce budworm infestations. The introduction states that Ingrisch and Bahn proposed a framework that represents resilience as a combination of both impact and recovery rate from a disturbance and the study states it applies this framework. The methods quantify resilience using spectral impact and spectral recovery rate and perturbation derived from Landsat surface reflectance time series and Table 2 defines recovery rate as the 5 year change in the spectral index normalized to baseline. Because resilience is quantified from time series trajectories of spectral indexes following the outbreak it is observed rather than inferred. The quantification is recovery rate because the methods explicitly calculate recovery rate as the slope of return over five post disturbance years and the framework includes recovery rate as a core axis. The target variables used to quantify resilience are the spectral indexes GNDVI MSI and NBR which the paper describes as greenness vegetation water content and structural characteristics and these are used as remote sensing indicators of ecosystem functioning. The study area is the closed canopy boreal forest sub ecozone of Quebec Canada between 48 N and 52 N so the average coordinate is N50.0 W72.0 and the range is within two degrees. The first author affiliation is the University of British Columbia in Canada. The disturbance is spruce budworm infestations described as a native defoliator so it is an unmanipulated biotic disturbance. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because pixels were selected where infestation stopped and stands remained infestation free for another five years enabling a defined recovery window. Observation duration spans Landsat composites from 1991 to 2022 which is 31 years or 11315 days and the resilience analysis uses the post 2006 outbreak with data through 2022 which is 16 years or 5840 days and this is the disturbance and resilience observation window used in the methods.

Framework: ecological stability = resistance + engineering resilience + temporal stability + final recovery
Justification: The study used a modularized lake mesocosm experiment with repeated sampling on days 1 4 7 9 14 and 28 and quantified responses relative to undisturbed controls. It explicitly quantified resilience for pulse disturbances as the slope of the linear regression of the difference between disturbed and control communities from day 7 to day 28 which is a rate of recovery based on observed time series. The paper states ecological stability as a framework with metrics including resistance engineering resilience temporal stability and final recovery. The main integrative metric OEV was measured as the area under the curve of the log response ratio of biomass or abundance and of community dissimilarity or similarity relative to controls over the entire observation period which uses observed functional and compositional trajectories. Functional targets were zooplankton biomass phytoplankton biomass derived from chlorophyll a and bacterial abundance and compositional targets were zooplankton genus composition and phytoplankton and bacterial ASV composition. The experiment was performed in five different lakes in Sweden and the first author affiliation is Lund University in Sweden. The pulse disturbance was fish addition for the first 7 days and the press disturbance was constant shading reducing incoming light by approximately 50 percent. Each experiment lasted 28 days so the duration between first and last measurement was 27 days.

The study erected rainfall manipulation structures to simulate severe drought and compared drought subplots with paired control subplots within each whole plot. It tracked seeded species cover and total plant biomass during experimental drought and in the post-drought growing season. The Methods define resistance as the difference between drought and control subplots during 2009 and recovery as the difference between 2010 and 2009 and resilience as combining resistance and recovery as the ratio of post-drought 2010 to control 2010 seeded species cover or biomass. This makes resilience a direct calculation from observed cover and biomass data rather than a proxy. Because resilience is quantified as a ratio of post-drought to control in 2010 it represents the degree of return relative to the control state rather than a rate or time. The target variables used for resilience are seeded species cover and aboveground biomass. These variables summarize the plant community within subplots so the level is community. The first author affiliation is Kansas State University in the United States. The field site is in South-Central Nebraska USA with coordinates given as 40.8410N 98.8350W. The drought treatment ran from mid-March through early October 2009 and roofs were removed in October 2009 with sampling continuing in 2010 so the disturbance is a manipulated climatic press and the observation window for drought and recovery spans May 2009 to August 2010.

The study uses a global meta-analysis of overfished marine fish and invertebrate stocks and models recovery using stock assessment data where population biomass declined below half of BMSY. It defines recovery times as the number of years between depletion defined as B less than 0.5 BMSY and recovery to BMSY. Resilience is evaluated through modeled recovery times and their uncertainty using a Bayesian regression model based on stochastic population dynamics with survival analysis for censored incomplete recoveries. The target variable used for resilience quantification is stock biomass relative to BMSY. The biological level is population because the analysis is of individual stocks. The approach is modeling and simulation because recovery times are predicted from a Bayesian regression model rather than measured as a direct experimental recovery. The disturbance is fishing and overexploitation described as fishing mortality and harvest rates which is biological resource use and it is unmanipulated in the sense of observational stock assessment time series rather than an imposed experiment. The disturbance is treated as press because fishing mortality during recovery is analyzed as a sustained rate such as F FMSY and scenarios include continued fishing at FMSY or 2 FMSY. The first author affiliation is Rutgers University in New Brunswick New Jersey USA. The field country and location are global because the analysis is described as global and includes 153 stocks without specifying a single country for data collection. Habitat is marine neritic because the subjects are marine fish and invertebrate stocks managed in fisheries and the paper discusses marine populations and fisheries recovery.

The study defines resilience as the capacity of an ecosystem to return to the condition previous to a disturbance once it is suppressed and it evaluates grazing histories as the disturbance context. It uses field measurements of vegetation and soil structure and seedling emergence and survival in April 2007 2008 and 2009 and survival tracking from April 2007 until April 2009. It identifies a critical threshold for values of SDI between 43 and 48 percent and states that the system could tolerate approximately 45 percent of change in vegetation and soil variables before key ecosystem processes significantly decrease which is a threshold magnitude framing. The paper explicitly states greater ecosystem stability is related to a greater resistance and resilience to a disturbance factor which supports the framework stability = resistance + resilience. Resilience loss is linked to crossing the critical threshold and to loss of recruitment and functional integrity. The target variable used to define the resilience loss threshold is the Structural Degradation Index built from vegetation cover patch structure and soil depth variables. The work was carried out at Pilcaniyeu Campo Anexo of EEA INTA Bariloche Río Negro Argentina with coordinates 70 35 21.8 W and 41 01 42 S. The disturbance factor is overgrazing with stocking rates sustained for over 15 years or over 80 years indicating a press type disturbance. The observation window for recruitment and survival spans from April 2007 to April 2009 which is two years.

The study quantified resilience using tree ring width index chronologies with Lloret et al. 2011 indices where growth resilience is defined as the capacity of the tree to reach pre disturbance growth levels and calculated as RWIpost divided by RWIpre. Resistance and recovery were also calculated and discussed together with resilience as growth resistance recovery and resilience indices. The target variable used in the resilience calculation was ring width index derived from tree ring width measurements. Drought years were identified retrospectively using a daily soil water balance model and treated as 2015 and 2018 to 2020 drought events. The disturbance was not experimentally imposed and was analysed as extreme drought events so it is unmanipulated climatic disturbance. The post drought reference period was chosen as 2 years and the pre drought reference period was 2010 to 2014 which makes resilience a comparison of post versus pre drought growth level. Trees were sampled in four forest plots in northeastern France and Table 1 provides site coordinates. The first author affiliation is Université de Lorraine INRAE in Champenoux France. The observation window for resilience metrics spans from the start of the pre drought period 2010 to the end of the post drought period for the 2018 to 2020 drought which is 2022.

Framework: compositional resilience + structural resilience + functional resilience
Justification: The introduction states that resilience is best measured as the time required for an ecosystem to regain a pre-disturbance state and the study tests whether moth communities returned to pre-logging composition species richness and guild diversity. The methods describe experimental timber harvest treatments including clear-cutting shelterwood harvests and group selection harvests with unlogged controls and moths were sampled one year prior to harvest in 2007 and postharvest in 2009 to 2011. Compositional resilience was evaluated with NMDS and MRPP using log-transformed species abundance data and the discussion states that community composition converged to a near preharvest condition three years after shelterwood management. Structural resilience was evaluated with repeated measures models using total species richness and richness of Quercus feeders and the discussion states species diversity converged to a near preharvest condition three years after shelterwood management. Functional resilience was evaluated with repeated measures models using richness within feeding guilds and richness of known pollinators. The field site is Morgan-Monroe State Forest in south-central Indiana USA with coordinates given as 39 31 02 800 N and 86 44 01 300 W. The first author affiliation is Drake University in Des Moines Iowa USA. The disturbance is timber harvest which is a logging practice and was applied in 2008 with postharvest recovery observed afterward making it a pulse disturbance. The observation window spans from 2007 to 2011 which is four years.

The study defines ecosystem productivity resilience as the ability to maintain and recover carbon sequestration function under disturbance and it quantifies it using critical slowing down metrics derived from satellite based gross primary production time series. The methods state that EPR was quantified using AR(1) and that lag one autocorrelation captures system memory and recovery rate. The framework section states that structure attributes are resistance latitude and precariousness and that these reflect recovery capacity limits systemic change resistance and proximity to critical thresholds. The target variable used for resilience quantification is gross primary production which the introduction describes as a proxy for productivity and ecosystem functioning. The analysis is applied across China from 2000 to 2018 using satellite products and flux tower validation which is field observation. The disturbance context used for EPR responses is climatic water availability represented by SPEI and documented severe droughts and flooding and the disturbance is not experimentally manipulated. The disturbance is treated as persistent climate variability across the full time series rather than a single imposed event. The first author affiliation is Beijing Forestry University in Beijing China. The field data are from forests across China and the paper does not provide explicit coordinates so an average national location is used. The observation duration corresponds to the 2000 to 2018 GPP time series used for EPR estimation.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as an ecosystem capacity to recover and resist disturbances and stabilize during crises. The methods quantify ecological resilience using a constructed Size-Density-Morphology framework corrected by NPP with ER computed as ERʹ multiplied by NPPi divided by NPPmean. The ER components are derived from built-up area relative to ecological infrastructure and ecological footprint relative to ecological carrying capacity and source sink landscape distances rather than from observed recovery after a disturbance. The study quantifies spatial structure conflict using a Spatial Structure Conflict Index based on landscape pattern indices and land use transitions among Production Living Ecological spaces. The data sources are multi-source remote sensing and socioeconomic statistics and land cover datasets for 2000 to 2022 which are used to compute indices at county level. Because resilience is assessed from indices and proxies without measuring post disturbance recovery dynamics the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The core resilience analysis is tied to land spatial structure and land use conflict metrics and thus the target variable type is land use. The analysis is conducted across 86 prefecture level cities and 544 counties and districts which is a landscape scale assessment. The first author affiliation is Guangdong University of Finance and Economics in Guangzhou China so the institution country is CN. The study area is China Southeast Coastal Areas within China so the field country is CN.
