The study includes multiple land cover types across provinces and coastal and inland areas so the habitat type is multi. No explicit disturbance event is imposed or tracked for recovery so disturbance type pattern and observation duration for disturbance response are not specified.

The study measured resilience using annual observational data because it states that they measured the percent ground cover of Sesuvium and abundance of Achyra annually for 10 consecutive years on islands with lizards present and absent. Resilience was explicitly operationalized as recovery rate because the Methods state they tested the hypothesis that lizards increase the recovery rate resilience of Sesuvium during a 1 yr interval following each hurricane by computing a recovery rate formula based on percent cover before and after hurricanes. The target variable used to quantify resilience was percent cover because the recovery rate formula uses P values defined as percent cover measurements. The approach is field observation because the work involved transects on islands and annual photographs and counts without experimental manipulation of hurricanes. The disturbance was hurricanes and treated as unmanipulated events and the text describes a series of hurricanes impacting the study site and recovery measured in the 1 yr interval following each hurricane which matches a pulse disturbance. The framework links stability properties to temporal variability and resilience because the Discussion concludes predators can affect two stability properties of producers temporal variability and resilience and the Methods describe testing temporal variability increased stability and recovery rate resilience. The field setting is the Exuma Cays in the central Bahamas. The first author affiliation is University of California Davis in the United States. Observation duration spans 10 consecutive years of annual measurements from 1997 to 2006 which is 3650 days.

The study states it estimated ecosystem resilience on 107 lakes employing an assessment framework and a long-term dataset of aquatic vegetation and algal blooms derived from Landsat imagery. The methods describe an ecological risk assessment framework that integrates SAV and AB monitoring to distinguish ecological states and risks and it classifies lakes into ADS HER MER and LER based on SAV trends coverage thresholds and AB frequency rather than measuring recovery after a defined disturbance. Resilience is discussed as being bolstered by high SAV coverage but the framework does not define resilience as an equation with other components. Because resilience is operationalized through state indicators and risk categories using SAV and AB patterns and thresholds it is inferred rather than directly quantified as recovery rate time degree resistance latitude or invariability. The target variables used in the core assessment are SAV coverage and AB frequency derived from satellite mapping which are ecosystem state indicators. The study area is the Yangtze Plain in China with bounds 28.0 to 33.50 N and 113.30 to 121.00 E and the average coordinate is the midpoint. The first author affiliation is in Nanjing China. The approach is remote sensing observation using Landsat imagery from 1989 to 2023. The habitat is shallow freshwater lakes which fit Wetland. The resilience related indicators include submerged aquatic vegetation which is a plant group.

The study states that it used a serial passage experiment with a 34 species defined bacterial community through different levels of pulse antibiotic disturbance and with or without species immigration. It explicitly defines ecological resilience as the ability of a community not only to withstand a perturbation ecological resistance but also to recover from it. It reports that resilience was quantified for each community separately as the KL divergence of community composition over time after the disturbance relative to the predisturbance state. KL divergence is computed from species composition data and compares post disturbance and post recovery states to the predisturbance state which is a recovery degree measure. The disturbance is an imposed streptomycin antibiotic pulse with concentrations 4 16 and 128 micrograms per millilitre and an antibiotic free control. The disturbance is therefore manipulated and chemical and treated as a pulse epoch. The observation window relevant to resilience quantification spans from before adding streptomycin day 16 to after the pulse day 32 and after recovery day 48 and the experiment lasted 48 days. The target variable used to quantify resilience is community composition derived from amplicon sequencing. The biological level is community because the metric is based on multispecies composition. The approach is a controlled serial passage in vitro experiment which fits field experiment among the provided options. The first author affiliation listed is Wellcome Sanger Institute Cambridge UK. No field country habitat type or geographic coordinates apply because the experiment was conducted in laboratory plates and the methods state the species are not representative of a particular natural system.

The study states it developed a database of post-fire environments and describes it as a database of post-fire forest recovery using dendrochronological samples and plots with observed post-fire seedlings dated to year of establishment and observed post-fire seedling abundance in plots. It quantifies resilience by comparing predicted post-fire seedling densities to 19th-century tree density thresholds and uses the percentage of the montane zone exceeding those thresholds as suitability which is a degree of recovery relative to a reference state. The target variable used for this resilience assessment is post-fire seedling densities expressed as seedlings per hectare for ponderosa pine and Douglas-fir. The focal taxa are Pinus ponderosa and Pseudotsuga menziesii which are plants. The disturbance context is wildfire because all surveyed fires occurred 1988 to 2010 and the analysis is post-fire recovery. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event because establishment is modelled as occurring in the year following fire occurrence and time since fire is used and fires have occurrence dates. The approach combines field observation with statistical models and future projections using general circulation models. The first author affiliation is University of Colorado Boulder in the United States. The field data are from the Southern Rocky Mountains Ecoregion in southern Wyoming central and western Colorado and northern New Mexico in the United States. No coordinates are provided in the text so the location is approximated as the central southern Rocky Mountains Ecoregion and the range is set to 0 because only an average point is reported. The observation window for reconstructed and projected dynamics is given as 1981 to 2099 which is 29 years plus 78 years in days.

The study states that it monitored coral and fish assemblages over 14 years on fixed sites and compared communities prior to the disturbance with those present in 1992 and subsequent changes through to 1998. Resilience is defined in the Introduction as returning to a similar pre disturbance structure after an initial deviation and it is presented together with resistance under stability as stability equals resistance plus resilience. Resilience is quantified with observed recovery to pre impact levels because hard coral cover became indistinguishable from pre impact levels by 1998 and most fish taxa recovered to pre impact levels by 1998 and species richness levels in 1998 were not significantly different from pre disturbance levels for three families. The target variables directly used in these recovery comparisons are live hard coral cover and fish abundance and fish species richness. These variables are community level because they are assemblage measures across multiple coral types and multiple fish species and families. The approach is field observation because surveys were conducted on reefs using manta tow video transects line intercept surveys timed swims and absolute transect counts. The disturbance is a natural storm related event because coral cover decreased probably as a result of storm damage and Doherty et al concluded a combination of storms was the most likely cause and it was not experimentally imposed. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because the loss occurred sometime between November 1987 and October 1989 and recovery was tracked afterward. The first author affiliation is the Australian Institute of Marine Science in Townsville Queensland Australia. The field setting is the southern Great Barrier Reef Australia including Capricorn Bunkers reefs and Swains sector reefs. Figure 1 indicates reefs at the southern end of the Great Barrier Reef and the study states sites were spread over 80 km so an average coordinate is provided with a nonzero range. The habitat is coral reef on the Great Barrier Reef which fits Marine Neritic. The observation duration spans 14 years which is 14 times 365 days.

The study is a multisite drought manipulation experiment that examines drought resistance and recovery at five grassland sites using field mesocosms. Drought is imposed by rain out shelters with 100 percent rainfall reduction for 72 to 98 consecutive days and is described as a pulsed drought event. Resistance and recovery are calculated from observed aboveground biomass harvests with resistance defined as B1 drought divided by B1 control and recovery defined as B3 drought divided by B3 control. Recovery is defined as the degree to which ecosystem characteristics return to control or pre disturbance levels after a disturbance and the recovery index equals 1 for complete recovery and values above 1 indicate overcompensation. The target variable used in the resilience related quantification is aboveground biomass production. The framework is explicitly stated as resistance and recovery being two important facets of ecological stability. The first author affiliation is Ernst Moritz Arndt University Greifswald in Germany. Table 1 provides site coordinates for Belgium Bulgaria Germany France and Turkey and the average of those coordinates is N45.64269 E13.17386 with the farthest site differing by 20.20044 degrees when summing maximum latitude and longitude differences. Biomass was harvested two weeks before drought at the end of drought and at peak biomass in the following growing season and recovery is assessed at peak following year which is within 1 year after the experimental drought.

The paper defines resilience as a direct dynamical response because it states that resilience r describes the per capita rate at which abundances recover following a disturbance and calculates r as log(N(t+τ)/N(t))/τ. The study quantifies resilience from simulated abundance time series because it introduces three models that simulate species abundance dynamics and states that for each model they simulated 1000 independent iterations of 100 time units and measured resilience following the empirical methods described. The framework treats resilience alongside resistance and invariance because it focuses on three common stability metrics resilience resistance and invariance and presents working definitions for all three. The quantification corresponds to recovery rate because resilience is explicitly defined as the rate of return towards equilibrium following a disturbance and as the per capita rate at which abundances recover. The target variable is abundance because resilience is calculated from N(t) and the models simulate species abundance dynamics. The level is population because the focal variable is species abundance and resilience is defined for species abundances. The approach is modeling and simulation because the Methods section describes stochastic dynamical systems models and simulated dynamics in R. The first author affiliation is in Leipzig Germany at the Helmholtz Centre for Environmental Research so the institution country is DE. The disturbance is modelled as discrete pulses because disturbances are abstracted into discrete pulses that lead to near instantaneous changes in species abundances and disturbances occur at discrete moments in time ti. Disturbance examples include extreme weather events and anthropogenic disturbances such as logging mowing or fishing so the disturbance types include climatic and biological resource use in a model context. The observation duration is 100 time units per simulation iteration and the paper does not provide a conversion from time units to days so it is expressed as 100 time units which equals 36500 days if one time unit is treated as one year is not stated so the only grounded duration is 100 time units and using the required days output with 365 per year yields 36500 days.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + structural soundness + resilience + ecosystem service maintenance + environmental loading
Justification: The study evaluates urban ecosystem health using an integrated framework combining the emergy-based Urban Ecosystem Health Indicator and Set Pair Analysis using panel data from 2000 to 2020. It states that five dimensions vigor structural soundness resilience ecosystem service maintenance and environmental loading are systematically evaluated which defines a framework where ecosystem health includes resilience as one component. Resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics of an observed ecological state variable after a disturbance but from emergy based indicators such as carrying capacity waste burden environmental stress index and import reliance listed under the resilience dimension. The methods describe comparing measured indicator values for each city against an optimal benchmark and a worst case benchmark and computing a relative health index which is a constructed index rather than observed recovery. Data acquisition relies on field surveys complemented by statistical yearbooks published by local governments indicating field observation of socio economic and resource flow data. The study sites are twenty representative Chinese cities so the field country is China. The first author affiliation is Hezhou University Guangxi China so the institution country is China. The context is explicitly urban ecosystems so the habitat type is Urban. No coordinates are provided in the text for the cities or figures so location and range are NA. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for disturbance response are not applicable.

The introduction defines resilience as having two components with the statement resilience can be broken down into two components the first is resistance and the second is recovery. The methods quantify resistance as the ratio of change in predation within a 24 hr period immediately after insecticide exposure and quantify recovery as the ratio of change in predation 5 days after exposure. These ratios are based on observed predation rates in a 24 hr period for each predator species after deltamethrin exposure treatments and are extrapolated to community level predation for predator communities associated with 256 arable fields in the UK. The disturbance is deltamethrin exposure applied to individuals with 3.1 ng dissolved in 1 microlitre water which is a manipulated chemical disturbance. The disturbance is treated as a discrete exposure event either immediately before the predation assessment or 5 days before the predation assessment which matches a pulse framing. The target variable used in the resilience metrics is community level predation change which is an ecosystem service and thus an ecosystem function. The laboratory component is an indoor experiment in a controlled environment room and the community component models community response ratios using FSE field community data. The first author affiliation is NERC Centre for Ecology and Hydrology Wallingford UK which is in GB. The field data are from UK arable fields and predators were collected in Oxfordshire and the FSE communities are UK arable fields so the field country is GB. The observation window spans the 5 day starvation period plus the 24 hr predation assessment giving 6 days between first and last measurement for the resistance and recovery treatments.

The introduction defines resilience as communities being capable of returning to their predisturbance structure and composition and contrasts it with resistance as no significant changes due to disturbance. The methods use three 20-year chronosequences with 25 sites that had experienced a single wildfire event during the last 20 years between 1994 and 2014 and paired burned and unburned plots. Plant cover and estimated biomass were measured in transects and soil microbial communities were measured using OTU relative abundance from DNA sequencing and phylogenetic diversity was calculated as abundance weighted stdMPD and phylogenetic beta diversity as weighted UniFrac. The methods state that to estimate the postfire recovery they used the difference between burned and unburned transects as the dependent variable and time since fire as the fixed effect and they calculated the average recovery times for all variables by interpolation or extrapolation through the equation of the fitted model. The results report recovery times such as plant cover recovered after 17 years and plant phylogenetic alpha diversity and beta diversity were estimated to recover unburned levels on average after 20 and 32 years and fungal and bacterial communities recovered their prefire phylogenetic beta diversity after 19 years. The disturbance is wildfire and sites experienced a single wildfire event indicating an unmanipulated pulse event. The study area is in Valencia E Spain and the first author affiliation is Centro de Investigaciones sobre Desertificacion in Valencia Spain. The habitat is described as typical Mediterranean scrublands on S SW oriented slopes. No coordinates are provided in the text for the sites or chronosequences.

The introduction states that the study focuses on resilience as resistance and that they measured it by evaluating changes in ecosystem functions in response to disturbance. The methods describe a manipulative field experiment in which hypoxia was induced by securing black plastic sheets to the seafloor to prevent oxygen exchange and oxygen production. The treatments included repeated three day hypoxic pulses and a long uninterrupted thirty day hypoxic period and measurements were done twenty four hours after the disturbance ended. Ecosystem multifunctionality was quantified from multiple measured ecosystem functions listed in Table 1 including oxygen consumption nutrient fluxes chlorophyll a secondary production pigment ratios and community bioturbation potential. Because resilience was operationalized as resistance the quantification is based on the magnitude of change in these functions under disturbance relative to control rather than recovery. The experimental site is given as Gulf of Finland northern Baltic Sea with coordinates 59 85002400N 2381503700E. The first author affiliation is at University of Helsinki in Finland. The habitat is a shallow coastal sedimentary ecosystem at five meter depth which fits Marine Neritic. The disturbance is manipulated hypoxia which the paper frames as a stressor involving low oxygen levels and is treated as both pulse and press in the experimental design. The observation window spans the longest treatment of thirty days plus the repeated pulse schedule and the final twenty four hour period before measurement which totals fifty four days.

The study used outdoor experimental stream channels in Kentucky USA and imposed simulated spates by increasing current and scouring tiles with a high pressure hose. Resistance was measured as change in algal biomass and taxonomic structure across a disturbance. Resilience was defined as convergence with a temporally changing undisturbed control community rather than return to pre disturbance state. Resilience was assessed using post spate rates of cell accrual on control and disturbed tiles with net algal growth rates estimated from the rate of change in natural log transformed cell densities during each recovery period. Resilience in community composition was assessed by comparing trajectories of control and disturbed communities on DCA ordination plots. The target variables directly used for resilience quantification were cell densities and diatom assemblage composition. The organisms quantified included diatoms and a chrysophyte and filamentous green algae. The study site was the University of Louisville experimental stream facility in Bernheim Forest Nature Preserve Bullitt County Kentucky. The experiment ran from the initial disturbance on day 0 through the final spate on day 33.

The study objective is to understand geographic variations in resistance to drought in Mediterranean sclerophyllous forests of central Chile. It uses MODIS NDVI temporal series with 729 observations recorded between 18/02/2000 and 18/02/2017 and quantifies proportional NDVI change between pre drought and drought periods and NDVI trend magnitudes as resistance response variables. NDVI is described as usable for estimating aerial net primary production and the paper states NDVI and therefore forest productivity. Resilience is discussed conceptually as an attribute that provides forest ecosystems with greater resilience and the text also states that resistance may provide valuable information and the aims use indicators of resistance to drought. The disturbance is the 2010 to 2017 mega drought described as the most prolonged drought and as reduced precipitation affecting central Chile. The drought is treated as an uninterrupted sequence of dry years starting in 2010 and analyzed as a drought period rather than a discrete event. The study area is central Chile between 71.5 and 70.2 W and 33.1 and 34.1 S and the average coordinate is reported. The first author affiliation is Universidad de La Frontera in Temuco Chile. The habitat is native Mediterranean sclerophyllous forest. Field validation identifies tree species and canopy loss but the resilience quantification is based on NDVI productivity metrics.

Framework: ecosystem resilience = engineering resilience + ecological resilience
Disturbance type: ['Unmanipulated_Fire', 'Unmanipulated_Hydrological', 'Unmanipulated_Landuse and infrastructure development', 'Unmanipulated_Climatic', 'Unmanipulated_Geophysical', 'Unmanipulated_Fire', 'Unmanipulated_Landuse and infrastructure development']
Justification: The methods state that sedimentary records were analysed using palynology and microcharcoal and that the records were used as indicators of mangrove forest dynamics and resilience. The data analysis section defines ecosystem resilience as engineering resilience and ecological resilience and states that engineering resilience was quantified using pollen data with timing variables and recovery time from Tmin to Tmax and recovery rate. Table 2 reports recovery time in years for multiple disturbance events and describes full recovery of pollen abundance ranging from 14 to 903 years. The study also evaluates compositional recovery using NMDS comparing Jaccard dissimilarity between recovered mangroves and a baseline range of variability prior to disturbance. The target variables used to quantify resilience are mangrove pollen abundance and mangrove pollen composition. These pollen measures represent multiple mangrove taxa so the level is community. The approach is field observation because the study collected sediment cores in July 2019 from Mantehage Island and Likupang and analysed fossil proxies rather than running an experiment or simulation. The first author affiliation is the University of Leicester in the United Kingdom. The field sites are in North Sulawesi Indonesia and the core coordinates are given for MH LK1 and LK2. The three coordinates are within less than two degrees so the location range is 0 and the average coordinate is approximately N1.688 E124.846. The habitat is mangrove intertidal forest which fits the Coastal category. The focal organisms are mangrove plants identified by pollen so the taxon is Plantae. Disturbances listed and analysed include fire hydrological change deforestation and aquaculture strong El Nino storms possible tsunamis and volcanic eruption uplift and these are treated as discrete events in Table 2 so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The observation window spans from 5955 BCE to 2019 CE in the MH core which is about 8000 years or 2921825 days.

The study analyzes long term empirical time series from five freshwater ecosystems and applies early warning indicators that quantify loss of resilience. It defines critical slowing down as a slowing return rate to equilibrium after a small perturbation and links this to increasing autocorrelation at lag 1 and other metrics. The EWIs are calculated on prebreakpoint residual time series of observed state variables including Secchi depth water transparency nonalgal attenuation phytoplankton biomass cyanobacteria cryptophytes zooplankton and mussel larvae and phytoplankton functional group biomasses. These are direct observational data from natural lakes and the analysis quantifies resilience through temporal metrics including AR 1 SD DR and SK rather than through static proxies. The first author affiliation listed is Leibniz Institute of Freshwater Ecology and Inland Fisheries in Berlin Germany. The case study lakes are explicitly listed as Lake Müggelsee Germany Lake Veluwemeer The Netherlands Lake Zwemlust The Netherlands Lake Washington United States and Lake Võrtsjärv Estonia. The paper attributes shifts to drivers and processes including reoligotrophication eutrophication and trophic cascade and predation pressure which are treated as ongoing drivers leading up to breakpoints rather than discrete imposed events. Exact coordinates and a unified observation start and end date across all time series are not provided in the text excerpt. The habitat is freshwater lake ecosystems which fits Wetland in the provided habitat options. Because the target variables span multiple taxa and also include abiotic and ecosystem level measures a single kingdom cannot be assigned from the resilience quantification targets.

Framework: stability = return to steady state after perturbation
Justification: The paper defines dynamic stability as the return to steady state after perturbation and states that if populations deviate by a small amount from their equilibria and then return the chain is locally stable. It then quantifies the response as the return time RT defined as the time required by the system to return to equilibrium after a perturbation and estimated as RT = -1 real(lambda max). Resilience is directly linked to RT because the text states ecosystem resilience increases decreased RTs and that results suggest feasibility and resilience are a function of productivity. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study develops models with and without detritus and assesses stability by evaluating eigenvalues of the community matrix and repeats the process 1000 times for each productivity. The target variable used for resilience quantification is RT derived from eigenvalues rather than a field measured state variable. The models were initialized with data from 5 to 10 year studies of actual soil food webs and Table 1 lists sites in Colorado and Georgia United States and Marknesse Netherlands and Orbyhus Sweden so the field countries are US NL and SE. The first author affiliation is University of Northern Colorado in Greeley Colorado so the institution country is US. The disturbance is a modeled perturbation around equilibrium described as populations deviate by a small amount from their equilibria so it is model structural. The perturbation is treated as a discrete deviation followed by return so the pattern is pulse. The observation duration is taken from the initialization data described as 5 to 10 year studies so 10 years equals 3650 days. Habitat type is coded as agricultural because Table 1 includes agricultural field stations and multiple farming systems and the soil food web parameterization includes conventional tillage and integrated farming and barley nitrogen treatments.

The introduction defines resilience as the time taken for ecosystem properties to return to equilibrium following a perturbation and defines resistance as the degree to which community properties change following a perturbation and it discusses managers responding to varying levels of resistance and resilience. The methods quantify change using a long term vegetation dataset with transects censused in 1983 2004 2009 and 2014 and species presence summed to a frequency score as a measure of species abundance and then use fuzzy clustering of species composition to track community membership through time. The methods state vegetation at a site was considered resilient to change if it showed reversible change returned to fuzzy membership at or above 0.5 for a particular vegetation community during the survey period which operationalizes return over time. The disturbance regime is described as fire with fire history compiled from the mid 1960s to the present and the discussion describes post fire transitions and reestablished membership with Banksia thicket over time. The study system is upland swamp communities within O’Hares Creek catchment in Dharawal National Park and Nature Reserve 45 km southeast of Sydney Australia and upland swamps are described as mire ecosystems. The observation window for resilience spans 1983 to 2014 which is 31 years or 11315 days.

The study states that it quantified engineering resilience as the degree of recovery of native cover and richness in unseeded disturbed vs unseeded undisturbed plots up to 4 yr after treatment. This uses observed plot data on native cover and native species richness measured repeatedly after a disturbance treatment. Disturbance was imposed by digging up and removing plant biomass from the plots. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event applied at the end of the growing season with recovery tracked for years which matches a pulse disturbance. The target variables used to quantify resilience are native cover and native species richness. These variables are community level because they summarize multiple plant species within 1 m2 plots. The approach is a field experiment because treatments were applied in situ in grasslands with replicated plots and blocks. The first author affiliation is Rocky Mountain Research Station United States Forest Service in the United States. Field data were collected in western Montana USA and the Caldenal of La Pampa Argentina with centroids given as 46.8 N 114.0 W and 37.3 S 64.6 W. The habitat is described as semiarid grasslands dominated by native perennial grasses. The observation duration for resilience is four years after disturbance.

The study states that it estimated the rate of vegetation recovery after disturbance and calls this resilience. It defines resilience as the rate of vegetation recovery up to six months after applying the soil disturbance treatment. Resilience was measured in no biocide and no seed addition plots and calculated from percent cover of vegetation at each sampling time using Timson’s index. Percent cover of each plant species was estimated repeatedly at half one two four six and nine months after the first rain. The disturbance was an applied soil turnover treatment described as turning over the soil within plots. Because the disturbance is a discrete treatment and recovery is tracked after it the disturbance pattern is pulse. The target variable used to quantify resilience is vegetation percent cover which is a cover variable and it represents the local plant community. The work is a field experiment with replicated 1 by 1 m plots across sites. The first author affiliation is in Santa Rosa La Pampa Argentina. Field sites were in Georgia Armenia Turkey California in the United States and La Pampa province in Argentina. The sites are described as natural grasslands with deep soils.

Framework: resilience = ability of a forest to absorb disturbance + maintain similar structure and function
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the ability of a forest to absorb disturbance and maintain similar structure and function. The study evaluates whether short-interval stand-replacing fires can erode lodgepole pine forest resilience via increased burn severity reduced early postfire tree regeneration reduced carbon stocks and slower carbon recovery. The methods state that fires reburned young forests during summer 2016 and that during summer 2017 the authors sampled plots in reburns and nearby young forests that did not reburn. The study also simulated stand development with and without reburns to assess carbon recovery trajectories. The simulations explicitly quantify recovery by stating that increased carbon loss plus sparse tree regeneration delayed simulated carbon recovery by more than 150 years and that they considered the stand to have recovered its aboveground C stock when it reached 150 Mg C ha−1. This is a recovery time measure because it is the time to reach a defined recovered carbon stock level. The target variable used for resilience quantification is aboveground carbon stocks and their recovery trajectory which is an ecosystem level function. The disturbance is wildfire that occurred naturally in 2016 and is treated as a discrete event followed by postfire measurements and modeled recovery. The first author affiliation is University of Wisconsin Madison in the United States. The study area is Greater Yellowstone in Wyoming United States including Yellowstone National Park and Grand Teton National Park. Plot center coordinates are mentioned as recorded in UTM NAD83 Zone 12N but no decimal degree coordinates are provided in the text. The observation window for disturbance and resilience measurements spans from the 2016 fires to the 2017 field sampling which is one year.

The study presents MarshRAM as a rapid assessment method that produces indices including the Wetland Disturbance Index and the Index of Marsh Integrity. The IMI is generated from a walking transect that tallies dominant salt marsh community cover types and then applies expert derived coefficients of community integrity based on perceived indication of degradation and habitat value. The coefficients were estimated through consensus of experienced salt marsh scientists and are not measured as recovery dynamics after a disturbance. The paper states that salt marsh community cover can serve as an indicator of salt marsh resilience which indicates resilience is inferred from community cover composition rather than directly measured recovery. No explicit resilience framework equation is defined in the introduction methods or discussion. The approach is field observation because data collection consisted of field and office components using onsite and remote observations and walking transects. The first author affiliation is Rhode Island Natural History Survey University of Rhode Island Kingston RI USA. The field sites are thirty one salt marshes in Rhode Island USA. Figure 1 shows distribution across Rhode Island and Table 2 lists Rhode Island sites and the text states the assessments were conducted in 2017 and 2018 during mid July through September but does not define a disturbance event window for recovery duration. The habitat is salt marsh which fits the coastal habitat type. The resilience related target variable is vegetation community composition and cover types such as Meadow High Marsh and Dieoff Bare Depression. The taxon is plants because the IMI is based on vegetation community cover types including Spartina and Phragmites.

The study quantified resilience from remote sensing time series using critical slowing down indicators with “the restoration rate (λ) calculated from both the autocorrelation at lag-1 (AC1) and variance” and it states that “Resilience, defined as the rate at which a system recovers to its equilibrium state following a disturbance”. The recovery rate was directly estimated as “λAC1” and “λvar” from time series equations and applied per pixel using GIMMS NDVI3g MODIS indices and GOSIF. The framework links resilience to stability because the introduction says “stability can be maintained under a certain degree of disturbance which is known as ecosystem resilience”. The target variables used to quantify resilience were vegetation indices and productivity proxies listed as “NDVI kNDVI LAI GPP and GOSIF” which are used as ecosystem function indicators in this analysis. The study area is Eurasia but the datasets are global remote sensing products and the paper integrates “global Orbiting Carbon Observatory-2 Solar-induced chlorophyll fluorescence” and global products while analyzing Eurasia so the data source is global. The disturbance context is climatic because the introduction lists “droughts heatwaves and wildfires” and the attribution focuses on “warming and variability in water conditions” and hydrothermal drivers without experimental manipulation. The disturbance is treated as ongoing climate change across multi-decadal periods with trend shifts around 2005 rather than discrete events so it is press. The observation window for the main resilience time series is given as “1984–2020” for Eurasia resilience change which is 36 years or 13140 days. The habitat coverage includes “nearly all vegetation types” and the analysis includes multiple vegetation types such as forests shrublands and grasslands so the habitat type is multi. The biological subject is vegetation and terrestrial ecosystems so the taxon is Plantae.

Framework: ecological stability = engineering resilience + bounce-back time + resistance + elasticity
Justification: The paper defines ecological resilience as the amount of disturbance a system can absorb before collapsing and reorganizing and it operationalizes resilience using the cross-scale resilience model. Resilience is quantified using metrics derived from functional diversity and functional redundancy across discontinuous body mass aggregations including cross-scale diversity cross-scale redundancy within-scale redundancy and number of body mass aggregations. These resilience metrics are computed from avian community census data and functional trait groupings rather than from direct recovery dynamics following a defined disturbance. The Methods describe calculating resilience metrics from presence absence data body mass discontinuity analysis and functional groups based on diet and foraging strategy. The analyses test relationships between these resilience metrics and community stability and collapse indicators such as species turnover DCA1 abrupt shifts and Jaccard similarity rather than measuring recovery rate time or degree after a disturbance. The Introduction explicitly presents a framework where ecological stability is described as the ability to return to equilibrium post disturbance and is also known as engineering resilience bounce-back time resistance and elasticity. The data source is the North American Breeding Bird Survey and routes are restricted to the continental United States and binned by EPA ecoregions. The first author affiliation is University of Nebraska in the United States. The study subjects are birds and the Methods use avian body masses and avian functional traits. No specific disturbance event is imposed or identified for the resilience metric calculations and the disturbance pattern is not defined. Although the time series spans 1967 to 2014 the resilience measurement is inferred from trait and aggregation structure rather than direct disturbance response so observation duration is not applicable under the instructions.

Framework: resilience = ability to absorb perturbations while remaining in the current stable state
Justification: The paper defines resilience as the ability of the ecosystem to absorb perturbations while remaining in the current stable state. It tests resilience using observational bottom trawl survey data spanning phase transitions in Pavlof Bay Gulf of Alaska 1972 to 2005 and the Scotian Shelf 1970 to 2003. The quantified indicator is spatial variance using the coefficient of variation of the log cod prey ratio calculated among sets in a year. The cod prey ratio is described as a measure of community composition and is the key ecosystem parameter whose variance is tracked. The study treats rising variance as a metric for tracking ecosystem resilience and as an early warning indicator. The Pavlof Bay transition is linked to a climate regime shift and the Scotian Shelf transition is linked to persistent overfishing. The Pavlof Bay location is given as 59 36 N 161 36 W and the Scotian Shelf is identified as the eastern region survey strata 40 to 66 without coordinates. The first author affiliation is Alaska Fisheries Science Center National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration in the United States. The habitat is continental shelf ecosystems and bottom trawl surveys indicating a shallow marine neritic setting. The observation window spans from 1972 to 2005 which is 33 years or 12045 days.

The study used nine years of ostrich movement records and constructed monthly movement networks and tracked network metrics over time before and after the 2011 highly pathogenic avian influenza outbreak. The Methods state that the pre-outbreak data set covered September 2005 to March 2011 and the post-outbreak data set covered September 2011 to March 2014 with outbreak months April to August 2011 excluded. The authors explicitly aimed to compare system structure before and after a major perturbation and to assess return toward pre-outbreak conditions using observed network trajectories. They quantified return time by estimating how long it would take for post-outbreak metric values to return to pre-outbreak values using half-life based temporal predictions and they report expected times such as reaching 90 percent of pre-outbreak values within 48 to 58 months and 58 to 87 months and they also summarize this as 4 to 5 full seasonal cycles. The target variables used for this resilience quantification were network metrics such as size of giant component and network diameter and number of birds moved which are properties of the movement network and thus interaction type variables. The disturbance was the 2011 outbreak of highly pathogenic avian influenza described as a major perturbation and it was not experimentally imposed. The outbreak is treated as a discrete event with containment after 5 months and subsequent post-outbreak recovery which matches a pulse framing. The first author affiliation includes University of Cape Town in South Africa. The data set is drawn from the ostrich industry of the Western Cape of South Africa. No coordinates are provided in the text for farms or the region. The system is an animal production system and farms and movements are central so the dominant habitat context is agricultural. Because the resilience target is a regional movement network the organizational level is landscape and taxon is not applicable under the instructions.

The study states that it uses an in vitro system and a serial passage experiment on a 23 species bacterial model community with patches subjected to different levels of disturbance by the antibiotic streptomycin. It explicitly says it adopts the ecological resilience framework focused on the degree and type of disturbance required to drive a system into a different state and that it examines a constant press disturbance. Resilience is quantified using community divergence from an undisturbed baseline because the figure caption states that divergence of communities ΔC from the no disturbance no mixing baseline is used as a proxy for ecological resilience and Fig. 4 defines resilience quantified as KL divergence of community composition from the streptomycin free condition. The target variable used for resilience quantification is community composition expressed as relative abundance vectors and KL divergence between distributions. The disturbance is experimentally applied streptomycin which is a chemical disturbance and it is treated as press because the text says constant press disturbance and communities were maintained in the appropriate streptomycin concentration across serial transfers. The observation window for the resilience measurement is the 46 day serial passage experiment because communities were serially passaged for 46 days and end point sequencing quantified community state. The first author affiliation is University of Turku and University of Helsinki in Finland. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work is laboratory based with synthetic assemblages.

The paper defines stability as having two main components resistance and resilience. It uses a natural experiment caused by a severe windthrow in June 1997 at a permanent monitoring site in an old beech forest in Lower Saxony Germany. Floristic composition was recorded before the windthrow in 1996 and after the windthrow from 1998 to 2002 and correspondence analysis was used to represent temporal change in floristic composition. Resistance was defined as the reciprocal of the maximum distance a plot moved away from the initial position in correspondence analysis space. Resilience was defined as the length between the maximum distance a plot moved from the initial position and the final plot position divided by the maximum distance and it is 1 if a plot finally fully returned to the initial coordinates and 0 if it does not show any tendency to return. This definition quantifies resilience as degree of return to the initial state rather than time or rate. The target variable used to quantify resilience is the change and return in floristic composition represented by plot positions in correspondence analysis space. The study is based on field monitoring of 42 permanent plots and annual observations after the storm. The first author affiliation is Martin Luther University Halle Wittenberg in Germany. The study site coordinates are given as 51 84004700 to 5184102700 N and 10 81505200 to 1081605100 E and the habitat is near natural beech forest. The disturbance is an unmanipulated storm windthrow treated as a discrete event followed by monitoring. The observation window spans from 1996 to 2002 which is six years.

The study states it examines resilience using models by quantifying both engineering resilience and ecological resilience. It defines engineering resilience as return rate after a perturbation and ecological resilience as the range of increasing stress before collapse. Engineering resilience is quantified by a perturbation experiment where plant individuals are randomly removed and recovery rate is measured as the number of time steps needed to recover to the original plant densities. Ecological resilience is assessed as environmental harshness gradually increases by increasing effective water acquisition radius to represent increasing water stress and comparing how plant density changes. These resilience quantifications use model outputs of plant individuals as points and plant density as the key state variable. The disturbance for engineering resilience is the modeled random elimination of 20% of plant individuals which is a pulse structural disturbance. The disturbance for ecological resilience is gradually increasing water stress which is a press climatic disturbance. The paper also uses field observation via high resolution Google Earth remote sensing images of 425 dryland sites across the globe but the resilience quantification described is performed in modeled ecosystems. The first author affiliation is Nanjing University in China. The field data are described as global drylands across the globe so the field country and location are global. The system is drylands and arid and hyperarid conditions so the dominant habitat type is desert. The focal organisms are plants described as vegetation and plant individuals.

Framework: stability = temporal stability + resistance + resilience + recovery
Justification: The study defines resilience as the rate at which an ecosystem returns to its pre disturbance state after disturbances. Functional resilience is quantified as the log response ratio of total submerged macrophyte biomass between post bloom 2023 and bloom 2022 with higher positive values indicating faster recovery. Compositional resilience is quantified as the Bray Curtis dissimilarity index between bloom and post bloom communities with higher values corresponding to greater resilience. The disturbance is a dinoflagellate algal bloom that occurred at the end of 2021 and the study uses pre bloom 2021 bloom 2022 and post bloom 2023 field survey data. The field survey is a three year seasonal tracking survey from December 2020 to December 2023 at 13 sites in the north of Erhai Lake. The first author affiliation is Yunnan University in Kunming China. The study area coordinates are given as 25 degrees 36 minutes to 25 degrees 58 minutes north and 100 degrees 06 minutes to 100 degrees 18 minutes east and the experimental station is at 25 degrees 56 minutes north 100 degrees 08 minutes east. The study subjects are submerged macrophytes and the stability metrics are calculated at the community level. The study also includes nutrient gradient control experiments lasting from 30 May 2022 to 30 August 2022 to determine stoichiometric homeostasis.
